醉茶志怪 - Accounts of Anomalies over Drunken Tea

Translation created with model: together-qwen3-14b-3step-fewshot / step-by-step-3step-fewshot

    Home
    1

    醉茶志怪 清 李庆辰

    　　余有句云：“事有难言聊志怪，人非吾与更搜神。”窃谓著述家之有说部，诚以蕴蓄于中者，既富且久，而长此寂寐，无以自达，不得已寄情儿女，托兴鬼狐。子虚乌有，感触万端，其志亦可悲矣!
    　　醉茶子，诗人也。落拓一衿，寒窗坐老。平居抚时感事，既见之于篇什，而以其余闲，复成此书。友人怂恿刊行，因持书问序于予。受而读之，奇情焕发，目不暇赏。篇终数语，尤如当头棒喝，发人猛省。观其自序，首引蒲留仙志异、文达公五种，是盖合二书之体例而为之者。读者不仅以怪视之，庶可得作者之大旨焉。其志怪也，殆犹是“不语怪”之义也夫。 光绪壬辰仲冬庸叟杨光仪识

    Explanation

    1

    Translation

    The Strange Tales of Drunken Tea
    By Li Qingchen, Qing Dynasty

    I have a line: “When things are difficult to express, one writes about strange occurrences; if one is not a confidant, one searches for the divine.” I believe that the creation of such works by writers is indeed a result of the rich and long-standing thoughts within them. If these thoughts remain silent for too long, they cannot be expressed, and one is left with no choice but to寄托情感于儿女情长，寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托寄托

    2

    自叙
    　　一编志异，留仙叟才迥过人；五种传奇，文达公言能警世。由今溯古，绝后空前。此外之才人，纵能灿彼心花，终属拾其牙慧。盖创之匪易，捷足者既已先登；而继之殊难，后来者莫能居上。言念及此，兴致索然。然而人各有怀，甘苦不同共际遇；士非得志，穷愁每见于词章。惟文字厌弃夫平庸，故搜讨乐言夫鬼怪。性有偏好，口讵能缄，文不求工，狂且弗顾。辟诸自鸣其天籁，岂能尽合乎人心？编中事迹，有与前贤仿佛者，乃词非虚构，事本直书。弃之何以生新，留之转如袭旧。有关风教，奚避雷同？知不免为博雅君子所指摘者矣。虽然，传记降神，易占载鬼，煌煌经史，昭著古今，固不同桑号子明、龟呼元绪，螺壳或藏彼美、鹅笼或寄书生也。

    Explanation

    2

    Myself, a man of no particular talent, have compiled a collection of strange tales, which I call Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio. The author, known as the "Immortal Retainer," possesses extraordinary talent; the five types of legendary stories, written by the "Literary Achiever," are words that can warn the world. Tracing back from the present to the past, these works are unparalleled and unprecedented. Other talented individuals, though they may be able to bloom their inner flowers, ultimately end up picking up the remnants of others' wisdom. For creation is not easy, and those who are quick-footed have already climbed first; following them is particularly difficult, and later comers cannot surpass them. Thinking of this, my interest wanes. However, people have different feelings, joys, and sorrows in their encounters; scholars who do not achieve their goals often express their poverty and sorrow in their writings. Only words reject the ordinary, so I enjoy discussing ghosts and monsters. Having preferences, how can one keep silent? Not seeking perfection in writing, and not caring about being wild, it is like a bird singing its own natural voice, how can it always align with human hearts? The events in this collection, some of which resemble those of past sages, are not fictitious; the stories are recorded as they happened. To discard them would be to lose newness, but to keep them would be to repeat the old. If they are related to moral teachings, why avoid sameness? I know that they will be criticized by learned gentlemen. Nevertheless, biographies and accounts of divine manifestations, and the Yi Jing (Book of Changes)记载鬼怪, the luminous classics and histories, clearly recorded in ancient and modern times, are indeed different from the stories of a mulberry tree calling out to a child named Ming, a turtle calling out to a man named Yuanxu, a snail shell containing a beautiful woman, and a goose cage containing a scholar.

    3

    仆半生抑郁，累日长愁，借中书君为扫愁帚，故随时随地，闻则记之，聊以自娱。于是二三良朋，时来蜗舍，此谈异说，彼述奇闻。谓夫千年华表，信可狐烹；三尺荒坟，真聆鬼唱。信以传信，清谈增鬼火之光；玄之又玄，雅谑生幽魂之色。再忆昔年游历，悉供今日搜罗。始欲米聚而为山，久遂裘成于集腋。维时风萧雨晦，人静夜凉，茶烟飞古鼎之香，兰炷吐秋灯之焰，濡毫吮墨，振笔直书，则此中之况味，真有不堪为外人道者也。吁嗟乎！高山流水，几多岑寂之人；弄月吟风，半是牢骚之客。东坡说鬼，言讵无稽；干宝搜神，意原有托。而况兰因絮果，尽得风流；贞木贪泉，微加月旦——事或关乎报应，词不背乎圣贤也。知我昔其谅之哉! 光绪壬辰冬日醉茶子自记

    Explanation

    3

    For half my life, I have been depressed, enduring long days of sorrow, so I borrowed the Chancellor as a broom to sweep away my worries, thus I would write down what I heard, wherever and whenever, just to amuse myself. Thus, a few good friends would come to my humble abode, and we would discuss strange tales and hear extraordinary stories. The thousand-year-old stone pillars, indeed, could be places where foxes were cooked; a three-footed neglected grave, truly, could be where ghosts sang. By believing and passing on true stories, our clear talks would enhance the light of ghostly fires; the profound and mysterious, elegant jokes would bring out the color of ghostly spirits. Recalling my travels from the past, they all provided material for my search today. At first, I wanted to gather rice grains to form a mountain, and in the end, I managed to create a fur coat by collecting bits of fur. At that time, the wind was howling and the rain was dark, the night was quiet and cool, the tea smoke rose from the ancient tripod, the incense burned and the autumn lamp flickered, I dipped my brush in ink, and wrote directly, then the taste of this, truly, was something that could not be told to outsiders. Ah, how many people are lonely like the high mountains and flowing water; how many are complaining like those who play with the moon and sing under the wind. Su Shi tells ghost stories, his words are not without basis; Gan Bao searches for the supernatural, his intent has a purpose. Moreover, the beautiful beginning leading to an unfortunate end, all are full of charm; the integrity of a person in a corrupt environment, slightly evaluated — the matters may relate to retribution, and the words do not contradict the sages. Can anyone understand and forgive me in the past? Winter of the year 1892, recorded by Drunken Tea Person.

    4

    6

    卷三
    张公 娄某 冯君三异 白塔寺 李志青 化犬 陶生 鬼吟诗 捉鬼 冥报 定兴城隍 冤妇 鼠怪 梦诗 鬼戏 黑山大王 龙眠穴 冯氏仆 於菟大鬼 稻田鬼 疟鬼 李茂才 花果楼 龚姓 陈氏怪 医术 妖术 娇娥 乩仙 矢魔 鼠媪 倭某 擒风 古瓦罐 铁叉 岳某 乩示题 古剑 磁鹤 茔中怪 山神 铜骡 猪异 蛤佛 鬼影 泥娃 产叟 产异 古磁器 徽商 狐帽 张氏妇 蝎异 江苏乙 山左布

    6

    Volume Three
    Zhang Gong, Lou Mou, Feng Jun's Three Anomalies, White Pagoda Temple, Li Zhiqing, Transformation into a Dog, Tao Sheng, Ghosts Reciting Poems, Catching Ghosts, Retribution in the Afterlife, Dingxing City God, Wronged Woman, Mouse Monster, Dream Poems, Ghost Performances, Black Mountain Great King, Dragon's Sleeping Hole, Feng Family Servant, Tiger Ghost, Rice Field Ghost, Malaria Ghost, Li Mao Cai, Flower and Fruit Tower, Gong Surname, Chen Family Monster, Medical Skills, Sorcery, Beautiful Lady, Divining Board Medium, Arrow Demon, Mouse Old Woman, Japanese Person, Catching the Wind, Ancient Clay Pot, Iron Fork, Yue Mou, Divining Board Title, Ancient Sword, Magnetic Crane, Strange Thing in the Tomb, Mountain God, Bronze Mule, Strange Pig, Shell Buddha, Ghost Shadow, Clay Doll, Childbirth Old Man, Strange Childbirth, Ancient Porcelain, Anhui Merchant, Fox Hat, Zhang Family Woman, Strange Scorpion, Jiangsu Person, Left Mountain Cloth

    7

    商 冤魂 大人迹 二仙 槐仙 张千总 王金铎 邵明 鬼窃饮 粤东童 营弁 夙冤 孙某 黄鼠 粥厂灾异 林承嗣 猪龙 二鼠 蝶仙 冥狱 观花爆 蜂异 白郎 鬼结婚 三世 妖诈食 缢致富 卵怪 溺簿 产怪 守宫 青蛙精 钱龙 石珠 土中鱼 蝎虎尾 焦某 鬼眼 陈姓 义仓怪 秦裕 蛇异 猬火 鸡异四则 产妖 羊怪 张杰 金化水 尸起 古磁缸 优伶某 河间乙 齐某 曹商 张老殿

    爱哥 狐师 伍明伦 鼠友 阿菱 汪某 刘玉厅 公输子二则 武清乙 张氏 海惠寺 献王墓 金氏祠 地震 木怪 竹生花 人面豆 鼬鼠 蛇异 银异 慧海 厉鬼 返魂 白夫人 二童 鬼恋妇 尸哭 沧州张 控鬼 孽报 泥桃 千里井 灶神 尸变 产蛆 陈差官 樊英 役夫 浙生 树怪 夜游神 张桂 双头豕 杜醒山 张绅士 陈某 郭氏妇 分水箭 土偶 剃发匠 蜈蚣 疟童 草偶 缸怪

    商 冤魂 大人迹 二仙 槐仙 张千总 王金铎 邵明 鬼窃饮 粤东童 营弁 夙冤 孙某 黄鼠 粥厂灾异 林承嗣 猪龙 二鼠 蝶仙 冥狱 观花爆 蜂异 白郎 鬼结婚 三世 妖诈食 缢致富 卵怪 溺簿 产怪 守宫 青蛙精 钱龙 石珠 土中鱼 蝎虎尾 焦某 鬼眼 陈姓 义仓怪 秦裕 蛇异 猬火 鸡异四则 产妖 羊怪 张杰 金化水 尸起 古磁缸 优伶某 河间乙 齐某 曹商 张老殿

    爱哥 狐师 伍明伦 鼠友 阿菱 汪某 刘玉厅 公输子二则 武清乙 张氏 海惠寺 献王墓 金氏祠 地震 木怪 竹生花 人面豆 鼬鼠 蛇异 银异 慧海 厉鬼 返魂 白夫人 二童 鬼恋妇 尸哭 沧州张 控鬼 孽报 泥桃 千里井 灶神 尸变 产蛆 陈差官 樊英 役夫 浙生 树怪 夜游神 张桂 双头豕 杜醒山 张绅士 陈某 郭氏妇 分水箭 土偶 剃发匠 蜈蚣 疟童 草偶 缸怪

    商 冤魂 大人迹 二仙 槐仙 张千总 王金铎 邵明 鬼窃饮 粤东童 营弁 夙冤 孙某 黄鼠 粥厂灾异 林承嗣 猪龙 二鼠 蝶仙 冥狱 观花爆 蜂异 白郎 鬼结婚 三世 妖诈食 缢致富 卵怪 溺簿 产怪 守宫 青蛙精 钱龙 石珠 土中鱼 蝎虎尾 焦某 鬼眼 陈姓 义仓怪 秦裕 蛇异 猬火 鸡异四则 产妖 羊怪 张杰 金化水 尸起 古磁缸 优伶某 河间乙 齐某 曹商 张老殿

    卷四
    爱哥 狐师 伍明伦 鼠友 阿菱 汪某 刘玉厅 公输子二则 武清乙 张氏 海惠寺 献王墓 金氏祠 地震 木怪 竹生花 人面豆 鼬鼠 蛇异 银异 慧海 厉鬼 返魂 白夫人 二童 鬼恋妇 尸哭 沧州张 控鬼 孽报 泥桃 千里井 灶神 尸变 产蛆 陈差官 樊英 役夫 浙生 树怪 夜游神 张桂 双头豕 杜醒山 张绅士 陈某 郭氏妇 分水箭 土偶 剃发匠 蜈蚣 疟童 草偶 缸怪

    商 冤魂 大人迹 二仙 槐仙 张千总 王金铎 邵明 鬼窃饮 粤东童 营弁 夙冤 孙某 黄鼠 粥厂灾异 林承嗣 猪龙 二鼠 蝶仙 冥狱 观花爆 蜂异 白郎 鬼结婚 三世 妖诈食 缢致富 卵怪 溺簿 产怪 守宫 青蛙精 钱龙 石珠 土中鱼 蝎虎尾 焦某 鬼眼 陈姓 义仓怪 秦裕 蛇异 猬火 鸡异四则 产妖 羊怪 张杰 金化水 尸起 古磁缸 优伶

    8

    醉茶志怪卷一
    折狱二则
    　　予七世祖讳珏，字德珮，为太仓州牧。赴任时，离州百里，投止旅舍。主人殷洽备至，请公寓偏室。公嫌隘甚，乃息正厅复室。墙壁光泽，设一板床，四周遮以布幄，遣仆展卧具，息偃在床。二更将尽，烛影凝青，剪之仍暗，不之怪也。甫交睫，觉有物拂面，骇而视之，顶格去脸咫尺。急起披衣，则顶格如故。俯视床前，一人浑身血腥，长跪叩首，问之不语，匍匐入床下。公乃秉烛搜之，见床底席裹一尸，重伤数处，棉塞口鼻。乃前日有布商寓此，主人贪利杀之，仓卒未得掩埋，暂藏诸床下也。公看毕，仍覆之，伪为不知。到任后，拘主人，一讯即服。

    Explanation

    8

    Records of Strange Tales: Drunken Tea, Volume One
    Two Cases of Judicial Decision-Making

    My seventh-generation ancestor, whose name was Jue and courtesy name Depei, served as the governor of Taicang. Upon assuming his post, he was about a hundred li away from the state capital and sought lodging at an inn. The host was extremely hospitable, asking him to stay in a side room. However, the official found it too cramped and decided to rest in the upstairs room of the main hall. The walls were glossy, and a wooden bed was set up, covered with cloth curtains. A servant was sent to prepare the bedding, and the official lay down on the bed. Near the end of the second watch, the candlelight turned greenish, even when trimmed, it remained dim, and he did not find it strange. As soon as he closed his eyes, he felt something brushing against his face. Startled, he looked and saw that it was just a few inches away from his face. He quickly got up and put on his clothes, but the object remained as before. Looking down at the foot of the bed, he saw a man covered in blood, kneeling and bowing his head. When questioned, the man did not speak and crawled under the bed. The official then lit a candle to search for him and discovered a corpse wrapped in a mat at the foot of the bed, with multiple severe injuries and cotton stuffed in its mouth and nose. It turned out that a day or two ago, a cloth merchant had stayed there, and the host had killed him for profit, hurriedly without being able to bury him, and temporarily hidden him under the bed. After examining the body, the official covered it and pretended not to know. Upon taking office, he arrested the host, and after one interrogation, the host confessed.

    9

    太仓富室，有女貌美而慧，诗画棋枰罔不精妙，父母咸钟爱之。年及笄，婿家犹未娶，使居好楼，遣一媪一婢服役焉。适来一少尼募缘，女遇于母所，倾谈大悦。尼亦粗知文字，善棋，与女对奕，胜负互分，益相亲爱，结为闺中良友。往来既稔，渐涉戏谑。一夕并枕谈心，媪婢皆倦寝，尼谓女曰：“处子亦动情乎？”连问之，女不答，乃探女怀云：“好个鹊巢，鸠将居之。”女亦笑曰：“痴姑子，尔颠耶？尔也鹊巢，何鸠居之有？”尼曰：“我固有鸠在。”问在何处，曰：“在此。”女笑曰：“如光鸠，骂毁尔巢。”遂扪其私，则小鸡竦而待矣。大惊曰：“予以尔为尼，尔固僧耶？”欲遁。尼抱而哀之曰：“娘子勿忧，予二形人也。平时与女无殊，然感女则男，感男则女，人不能窥其奥也。且深夜无人知，何所患焉？”女许之。入帐事讫，令女验之，则惟有鹊巢而已。

    Explanation

    9

    A Wealthy Family in Taicang had a daughter who was both beautiful and intelligent, excelling in poetry, painting, and chess. Both her parents loved her deeply. When she reached the age of marriageability, her intended husband's family had not yet arranged the marriage. She was made to live in a luxurious room, with an old woman and a maid assigned to serve her. One day, a young nun came to collect alms and met the girl at her mother's place. They talked deeply and were greatly pleased with each other. The nun also had a basic understanding of literature and was skilled in chess. She played chess with the girl, and the wins and losses were evenly distributed. They became even more close and formed a close friendship. After frequent meetings, they gradually became playful. One night, they lay together on the same pillow and talked. The old woman and the maid were all tired and asleep. The nun asked the girl, "Are you also moved emotionally, virgin?" She asked repeatedly, but the girl did not answer. Then she reached into the girl's bosom and said, "What a fine magpie's nest, a dove will take up residence." The girl also laughed and said, "Foolish nun, are you crazy? You are also a magpie's nest, how can a dove take up residence?" The nun said, "I indeed have a dove." When asked where it was, she said, "It is here." The girl laughed and said, "Like a bright dove, to curse and destroy your nest." Then she touched the girl's private parts, and a small chicken stood erect and awaited. The girl was greatly surprised and said, "I thought you were a nun, are you actually a monk?" She wanted to flee. The nun embraced her and said with sorrow, "Do not worry, my lady. I am a two-shaped person. Normally I am no different from a woman, but when I feel a woman, I become a man, and when I feel a man, I become a woman. No one can see its mystery. And at night no one knows, what is there to fear?" The girl agreed. After entering the tent and finishing the matter, she told the girl to check, and there was only a magpie's nest.

    10

    女笑曰：“出没不测，真逢时之利器也。”从此益亲，往来无间。女之聘期巳迫，腹彭彭而有娠矣，诡云病蛊，欺父母也。未几，亲迎礼毕，三月居然生子。夫丑之，迫令大归。女未归而仰药死。父痛女之死也，健讼不休。宰未深察，收其婿于狱，将拟抵。越半载，官迁，我公接篆，阅是案，颇疑生冤。拘富室讯之，云：“汝女不贞，何得妄控尔婿？”富室云：“女素楼居，终萝不见男子，何孕之有？果得奸夫，死自其分，敢赧颜诬告耶？”公令其退，阴遣卖花媪密访之，知与女最善者有一尼，然自女遭事，遂绝迹矣。拘尼到案，验之，女僧也。尼惭，忿语诮公云：“如此愦愦，尚作民父母!焉有二女同居而能生育者？”众俱愕然。公曰：“汝之劣迹，吾已勘破，尚强辩而不服耶？”遣官媒以小犬舐其阴，片刻则蛰虫出户，阳见于外矣。尼恐惧，变色，尽吐其实，叩头乞命。盖与女私交二载，并无人知也。遂置于法。

    10

    The woman laughed and said, "Appearing and disappearing unpredictably, this truly is a valuable asset for the present situation." From then on, she grew closer to the man, and they moved freely together. The wedding date was approaching, and she was pregnant, her abdomen swollen. She falsely claimed to be suffering from a disease caused by a venomous insect or parasite, deceiving her parents. Not long after, the wedding ceremony was completed, and after three months, she unexpectedly gave birth. The husband found her repulsive and forced her to return to her parents' home. Before she could return, she committed suicide by taking poison. The father, grieving over his daughter's death, persistently engaged in litigation. The magistrate did not thoroughly investigate and imprisoned her husband, intending to punish him severely. After half a year, the official was transferred, and I, as the new official, took up my duties and reviewed this case, suspecting that there might be a miscarriage of justice. I arrested the wealthy man and interrogated him, saying, "Your daughter is unchaste, how could you falsely accuse your husband?" The wealthy man replied, "My daughter always stayed in the upper room and never saw a man. How could she be pregnant? If she had a lover, death would be her due. How dare you shamelessly make a false accusation?" I ordered him to leave and secretly sent a flower seller to investigate. I found out that there was a nun who was closest to her, but since the incident, she has disappeared. I arrested the nun and investigated, finding that she was a female monk. The nun felt ashamed and angrily said to the official, "So foolish, still acting as the people's parents! How can two women living together give birth?" Everyone was startled. I said, "Your wicked deeds have been uncovered. Still stubbornly arguing and refusing to admit it?" I sent an official matchmaker to have a small dog lick her private parts. In a moment, insects emerged from her body, showing that she was a man. The nun became fearful, changed her expression, confessed everything, and begged for mercy. It turned out that she had been secretly involved with the woman for two years, and no one knew about it. She was then punished according to the law.

    11

    醉茶子曰：逢时利器，乃在此耶？无惑乎善揣时艺者，皆如毛锥之脱颖矣。虽然，落第频频，理宜雌伏。
    苏某
    　　苏某，晋人，传者忘其郡邑。为某官仆，随任辽东。夜有奔女，红裙蓝帔，貌殊娴雅。自云为狐，请独居后楼，妾当就之。如其言，夜果至。与之寝，樱口喷香，花容含笑，旷世真无其偶。从此遂为琴瑟。有老仆巡更，闻楼中笑语，疑其纳妓，叩扃盘诘，则见苏独坐，惊为遇妖，劝其速绝。勿听。又半载，形容憔悴。侪辈悉劝之，苏云：“身无疾病，但倦怠耳。”未几，呕血勿起，遂自恐，向女云：“予昔颜如渥丹，今则骨将委土，家无兄弟，奈老亲何？幸留蚁命，得归故乡，卿之惠也。”言毕泪下如雨。女曰：“妾蓄有灵丹，明日携来服之，又何虑焉？”苏有僚仆甲与乙者，苏为述其事。甲曰：“噫！君其危矣，既竭尔精，更投以鸩，是速其死也。”苏哭求计。甲曰：“彼能隐形，何能为力？”苏云：“猝与之遇，形不能隐也。

    Explanation

    11

    醉茶子曰：逢时利器，乃在此耶？无惑乎善揣时艺者，皆如毛锥之脱颖矣。虽然，落第频频，理宜雌伏。苏某，晋人，传者忘其郡邑。为某官仆，随任辽东。夜有奔女，红裙蓝帔，貌殊娴雅。自云为狐，请独居后楼，妾当就之。如其言，夜果至。与之寝，樱口喷香，花容含笑，旷世真无其偶。从此遂为琴瑟。有老仆巡更，闻楼中笑语，疑其纳妓，叩扃盘诘，则见苏独坐，惊为遇妖，劝其速绝。勿听。又半载，形容憔悴。侪辈悉劝之，苏云：“身无疾病，但倦怠耳。”未几，呕血勿起，遂自恐，向女云：“予昔颜如渥丹，今则骨将委土，家无兄弟，奈老亲何？幸留蚁命，得归故乡，卿之惠也。”言毕泪下如雨。女曰：“妾蓄有灵丹，明日携来服之，又何虑焉？”苏有僚仆甲与乙者，苏为述其事。甲曰：“噫！君其危矣，既竭尔精，更投以鸩，是速其死也。”苏哭求计。甲曰：“彼能隐形，何能为力？”苏云：“猝与之遇，形不能隐也。”

    Translation:
    The Drunken Tea Master said: "Is it not a sharp tool in the right time? It is no wonder that those skilled in understanding the times all rise like a fine brush emerging from its sheath. However, failing repeatedly, it is reasonable to lie low." Su, a man from the state of Jin, whose county and district are forgotten by the storyteller, served as an attendant to a certain official and followed him to Liaodong. One night, a woman came running, dressed in a red skirt and a blue shawl, with an exceptionally graceful appearance. She claimed to be a fox and asked to live alone in the back building, saying, "I will come to you." As he said, she indeed came at night. They had intercourse, and her mouth was like a cherry, exuding fragrance, her face like a flower, with a smile. Truly unparalleled in the world, from then on they became husband and wife. There was an old servant on night watch who heard laughter and conversation from the building and suspected that Su was taking in a prostitute. He knocked on the door and questioned, then saw Su sitting alone, shocked to think he had met a demon. He advised Su to break it off quickly, but Su did not listen. After another half a year, Su looked emaciated. All his peers advised him, and Su said, "I have no illness, just feeling tired." Not long after, he vomited blood and could not get up, then became afraid and told the woman, "My face was once as red as rouge, now my bones will be buried in the earth. I have no brothers, what to do with my elderly parents? Luckily saved my life, to return to my hometown, it is your kindness." After he finished speaking, tears streamed down like rain. The woman said, "I have a miraculous elixir; I will bring it tomorrow for you to take. What more is there to worry about?" Su had two colleagues, Jia and Yi, who served the same official. Su recounted the story to them. Jia said, "Ah! You are in danger. Having exhausted your strength, you are now being poisoned, which will hasten your death." Su cried and sought advice. Jia said, "She can turn invisible, how can she be of any use?" Su replied, "If we meet suddenly, she cannot remain invisible."

    12

    ”甲使系铃楼外，索引其端，索动铃响，闻声辄至。次日女来，取温水半瓯，吐口中红丸，对烛润化，将饮病者。苏急引动铃响，甲乙猝至。女起立，问将何为？乙云：“有何怨仇而杀吾友？”女云：“疾病，人之常事，医之则健壮如初，何以云杀？”乙窥女美，爱之，执其袪云：“尔藏凶器，非杀而何？”女问：“凶器安在？”乙云：“绣裆中所藏双股剑，予试扪之。”遽探其隐。女与撑拒，甲乘间取瓯中药汁，一吸而尽。女视之失色曰：“尔真杀我夫也！”忿以手推乙倒地，遂失所在。苏视，爽然悔悟，大詈二人无良。二人惭退。女来，握苏手痛哭云：“妾恃有妙药，贪欢不已，致君如此狼狈。若能服丹，寿同金石，且换凡骨，伉俪不仅百年。孰意君生疑忌，彼施计巧，是殆命也。五日后君必死，妾以君故，亦不免于雷殛。奈何奈何？”苏亦泣，劝女报之。女愀然曰：“祸由自取，骨骸且不能保，更何能报复哉？”苏日，

    Explanation

    12

    Jia had a bell tied outside the tower, following the beginning of the rope. When the rope moved, the bell rang, and they came immediately upon hearing the sound. The next day, the woman came, took half a bowl of warm water, spat out a red pill from her mouth, and moistened and dissolved it in front of the candle, about to drink the potion for the sick person. Su quickly pulled the rope, causing the bell to ring, and Jia and Yi arrived suddenly. The woman stood up and asked what would be done. Yi said, "What kind of grudge would cause someone to kill my friend?" The woman replied, "Illness is a common occurrence for people. If treated, it will be healthy and strong as before. Why say killed?" Yi peeked at the woman's beauty and loved her, holding her sleeve and saying, "You are hiding a dangerous weapon. What else could it be but killing?" The woman asked, "Where is the dangerous weapon?" Yi said, "The double-edged sword hidden in the embroidered pouch. I will try to touch it." He immediately explored its hidden part. The woman resisted, and Jia took advantage of the moment to take the medicine juice in the bowl, finishing it in one sip. The woman looked at it, paled, and said, "You truly killed my husband!" She angrily pushed Yi to the ground with her hands, and then she lost her place. Su looked, suddenly repented and realized, and scolded the two people loudly for being without virtue. The two people felt ashamed and retreated. The woman came, held Su's hand, and cried bitterly, saying, "I relied on having wonderful medicine, greedy for joy and not stopping, causing you to be in such a plight. If you could take the elixir, you would live as long as metal and stone, and exchange your mortal bones, so our marriage would not be limited to a hundred years. Who would have thought that you would harbor suspicions, and they would use cunning plans, this is probably fate. Five days later, you will certainly die. Because of you, I will also not escape being struck by lightning. What can I do? What can I do?" Su also cried, urging the woman to seek revenge. The woman said sorrowfully, "Disaster comes from oneself. Even the bones cannot be preserved, how can one seek revenge?"

    13

    “彼服丹成仙，殊令人切齿！”女曰：“彼心术不正，安能得道？不过多延年耳。”言毕，悲哽不已。至期，苏亡，女出金易棺殓已，遂杳。后，甲须发苍而转黑，八十余犹能夜御数女，颜如童子焉。

    Explanation

    13

    "His taking elixirs to become an immortal truly makes one's teeth grind!" The woman said, "He has an unrighteous heart, how could he attain the Dao? It's just about extending life a bit longer." After she finished speaking, she sobbed uncontrollably. By the time the time came, Su died, and the woman spent money to buy a coffin and prepare the body, then she disappeared. Later, Jia's hair turned from gray to black, and in his eighties, he could still have sex with several women at night, his face looking like that of a child.

    14

    醉茶子曰：贪欢忘死，深于情者，死快于生，况生而不死乎？龌龌儿何愚昧至此哉！吾独恨甲之忍，明知良药，不使苏服，自饮之，鹤发童颜，优游岁月。天龙有知，何不奋雷一击哉！
    张顺
    　　张顺者，武定府人。值寇横恣，窜身荒野。日暮途穷，恐为贼获，四顾无赴藏匿，乃伏乱尸中。云霾月黑，悲风四号，毛发森森俱竖。忽高处仰见灯光，一官居中坐，旁列数卤簿。展册唱名，便见断手缺臂者蹒跚而上。须臾点毕，纷纷并倒。官指顺问曰：“渠何伏而不动？”左右曰：“此宜毙于扬州狱中，非此案事。”怪风一卷，万象俱无。顺大恐，伏至晓，贼过如未见者，遂得免。自此懔懔怀刑，恒以南游为戒。会岁大饥，饮食不给，丐食入都。至一官第，有老妪抱儿立门外，儿大啼，饲以果饴，啼愈甚。举头见顺，啼即止，且求顺抱。顺喜抱之，儿悦甚。顺辞欲行，儿又大号。好事仆白于主人，主人唤入，视之，丐也。易其衣履，留宅内，使侍公子。

    Explanation

    14

    A Drunken Tea Drinkers Says: Those who greedily pursue pleasure and forget death, deeply immersed in emotion, find death quicker than life; how much more so if one lives but does not die? How foolish and ignorant can one be? I alone hate the cruelty of A, knowing the good medicine, he does not make him take it, drinking it himself, with white hair and a youthful appearance, enjoying his years leisurely. If the heavenly dragon knew, why not strike with a thunderbolt!

    Zhang Shun
    Zhang Shun, a man from Wuding Prefecture. Encountering bandits acting wildly, he fled into the wilderness. At dusk, at the end of the road, fearing capture by the bandits, looking around, there is nowhere to hide, he then hides among the corpses. Clouds cover the sky, the moon is dark, grief-filled winds howl all around, hair stands on end. Suddenly, looking up, he sees lights; an official sits in the middle, with several attendants standing around. Opening the register and calling names, he sees people with missing limbs staggering up. In a moment, the process is complete, and they all fall down. The official points to Zhang Shun and asks, "Why is he lying down and not moving?" The attendants say, "He should have died in the prison of Yangzhou, not in this case." A strange wind blows, and everything disappears. Zhang Shun is greatly afraid, hiding until dawn, the bandits pass by as if they did not see him, thus he escapes. From then on, he is always mindful of the law, always taking a southern journey as a warning. During a year of great famine, food and drink are insufficient, begging for food in the capital. Arriving at an official's residence, an old woman stands outside the door with a child in her arms, the child cries loudly, feeding him with fruit and candy, the crying becomes even louder. Lifting his head to see Zhang Shun, the crying stops, and he asks to be held. Zhang Shun happily holds him, and the child is very happy. Zhang Shun tries to leave, and the child cries again. A kind servant informs the master, the master calls him in, looks at him, and sees he is a beggar. He changes his clothes and shoes, keeps him in the house, and makes him serve the young master.

    15

    顺喜出望外，得免饥寒，且获赏赍。细询问同人，始知主人官工部主政，公子生而善啼，每啼则昼夜不已，凡仆媪辈无一当意者，积今五载矣。顺得其故，曲意逢迎，凡公子眠餐，离顺则郁郁不乐也。越数载，主人官选扬州，指日就道。顺固辞。公子知之，涕泣不食。主人益其工价，顺仍不许。主人怒，痛鞭之。顺不能隐，举前事以对，跪求乞免。主人笑曰：“尔妖梦是践，愚已甚矣。苟能守法，安能陷于囹圄？即使陷之，我力不难出也，何忧为？”顺不获已，随之行。甫至扬州三日，公子曳与遨游，至扬子江，公子失足落水。顺大窘，计无所逃，负荆长跪，泣诉于主人。主人止此子，爱如拱壁，闻其死，拍案大怒，痛笞而系于禁。狱隶以顺无关说，遇之甚虐。不堪其苦，忿而坠锁，导毙。乃知生死之数，卒难逃也。

    Explanation

    15

    Shun was overjoyed beyond expectation, having escaped from hunger and cold and also received rewards. After carefully inquiring of his fellow servants, he learned that the master was an official in charge of the Ministry of Works, and that the young master was born with a habit of crying incessantly, day and night, and none of the servants or maids could satisfy him. This had been going on for five years now. Shun understood the reason and catered to the young master, ensuring that whenever the young master ate or slept, he was unhappy without Shun. Several years later, the master was appointed to Yangzhou and was about to leave. Shun firmly declined to go. The young master heard of this and wept bitterly, refusing to eat. The master increased Shun's wages, but Shun still refused. The master became angry and severely whipped him. Unable to hide any longer, Shun recounted the previous events and knelt, pleading for forgiveness. The master laughed and said, "Your dream of misfortune has come true; you are indeed foolish. If one can adhere to the law, how could one be imprisoned? Even if one were imprisoned, it would not be difficult for me to release them, so why worry?" Shun had no choice but to follow. Upon arriving in Yangzhou, after just three days, the young master dragged Shun along on a trip, and they reached the Yangtze River, where the young master accidentally fell into the water. Shun was in a difficult situation, with no way to escape, and he knelt with a bundle of thorns on his back, crying to the master. The master loved the young master dearly, like a precious jade disc, and upon hearing of his death, he banged the table in rage, severely whipped Shun, and imprisoned him. The prison guards treated Shun harshly because he did not intervene. Unable to bear the suffering, Shun angrily broke the lock, leading to his death. Finally, it was understood that the fate of life and death cannot ultimately be avoided.

    16

    醉茶子曰：一见辄喜，不知者以为前缘也，又岂知祸患即伏于中乎？故天下事，厚我者未必不祸我，祸我者未必不福我。横逆之来，平情处之，安知非如塞翁之失马哉！

    Explanation

    16

    醉茶子曰：Upon first meeting, one feels immediate joy, and those who do not understand may think it is due to a karmic connection, yet how can one know that disaster and trouble are already lurking within? Therefore, in all matters of the world, those who treat me well may not necessarily harm me, and those who harm me may not necessarily bring me good fortune. When adversity comes, one should handle it with calmness; how can one know it is not like the story of the old man of the border losing his horse?

    17

    鲁班
    　　袁某，邑之李富德庄人，嗜酒病噎，饮食不进，群医束手，已濒于死。妻李氏最贤，日祷于神，罔效。忽门外有款关者，出视，其人则肩披双橐，中盛斧锯，殆木作工也。谓李曰：“令亲冯某，遣予视疾。”李云：“夫子之病革矣，归烦寄语冯君，身后事切求援手。”其人请视病者，李辞以居庐不洁，难屈嘉宾。其人曰：“薄有小术，可施治之。”问：“客能治何疾？”曰：“秃发者不能治，余无难耳。”李喜，引入。诊视毕，出一红丸，使服之。李有难色，其人曰：“请勿迟疑。”乃索纸笔书方授之，纳丸于袋，负橐即行。李求其姓字里居，其人曰：“仆暂寓某店，将往前村高商家佣工，不能再来，亦不劳再访。此药三剂，保必愈矣。”出门遽去。李思村内无通医者，乃诣塾师范某，以方诘之。范笑云：“药平常耳，服之无益亦无损。”李归，买药进之。

    17

    Lu Ban
    Yuan, a resident of Li Fude Village, suffered from alcoholism and choking due to illness, and could not eat or drink. All the doctors were at a loss, and he was on the verge of death. His wife, Li, the most virtuous woman, prayed to the gods daily, but it had no effect. Suddenly, there was a knock on the door. When she went to see who it was, she found a man with a double bag on his shoulder, containing axes and saws, probably a carpenter. The man said to Li, "Your relative Mr. Feng sent me to see the illness." Li replied, "The master's illness is critical. Please deliver a message to Mr. Feng when you return, and urgently request assistance with funeral arrangements." The man asked to examine the sick person, but Li refused, saying the house was not clean and it would be disrespectful to have a guest. The man said, "I have a slight skill and can treat it." Li asked, "What diseases can you treat?" The man replied, "I cannot treat baldness, but the rest is easy." Li was pleased and led him inside. After the examination, the man produced a red pill and instructed her to make Yuan take it. Li hesitated, and the man said, "Please do not hesitate." He then wrote a prescription and gave it to her, put the pill in his bag, and left immediately. Li asked for his name, address, and hometown, and the man said, "I am temporarily staying at a certain shop, will go to the village ahead to work for Mr. Gao, and will not come back. There is no need to visit again. This medicine in three doses is guaranteed to cure." He left the house hurriedly. Li thought there was no doctor in the village, so she went to the village school teacher, Mr. Fan, and asked about the prescription. Mr. Fan laughed and said, "The medicine is ordinary; taking it will neither help nor harm." Li returned, bought the medicine, and gave it to Yuan.

    18

    病者肠腹雷鸣，片刻三遗矢，顿索食饮。与以汤饵，居然下咽。再服两剂，疾大愈。袁喜曰：“吾其遇和、缓乎？何其术之神也？”乃诣冯致谢。冯茫然不解。哀告以故，相与猜异。因辞冯诣高，高方大兴土木，鸠工如云。问工而医者谁？众言其无。诧异而返，告其故于妻。妻劝访诸逆旅。袁新瘥体倦，次日朝往，店主人方启关，延之入。询客有木匠而医者耶？曰：“不知。”袁乃遍视诸客，悉非其人。至院西隅有静室，内奉鲁班神像，厥貌惟肖，恍然顿悟。顶礼毕，归，遣妻亦往礼之。由是鲁班之灵大著，焚香祈福者肩摩毂击矣。后凡有病噎者，服其方无不立效。无何，邻村有张髡者，患是病，遣人索方于袁。袁他出，其妻捡方付之，告使者曰：“神方只此一纸，宝藏之勿失也。”使藏而去。至半里许，遽中暑，踣于路，舁至家卒，方遂不可问矣。未几髡亦毙，始悟神言不治秃发者之有因也。

    Explanation

    18

    A Patient with Severe Gastrointestinal Distress

    The patient's abdomen was making loud rumbling sounds, and within a short time, he defecated three times. He suddenly craved food and drink. When given soup and medicine, he unexpectedly managed to swallow it. After taking two more doses, his condition significantly improved. Yuan was delighted and said, "Have I encountered a skilled doctor like He or Huan? How miraculous his skill is!" He then went to Feng to express his gratitude. Feng was confused and did not understand. Yuan explained the reason with sorrow, and they mutually speculated and disagreed. Yuan left Feng and went to Gao, who was building something grand, with many workers gathered. Yuan asked which worker was a doctor, but everyone said there was none. Surprised, Yuan returned home and told his wife the reason. His wife suggested visiting inns. Yuan, who was weak and tired after recovering, went to an inn the next morning. The innkeeper was just opening the door and invited him in. Yuan asked if any guest was both a carpenter and a doctor. The guests replied, "I don't know." Yuan looked at all the guests and found none of them the right person. In the west corner of the courtyard, there was a quiet room with a statue of Lu Ban, the god of carpentry, which looked very lifelike. Yuan suddenly realized something and bowed in reverence. He returned home and sent his wife to pay respects as well. From then on, the spirit of Lu Ban became very famous, and many people came to burn incense and pray for blessings, so crowded that their shoulders and wheels touched. Later, anyone with throat problems, when taking the prescription, always saw immediate results. Not long after, there was a bald man named Zhang from a neighboring village who suffered from this illness. He sent someone to ask Yuan for the prescription. Yuan was out, so his wife found the prescription and gave it to the messenger. She told the messenger, "The divine prescription is just this one paper; treasure it and do not lose it." The messenger stored it and left. Halfway, he suddenly got sunstroke, fell down on the road, was carried home, and died. The prescription was lost. Not long after, the bald man also died. They finally realized why the god said it wouldn't work for bald people.

    19

    醉茶子曰：公输子，古之巧人。刻术为人、为鸢，悉能飞走。未闻以医传。岂巫医小术，特古人之余事乎？而今之医士，投药卤葬，与运斤纵斧者无殊。是不但有愧卢、扁，直弄斧班门耳，而况执斧者公然为医哉。洵可叹矣！
    王建屏
    　　王建屏，晋之世家子。从其祖迁居于津。父母旱逝，年十八，与邑之杂货商为伙友。肆后厦屋五楹，王独后复室。更深扃户，煨酒自饮。忽布帘一展，有女子自外入，缟素衣裳，面无脂粉，虚鬟笼雾，腻颊凝花，淡雅别饶风韵。王知其狐仙，毛发几竖，曰：“素无不敬，何故来扰？”盖商最敬仙，堂中常设仙位，王虔拜尤勤，故云然也。女曰：“谁见尔敬我耶？”王曰：“朝奉香，夕参拜，非敬而何？”女曰：“痴郎，我未见有敬妇者。

    Explanation

    19

    The Tea Drinker said: Gongshu Ban was a skilled craftsman of ancient times. He could carve figures and birds that could all fly and move. I have not heard of him being known for medicine. Is the practice of witchcraft and minor medical skills merely the leftover skills of ancient people? Modern-day physicians prescribe medicine carelessly, no different from those who wield axes recklessly. This is not only a disgrace to Lu Ban and Bian Que, it's like showing off an axe in front of the master craftsman, and what about those who openly claim to be doctors while holding an axe? Truly it is a pity!

    Wang Jianping
    Wang Jianping was a noble family member from the Jin dynasty. He followed his grandfather to move to Tianjin. His parents died early, and at eighteen, he became a partner with a local general store merchant. The store had a five-bay hall behind it, and Wang had a separate room at the back. At night, he locked the door and warmed wine to drink alone. Suddenly, a curtain was drawn, and a woman entered from outside. She wore white and plain clothes, her face had no makeup, her hair was loosely coiled as if veiled in mist, her cheeks were smooth and flower-like, and she was elegant and had a unique charm. Wang knew she was a fox spirit, and his hair stood on end. He said, "I have always shown respect, why come to disturb me?" The merchant was the most respectful of spirits, and the hall often had an altar for spirits. Wang was especially diligent in his worship, hence his words. The woman said, "Who has seen you showing respect to me?" Wang replied, "I offer incense in the morning and worship in the evening, is that not showing respect?" The woman said, "Foolish man, I have never seen anyone show respect to a woman."

    20

    ”王曰：“予固未娶，何便诬我为敬妇？”女曰：“痴哉！痴哉！缘何不娶？”王曰：“家道赤贫，谁肯媒妁？”女曰：“我为媒，为尔择一佳妇，愿乎？”曰：“愿。”女曰：“麻面兔唇，且眇一目。君如愿之，是君白首之偶。”王曰：“不可！不可！必美如姐姐者，方可为之。”女曰：“俗云癞头蛙想吃天鹅，不亦难乎？”王曰：“我诚不敢妄想，谚云：野老食蝗，飞来口福，不劳君子好逑耳。”女大笑曰：“君非学究，何太腐气？岂日居市井，尚未忘之乎者也耶？实告君，妾与君有前缘，故来燕好。

    20

    The king said, "I have not yet married, how can you easily accuse me of being a virtuous wife?" The woman replied, "How foolish! How foolish! Why haven't you married?" The king answered, "Our family is extremely poor, who would agree to act as a matchmaker?" The woman said, "I will be your matchmaker and choose a beautiful wife for you. Do you wish to?" The king said, "Yes." The woman then said, "A woman with a pockmarked face, rabbit lips, and one blind eye. If you wish for such a woman, she will be your lifelong companion." The king exclaimed, "No! No! Only a woman as beautiful as my sister can be considered." The woman responded, "It's as difficult as a toad wanting to eat a swan." The king said, "I truly dare not have such unrealistic expectations. As the saying goes: 'A country bumpkin eating locusts, it's a gift from heaven, no need for a gentleman to seek a good match.'" The woman laughed and said, "You are not a scholar, why do you have such a rigid attitude? How could you, living in the marketplace, still hold onto such old-fashioned ideas? Let me tell you, we have a predestined connection, which is why I came to be with you."

    21

    君宜秘之，保不为君祸也。”言毕而出，旋从帘外挈一小竹篮，中盛四簋，又取出水晶壶、玉杯以及象箸等，罔不精洁。酌酒劝王。王有难色，女夺杯曰：“此岂鸩毒耶？”先吸其半，复令王饮。王尝之，芳洌沁齿，果良酿也，遂酣饮。饮毕，则杯盘自无，亦不见人搬去。既而灭烛共寝，极尽欢娱。及晓始去，人无知者。如此二三年，恩爱备至。尝谓王曰：“君之福泽太薄，而衣食粗足。如操作时思食，可取诸釜；思衣，可取诸箧。”试之果然。一夕携来奇珍异味，穷极奢丽。

    Explanation

    21

    The king should keep it a secret; it will not be a disaster for you." After speaking, she left, and then took a small bamboo basket from outside the curtain, which contained four bowls. She also took out a crystal pot, jade cup, and ivory chopsticks, all of which were extremely clean. She poured wine and offered it to the king. The king had a look of difficulty, but the woman took the cup and said, "Is this poison?" She drank half of it first and then made the king drink. The king tasted it and found it fragrant and cool, refreshing his teeth; indeed, it was good wine. They then drank heartily. After drinking, the cups and dishes disappeared on their own, and no one moved them away. Then they extinguished the candles and went to bed together, enjoying themselves to the fullest. They left at dawn, and no one knew about it. For two or three years, they were deeply in love. She once told the king, "Your blessings are too thin, but your food and clothing are just enough. If you think of food while working, you can take it from the pot; if you think of clothes, you can take them from the chest." When tried, it was indeed so. One night, she brought rare delicacies and exotic flavors, reaching the utmost in luxury.

    22

    驼峰熊掌，豹胎鲂腴，皆不能指其名。生喜云：“朝朝相扰，何故为此盛馔？”女不言，劝生食，己则倚灯背坐不食。王视之，愁眉锁翠，香泪抛红，嫋嫋然欲言复止。王再三询故，女曰：“缘尽矣，此别筵也。”王闻言大痛。女拭泪曰：“黄鹤一去，非无再见之期。他年会于苏州，当为君脱急难，志之勿忘也。”未几，斜月欲堕，野鸡四号。女握生手至房外，曰：“若再留数日，后会不可得矣，君宜珍重，妾去也。”如电而没。王自是思念成疾，半载始瘥。后娶妇缺唇眇目，一如女言。仙能前知，信夫。

    Explanation

    22

    The camel hump, bear paws, leopard fetus, and fat carp, all of which cannot be named, were served. The man asked, "Disturbing day after day, why for this grand feast?" The woman did not reply but urged him to eat, while she leaned against the lamp and sat silently, not eating. The king saw her, her frowning brows like locked emerald, fragrant tears shed in red, and she seemed to want to speak but hesitated. The king repeatedly asked her why, and she said, "Our缘分 (fate) has ended; this is a farewell feast." The king was deeply saddened. The woman wiped her tears and said, "The Yellow Crane may fly away, but there is no end to our reunion. We will meet again in Suzhou; I will help you overcome your difficulties. Remember this, do not forget." As the slanting moon was about to set and the wild chickens were calling, the woman took the man's hand to the room's entrance and said, "If you stay a few more days, we may not meet again. Take care, I must go." She disappeared like lightning. The king was sickened by longing and took half a year to recover. Later, he married a woman with a cleft lip and one eye, just as the woman had said. Indeed, immortals can foresee the future.

    23

    醉茶子曰：建屏乃吾友赵价人之内兄，予于赵氏识之。其为人诚朴笃信。价人为予言其梗概。予固好奇，询诸王。王曰：“君别号醉茶子乎？”予曰：“然。”王曰：“若然，可以言矣。”予骇问故。王曰：“昔仙谓予曰：他日有李某编记事诸书，可烦渠作佳传。他人可勿告也。”乃向予细言颠末，语犹欷嘘。惜予笔墨拙涩，有负重托。然事有可传，敢不勉强为之？同治纪元，建屏如苏买茉莉遇盗，竟得生还，归家，旋病故。不知又与仙遇否，惜未得深究之也。乃知古今丛说，实事不少，不可尽以子虚乌有视之。

    Explanation

    23

    The Drunken Tea Seed says: Jianping is the brother-in-law of my friend Zhao Jiaren, whom I came to know through the Zhao family. His character is sincere, simple, and trustworthy. Jiaren told me the general outline of his story. Naturally curious, I asked Wang about it. Wang said, "Is your pen name the Drunken Tea Seed?" I replied, "Yes." Wang then said, "If so, then it can be told." I was startled and asked why. Wang explained, "The Immortal once told me: 'In the future, there will be a Mr. Li who compiles books of various records. You may trouble him to write a good biography. Do not tell others.'" Then he told me in detail about the whole story, his words still filled with sighs. I regret that my writing is clumsy and awkward, and I have failed to live up to the heavy trust. But there are things that can be passed down, so how dare I not try my best? In the first year of the Tongzhi era, Jianping went to Suzhou to buy jasmine and encountered bandits, but he managed to return alive. He returned home, only to die soon after. I don't know if he met the Immortal again, but unfortunately, I did not have the chance to investigate further. Thus, I came to know that among the myriad stories of ancient and modern times, there are many true events; they cannot all be dismissed as mere fantasies.

    24

    申仲权
    　　申仲权，燕之士人，屡试不第，落魄难堪。有中表亲冉某，为关外将军幕府，往投之。至则官已迁，冉随之去。申殊怏怏，思再往寻，而囊赀己罄。不得已寻同乡人劚参者依之。乡人四五辈事皆贫苦，食不能自给，授申长铲，使随众操作。申不胜其苦，众又嫌其累，然未忍拒之。一日，与众侣入山，申步蹇，憩松下。须臾，众不知何往。申走寻之，至一处，山重水复，路极曲折，迷不识道。会红日西坠，悲风怒号，思觅一石窟，借避虎豺。踟蹰间，见一女子，容貌姣丽，腰系白鹿皮裙，至溪边掬饮溪水。申至前，解腰间瓢与之。女欣然饮毕，还其器。申询路，女云：“此山中无多居人，前峰峦密处是我所居。彼处无甘泉，故每饮必须至此。然此去大路绝远，君何以来也？”申告以故，且求寄宿。

    Explanation

    24

    Shen Zhongquan
    Shen Zhongquan, a scholar from the state of Yan, failed the imperial examinations repeatedly and found himself destitute and unable to cope with his circumstances. He had a cousin named Ran, who served as an aide-de-camp to a general in the frontier region. Shen went to seek him out, but by the time he arrived, Ran had already been promoted and left. Shen felt deeply disappointed and thought of going to look for him again, but his money had run out. Unable to do so, he sought out a fellow townsman who was digging for ginseng and stayed with him. Several fellow townspeople were also poor, and they could not even feed themselves. They gave Shen a long shovel and asked him to work with them. Shen could not endure the hardship, and the others also found him a burden, though they did not have the heart to reject him. One day, with the others, they entered the mountains. Shen walked slowly and rested under a pine tree. In a moment, the others did not know where they had gone. Shen went to look for them and reached a place where mountains and rivers repeated themselves, indicating a complex and winding landscape. The path was extremely winding, and Shen was lost. Just as the red sun set, a mournful wind howled, and Shen thought of finding a cave to hide from tigers and wolves. As he hesitated, he saw a woman with beautiful features, wearing a white deer-skin skirt. She went to the stream and scooped water to drink. Shen went forward and offered his gourd from his waist to her. The woman happily finished drinking and returned the gourd. Shen asked for directions, and the woman said, "There are not many people living in this mountain. The dense area of peaks ahead is where I live. There is no sweet spring there, so I must come here to drink every time. But this place is very far from the main road, how did you come here?" Shen told her the reason and asked to stay overnight.

    25

    女不应，返身欲行。申固哀之，至泣下。女乃引之行。转过山坡，横亘一小桥，桥下芦苇杂生，蛙鸣蚓吹之声极其凄楚。又数里，一大石桥，其长不知多远，石栏雕镂，宛若天成。桥尽有庄，南向一石门，入则石室精洁，连亘甚远。引申至一室曰：“尔姑在此，俟为尔备食。”女自入去。申视室中床几，皆石为之。壁上石纹如画，花木人物，神色生动。纵览间，门外气咻咻一虎冲门过，申战栗不敢少动，伏石床上。俄而女至，赠一物如小儿臂，红润鲜软，莫识其名。云：“食少许，即可饱。携归可以致富。”语毕自去。申擘食之，味甘易饱。留其半藏诸怀。天将曙，女至，示以归路。拨云寻道，越岭攀藤，始至山外。回顾则重峦绝巘，无路可登。诧异而返，出其物示同人，乃美参也，售之得一百金，治装归里焉。

    Explanation

    25

    The woman did not respond and turned to leave. Shen persisted in pleading with her, even to the point of weeping. She then led him on. Turning the hillside, they came across a small bridge spanning the landscape. Below the bridge, reeds grew in abundance, and the sounds of frogs and earthworms were extremely mournful. After several miles, they encountered a large stone bridge, its length unknown, with intricately carved stone railings that seemed naturally formed. At the end of the bridge, there was a village with a south-facing stone gate. Inside, the stone rooms were clean and well-kept, stretching far and wide. She led Shen to a room and said, "Wait here while I prepare food for you." The woman then went in herself. Shen looked around the room and saw that the beds and tables were all made of stone. The stone patterns on the walls resembled paintings, depicting flowers, trees, people, and animals with lifelike expressions. As he was admiring the surroundings, a tiger rushed past the door with a loud roar. Shen trembled and dared not move even a little, hiding under the stone bed. Soon after, the woman returned and gave him an object about the size of a child's arm, which was red, moist, and soft, but Shen did not know what it was. She said, "Eat a little bit, and you will be full. Take it back, and it can make you rich." After saying this, she left. Shen broke off a piece and ate it, finding it sweet and easy to satisfy hunger. He kept half and hid it in his bosom. As dawn approached, the woman returned and showed him the way back. They cleared the clouds to find the path, crossed the mountains, and climbed vines, finally reaching outside the mountain. Looking back, they saw towering peaks and cliffs with no way up. Surprised, they returned, showed the object to others, and it turned out to be a fine ginseng, which they sold for a hundred gold coins, then prepared to return home.

    26

    刘氏子蠡县农人刘翁，生二子。长者甫三龄，少者在襁褓。适二子俱患痘，少者毙，长者亦垂危。翁见无望，欲俱埋之。妻不可，固止之。未几气绝，乃并瘗诸村外。有贩皮条马鞭者，乐姓，齐东人。过其侧，闻冢中儿啼声，发之，儿固未死。大号村人，无有应者，乃携归，以为子焉。乐妻久不育，得儿甚喜。越数载，儿长，妻亦生一子。儿习父业颇勤，父子经营，家道小康。弟不驯，兄教之。弟不服，语侵兄。兄怒，挞之，弟呼曰：“尔非我兄，何敢责我？使我父不将尔来，葬诸犬腹矣，尚得有今日哉！”儿以其言异，私询诸父。父不能隐，具以实告。儿大痛，力辞父母，欲归故乡。止之不可，竟裹粮夜遁。

    Explanation

    26

    A man from Liu family in Lixian, Hebei, named Liu翁, had two sons. The elder one was just three years old, and the younger one was still in swaddling clothes. Just as both sons contracted smallpox, the younger one died, and the elder one was also on the brink of death. Seeing no hope, Liu翁 wanted to bury them both. His wife, however, firmly stopped him. Soon after, the elder son breathed his last, and they buried them together outside the village. A passerby who sold leather straps and horse whips, surnamed Le and from Qidong, passed by and heard the cries of a child in the tomb. He dug it up and found that the child was still alive. He shouted loudly to the villagers, but no one responded. So, he took the child home and adopted him as his own son. Le's wife had been unable to bear children for a long time and was very happy to have a son. After several years, the child grew up, and Le's wife also gave birth to a son. The adopted son worked very diligently in his father's trade, and together with his father, they managed their household and achieved a modest prosperity. However, the younger brother was unruly, and the elder brother tried to teach him. The younger brother refused to listen and even spoke disrespectfully to his elder brother. Angered, the elder brother beat him, and the younger brother cried out, "You are not my brother, how dare you scold me? If my father had not brought you here and buried you in the dogs' stomach, would I even have this day today!" The adopted son found his words strange and privately asked his father about it. The father could not hide the truth and told him everything. The adopted son was greatly distressed and strongly refused to stay with his adoptive parents, wanting to return to his hometown. His parents tried to stop him, but he secretly packed food and escaped at night.

    27

    至蠡县，徘徊村外，或问之，云：“予固此乡人，幼离家，今归而寻父者。”问尊翁姓字，则莫能对。众笑之，窥其貌，颇类刘翁，戏谓翁曰：“若儿容貌肖翁，得毋翁之子乎？”翁笑唾之。好事者详诘端末，儿曰：“予固非妄，乃确有凭。”出小衣二件，遍示众人。翁告诸媪，媪至，视其衣，即当年殓物也，始恍然悟为己子。翁媪牵儿大悲，纳诸其家，如获珍宝。翁富于财而无子，至是为儿娶妇，厚酬乐姓，拜为义父母，往来如至戚云。我不绝人之嗣，天亦不绝其嗣，未必非恻隐之心所感动也。顾抚养教育，至于成人，则义父之恩，又何殊于生父？刘子有良，当必肝脑涂地以报之。乃一言之怒，弃而不顾，是亦非人情矣。

    Explanation

    27

    Upon arriving in Lixian County, the man lingered outside the village. When asked, he said, "I am indeed a native of this village, having left home as a child and now returning to find my father." When questioned about his father's name, he could not provide an answer. The villagers laughed at him, but upon seeing his appearance, they noticed that he somewhat resembled Mr. Liu. They jokingly said to him, "If your appearance resembles your father, could you be his son?" Mr. Liu laughed and spat at him. A curious bystander inquired further, and the man replied, "I am not lying; I have solid proof." He then produced two small garments and showed them to everyone. Mr. Liu told the women, who came and examined the clothes, realizing that they were the same garments used in his burial. At this, he suddenly understood that the man was his son. Mr. Liu and his wife pulled the boy close, overwhelmed with emotion, and took him into their home, treating him as if they had found a precious treasure. Mr. Liu, who was wealthy but childless, then arranged for the boy to marry, generously rewarded the family, and formally adopted him as their son, treating him like a close relative. It seemed that just as heaven did not abandon his lineage, it was also not abandoned by compassion. However, in raising and educating him until he became an adult, the kindness of an adoptive father is no different from that of a biological father. Mr. Liu would surely repay him with utmost loyalty. Yet, a single word of anger caused him to be abandoned without a second thought, which is also contrary to human nature.

    28

    阴司
    　　李某，邑之富室仆，性诡谲，善迎合主意，主甚悦宠。先有老仆殷某，朴拙无才，主人得李，益厌之，以故逐去，抑郁而死。李愈得主欢，事之巨细皆任之。一旦，方出门，门旁立二公人，以索系其项，云：“殷某控汝，须到案质对。”问：“何事被控？”役云“不知”，遂牵之去。三人同走，俱贴墙阴。每过横巷透日光处，便觉如隔溪水，须待人来，始随其农影过之。至城隍祠，李惊问故，役云：“入自知之，何劳絮聒？”既入，则商贾云集，公役纷繁，居然衙署。引李至一矮屋，扃闭而去。屋中漆黑，莫辨朝昏。历有二昼夜，饥肠雷动，愁思火燔，焦燥不可言状。

    Explanation

    28

    In the Underworld

    Li, a servant of a wealthy household in the county, was cunning and deceitful, skilled at pleasing his master's will. The master was very pleased with him and favored him greatly. There was an older servant named Yin, who was simple and honest but lacked talent. After the master gained Li's favor, he became increasingly dissatisfied with Yin and eventually dismissed him. Yin died of depression. Li became even more favored by his master and took on all matters, big or small. One day, as he was about to leave the house, two officials stood by the door and tied a rope around his neck, saying, "Yin is accusing you, and you must come to court for questioning." Li asked, "What am I being accused of?" The official replied, "I don't know," and then took him away. Three people walked together, all sticking close to the wall. Whenever they passed through narrow alleys where sunlight filtered through, they felt as if separated by a stream, and they had to wait for someone to come before following their shadow. They arrived at the City God Temple, and Li was surprised and asked why. The official said, "You will know when you enter, why bother asking?" Upon entering, they found a gathering of merchants and traders, with officials bustling about, and it turned out to be an official residence. The official led Li to a low hut, locked it, and left. The room was pitch black, and he could not distinguish day from night. For two days and two nights, his stomach growled like thunder, his worries burned like fire, and he was so anxious that it could not be described.

    29

    忽役来谓曰：“今日不审，聊放汝还。”李出，寻途而返。至家门，爽然而苏，盖死去三日矣。初，李为二役摄去，身倒主人门外，口气如丝，身犹未冷，使舁至其家。至是始醒，奄奄遂如抱病，数日略能举步，又为二鬼勾去，越二日遣还。计半载，五入阴曹，往来直如熟境。李甚厌惧，谓二鬼曰：“赏罚生死，即求速决，数被纠缠，实不能耐。”旁一人笑曰：“君何太迂，阴曹与阳世相同，公门可白手入乎？”李爽然曰：“放归当有以报厚意，但乞二兄援手耳。”役云：“敢不效力！但公差不自由，有疏照拂。然延迟几日，即可判审。归请敬候，有确信，当走相延。

    Explanation

    29

    A sudden messenger came and said, "I am not sure today, let's let you go back for now." Li left, retraced his steps, and returned home. Upon reaching the door, he suddenly woke up, as if from a deep sleep, and it seemed that he had been dead for three days. Initially, Li was taken by two officials, and his body fell outside the host's door. His breath was as thin as a thread, and his body was still not cold. They carried him to his home. He gradually became weak as if he were ill and could barely take a few steps after several days. He was again taken by two ghosts, and after two more days, he was sent back. In half a year, he entered the underworld five times, and going back and forth was like being in a familiar place. Li was very tired and afraid. He said to the two ghosts, "If you want to decide quickly on rewards and punishments, life and death, I can't bear to be纠缠 so often." One of them laughed and said, "Why are you so stubborn? The underworld is the same as the world of the living. Can you enter the official world without any connections?" Li replied, "If you let me go, I will find a way to repay your kindness. But please help me." The official said, "How dare I not help! But official duties are not within my control, and there may be some lapses in care. However, if we delay for a few days, then it can be judged and reviewed. Please wait respectfully when you return. When there is definite news, I will come to invite you."

    30

    至于酬赏，则一听之于君耳。”李苏，谋诸妇，使央主人。主人赐钱十贯，货冥镪香楮焚化。是夜役来，握手殷勤，笑容可掬，相将至衙外偏室坐。片刻，有人负铁钱十串、白金数十锭置地上，曰：“李君所赠之物。”交役而去。役云：“蒙君厚贿，敢不尽心。今日不审，不复相见。”旋闻衙鼓彭彭，声如雷震。役云：“官升堂矣。”导李入，立堂下。阶左覆一黄铜大釜，高几如发，二健夫监守其侧，有犯人至，即以铁锸掀起，推入釜下。中有坑如井，见人首半露，攒动甚众。

    Explanation

    30

    As for rewards and compensation, it is entirely up to the lord." Li Su consulted his wife and asked the host to intercede. The host gave him ten strings of cash and provided paper money and incense for the dead, which were burned. That night, the servant arrived, greeting warmly with clasped hands, smiling so broadly that it seemed to be overflowing, and led him to a side room outside the office. After a short while, someone placed ten strings of iron money and dozens of ingots of white gold on the ground, saying, "This is the gift from Mr. Li." The servant then left. The servant said, "How dare I not do my best with your generous gift. Today, without scrutiny, we will not meet again." Soon after, the sound of the office drum was heard, booming like thunder. The servant said, "The official has ascended the hall." He led Li into the hall, where Li stood below it. On the left side of the steps was a large yellow copper cauldron, tall almost reaching the hairline, guarded by two strong men on the side. If a criminal arrived, they were immediately lifted with an iron shovel and pushed into the cauldron. Inside there was a pit like a well, with many heads half-exposed and moving.

    31

    忽堂上呼李名，李伏墀下，仰视堂中，漆漆然暗不辨物，惟见上坐神人，方面长髯，白如敷粉，迥不狞恶。至其衣冠服色，则不得辨也。神问：“汝因何事而杀殷某？”李叩首，力辨其无。神命左右取册检视。须臾又云：“固非汝杀，然汝炀灶媚主，罪亦当责。今姑看尔后效，且放尔还。日后不速改过，定置汝于拔舌狱中。”李唯唯。旋命杖殷三十，责其诬告之罪。即闻拷比声，殷哀号乞免声。循声偷视，杳不可见。神命释李。径牵下，开其锁，引至故处，众役来贺，温语百端，送李还。途间饥渴，欲买饮食，径急止之曰：“食冥中物，即勿能还矣。不然，君到此间，我辈交好，焉有不勉备东道者？”至家，役辞去，李霍然苏。自此，健壮如昔，长斋奉佛，勉为善焉。

    Explanation

    31

    Suddenly, someone called out Li's name from upstairs. Li knelt on the floor, looked up into the hall, and saw it was so dark that nothing could be seen. He only saw a deity sitting above, with a square face and long beard, white like powdered, but not at all fierce or evil. As for the deity's attire and color, he could not distinguish them. The deity asked, "Why did you kill Yin?" Li knelt and vigorously denied it. The deity ordered the attendants to fetch a book and check it. After a while, the deity said, "It was not you who killed him, but your flattery and deceit towards the master also deserve punishment. For now, I will observe your future actions and let you return. If you do not quickly reform in the future, you will surely be placed in the tongue-pulling hell." Li nodded in agreement. Then the deity ordered thirty strokes for Yin, for his false accusation. Immediately, he heard the sound of torture, and Yin cried out in pain, begging for mercy. Following the sound, he secretly looked, but it was nowhere to be seen. The deity ordered Li to be released, and he was led down directly, the lock was opened, and he was led back to his original place. The guards came to congratulate, with warm words in many ways, and sent Li back. On the way, he felt hungry and thirsty and wanted to buy food and drink. But the guard quickly stopped him and said, "If you eat food from the underworld, you will not be able to return. Otherwise, if you came here, we would be friends, and how could we not prepare for your arrival?" When he arrived home, the guard bid farewell, and Li suddenly woke up. From then on, he was as strong as before, began to keep long-term vegetarianism and worship Buddha, striving to do good.

    32

    醉茶子曰：白手难入公门，阴阳相等，不令人生不能伸冤、没不能报怨哉？然堂上片言，曲直立判，非聪明正直者，其孰能之？而贪隶好财，遂令冤苦壅于上闻，不亦可慨哉！予邑贾君子贞孝廉之兄，昼寝，梦至城隍庙，入门一探，为人捉住，压于釜底。其中迭肩压骨，厥人甚夥，闷不可言。视釜下一隙，微露光明，伏身蛇行而出，寻路遁归。一路所经景物历历在目，至家始醒。曾见巷内有一卖食物者，是其素识，令人视之，果如所见。

    Explanation

    32

    The Drunken Tea Master said: It is difficult to enter official matters without resources or connections, and the balance of yin and yang should ensure that no one is left without the ability to seek justice or avenge grievances. Yet, a few words in court can immediately determine right from wrong; who but the wise and upright could do this? However, corrupt officials love money, thus causing grievances to be blocked from reaching the authorities, which is indeed a cause for lament! The brother of the respected local scholar-official Jia Zhongxin, Jia Zhen, slept during the day and dreamed of going to the City God Temple. Upon entering, he took a look around and was caught by someone, pressed down at the bottom of a pot. There were many people stacked shoulder to shoulder, bones to bones, and the suffocation was unbearable. He saw a small gap at the bottom of the pot, revealing a faint light, and crouched low, slithering out like a snake. He found his way back home, and the scenery along the way was vividly clear. He woke up when he arrived home. He had once seen a food seller in the alley, someone he knew well, and when he asked someone to check, it turned out to be true.

    33

    黄教
    　　江西细民某，自称道接宣圣，骑瘦蹇骡，衣服蓝缕，从者数十人，率皆如丐。骑前二人执黄布旗，书“黄教圣人江西某”云。至元城诣县谒见，阍人呵逐，不听，乃坐厅前。门人以破布黄垫铺地上，稍偏，则云：“席不正不坐。”门人正之。进以餱粮莱餔，端坐拱揖后食，云：“虽疏食菜羹，必祭必斋如也。”食毕呵欠，门人请先生吸烟，正色曰：“二三子以我为瘾乎？吾无瘾乎尔。”所言大率类此，观者如堵。

    Explanation

    33

    The Yellow Sect

    A humble man from Jiangxi claimed to be a follower of the Great Teacher (Confucius), riding a lean and slow donkey, dressed in tattered clothing, with a ragtag group of followers, mostly beggars. Two men in front of him carried yellow cloth flags inscribed with "The Saint of the Yellow Sect from Jiangxi." When they arrived in Yuancheng and sought an audience with the county official, the gatekeeper tried to drive them away, but they refused and sat in front of the hall. Their disciples spread a yellow cloth mat on the ground, and if it was slightly uneven, the leader would say, "If the mat is not straight, I will not sit." The disciples would then adjust it. They were offered a simple meal of dry food and vegetable soup, which they ate after sitting upright and bowing, saying, "Though the food is simple, we must perform rituals and fast." After finishing, the leader yawned, and the disciples asked him to smoke, but he sternly replied, "Do you think I have a habit? I have no such habit." His words were largely of this nature, and the crowd watching was dense.

    34

    有赵晴轩者，诙谐善辩，见而笑之，问曰：“昔圣人狐貉以居，今先生如悬鹑结，何也？”曰：“君子固穷，若耻恶衣恶食者，未足与议也。”又云：“昔圣人一车两马，而先生仅一瘦骡，得毋寒俭？圣人弟子三千，而先生仅此数子，岂不冷落？且昔贤贫富不同，今贵及门一皆贫窭，何子路、原宪之多，而子华、子贡之少耶？请先生明以教我。”某端坐不答。好事者白于令，令赵公性方鲠，素恶异端，闻之盛怒，升堂遣役牵入，将用刑比。某叹曰：“天生德于予，知县其如予何？”卒无他语。公掷签于地，幕宾亟谏止之。及行文详上，发回本藉焉。

    Explanation

    34

    There was a man named Zhao Qingxuan, who was witty and eloquent. When he saw him, someone laughed at him and asked, "In ancient times, the sages wore fox and badger skins for clothing, but now you look like a cormorant with its feathers falling out. Why is that?" Zhao Qingxuan replied, "A gentleman remains steadfast even in poverty. If one is ashamed of poor clothing and food, it is not worth discussing with such people." The questioner continued, "In ancient times, the sages had one carriage and two horses, but you only have one thin donkey. Isn't it cold and frugal? The sages had three thousand disciples, but you only have a few. Isn't it lonely? Moreover, in ancient times, sages had different levels of wealth, but now all those who come to your door are poor. Why do you have so many like Zilu and Yuan Xian, but so few like Zihua and Zigong? Please, sir, enlighten me." Zhao Qingxuan sat calmly without answering. Someone who liked to gossip reported to the magistrate, who was straightforward and honest and always hated heretical ideas. He heard it and became very angry, went up to the hall, and sent a servant to bring Zhao Qingxuan in, intending to punish him. Zhao Qingxuan sighed, "Heaven has bestowed virtue upon me. What can the magistrate do to me?" He finally said nothing else. The magistrate threw the slip of paper on the ground, and his attendants urgently advised him to stop. When the document was sent for approval, it was returned to its original place.

    35

    奸如新莽，伪托元公；贤若王通，貌学孔子。后世并招毁谤。所以然者，圣人既没，不必再有圣人。即使复生圣人，亦不必如当年之情事吻合、举动毕肖，始得谓之圣人。夫孔子，圣之时者也，时字最为生动。使圣人生于今，断不能如当年之行事。故曰“生今反古，灾及其身”，良有以也。譬如当今之世，再行井田等法，其可得乎？王安石诸人，为可监也。独怪今之异端惑人，恐众不易惑，则必托为正教，口讲《论语》《大学》，以为独得真传，其立教之名目，则又超乎杨墨佛老，以及白莲、白衣等教之外者，如所谓大成教、忠恕道等是也。无知愚氓，翕然相从，固不足异。所可异者，读书之士亦受其惑，真世道人心之变矣。吾尝谓：人生斯世，欲事事与圣贤无殊，斯亦大难。朱柏庐先生云：读书志在圣贤，斯亦可矣。

    Explanation

    35

    Wicked as Wang Mang, who falsely claimed the title of Grand Tutor; virtuous as Wang Tong, who imitated Confucius. Both were criticized in later generations. The reason for this is that after the death of the sages, it is not necessary for there to be another sage. Even if a sage were to be born again, it would not be required for them to perfectly match the circumstances and actions of previous sages to be considered a sage. Confucius, being a sage of his time, had the term "timely" most vividly described. If a sage were to be born today, they would certainly not be able to act exactly as Confucius did. Hence the saying, "Born in the present but returning to the past, disaster will befall them," which is well-founded. For example, in today's world, could the well-field system and other ancient laws be implemented again? Figures like Wang Anshi serve as cautionary examples. What is surprising is that modern heretics who seek to deceive people, fearing that the masses will not easily be deceived, will claim to be orthodox by quoting the Analects and the Great Learning, claiming to have received the true transmission. The names of their teachings go beyond those of Yang, Mo, Buddhism, and Taoism, as well as the White Lotus and White Robe sects, such as the so-called Great Completion Teachings and the Way of Loyalty and Forbearance. The ignorant and foolish follow them, which is not surprising. What is surprising is that scholars are also deceived, truly a change in the world and human nature. I have said: "To be exactly like a sage in every aspect in this world is also very difficult." Master Zhu Boli said: "Aspiring to be a sage through reading is also commendable."

    36

    马生
    　　徐若玉，青齐人，以故入都，寓涿鹿客舍。夜卧吸烟，忽灯光青黯，烟筒塞窒，遣仆探以铁签，再试如故。乃祝曰：“倘有幽魂亦嗜此味，不妨略尝。仆非吝啬者，何必作此惊怪？”因烧烟向空虚举，旋闻筒响飕飕，一口居然吸尽。如是者再。徐曰：“既是同好，必是良朋，盍现形共谈，亦足释闷。”即见对面枕上卧一人，年二十许，面目黧黑，衣裳篮缕，举手作揖状，形容足恭，笑曰：“仆名君妍，马姓，燕都人。幼时业儒，酷嗜烟。家君督责勿改，遂抑郁以没。服阕，有数人力劝改行，赠金使入都应童子科。至试期，贪烟未起。及醒，则红日半窗，试院门扃。乃淹留于烟肄，金尽被遂，寄身野寺，为僧服役。偶盗僧钱，僧徒重挞几死，乘机夜遁，乞食北行。途中病瘾，困惫卧柳下，不图葬诸犬腹。

    Explanation

    36

    Mysterious Encounter

    Xu Ruoyu, a man from Qingzhou and Qilu, entered the capital for some reason and stayed at an inn in Zhuolu. One night, while smoking, he noticed the lamp light turn dim and the smoke pipe become clogged. He sent a servant to clear it with an iron wire, but the problem persisted. He then prayed, saying, "If there is a ghost who also enjoys this flavor, it would be no harm to let it have a taste. I am not a stingy person, so why act so surprised?" He lit the pipe and held it toward the air. Soon, he heard a whistling sound from the pipe, and the ghost took a long, satisfying puff. This happened twice more. Xu said, "Since we share the same interest, you must be a good friend. Why not show yourself and talk together? It would relieve my boredom." Suddenly, he saw a man lying on the pillow opposite him, about twenty years old, with a dark complexion and ragged clothes. The man bowed respectfully, looking very polite, and smiled, saying, "My name is Jun Yan, and I am from Yan Capital. In my youth, I studied Confucianism and had a strong liking for tobacco. My father scolded me for not changing, and I died of depression. After the mourning period, several people encouraged me to change my ways and gave me money to take the examination for young scholars. On the day of the exam, I was too greedy for tobacco to wake up. When I woke up, the sun was halfway up the window, and the exam hall door was locked. So, I stayed in a tobacco shop, spent all my money, and ended up living in a remote temple, serving the monks. I accidentally stole the monks' money, and they severely beat me almost to death. I took the opportunity to escape at night and begged for food as I traveled north. On the way, I fell ill with addiction, tired and weak, and lay down under a willow tree. Unfortunately, I was buried in the dogs' stomachs."

    37

    家君在冥曹为六路司吏总管，深恶痛绝，闭予于幽室，烦苦殆不可言。有父执数人知之，力谏家君，乃出诸幽室。时冥间考取遗才，以补司吏之缺，遣予应考，途行经此，闻烟气飞空，不觉喉中奇痒难耐，故此相扰。”问考期何日，曰：“即今日丑刻入场，明日午刻出场。”徐曰：“此其时矣！君胡不行？”曰：“再求少赐恩膏，便当贾勇前进。”徐又与之。未几鸡声动野，明星有烂。徐曰：“天将晓矣，尚流连耶？”生曰：“予酷好此，每吸烟一口，便觉两腋风生，飘飘然如上九霄而登大宝，虽玉皇香案吏亦不屑为，况考取冥差耶？即使补作冥王，予亦不去。”徐闻大怒，声色俱厉曰：“此物非不可尝，苟文人墨客，浅尝辄止，用以陶悦性情，有何不可？若因此丧产败家，寡廉鲜耻，断不可为！”生云：“君言差矣！大抵我辈皆应运而生。昔人嗜酒，今人嗜烟，气运使然也。若再历数百年，更不知又有何物之可嗜也。

    Explanation

    37

    My father, the Six-Department Chief in the Underworld, was deeply averse to me and confined me to a dark room, where my suffering was almost unbearable to describe. Several respected elders learned of this and strongly advised my father, who then released me from the dark room. At the time, the Underworld was conducting examinations to recruit talented individuals to fill the vacancies among the officials, and they sent me to take the exam. On my way, I passed by this place and smelled the smoke rising into the air, which caused an unbearable itch in my throat, so I came here to bother you. I asked when the exam would take place, and you replied, "Today at the hour of the Ox (1 AM to 3 AM) for the entrance exam, and tomorrow at the hour of the Horse (11 AM to 1 PM) for the exit exam." You said, "This is the time! Why don't you go?" I replied, "Please grant me a little more favor, and I will muster the courage to proceed." You granted me the favor. Soon, the sound of chickens crowing in the wilderness and the appearance of stars in the sky indicated that dawn was approaching. You said, "It is almost morning, yet you still linger?" I replied, "I am deeply fond of this. Every time I take a puff of smoke, I feel a breeze in my armpits, and I soar as if ascending to the heavens and ascending to the throne. Even the officials serving the Jade Emperor would not be worth it, let alone being recruited as an official in the Underworld? Even if I were to be appointed as the Underworld King, I would not go." You heard this and became furious, speaking sternly, "This substance is not something that cannot be tasted. If scholars and literati indulge only lightly to cultivate their temperament, there is nothing wrong with it. But if it leads to the loss of one's property and the destruction of one's family, to lack shame and moral principles, it is absolutely forbidden!" I said, "Your words are mistaken! Generally, we are all born according to our destiny. In the past, people enjoyed drinking, and now people enjoy smoking, which is determined by the course of nature. If we live for another few hundred years, who knows what else we might enjoy?"

    38

    使古时有烟，吾知嵇康、阮藉、刘伶、陶潜诸人，必溺烟而不起矣。且必有人云：若使某人为烟帝，定须封我隐乡侯矣。嗜酒为名士，岂嗜烟非名士乎？”徐曰：“嗜己之烟已非名士，况嗜人之烟而要为名士乎？”生曰：“毕吏部盗酒，不拘小节，古今称之。我马君妍直与毕卓并著。”徐怒，欲忿老拳。仆闻，入室助之。生跪而哀曰：“冥律不比阳世愦愦，凡投考不到者，便捉去下刖足狱。此刻试期已误，罚必不免，况家君不能容。叩求长者仁慈，许寄床下。此后吸烟所不敢望，乞取贵斗中余黏可耳。”徐骂曰：“何物饿鬼，无故缠人!仆为我力搏之。”方格斗间，忽帘钩作响，一牛头厉鬼持钢叉入，大呼曰：“尔在此耶！吾奉王旨，搜罗考试不到者，牵赴市曹行刑。王曾有例，患病有事故者均兔，独吸烟、赌博、宿娼三等人，例所不赦。

    Explanation

    38

    If there were smoking in ancient times, I know that Ji Kang, Ruan Ji, Liu Ling, Tao Qian, and others would become so addicted to smoking that they could not stop. And there would certainly be someone who would say, "If someone were to become the emperor of smoking, they would definitely have to grant me the title of Marquis of Hidden Village." Loving alcohol makes one a scholar-official, how could loving smoking not make one a scholar-official?" Xu said, "Loving one's own smoking is already not being a scholar-official, how much more so if one loves others' smoking and still wants to be a scholar-official?" The student said, "Bi Sheng, the Minister of the Imperial Secretariat, stole wine, did not care about minor details, and was praised by people throughout history. My friend Ma Junyan is equally famous as Bi Zhuo." Xu became angry and wanted to beat him up with his fists. I heard and went into the room to help. The student knelt and pleaded, "The laws of the underworld are not as chaotic as those in the mortal world; anyone who fails to take the exam will be caught and sent to the prison for amputation. Now that the exam time has been missed, punishment is unavoidable, and my father cannot tolerate this. I beg for your mercy and ask you to let me hide under your bed. From now on, I dare not hope to smoke, I just ask for a little of the tobacco left in your pouch." Xu shouted, "What kind of hungry ghost are you, bothering me for no reason!" I will fight for you." While they were fighting, suddenly the curtain hook made a sound, and a fierce ghost with a bull's head holding a steel fork entered, shouting, "Are you here? I am here on the king's orders to search for those who did not take the exam and bring them to the market for execution. The king has a rule that exempts those who are sick or have other reasons, but smoking, gambling, and prostitution are not excused."

    39

    ”生闻言，若崩厥角，乃谓曰：“牛兄请息怒，此间烟味颇佳，曷不吸食？”即取盘中铜盒捧献牛鬼。鬼接盒，颜稍解。揭视盒中，已无余沥，大怒，骂曰：“无耻贼！以他人之物媚人，而又诳人，予誓擒尔去！”徐曰：“何不速叉？”生急取烟灰，徐力夺而弃于地。生乃伏身就舐，向鬼曰：“牛兄试尝尝，味胜刍豆多矣！”牛怒曰：“我虽牛首，而食人食者，汝以我为畜耶？”以叉刺其胫。生长号，如斩豕。徐劝勿毙其命，视之已死。

    Explanation

    39

    The man, upon hearing this, collapsed in despair, and said, "Brother Cow, please calm down. The smoke here is quite good. Why not try it?" He then took the copper box from the plate and offered it to the cow ghost. The ghost took the box, and his expression softened slightly. Upon opening it, he found it empty and flew into a rage, shouting, "Shameless thief! You flatter me with someone else's possessions and deceive me. I swear to catch you and take you away!" M said, "Why not quickly use the fork?" The man hastily took the smoke ash and, with great force, threw it to the ground. The man then crawled down to lick it and said to the ghost, "Brother Cow, try it. It tastes much better than grass and beans!" The cow ghost roared, "Although I have a cow's head, I eat human food. Do you think of me as an animal?" He then stabbed the man's leg with the fork. The man screamed loudly, like slaughtering a pig. M tried to stop him from killing the man, but when he looked again, the man was already dead.

    40

    徐深怨牛，牛曰：“无妨无妨。此非真死，乃咽喉科所谓斗底风也，嗅以烟灰立愈。”试之果苏，乃令牵去。鬼觅锁，生脱然而逃。徐惊曰：“可为奈何？”鬼曰：“此子狡滑，闵不畏死。然去当不远，君东邻有煮烟者，定往依之。予别矣。”乃持叉去。徐遣仆往探东邻，见烟沈淋漓满地，问故，邻人曰：“适有怪风一旋，炉鼎倾覆，实不知其故。”仆语其由。邻人急请术士驱遣三日，屋中旋风不休，直至地干余沈，风始寂然。意其又寻他赴也。
    　　醉茶子曰：烟之为累，如此其甚哉！倾家败产，犹不改悔，真口腹之害为心害矣。予尝戏作《陋室铭》体，附录以博一笑：“灯不在高，有油则明；斗不在大，过瘾则灵。斯是烟室，惟烟气馨。烟痕粘手黑，灰色透皮青；谈笑有荡子，往来无壮丁。可以供夜话、闭月经。笑搓灰之入妙，怪吹笛而无声。长安凌烟阁，余杭招隐亭。燕人云：欲罢不能。”

    Explanation

    40

    Xu deeply resented the cow, but the cow said, "No problem, no problem. This is not true death; it's what the throat doctors call 'throat wind.' Inhaling some tobacco ash will cure it immediately." When Xu tried it, the cow indeed revived, and he ordered it to be taken away. The ghost searched for a lock, and Xu suddenly escaped. Xu was startled and said, "What can be done about it?" The ghost replied, "This creature is cunning and doesn't fear death. However, it won't go far. Your neighbor to the east has someone who smokes, and it will surely go there. I'll take my leave." The ghost then took a fork and left. Xu sent a servant to check on the neighbor to the east. The servant found tobacco ash scattered all over the ground and asked what had happened. The neighbor said, "Just now, there was a strange wind that spun around, and the stove and tripod overturned. I really don't know the reason." The servant told him the cause. The neighbor quickly called in a sorcerer to exorcise the place for three days. The whirlwind in the house kept spinning until the ground dried up and the ash remained. Only then did the wind finally subside. It seems the ghost went to seek another place.

    The Drunken Tea Master said, "The burden of smoking is so severe! Even if it ruins one's family and property, one still doesn't repent. Truly, the harm of the mouth and stomach becomes a harm to the heart." I once playfully wrote an essay in the style of the "Inscription of a Modest Abode" to amuse myself: "A lamp doesn't need to be high; as long as it has oil, it will shine. A pot doesn't need to be big; as long as it's satisfying, it works. This is a smoking room, where only the smoke lingers. Tobacco stains make the hands black, and the gray color penetrates the skin. There are idle people who chat and laugh, and no strong men coming and going. It can be used for nighttime conversation and to close the menstrual cycle. I laugh at the skill of mixing ash and find it amazing, but strange that the flute has no sound. The Hall of Worthies in Chang'an, the Pavilion of Seclusion in Yuhang. The people of Yan say: 'Wanting to stop but unable to do so.'"

    41

    介休令
    　　马广文莲溪先生云：介休令某将赴任，路经井陉，晚投旅邸。店主人以客满辞。令思舍此无止宿处，固强之。不得已，息于主人之室，主人自归家，遣从人休于后院。令掩扉独卧，一人推扉入，手持霜刃，直逼卧榻。方欲骇问，其人力决其首，弃刀床下，返身遽去。令觉刃过其处凉如冰，殊不痛苦，头划然落枕畔，目瞠瞠，心了了，但口不能言耳。旋见一老人唐巾阔服，仿佛社公，至榻前，顿足撮手，不胜悔恨。俄闻窗外有人云：“城隍驾到！”老人伏地膝行，迓入，见城隍金冠玉蟒，气象不凡，责老人曰：“汝为一方土地，所司何事，而疏忽至此？”老人顿首谢过，屏息不敢言。

    Explanation

    41

    The County Magistrate of Jie County

    According to Master Lianxi, a man of letters named Ma Guangwen, the county magistrate of Jie County was about to take up his post and traveled through Jingxing. In the evening, he sought lodging at an inn. The innkeeper refused due to full occupancy. The magistrate thought there was no other place to stay, so he insisted. Reluctantly, he rested in the innkeeper's room. The innkeeper left home and sent a servant to rest in the back courtyard. The magistrate closed the door and lay down alone. Suddenly, a person pushed the door open and entered, holding a cold blade and approaching the bed directly. The magistrate was about to be startled and ask, but the person forcefully severed his head, dropped the knife on the bed, and turned around and left quickly. The magistrate felt the blade pass by, as cold as ice, but did not feel pain; his head fell onto the pillow beside him, his eyes wide open, his mind clear, but he could not speak. Soon, he saw an old man with a broad hat and loose clothing, resembling a village god, come to the bedside, stomping and clapping hands, unable to bear the regret and anger. Soon, someone outside the window said, "The City God has arrived!" The old man knelt and crawled on his knees, welcoming the City God inside. The City God, with a golden crown and jade dragon robe, had an extraordinary presence and rebuked the old man, saying, "You, as the land deity of this area, what are you responsible for, and how could you be so negligent?" The old man bowed his head in apology and remained silent, unable to speak.

    42

    神乃双挽袍袂，奉其颅置肩上，使老人按摩伤处。令初觉项冷沁骨，徐而暖气蒸腾，身首居然相合。神乃起，嘱老人曰：“谨为防守，勿令凶暴再来。”老人唯唯。神出，老人膝行送之。既去，老人严扃屋门，以木椅靠门坐。令欲问故，苦不能声，半晌昏昏睡去，即不知老人作何状矣。明日将午，店主怪其晏起，使从人唤之，莫应，门则牢不可开。因破窗而入，见令昏睡枕上，殷血淋淋，床下横铡刀一具，相与惊诧。而令亦醒，备言夜间之事。主人细询凶人年貌，始悟前日有本处地保某，固赌博无赖，索钱不遂，怀恨主人而误杀令也。令诣县自陈，拘地保讯之，果不诬焉，遂置于法。

    Explanation

    42

    The deity then pulled up the sleeves of their robe, placed the severed head on their shoulder, and had the elderly person massage the injured area. The official first felt a coldness penetrating his neck, but gradually, warmth began to rise, and his head and body were miraculously reunited. The deity then stood up and instructed the elderly person, saying, "Be careful to guard this place and prevent the violent person from returning." The elderly person nodded in agreement. As the deity departed, the elderly person crawled on his knees to see them off. After the deity left, the elderly person locked the door tightly and sat with a wooden chair leaning against it. The official wanted to ask what had happened but could not speak, and after a while, he fell into a deep sleep, unaware of the elderly person's actions. The next day, just before noon, the innkeeper was surprised that the official had not yet risen and sent a servant to wake him. There was no response, and the door was impossible to open. They broke through the window and entered, seeing the official lying unconscious on the bed, with blood flowing profusely, and a large cleaver lying across the foot of the bed. They were all shocked. The official also woke up and recounted the events of the night. The innkeeper carefully inquired about the appearance and age of the violent person, and then realized that the previous day, a certain village headman from the area had been a habitual gambler and had failed in demanding money, harboring resentment against the innkeeper and mistakenly killing the official. The official went to the county office to report the incident, and the village headman was arrested and interrogated. It was confirmed that the accusation was true, and he was punished according to the law.

    43

    张兴
    　　张兴，中山人，家有饫田。八口藉以无馁，日惟纵饮都市。一日薄醉，遨游市中，忽遇其故窗友王某。张忘其死，欣然把臂，相将至枣林下，据地而谈。暮色昏黄，纤月东上，张觉腹馁，邀王入市共饮。至则酒楼灯息，扣门无有应者。盖时已三鼓矣。约王至家，出肴酒，挑灯共饮。笑谈间，晓鸡乱唱，王起与辞，张挽不释。王云：“痛饮甚快，但官府将理奇案，欲往一探，不可再为迟留。”张请偕往，王不许。固强之，王云：“去亦不妨，但有所见，无庸骇怪，不然两俱无益。”张叩其故，王云：“实告君，此地将有大变。今日冥君点视劫鬼，此阴曹之大典也。”张闻，益欲快睹。王云：“如此相缠，即随我去，胆怯时勿悔。”携其手至阈而踬，曳起同行。张觉身轻如叶，飘忽至一处，宫阙巍峨，榱题黮楗。

    Explanation

    43

    Zhan Xing, a man from Zhongshan, had ample fields and sufficient resources to support his family of eight without hunger. Every day, he indulged in drinking in the city. One day, slightly intoxicated, he wandered through the market and unexpectedly met his old schoolmate, Wang, who had since passed away. Zhan Xing, forgetting his friend's death, joyfully clasped his arm and led him to a jujube grove, where they sat on the ground and talked. As the evening grew dim and a slender moon rose in the east, Zhan Xing felt hungry and invited Wang to return to the city for a drink.
