    　　邑有孝女某，已许字于人矣。其父母老而无子，日以嗣续为忧。女抑郁不乐，遂日夜虔拜北斗，诚敬有年。一夕，神障于庭，赤发朱髯，面貌狞恶。问何所求，女对以愿化男子，以承宗祧。神颔之，遂不见。次日觉腹中暖气蒸蒸，下达隐处，扪之则阳在下也，俨然丈夫矣。言其情于婿家，遂绝婚。好事者以其婿之妹妻之，生二子，奉亲终老焉。

    Explanation

    124

    Water Ghost

    A blind man was about to leave the western outskirts, on the way to the city god's temple, when someone pulled his staff and said, "Go a few steps from here, and please, sir, push my humble creation." The blind man followed him to the pond behind the temple. Recognizing that there were no houses in the area, he was surprised and asked, "What do you want?" The person replied, "I have no other request, just that the water is cool, and I would like to bathe with you." The blind man strongly refused. The person said, "There is a different, better place in the water, far superior to living on land." The blind man became very upset, and the person finally left, likely a water ghost seeking a replacement. The next day, a boy from the Zhu family drowned in that place.

    The Drunken Tea Master said: Seeking a replacement and deceiving the blind, the ghost is truly deceitful and not upright. Those in the world who plot to trap people in pits and difficult situations, all treat people as if they were blind, how can one help but lament!

    A Woman Transforms into a Man

    In a certain town, there was a filial daughter who had already been engaged to be married. Her parents were old and had no children, and they were daily worried about continuing the family line. The daughter was depressed and unhappy, so she devoted herself to worshipping the Big Dipper night and day, with sincere reverence for many years. One night, a deity appeared in the courtyard, with red hair and a red beard, a fierce and ugly appearance. When asked what she wanted, the daughter replied that she wished to transform into a man to carry on the family line. The deity nodded, and then disappeared. The next day, she felt warmth rising in her abdomen, down to her private parts, and upon touching, she found herself with a male organ below, clearly a man. She explained the situation to her future husband's family, and they broke off the engagement. Someone who liked to meddle in others' affairs married her to her husband's sister, and she gave birth to two sons, serving her parents until old age.

    125

    醉茶子曰：女岂木兰、缇萦之流与？何其志之诚也！夫诚能格天，况诚而出于孝，天有不悯之者乎？而造物之巧，卒能易巾帼为须眉，盖许其孝而成其志矣。若非孝，则人妖耳，又何足贵？
    吴恭人
    　　吴恭人，邑杨君绍庭之侧室。夫死不悲，人皆疑其有异志，而吴自若也。一夕，沐浴更衣，入室自缢。延僧超度，有童子见杨公与恭人皆冠带立坛下。
    　　醉茶子曰：之死靡他，烈气不泯。宜其相从地下，神之格思也。不然，铙鼓香花，岂郎能召致烈魄哉！
    孟都阃
    　　孟都阃泽厚，乡先达也。官胶州时，公余夜坐，见檐际一人，践瓦阔步，轻捷如猱。盂呼曰：“丈夫有此绝技，当尽力疆场，胡为作梁上君子？”其人跃下，跪云：“此固非小人所愿，迫于贫耳。公能援引，敢不自爱？”公许之。次日来谒，唤入谆诫再三，使充营卒。后以战功膺职，卒为善士。惜传者忘其姓名耳。

    Explanation

    125

    The Drunken Tea Master says: Are you of the same caliber as Mulan or Ti Ying? How sincere is your resolve! If sincerity can move heaven, how much more so when it comes from filial piety? Heaven would not fail to be moved! And the ingenuity of creation could ultimately change a woman into a man. This is why they allowed her filial piety to fulfill her aspirations. If it were not for filial piety, it would be mere human pretense, and what value would that have?

    Madam Wu
    Madam Wu was a concubine of Yang Jun Shao Ting from the town. She did not grieve over his death, and everyone suspected she had ulterior motives. But Madam Wu remained calm. One night, she bathed, changed her clothes, and hanged herself in her room. She invited monks to perform a ritual for her soul, and a child saw both Madam Wu and her husband standing in official attire at the altar.

    The Drunken Tea Master says: Her loyalty to death was unwavering, and her fierce spirit remained unextinguished. It is fitting that they follow each other in the afterlife, a manifestation of divine will. Otherwise, how could the drumming, incense, and flowers summon such a fierce spirit!

    Madam Meng Zehou
    Madam Meng Zehou, a respected elder of the village, served as the magistrate of Jiaozhou. After official duties, she would sit in the evening. One night, she saw a person on the eaves, walking across the tiles with wide strides, as agile as a monkey. She called out, "A man with such extraordinary skills should serve on the battlefield, why become a thief?" The person jumped down and knelt, saying, "This is certainly not what I desire, but I am forced by poverty." She said, "If you can help me, I will not disgrace myself." The next day, he came to visit, and she called him in, repeatedly instructing him to serve as a soldier. Later, he earned a position through his military achievements and became a good man. Unfortunately, the transmitter forgot his name.

    126

    醉茶子曰：一言之悟，翻然改途，此其所以为豪杰。若心怀叵测，始则欣然从，久则变故生，与引狼入室者何异？
    林某
    　　林某者，酗酒滋事，邻里成厌恶之。时村中有怪，恒夜出为祟，众激林曰：“尔素以胆力自诩，敢与怪遇乎？”林曰：“是不难。能为我备牛酒，当擒怪来。”众如其言，林携酒一大瓶，乘醉坐村外。夜有物，高八尺许，黑暗莫辨形貌，问林为谁，林曰：“我妖怪也。尔为谁？”物曰：“我亦如君，但肢体未备，不若君酷肖人形耳。所携何物？”林对以酒，且劝之尝，物曰：“予腰不能屈，烦君倾我口内。”林扪其口，大如杯，提壶灌之。物赞曰：“快哉！”颓然而倒。林急斫以斧，其声丁丁，呼人照之，败棺板也。毁之，怪绝。
    　　醉茶子曰：一醉而玉山倾倒，兴酣之际，更不知害事也。使怪不贪酒，林某奈何？即劝以酒，怪若曰“吾不为一斗折腰”，又焉知林不中馁哉？

    Explanation

    126

    醉茶子曰：一言之悟，翻然改途，此其所以为豪杰。若心怀叵测，始则欣然从，久则变故生，与引狼入室者何异？
    The Drunken Tea Master said: A single word of enlightenment can lead one to suddenly change their path, and this is why such a person is considered a hero. If someone harbors treacherous intentions, they may initially seem agreeable, but over time, unforeseen troubles will arise—how different is this from inviting a wolf into one’s home?

    林某
    　　林某者，酗酒滋事，邻里成厌恶之。时村中有怪，恒夜出为祟，众激林曰：“尔素以胆力自诩，敢与怪遇乎？”林曰：“是不难。能为我备牛酒，当擒怪来。”众如其言，林携酒一大瓶，乘醉坐村外。夜有物，高八尺许，黑暗莫辨形貌，问林为谁，林曰：“我妖怪也。尔为谁？”物曰：“我亦如君，但肢体未备，不若君酷肖人形耳。所携何物？”林对以酒，且劝之尝，物曰：“予腰不能屈，烦君倾我口内。”林扪其口，大如杯，提壶灌之。物赞曰：“快哉！”颓然而倒。林急斫以斧，其声丁丁，呼人照之，败棺板也。毁之，怪绝。
    Lin
    　　Lin was a man who, through excessive drinking and causing trouble, had become despised by his neighbors. At that time, there was a strange occurrence in the village: a creature would often appear at night to cause mischief. The villagers provoked Lin, saying, “You always boast of your courage and strength—dare you to confront this creature?” Lin replied, “It’s not difficult. If you can prepare ox and wine for me, I will capture the creature for you.” The villagers followed his words, and Lin carried a large bottle of wine, sitting drunk outside the village. At night, there appeared a creature, about eight feet tall, whose form could not be discerned in the darkness. It asked Lin, “Who are you?” Lin said, “I am an evil spirit. Who are you?” The creature replied, “I am like you, but my body is not fully formed, unlike you who closely resemble a human. What do you carry?” Lin answered with wine and urged it to taste it. The creature said, “My waist cannot bend, so please pour it into my mouth.” Lin felt its mouth, which was as large as a cup, and poured the wine into it. The creature exclaimed, “How delightful!” and collapsed. Lin quickly chopped at it with an axe, the sound of the chopping echoing, and called others to see. It turned out to be a broken coffin plank. After destroying it, the strange occurrence ceased.

    醉茶子曰：一醉而玉山倾倒，兴酣之际，更不知害事也。使怪不贪酒，林某奈何？即劝以酒，怪若曰“吾不为一斗折腰”，又焉知林不中馁哉？
    The Drunken Tea Master said: Drunk and collapsed like a jade mountain, in the midst of one’s excitement, one is even more unaware of causing trouble. If the creature did not crave wine, what could Lin do? Even if he persuaded it with wine, if the creature said, “I will not bow down for a single measure of grain,” how could Lin avoid losing heart?

    127

    铁猫
    　　邑某贾，贩铁为业，家渐裕。有善水者于海河底得铁猫四，古色斑驳，锈花灿然，每器重数百斤，上铸贞观年号。贾以贱价买之，拟毁作他器，则利倍蓰。夜梦四老人来谒，庞眉皓首，阔服唐巾，神采俱俊逸。云：“予兄弟四人，姓毛，自唐太宗皇帝征高丽时留寓于此，已有年矣。不意为弄潮儿所获。今到君家，乞施仁惠。若肯置诸故处，予等当厚报长者。不然，有损于我，亦无益于君也。”某醒后，知为四器之灵，欲弃诸汪洋，而心生觊觎，以梦为幻，入炉熔焉。未几，家遇回禄，不能再振，子孙零落为丐。
    　　醉茶子曰：或曰器果有灵，则当自庇，是数所应然。何祸人为？不知贾遭回禄，不必即是器之报复。然既能幻形示梦，灵固昭然。且彼卑辞乞命，毁之毋乃不仁。若能舍之，安知物即无情耶？夫利令智昏，斯人类然。况放利之流，更无足异矣。

    Explanation

    127

    Iron Cat
    In a certain district, there was a merchant who made a living by trading iron, and his family gradually became prosperous. A skilled swimmer retrieved four iron cats from the bottom of the sea. The cats had an ancient, weathered appearance, with patches of rust and bright, ornate patterns. Each weighed several hundred pounds, and they bore the inscription of the reign title "Zhen Guan." The merchant bought them at a low price, intending to melt them down into other objects to make a substantial profit.

    That night, he dreamed of four elderly men coming to visit. They had long, bushy eyebrows and white hair, wore wide sleeves and Tang-dynasty-style headbands, and had a noble and elegant demeanor. They said, "We are four brothers, surnamed Mao. We were left here during the time when Emperor Taizong of Tang campaigned in Goguryeo. We have been here for many years. We did not expect to be retrieved by those who play with the waves. Now that we have come to your home, we ask for your kindness. If you would return us to our original place, we will reward you generously. Otherwise, it will be harmful to us and will not benefit you."

    After waking, the merchant realized that the four objects were the spirits of the elderly men. He considered abandoning them in the sea but was tempted by greed. He dismissed the dream as an illusion and melted the objects down. Soon after, his family suffered a fire disaster and could not recover. His descendants eventually became beggars.

    The Drunken Tea Drinker said: Some argue that if objects truly have spirits, they should protect themselves. This is the way of fate. Why should they harm others? But it is not necessarily the case that the fire disaster was a direct punishment from the objects. However, since they could appear in a dream to beg for their lives, their spirits are clearly evident. Moreover, to destroy them after they pleaded for mercy is unkind. If he had returned them, who can say that the objects were not sentient? Greed clouds one's judgment, and this is true of humans. As for those who pursue profit, it is no wonder they act in such a way.

    128

    三疯
    　　乞儿三疯者，未详其姓氏，日行乞于市。人怜其痴，佥乐饭之。后月余始一见，或问之，疯云：“前者饥饿将死，幸三姑怜我，招至其家，食尽珍羞，眠卧锦绣，真平生之奇遇也。不然，焉有今日？”问：“三姑为谁，果美乎？”曰：“彼神仙中人也，乌得不美？”问：“何谓神仙？”曰：“跃舞檐端，飞升树杪，非神而何？”问居何处，则指破寺中。次日，见疯面有搔痕，穷诘其故。初犹隐讳，继云：“三姑恶我多口，已受责矣，何敢再泄？”好事者偕友人入寺瞰之，寺中殿宇倾圮，蓬茅塞径。墙坳一古槐，绿影匝地，仿佛似有人迹。就之，乃三疯枕石酣睡，身旁覆一大蝎虎，长与人等，尾摇摇掀动。众骇奔出，始悟所谓三姑者，即此物也。后不知三疯所终。

    Explanation

    128

    A Mad Beggar

    There was a beggar known as "San Feng," whose surname was unknown. He begged daily in the market. People pitied his foolishness and were all glad to feed him. After more than a month, he was seen again. Someone asked him about it, and San Feng said, "Before, I was on the verge of starvation and death. Fortunately, San Guo pitied me and invited me to her home. I ate all kinds of delicacies and slept on luxurious bedding. It was truly a remarkable encounter in my lifetime. Otherwise, how could I be here today?" When asked, "Who is San Guo? Is she beautiful?" he replied, "She is a celestial being; how could she not be beautiful?" When asked, "What is a celestial being?" he said, "She leaps and dances on the eaves, flies and ascends to the treetops. Who else could she be?" When asked where she lived, he pointed to a ruined temple. The next day, it was noticed that San Feng had scratches on his face. After thorough questioning, he initially kept silent but then said, "San Guo disliked my talkativeness and punished me already. How dare I reveal it again?" Curious people accompanied by friends entered the temple to see him. The temple's halls were in ruins, and the path was blocked by overgrown vegetation. In the corner of the wall stood an ancient locust tree, with green shadows covering the ground, seemingly showing signs of human activity. Approaching him, they found San Feng sleeping soundly on a stone, with a large scorpion-like creature covering him, as long as a person, and its tail swaying and moving. The people were frightened and ran out. They finally realized that the so-called San Guo was this creature. Later, no one knew what happened to San Feng.

    129

    醉茶子曰：枕石栖林，妖姬相伴，自三疯视之，不知作何景象矣。诗云：“闻道神仙不可接，别有天地非人间。”此之谓乎？
    狐革
    　　邑北仓村赵公，忘其名字。读书别墅。每月明见有物，兽首人身，潜入空室，窥之，有狐皮一具，折叠地上。公持归藏之。夜半，物至公斋，跪而人语曰：“我等修炼功成，即无须此。今道力尚微，首未能脱，天明依然故态。倘不赐还，日出即成鬼物矣。”哀求不已。赵不答。跪至晓，扑地化为狐，自颌下如新剥者然。

    Explanation

    129

    Master Tea-Drinker said: Resting on stones and dwelling in the woods, accompanied by beautiful women, from the perspective of the Three Fools, one cannot know what the scene would be like. The poem says: "Hearing that immortals cannot be reached, there is another world, not of this earth." Is this what it means?

    Fur of the Fox

    A gentleman from the village of North Warehouse in the northern outskirts, whose name has been forgotten, studied in a villa. Each month, he would see something under the moonlight, a creature with the head of an animal and the body of a human, slipping into an empty room. He observed it, and there was a piece of fox skin folded on the ground. The gentleman took it home and hid it. At midnight, the creature returned to the gentleman's study, kneeling and speaking in human language: "We have completed our cultivation, so we no longer need this. Now our spiritual power is still weak, and we cannot yet shed our heads. At dawn, we will return to our former state. If you do not return it, at sunrise, we will become ghostly beings." The creature continuously pleaded in sorrow, but the gentleman did not respond. It knelt until dawn, falling to the ground and transforming into a fox, from the chin down, it was like freshly skinned.

    130

    醉茶子曰：妖不扰人，纵之而不必杀之。赵公所为，吾不谓是。
    巨头鬼
    　　邑萧某，提灯夜行，遇一鬼，高三四尺许，头大如瓮，面色深青，目炯双灯，齿森长戟，以两手捧颊，行步蹇缓。见人至，退避墙隅。萧固胆壮，以灯柄击其头，鬼目蹙眉攒，似甚痛恶。释之。归病，月余始瘥。
    　　醉茶子曰：阴阳对待，人鬼殊途。人不能见鬼者，阳气胜也。若阳气衰竭，猝然见鬼，吾知其不死即病。昔有走无常者云：“人不见鬼，鬼无时不见人。”问其何故，则茫然不解。予曰：“暗处视明处易，明处视暗处难，非其理乎？”走无常者深然其言。
    小夜叉
    　　保阳士人，晓起入书室理文具，见墨床上卧一物，如夜叉状，赤发蓝身，袒臂着红裤，枕墨酣眠，翕翕犹未醒也。士惊愕间，物已觉，翻身一跃化为蝶，翩翩然破窗飞去。

    Explanation

    130

    Master Tea-Drunken says: If a spirit does not trouble people, let it be and there is no need to kill it. What Zhao Gong did, I do not consider right.

    Giant-Headed Ghost
    A certain Mr. Xiao from the town, carrying a lamp and walking at night, encountered a ghost. The ghost was about three or four feet tall, with a head as big as a jar, a deeply blue complexion, eyes shining like two lamps, and teeth like long spears. Holding his cheeks with both hands, he walked slowly and awkwardly. When he saw someone coming, he retreated to the corner of the wall. Mr. Xiao, who was brave and unafraid, hit the ghost's head with the lamp handle. The ghost's eyes squinted and his brows furrowed, as if in great pain and disgust. Mr. Xiao let him go. He fell ill and took more than a month to recover.

    Master Tea-Drunken says: Yin and Yang are opposed, and the paths of humans and spirits are different. People cannot see spirits because their yang energy is strong. If one's yang energy is exhausted, and they suddenly see a spirit, I know they will either die or fall ill. A person who goes on errands for the underworld says, "People do not see spirits, but spirits always see people." When asked why, he is at a loss to explain. I say, "It is easier to see from the dark to the light than from the light to the dark; is this not the case?" The person who goes on errands for the underworld deeply agrees with my words.

    Small Night Demon
    A scholar from Baoyang, in the morning, entered his study to tidy up his writing materials. He saw something lying on the inkstone bed, resembling a night demon, red-haired and blue-skinned, with bare arms and wearing red pants, lying on the inkstone, sleeping soundly, his breaths still deep and slow. While the scholar was shocked, the creature had already awakened, flipped over, and leaped, transforming into a butterfly, gracefully flying out through the window.

    131

    妖宅
    　　邑宋氏，家巨富，宅多妖，饮食中常掷秽物，夜抛瓦石，毁器具，种种恶作，不足为怪。一夜月明，窗有手影，由小而大。渐渐黑影满窗，未几如豆菽而没。又一夕，有人以舌舐窗，片刻全窗尽湿，似经暴雨。忽怪风一卷，窗破灯灭，窗棂格格，透露星辰，历历可览。急以火烛之，窗纸并无破缝。
    潘茂才
    　　邑潘秀才钺，晚年贫苦无子。病笃时，邻人某怜之，扶持备至。及死，惟余缊袍，破被污秽，已不堪用。某将弃之，梦潘云：“感君高义，愧无以报，被角内有钱票一纸，聊以相赠。”醒而异之，拆视果然。
    　　醉茶子曰：生不妄费，死不轻弃，秀才之命穷，洵可哀矣！而报恩不爽，与古君子何异？天胡为阨其遇哉！
    灯异
    　　邑陈某赴都，寓通州客店。对灯坐，忽灯花砰然作响，光大如斗。中露一人面，容颜愁惨，仿佛悲啼。陈惊奔出，卧车上。天将明即行，不知何怪。

    131

    Haunted House

    The Song family of the county was extremely wealthy, but their residence was plagued by supernatural occurrences. Food was often contaminated with foul substances, and at night, tiles and stones were thrown, destroying household items. Such mischievous acts were not uncommon. One clear night, a hand shadow appeared on the window, starting small and gradually growing larger. Soon, a black shadow filled the entire window, and in a moment, it vanished like a bean or pea. Another night, someone licked the window with their tongue, leaving it completely wet as if it had been through a heavy rain. Suddenly, a strange wind swept through, breaking the window and extinguishing the light. The window lattice creaked, and the stars became visible, clearly outlined in the night sky. In a hurry, a candle was lit, but there were no visible breaks or tears in the window paper.

    Mr. Pan, a Scholar

    In the county, there was a scholar named Pan Yue, who, in his later years, was poor and childless. When he was gravely ill, a neighbor, moved by his plight, took care of him thoroughly. Upon his death, the only things left were a coarse robe and a dirty, worn blanket, which were already unusable. The neighbor was about to discard them when he dreamed that Pan said, "I am grateful for your noble virtue and ashamed to have nothing to repay you. There is a piece of money note inside the corner of the blanket; I give it to you as a token of my gratitude." Upon waking, the neighbor found the note inside the blanket, just as Pan had said.

    Drunken Tea said: "To live without wasting and to die without lightly discarding—Pan's life was indeed poor, truly pitiful! Yet, his act of gratitude was sincere and effective, not different from the ancient gentlemen. Why did heaven make his fate so difficult?"

    Strange Light

    A man from the county named Chen went to the capital and stayed at an inn in Tongzhou. Sitting facing a lamp, he suddenly heard a loud noise as the lamp flower burst. The light grew as large as a winnowing basket, revealing a human face with a sorrowful and distressed expression, as if it were crying. Chen was frightened and ran out, lying down on the carriage. As dawn approached, he left, not knowing what kind of ghost it was.

    132

    星异
    　　邑朱氏更夫毕姓，夜巡更。仰见天上星斗攒动，聚于一处，矫若游龙，光芒辉灿，片刻始散，四外仍如棋布。晓天文者当必有说，志之以俟博物君子。
    红衣女
    　　予戚韩公理堂，与友人邀游城隍祠。时廊中新塑一美人像，云鬟笼翠，艳色动人。郭视之心荡。出寺，有风旋其前，中有一红衣女子，随风飞舞，颓臾冲霄而去。自此惊怖而成颠疾。
    　　醉茶子曰：按纪文达公《槐西杂志》载，红衣女飞空中者，乃飞天夜叉。郭所见者即是。正不必谓邪心感触，土偶有灵也。
    刘廷桢
    　　邑儒童刘廷祯，夜出不返，粘壁一纸云：“因家中房屋窄小，难以用功。寻至渡口，有瓦房一所，颇甚清雅，移居于此。此后听水声淙淙，与河伯为伍，亦足消遣世虑哉。”家人寻之不得。次年冰泮，得其尸于河，即所谓渡口处也。泉路悠游，闵不畏死，是必有鬼惑之使然也。

    Explanation

    132

    Unusual Celestial Phenomena

    A night watchman from the Zhu family, named Bi, was on his patrol duty one night. He looked up and saw the stars in the sky moving and clustering together, twisting like a dragon, with dazzling light. The phenomenon lasted for a short time before dispersing, and the stars returned to their usual, orderly arrangement. Someone knowledgeable in astronomy would surely have an explanation, so the event is recorded for future scholars to study.

    The Red-Clothed Woman

    My relative, Han Gong Litang, went with friends to visit the City God Temple. At the time, a newly sculpted statue of a beautiful woman was in the hall, with her hair styled like a cloud and her appearance captivating. Guo, who saw her, was overwhelmed by her beauty. As they left the temple, a wind spun around them, and in the middle of it, a red-clad woman was dancing with the wind, suddenly flying up into the sky. Since then, Guo became mentally deranged due to the terrifying experience.

    Zui Cha Zi said: According to the Huaixi Zazhi by Ji Wenda, the red-clad woman flying in the sky was a flying night demon. What Guo saw was indeed the flying night demon. It is not necessary to attribute the event to Guo's evil thoughts or the spirits of statues.

    Liu Tingzhen

    A young scholar from the town, Liu Tingzhen, did not return home after going out at night. He left a note on the wall that read: "Because my home was too small for studying, I found a small, elegant house by the riverbank and moved in. Since then, I have listened to the sound of the river and associated with the river god, which is sufficient to escape worldly concerns." His family could not find him. The following year, when the ice melted, his body was found in the river, at the riverbank where he had moved. In the afterlife, he lived a carefree life, showing no fear of death. It must be that a ghost influenced him, leading to his death.

    133

    鼋精
    　　邑城中东北隅有池，冬夏不涸。池上一民家妇，夫久外出，病瘠而有孕。姑察其无他，怪诘之。妇忸怩云：“夜深每来一绿衣男子。辗转间，即昏不知人，任其所为而去。积今半载余矣。”姑戒其勿宣，夜起窥之。见男子自妇室出，尾其后，至门外，跃入水中。乃市香楮哭祷于水。至夜，男子来，谓妇曰：“予河伯之子也，与汝交好，终未明言，恐以异类见摈。昨汝姑祷于水，河伯知之，将杀予矣。势难再聚，故来一别。尚有一事相求，予齿长而无嗣，汝早晚临耨，请将所产者悉送诸水，感德多矣。”言毕，泪如雨，脱明珠四枚相赠，乃浩叹而去。妇白诸姑。未几，产四小鼋，姑送诸水。忽波浪翻沸，漂上一鼋，大如釜，置头背上，盖已斩矣。

    Explanation

    133

    The Turtle Spirit

    In the northeast corner of the city, there is a pond that never dries up, neither in winter nor in summer. A woman from a household near the pond was married to a man who had been away for a long time. She was sick and emaciated while pregnant. Her grandmother noticed that she was not acting suspiciously and asked her in a surprised manner. The woman, feeling embarrassed, said, "Every night, a man dressed in green clothes comes. During the process of turning over, I fall into a state of unconsciousness and do not know what happens. This has been going on for more than half a year."

    The grandmother warned her not to speak about it and watched one night. She saw the man leave the woman's room and jump into the water outside the door. The next day, the grandmother bought incense and paper money and prayed to the water. That night, the man came again and said to the woman, "I am the son of the River God. I have been with you, but I have never told you that I am a different kind of being, fearing that you might reject me. Yesterday, your grandmother prayed to the water, and the River God knows about it. He will kill me. It is difficult to meet again, so I come to say goodbye. There is still one thing I request: I am old and without descendants. Whenever you till the field, please send all the offspring to the water. Thank you very much for your kindness."

    He sighed deeply and left. The woman told her grandmother about this. Not long after, she gave birth to four small turtles. The grandmother sent them to the water. Suddenly, the waves became turbulent, and a large turtle floated up, as big as a pot. It placed its head on her back, and it seemed to have been beheaded.

    134

    醉茶子曰：鼋而淫，知淫而无不鼋者，彼苍之报施不爽也。然一经哭诉，即杀其子，老鼋之家法森严，以视纵子淫荡不忍置问者，得毋有愧老鼋乎！
    　　按：鼋不能形交而非胎生，然幻形为人，故不可以常理论也。理所必无，事或须有，姑妄言之，姑听之可耳。
    一斗泉
    　　房山山上有泉，名一斗。初，山上无水，居民患之。有道士募缘村外，谓村民曰：“今日神龙过此，盍乞甘泉？”众罗拜求术。道士曰：“来矣。”遥见一人，担水二筒，飞奔而过。道士止之，其人力拒。道士曰：“汝不见怜，一方渴死矣。”强以钵取水，置地上。其人怒目：“泄我机密。深恨老道士多口。”言毕不见，而道士亦渺。村人取水于钵，用之不竭，甘如醴。钵大于斗，故名。
    潮异邑有潮不过杨之说，事亦甚奇。每潮溢时，御河潮至杨柳青止，北河潮至杨村止，西河潮至杨汾港止，过此无潮。

    Explanation

    134

    The Drunken Tea Drinker said: "The turtle, when lustful, knows lust and there is no turtle that is not involved. The heavens' retribution is without error. Yet once they cried and complained, they immediately killed their son. The strict family law of the old turtle, compared with those who do not punish their sons for their debauchery, would they not feel ashamed of the old turtle!"

    According to: The turtle cannot have physical intercourse and is not born from a womb, but it can transform into a human form. Therefore, it cannot be judged by ordinary logic. What is logically impossible may actually occur. Let me speak it casually, and you may listen to it casually.

    One Dou Spring
    On the mountain of Fangshan, there is a spring named "one-dou." Originally, there was no water on the mountain, and the residents were troubled by it. There was a Taoist priest who collected alms outside the village and told the villagers, "Today the divine dragon is passing by, why not ask for sweet spring water?" The crowd knelt and asked for a method. The Taoist priest said, "Here he is." He saw a person from afar, carrying two buckets of water, running by. The Taoist priest stopped him, and the person resisted. The Taoist priest said, "You do not see the plight, the entire area will die of thirst." He forcibly took water in a bowl and placed it on the ground. The person glared angrily, "You have revealed my secret. I deeply hate the old Taoist priest for his many words." After speaking, he disappeared, and the Taoist priest also vanished. The villagers took water from the bowl, and it never ran out, sweet like liquor. The bowl is larger than a dou, hence the name.

    In the strange county, there is a saying that the tide does not pass beyond Yang, and the matter is also very strange. Every time the tide rises, the imperial river tide stops at Yangliuqing, the northern river tide stops at Yangcun, and the western river tide stops at Yangfen Port. Beyond this, there is no tide.

    135

    大蛤
    　　津门某家有水缸，磁质古朴，相传前明时物。灌注河水，不投矾而自清，益以为宝。有客寄宿其家，夜月未寝。忽有物大如栲栳，黑亮如漆，旋舞空际，至床边。客麾以肱，臂脆然折，即失物所在。大哗。主人破扉入，客已不起。寻其血迹，入缸后发之。缸下清水盈盈，一大蛤长三尺，阔半之。破其壳，得客半臂，方悟水自清者，蛤在缸下故也。
    城南三则
    　　邑城南三里许，名黑牛城。地本旷野，每雾天，辄有城郭，雉堞敌楼俱备，天晴即杳。或云辽萧后故址，未可考也。我津去海非遥，或即海市山市之类。
    　　有南人在津候补，术精堪舆。望城南有旺气，迁葬其先人骨。夜风雷大作，破冢出棺。又瘗又破，几为雷火所焚，惧而止焉。
    　　有邑人卜葬其处，掘土三尺，见平砖一层。旁一人止之曰：“发砖则气泄而地无用矣。”术人力主发之。砖下皆清水，有一蛇一蟹，缠绕而出。其人浩叹而去。

    Explanation

    135

    Translation of the Source Text

    The Great Clam

    In a certain household in Tianjin, there was a water jar of ancient and rustic ceramic quality, said to be from the Ming Dynasty. When river water was poured into it, it became clear without the addition of alum, and was thus considered a treasure. One night, a guest staying at the house was unable to sleep under the moonlight. Suddenly, an object as large as a basket appeared, black and shiny like lacquer, spinning and dancing in the air, approaching the bed. The guest waved his arm to fend it off, but his arm broke with a crisp sound, and immediately the object vanished. A great commotion ensued. The owner broke open the door and entered, but the guest was already unable to move. Following the blood trail, they found the guest’s arm inside the water jar. Beneath the jar, there was a pool of clear water, and a large clam, three feet long and half as wide. When the shell was broken, the guest’s half arm was found inside. Only then did they realize that the water remained clear because the clam was beneath the jar.

    Three Tales from the South of the City

    Three miles south of the city lies a place called Black Ox City. Originally a vast wilderness, on foggy days, a city would appear, complete with battlements and watchtowers, but it would disappear when the sky cleared. Some say it is the former site of Empress Xiao of Liao, but this is uncertain. Tianjin is not far from the sea, and this phenomenon may be akin to sea or mountain mirages.

    A man from the south was in Tianjin awaiting an appointment and was skilled in feng shui. He noticed auspicious energy in the south of the city and decided to relocate the remains of his ancestors. That night, a violent storm broke open the tomb and revealed the coffin. The tomb was dug up again, and it nearly caught fire in the lightning, causing him to fear and stop.

    Another local person sought to bury someone in that spot and dug three feet into the ground, uncovering a layer of flat bricks. A bystander warned him, “If you disturb the bricks, the energy will be lost, and the site will be useless.” Despite this, the feng shui expert insisted on disturbing the bricks. Beneath the bricks, there was a pool of clear water, and a snake and a crab were seen entwined and emerging. The person sighed deeply and left.

    136

    张七十
    　　武遂农夫张七十，夜闻扣门声甚急。出视，有二役立门外，见张出，曳衣急窜，奔波不知近远。至一处，仿佛兰若，入则差役纷纭，又类衙署。惊疑间，忽遇邻僧。僧死已久，见张，惊谓二役曰：“此不在数，何便勾来？”二人亦悔，弃张各去。张挽僧求救，僧云：“可随我来。”引至一院，曲榭回廊，栋梁烟黑，弯环甚远。又入一门，见骸骨狼藉，尸积如山，院中连列十余巨釜，众健夫折骸以爨，碎割尸骸投沸鼎中，烈焰飞红，腥风殆不可忍。张不觉失声。僧云：“毋畏，不杀尔。”向众喃喃，不知何词。即有数人迭尸墙下，高与垣齐。僧挽张踏尸而上，至墙头，僧力推之。张踬墙外，惊定审视，乃饶阳城下。徘徊间，家人寻至，盖失去五日矣。此事殊令人莫解。果为阴曹，何以勾生人？谓为阳世，何以有鬼役？究不知是何幻境。

    Explanation

    136

    Zhang Seventy
    In Wusu, there was a farmer named Zhang Seventy. One night, he heard the sound of someone knocking on the door very urgently. He went out to see, and saw two figures standing outside the door. When Zhang Seventy appeared, they dragged their clothes and fled urgently, running without knowing how far they went. He arrived at a place that resembled a Buddhist monastery, but upon entering, he found it was an official government office. In his surprise, he suddenly met a neighboring monk. The monk had been dead for a long time, but when he saw Zhang Seventy, he was surprised and said to the two figures, "He is not on the list, why have you summoned him?" The two figures also regretted their actions and left separately. Zhang Seventy tried to hold onto the monk and ask for help, and the monk said, "Follow me." He led Zhang Seventy to a courtyard with winding corridors and blackened beams, and the surroundings were very far-reaching. They entered another gate, where they saw bones scattered in disarray and corpses piled up like mountains. In the courtyard, there were over ten large cauldrons, and many strong men were breaking bones to cook, cutting and throwing the corpses into boiling cauldrons. Flames shot red, and the stench of the wind was almost unbearable. Zhang Seventy couldn't help but gasp in shock. The monk said, "Do not be afraid, they will not kill you." He spoke to the crowd in a low voice, but Zhang Seventy didn't know what he was saying. Suddenly, several people piled up corpses against the wall, as high as the wall itself. The monk pulled Zhang Seventy and stepped on the corpses to climb up to the wall. The monk pushed him, and Zhang Seventy fell outside the wall. After calming down, he looked around and realized he was at the foot of RaoYang City. While hesitating, his family found him. It turned out he had been missing for five days. This is indeed strange and hard to understand. If it is the underworld, why were living people summoned? If it is the world of the living, why were there ghost officials? In the end, it is still unclear what kind of dream or illusion this was.

    137

    白衣妇
    　　杨青驿舟人，夜泊河干。有少妇呼渡，颜貌甚丽，凶服练裙。诘其何往，云：“自母家归。”问家何处，不答。既渡，酬以钱十枝。舟子怪而尾之，迤逶数里，至一村寺，推扉遽入。舟子邀村人共搜之，见妇面墙立。近迫之，乃白杨棺板也，火之。次日薄暮，妇至河干，责舟子曰：“吾丧神也，阎君命我有要差，干尔甚事？且我渡河酬以钱，更不负尔。何故毁我所凭，致我误公受责？此怨誓必相报！”舟子大号，邻舟咸至。妇展白袖风旋而去，至夜，舟子溺死。

    Explanation

    137

    The White-Clothed Woman
    At Yangqing Post Station, a boatman moored his boat at night by the riverbank. A young woman called out for a ride, her appearance very beautiful, dressed in mourning attire and a white mourning dress. He asked where she was going, and she replied, "I am returning from my mother's home." When he asked where her home was, she did not answer. After she crossed the river, she paid him ten pieces of money. The boatman found her strange and followed her, traveling several miles until they reached a village temple. She pushed open the door and rushed inside. The boatman invited the villagers to search for her and found a woman facing the wall. When they approached her, they discovered it was a white poplar coffin board, which they burned. The next evening, as dusk fell, the woman returned to the riverbank and scolded the boatman, saying, "I am a lost soul, commanded by the King of Yama to carry out an important task. What business is it of yours? I paid you to cross the river and owe you nothing more. Why did you destroy my support, causing me to make a mistake and be blamed? This grudge I swear I will take revenge!" The boatman cried out loudly, and neighboring boats all came. The woman spread her white sleeves and left with a swirling wind. By night, the boatman drowned.

    138

    醉茶子曰：少妇夜渡，穷诘颠末而尾之，其心之叵测可知，死于非命，不亦宜哉！邪僻者盍鉴诸此。
    殃神
    　　张某好私窥人室。夜至一家，茅屋逼仄，环以秫篱。闻窗中细语哝哝，似兴云雨。舐窗窥之，见床前停一尸，覆以纸衾，旁一白衣妇掩面悲泣。灯光暗而忽明，几上坐一大鬼，高几齐屋，黑面红须，状甚丑恶。左臂架苍鹰，双翼鼓动。张惊毙。及苏，天已明，狼狈而返。
    小猕猴
    　　束鹿田叟，薄暮立枣林下纳凉。一物堕肩，视之，小猕猴也。如蛛长寸许，茸毛赭色。拂之落地上，缘木而升。目送之，直至林巅。复有两三头，若相接引。急唤人至，共扑之，不可得矣。或云：“狐所幻也。”渔洋《池北偶谈》载：东粤顺德县有赤虾子，状如婴儿，极小，坐于树杪。又有猪都、人都、鸟都，亦小儿女生树上者，此或其类与？

    Explanation

    138

    醉茶子曰：少妇夜渡，穷诘颠末而尾之，其心之叵测可知，死于非命，不亦宜哉！邪僻者盍鉴诸此。
    The Drunken Tea Master said: A young woman crossed at night, thoroughly interrogated the beginning and end and followed someone; her intentions were difficult to fathom, and her death by unnatural means, was not it fitting? Those with evil intentions, why not take this as a warning!

    殃神
    　　张某好私窥人室。夜至一家，茅屋逼仄，环以秫篱。闻窗中细语哝哝，似兴云雨。舐窗窥之，见床前停一尸，覆以纸衾，旁一白衣妇掩面悲泣。灯光暗而忽明，几上坐一大鬼，高几齐屋，黑面红须，状甚丑恶。左臂架苍鹰，双翼鼓动。张惊毙。及苏，天已明，狼狈而返。
    Disaster Spirit
    　　Zhang had a habit of secretly peering into people's homes. One night, he arrived at a house, noting its small size and surrounding fence made of sorghum. He heard soft, intimate sounds from inside the window, suggesting sexual activity. He licked the window to peer inside, seeing a corpse on the bed covered with a paper shroud, with a white-clad woman covering her face and weeping. The light flickered, revealing a large ghost sitting on a table, as tall as the house, with a dark face and red beard, looking very ugly and evil. The ghost held an eagle on his left arm, with the eagle's wings flapping. Zhang was so shocked that he died. When he came to consciousness, it was already morning, and he returned in a disheveled state.

    小猕猴
    　　束鹿田叟，薄暮立枣林下纳凉。一物堕肩，视之，小猕猴也。如蛛长寸许，茸毛赭色。拂之落地上，缘木而升。目送之，直至林巅。复有两三头，若相接引。急唤人至，共扑之，不可得矣。或云：“狐所幻也。”渔洋《池北偶谈》载：东粤顺德县有赤虾子，状如婴儿，极小，坐于树杪。又有猪都、人都、鸟都，亦小儿女生树上者，此或其类与？
    Small Monkey
    　　An elderly man from Shu Lu stood under a jujube tree in the late afternoon to cool off. Something fell onto his shoulder, which he saw was a small monkey. The monkey was about an inch long, with soft, reddish-brown fur. When he brushed it off, it fell to the ground and climbed the tree. He watched the monkey climb until it reached the top of the forest. Then he saw two or three more monkeys, as if they were guiding each other. He quickly called others to help catch the monkeys, but they could not be caught. Someone said, "These might be illusions created by fox spirits." In Yu Yang's Pool North Casual Conversations, it is recorded: In Dong Yue Shunde County, there are red shrimp-like creatures that look like infants, very small, sitting on tree branches. There are also pig people, human people, and bird people, also small children sitting on trees, suggesting they might be of the same kind.

    139

    泥女
    　　庆都有古寺，多年剥落，亦无僧住持。后殿站像有侍婢，衣裳鲜洁，容光鉴人。有狂生姚某，见而情动，戏题一诗于其襟云：“巫峡巫山几万重，不知神女在何峰。阳台料得难相遇，从此思萦五夜钟。”归斋冥想，梦寝颇劳。忽闻院中环佩琤然，隔窗视之，一女子步月而来，叩扉请入，即庙中之侍婢也。士狂喜，纳入求欢。女曰：“如此狂暴，妾即去。”生释手。女含笑坐生膝，回顾流盼，芳香袭衣。生神荡情摇，莫知所措。俄觉双股重压，痛不可忍。细视非女，乃庙中石屃赑也。转侧不能，腿骨欲折。急呼人至，觅数健夫始舁下，双膝废矣。轻薄之行出于士子，岂习气然与？而以淫词亵渎神，宜其获显报也。舞笔弄墨者戒之。

    Explanation

    139

    A Tale of the Clay Maiden

    In Qingzhou, there was an ancient temple that had been neglected for many years, with its walls and statues deteriorating, and no monks in charge. In the rear hall of the temple stood a statue of a maiden, her clothes fresh and clean, her radiant beauty reflecting a person's face. A reckless young man named Yao, upon seeing her, was moved by her beauty and playfully wrote a poem on her garment: "The Wuxia and Wushan mountains are thousands of layers deep; I wonder on which peak the goddess of love resides. I suppose it will be hard to meet her on the terrace; from now on, my thoughts will be entangled with the five-night bell." Returning to his study, he pondered the poem in his mind, and his dreams were troubled. Suddenly, he heard the sound of jade pendants ringing clearly in the courtyard. Looking through the window, he saw a woman walking under the moonlight, knocking on the door and asking to enter. It was the temple's maiden. The young man was ecstatic and took her in to seek pleasure. The woman said, "If you are so rough, I will leave." The young man let go of her. The woman smiled and sat on his knees, looking back and glancing around, her fragrance permeating his clothes. The young man's spirit was agitated and his emotions were swayed, and he did not know what to do. Suddenly, he felt a heavy pressure on both his legs, the pain unbearable. Upon closer inspection, he realized that it was not a woman, but the stone tortoise in the temple. He could not turn or move, and his leg bones were about to break. He quickly called for someone to come, and found several strong men to lift him down, but both his knees were broken. The frivolous behavior of scholars, is it due to habit or custom? And to use lewd words that defile the gods, it is appropriate that he receives a clear punishment. Those who play with words and ink should be warned.

    140

    小老虎
    　　邑有农夫，见田间乌与蛇斗。掘其下，得元宝二枚。怀归，自以为暴富，杜门不出。为佣家人促之，辄云：“有此壮胆物，何屑为人役！此后须人求我，我更何求于人？”呼银为小老虎，闲时把玩，或抛向空中，以为戏。一日误落头上，患风身毙。医药棺殓，洽敷其用。

    Explanation

    140

    In a small town, there was a farmer who saw a crow fighting with a snake in the fields. He dug below the spot where they were fighting and found two silver ingots. Carrying them home, he thought himself suddenly wealthy and decided to stay at home and avoid social interaction. His family members urged him to work as a hired hand, but he said, "With this bold companion, why would I stoop to being someone's servant? From now on, people will need me; what do I need from others?" He called the silver "little tigers" and played with them during his leisure time, sometimes throwing them into the air, considering it as play. One day, he accidentally threw one onto his head, and he suffered from wind illness and died. Medical treatment, coffin, and burial were exactly sufficient for the task.

    141

    醉茶子曰：垂死而得金，何幸也。然无金不死，岂农无纳财之福与？果使不贪为宝，又何致死于金哉！呜呼，农谓金为虎，吾谓猛于虎也。彼虎视眈眈者，可不慎耶？
    瓜异
    　　房山张姓有瓜园，遣佣某独守。适有布客经其地求饮，佣与之水。窥其货物，利之，乘其不意，突以铁锸斫其脑，立毙，瘗尸畦下。人不知也。及瓜熟时，畦中苗蔓尽枯，独一畦枝柯茂盛，结一瓜大倍于常。园主奇之，献诸驿官。官喜，剖食，既破，并无瓠瓤，腥血流溢。怪而招园主询之，主莫解其故。于是同官往验，见残柯断蔓犹存，使人掘畦下，得尸，根自口中出。严讯佣，备言其实，乃详县而置诸法。

    Explanation

    141

    The Drunken Tea Master said: "On the verge of death and obtaining gold, how fortunate! Yet, without the gold, one would not have died. Is it not that the farmer lacks the blessing of receiving wealth? If greedlessness were a treasure, how could one die because of gold? Alas, the farmer considers gold as a tiger, I say it is more dangerous than a tiger. Those who look at it with greedy eyes, should one not be cautious?"

    Strange Melons

    Mr. Zhang of Fangshan had a melon garden and sent a hired man to guard it alone. Just as a cloth merchant passed through and asked for water, the hired man gave him water. The merchant saw his goods and coveted them, taking advantage of his lack of expectation, suddenly struck him on the head with an iron hoe. He died immediately, and the body was buried in the field. No one knew about it. When the melons matured, all the vines in the field withered, except for one row of branches and vines that was lush, and a melon grew much larger than usual. The garden owner found it strange and presented it to the station official. The official was pleased and cut it open to eat. After cutting it open, there was no melon flesh, only blood and entrails flowed out. He was surprised and called the garden owner to ask about it. The owner could not understand the cause. Then the officials went together to inspect, they saw the broken branches and vines still present, and had someone dig under the field and found the body, with roots coming out of the mouth. They severely interrogated the hired man, who fully confessed the truth, and then they detailed the case to the county and dealt with him according to the law.

    142

    醉茶子曰：瓜其冤塞之气乎？何其报之奇也。佣以为人无知者，必朽于地下耳，岂料有瓜哉！《传》曰：“无使滋蔓，蔓难图也。”其此之谓乎？
    头飞
    　　襄阳刘雨汀言：昔在豫省，假馆某氏花园。暑夜乘凉，坐庭中，对月啜茗。忽一物堕几上，视之，新割头颅也。骇而呼仆，未至，又从空飞坠数级，势如急雹，左右上下触人。急避入屋，闭扉。物碰墙窗，砰砰作响，一夜不休。次早视窗上有血迹，而头则乌有。刘自知不祥。未几，襄阳盗起，阖家遇难。刘飘泊一身，庚申秋，自津之保阳，遂不知所往。
    　　醉茶子曰：头从何来，不过狐鬼之幻术耳。乘人危难，而鬼来揶揄。异类不仁，与小人之乐灾幸祸者何殊？良可慨矣。

    Explanation

    142

    The tea-drunk one said: Is this the head, filled with the aura of injustice and oppression? How strange and unusual is its appearance! I thought people were ignorant, and it must rot in the ground, who would have thought there would be a head like a melon! It is said in The Classic of History: "Do not let it spread; once it spreads, it is hard to control." Is this what it means?

    Heads flying.

    Liu Yuting from Xiangyang said: Once, while staying in Henan, I lodged temporarily in a garden belonging to a certain family. On a hot summer night, I sat in the courtyard, sipping tea while facing the moon. Suddenly, something fell onto the table, and upon looking, I saw a freshly cut head. Startled, I called out to the servant, but before they arrived, another head flew down from the sky, falling several levels, its descent as swift as hail, striking people from all directions. In a panic, I rushed inside and closed the door. The object hit the walls and windows, making a loud banging sound, and the noise continued throughout the night. The next morning, I saw bloodstains on the window, but the head was nowhere to be found. Liu knew this was an omen. Soon after, bandits rose up in Xiangyang, and his entire family perished. Liu wandered alone, in the autumn of the year of the Earth Monkey, he traveled from Tianjin to Baoyang, and then disappeared without a trace.

    The tea-drunk one said: Where did the heads come from? They are nothing but the trickery of foxes and ghosts. Taking advantage of someone's misfortune, the ghosts come to mock. Beings of different species are unkind, how different are they from those who take pleasure in others' misfortunes and disasters? Truly, it is a cause for regret.

    143

    袁某
    　　袁某，素暗酒，多饮不醉。偶觉腹中不爽，呕出一物，圆如鸡蛋，色黄而坚，堕地旋转不休。俄又吐如前状。医莫识其症，调养数日而愈。但不能如前善饮耳。其友云：“君何不再吐一枚？”问何说，曰：“俗所谓三打金弹。”
    　　醉茶子曰：牛鸣不已，其胸有黄；犬吠不休，其腹有宝。人之嗜饮，致凝结成团，得毋曲蘖之精与？抑亦牛黄狗宝之类也？
    高烈妇
    　　女子生须，见段成式《诺皋记》，谓是文人戏言耳。邑有杨氏女，适高氏子，夫妻相得。年余夫死，女矢志守节。母与姑佥劝改嫁，女不肯。有武弁闻女美，以百金饵其姑。女知其谋，闭户自经，奔救，不果。弁索女急，不得女，逼还原金，姑使媒缓颊。弁云：“得博一夕欢，即不索偿。”殆将污而后夺之。姑引弁至，女寻自尽，苦为众守，急而自挝。觉两颊如火，颓臾连腮带鬓，鬖鬖然丛生长须，宛如伧叟。弁至，一笑而罢，乃逼原金。姑称贷盈其数。弁得金，事遂止。

    Explanation

    143

    Final Translation

    袁某

    袁某, usually not sensitive to alcohol, could drink a lot without getting drunk. Suddenly, he felt discomfort in his stomach and vomited out something round like an egg, yellow and hard, which fell to the ground and kept spinning. Soon, he vomited again in the same manner. The doctor could not diagnose the illness, and after resting for several days, he recovered. However, he could no longer drink as well as before. His friend asked, "Why don't you vomit another one?" When asked what he meant, the friend replied, "What people commonly call 'three golden bullets.'"

    醉茶子 said: When a cow keeps mooing, there is something yellow in its chest; when a dog keeps barking, there is something valuable in its belly. A person's love for drinking causes it to coagulate into a lump. Is it not the essence of fermentation? Or perhaps it is something like cow bile and dog treasure?

    高烈妇

    A woman growing a beard, as recorded in Duan Chengshi's Nuogao Ji, is often considered a literary joke. In a certain county, there was a woman from the Yang family who married a man from the Gao family. The couple got along well. After a year, her husband died, and she vowed to remain chaste. Her mother and mother-in-law both advised her to remarry, but she refused. A military officer heard that the woman was beautiful and offered her mother-in-law a hundred gold coins. The woman knew his plan and closed the door to hang herself. People rushed to save her, but it was too late. The officer urgently sought the woman, could not find her, and forced her mother-in-law to return the money. Her mother-in-law sent a matchmaker to plead. The officer said, "To get a night of pleasure, I will not demand repayment." He would defile her before taking her. Her mother-in-law led the officer to her, and the woman soon hanged herself. She struggled to be protected by the crowd, but in desperation, she struck herself. Her cheeks felt as hot as fire, and soon her cheeks and temples were covered with a bushy beard, resembling a rough old man. The officer arrived, smiled, and then demanded the return of the money. Her mother-in-law borrowed enough to repay him. The officer got the money, and the matter ended.

    144

    醉茶子曰：友人戏曰：“设弁亦云花无蕊不妍，女无须不美，将何以堪？”予曰：“不然。弁所悦者在色，女所贞者在心。即使无须，岂能强夺？而仓卒之际，计无所出，遂使天公之巧，得以保其坚贞，则巾帼与须眉并列矣。”
    小毛人
    　　深州民人拆房，下得一方坎，四围宽尺许，深亦如之，中有二小毛人对卧。赭色红目，长不盈尺。捉其一，其一惊遁。或云旱魃之类也。
    蛤珠
    　　西淀渔人，每夜渔，辄见水上有光荧然，及晓始灭，如是有年。一夕，于放光处网得一大蛤，持归其家，旋入市。及还，其妇已煮食，壳中得一大珠如指顶。渔人取珠视之，色黄暗，深怨其妇，妇亦悔。方诟谇间，有二人款门入，谓曰：“珠已熟，无用矣。如愿售，请赠十千。”渔人诺之。二人得珠，欢跃而去。渔人悔，追之已渺。或云仙也。

    Explanation

    144

    The Tea Drinker said: A friend joked, "Even a hat might say that a flower without its stamen is not beautiful, and a woman without a beard is not pretty. How can one bear it?" I replied, "Not so. The hat (man) is pleased with appearance, while the woman is true to heart. Even if there is no beard, how can it be forcibly taken? In a sudden moment, with no plan, it allows the skill of heaven to preserve its firmness and purity, then the women and men will stand side by side."

    Small Furred Creatures
    During the demolition of a house in Shenzhou, people found a small pit, about a foot wide and deep, containing two small furred creatures lying face to face. They had reddish-brown fur and red eyes, and were no more than a foot long. Catching one, the other fled in fright. Some said they were like the drought demon.

    Pearl from a Clam
    Fishermen from the West Dike often saw a faint light on the water at night, which disappeared at dawn. This phenomenon occurred for many years. One evening, they caught a large clam in the spot where the light appeared. They took it home, then sold it in the market. When they returned, their wife had already cooked it, and they found a large pearl the size of a fingertip in the shell. The fisherman examined the pearl, which was yellow and dull, and deeply resented his wife. His wife also regretted her actions. In the midst of their scolding, two people knocked on the door and entered, saying, "The pearl is already mature, it is useless. If you wish to sell it, please give us ten thousand." The fisherman agreed. The two people took the pearl and left joyfully. The fisherman regretted his decision but, when he tried to chase them, they were already gone. Some said they were immortals.

    145

    醉茶子曰：按珠无光，以乳汁煮之，则光复明。是珠应无熟一说。果使熟真无用，又何以十千购之哉？渔人惑矣。
    鬼诗
    　　杨精易，直隶人。往山左访友，未遇，寓居破寺，资斧告竭，夜愁不寐。闻窗外咿晤，似人吟咏。穴窗窥之，草际似有人影，知为鬼。晓询诸僧，僧云：“予闻之熟矣，奚足怪？如再闻时，大声一喝即止。”越数夕又闻之，字略可辩。杨固好诗，心甚向慕，开扉四顾，杳无声迹，乃祝曰：“羁旅之人，独居岑寂。君虽物故，何拘幽明？况风雅如此，自非凡才。倘蒙不弃，请至敝斋一谈胸臆，亦足以破我寂寥。”言毕，酬以酒。

    Explanation

    145

    The Tea-Drinking Scholar said: Pearls lose their luster when not treated properly; if they are boiled in milk, their luster returns. There should be no such thing as "cooked pearls." If they were truly cooked, they would be useless. Why, then, would one buy them for ten thousand coins? The fisherman is confused.

    Ghost Poem
    Yang Jingyi, a man from Zhili, went to Shandong to visit a friend but did not meet him. He lived in a dilapidated temple and ran out of money. At night, he was sorrowful and unable to sleep. He heard a muffled sound outside the window, like someone reciting poetry. Peering through a hole in the window, he saw what seemed to be a human figure among the grass and realized it was a ghost. In the morning, he asked the monks, who said, "I have heard of this many times; what is there to be surprised about? If you hear it again, shout loudly, and it will stop." After several nights, he heard it again, and the words could be vaguely distinguished. Yang was fond of poetry and was very much in awe. He opened the door and looked around, but no sound or trace was found. He then prayed and said, "A traveler away from home, living alone in solitude. You have passed away; why should we be bound by the distinction between the living and the dead? Considering your refined taste, you are certainly no ordinary talent. If you do not reject me, please come to my humble abode to discuss our thoughts and feelings; it would also be enough to break my loneliness." After saying this, he offered the ghost wine.

    146

    忽墙阴清风徐来，仿佛款接。归斋就枕，梦一人儒雅非常，长揖而坐，曰：“辱蒙垂盼，感慕实深。欲与君子定交，奈阴阳睽隔，有俚词一首，请君指正。仆骆姓，前游县幕，没厝于此。明日五更，在敝柩畔恭呈一览，日出则不可见矣。”杨惊寤起，挑灯住观，见墙隅一败棺，上书句云：“寂寞辩宫暮复朝，清明寒食倍无聊。空斋有佛垂头顾，浊酒何人常泪浇。半夜冷云埋塔顶，五更残月挂墙坳。家乡万里归难得，肠断江南廿四桥。”字迹如淡墨，天明即不复见。

    Explanation

    146

    A Sudden Breeze from the Wall's Shadow

    Suddenly, a gentle breeze came from the shadow of the wall, as if greeting me. Returning to my study and lying down to rest, I dreamed of a person of extraordinary refinement and elegance who bowed and sat down, saying, "I am honored by your favor, and I feel deep admiration and respect. I wish to establish a friendship with you, but the boundary between the living and the dead separates us. I have a simple poem for you to review. I am surnamed Luo, formerly serving as a county official, and I was buried here. Tomorrow at dawn, I will humbly present it for your review beside my humble coffin. Once the sun rises, I will no longer be visible." I woke up in shock, lit a lamp, and stayed to observe, seeing a dilapidated coffin in the corner of the wall. The inscription on it read:

    Lonely palace at dusk and dawn,
    On Qingming and Hanshi, the feeling of boredom is doubled.
    In the empty study, a Buddha statue hangs its head in contemplation,
    Whoever drinks this turbid wine often drowns their tears in it.
    At midnight, cold clouds cover the top of the pagoda,
    At the fifth watch, a waning moon hangs in the wall's depression.
    Returning home from a distance of ten thousand miles is difficult,
    My heart breaks at the twenty-four bridges of Jiangnan.

    The writing was as faint as light ink, and it disappeared at dawn.

    147

    余某
    　　常熟余某，为直隶县尹，卑鄙贪污。与同僚谒圣庙，方鞠躬行礼，见数步外有一钱，急拾之纳耳中。后改教职，尝与市人争数钱，致挥拳破额。妻田氏颇有风致，为狐蛊媚，余常受其虐，将往乞天师符箓。狐知之，谓妇曰：“尔夫见钱则喜，请每日赠以酒资，劝其勿恶作也。”明日，于席下得钱三百，余大喜，退避他舍，任狐所为，从不置问矣。年余，狐欲行，余知之，谓妻曰：“狐，卿之面首，我之心腹也。卿可揽其祛而留之，但云我愿常醉不愿醒也。”妻告狐，狐曰：“予固非惜小费。且与卿情亦难舍，奈西山道侣相招，不容再放荡也。为尔夫薄留微资，以报年余相让之情。”遂出门，不复返。次日，于故处得绿布二尺许，包银一裹，外粘红笺，书二字，云“臊敬”。

    Explanation

    147

    Mr. Yu from Changshu, who served as the magistrate of a county in Zhili, was lowly, base, and corrupt. During a visit to the Confucian temple with his colleagues, as he was bowing to perform the ritual, he saw a coin a few steps away and quickly picked it up, putting it in his ear. Later, he was transferred to a teaching position, where he once fought with a market person over a few coins, resulting in a broken forehead. His wife, Tian, had a certain charm, but she was bewitched by a fox spirit, and Mr. Yu often suffered from her abuse. He planned to seek a talisman from a Taoist priest. The fox knew about it and told the wife, "Your husband loves money; please give him daily drink money to encourage him to be less harsh." The next day, Mr. Yu found three hundred coins under the table and was very happy. He retreated to another room, letting the fox do as she pleased, and never questioned it again. After a year, the fox wanted to leave. Mr. Yu knew this and told his wife, "The fox is your paramour, and I am your confidant. You can hold her sleeve and keep her, but just say I want to stay drunk and not wake up." The wife told the fox, who replied, "I do not really care about small expenses. Also, I cannot bear to part with you, but the monks on Xishan Mountain have called me, and I cannot stay here anymore. For your husband, I will leave a small amount of money as a token of our long-standing friendship." She then left and never returned. The next day, Mr. Yu found a piece of green cloth about two feet long, wrapped around a bundle of silver, with a red note attached, written with two characters, "臊敬" (a vulgar term).

    148

    王鹾贾
    　　鹾贾王某，除夕祀神，所供猪首，忽目动喙张，大啖肴馔，旋下几逐人。众惊哗叫，乱如鼎沸。物跳跃出门，不知所往。家自此零落。
    　　醉茶子曰：衰气所感，物或凭焉。不然，蠢然猪首，安能为厉？闻王某悭吝已极，供客从无酒肴。猪首大啖，亦快人心之一事。
    匏异
    　　顺邑李生闲游野寺，见篱上悬一匏，肥白可爱，摘而怀之。途中小解缓裳，匏堕于地，裂一隙，有物突出如鸡。破卵视之，小和尚也，帽脱露顶，神色张皇，转瞬高如常人，惨然曰：“君勿惊悸，予孽僧也。募化财物，悉供淫赌。寺有木佛，予摧为薪。神怒鞭背，疽发身死。闭魂幽穴，土人掘地出之。飘然一身，恐神究责，匿匏中。不图为君所摘，神责将至矣，奈何？”言毕，长叹而没。

    Explanation

    148

    A Salt Merchant

    A salt merchant named Wang, on New Year's Eve, was worshipping the gods. The pig's head he had offered suddenly moved its eyes and opened its mouth, eating heartily from the dishes before it, and then quickly jumped down to chase people. The crowd was shocked and cried out in confusion, the scene was chaotic like a boiling pot. The pig's head leaped out the door, and no one knew where it went. From that day on, the family's fortunes declined.

    The drunken tea drinker said: "It must be that the spirit of decline has affected the object, allowing a spirit to attach itself to it. Otherwise, a stupid-looking pig's head, how could it cause harm? I heard that Mr. Wang was extremely stingy, never providing alcohol or food for guests. The pig's head eating heartily was also a delightful thing for people."

    A Gourd Anomaly

    A man named Li from Shun County was leisurely wandering through a wild temple. He saw a gourd hanging on a fence, plump and white, looking very appealing. He picked it up and put it in his bag. While taking a break to relieve himself, the gourd fell to the ground and split open, revealing something that looked like a chicken. He broke open the gourd to see a small monk inside, who had lost his hat and was exposed, looking terrified. In an instant, the monk grew to the size of a normal person and said sorrowfully, "Do not be startled, I am a wicked monk. I begged for money and goods, all of which I spent on gambling and debauchery. There was a wooden Buddha in the temple, and I broke it down for firewood. The god was angry and whipped me on the back, causing an abscess that led to my death. My soul was confined in a dark, hidden place, and the local people dug me up from the ground. Now I am alone and free, fearing that the god will hold me accountable, so I hid in the gourd. I did not expect you to pick it up, and now the god's punishment is coming. What can I do?" After saying this, he sighed deeply and disappeared.

    149

    任住
    　　山左某县署后有隘巷，宽仅容身，中多怪异，日暮即断人行。有任住者，谓县隶某曰：“君素豪强，敢夜入此巷，我愿备东道。”隶诺之。住邀数人，俟于巷外。隶思吓众，乃披发涂面，着白袍以往。至巷内，见对面来一人，状与己同。隶疑任住所为，不之恐。及近，萁人递一物与隶。隶接而纳诸怀，相与侧身而过，卒无一语。至巷口，众见隶，大惊。隶曰：“胆壮者固如是乎？”众闻声，始知其伪，遂相戏笑而归。酒筵已设，隶询众曰：“适装鬼而与我遇者谁耶？”众言其无。隶亦愕然，出怀中物视之，乃城隍勾牒一纸，差隶勾致任住者也。众失色，而任住倒地毙矣。隶无恙。

    Explanation

    149

    In the eastern part of a certain county, there was a narrow alley behind the county office, so narrow that it could barely accommodate a person, and it was filled with many strange and unusual occurrences. At dusk, it would stop people from passing through. There was a man named Ren Zhu who said to a certain county clerk, "You are usually bold and powerful; dare to enter this alley at night, and I will prepare the way for you." The clerk agreed. Ren Zhu invited several people and waited outside the alley. The clerk thought to scare the others, so he shaved his head, smeared his face, and wore a white robe before entering. Inside the alley, he saw someone approaching from the opposite direction, who looked just like him. The clerk suspected it was Ren Zhu and did not fear him. As they got closer, the other person handed something to the clerk. The clerk took it and put it in his bosom, and they passed each other sideways, finally without a word. When they reached the mouth of the alley, the others saw the clerk and were greatly surprised. The clerk said, "Is it like this for the brave?" Upon hearing his voice, the others realized it was a trick and began to mock and laugh as they returned. A wine banquet was already set up, and the clerk asked the others, "Who was the one dressed as a ghost and met me?" The others said there was none. The clerk was also startled and took out the object from his bosom to look at it, which turned out to be a summons from the city god, ordering the clerk to arrest Ren Zhu. The others were shocked, and Ren Zhu fell to the ground and died. The clerk was unharmed.

    150

    醉茶子曰：予尝见公门差隶，狼贪虎视，无不令人望而生畏。天下如是，即知古今如是，岂其习气使然与？抑狐假虎威，非若此，即公差不能办，私橐不能充与？乃冥差遣阳隶，可见为隶者多半非人。
    人面瓜
    　　保阳老农，种瓜为业，有甜瓜数亩，手自灌溉。瓜熟摘视，形如人面，口眼耳鼻备具，其容多惨而少舒。自以为不祥，弃诸水。是年，其一子一侄客山东，为盗所杀。

    Explanation

    150

    Master Tea Drinker said: I have often seen officials' hired assistants, who are greedy and menacing, like wolves and tigers, and their presence always inspires fear. If this is true of the world today, it must also have been true in ancient times. Is this due to their habitual behavior? Or is it that they use the power of the officials to bully others, and without such power, they cannot accomplish their tasks or satisfy their greed? It seems that these "officials" are not truly human but rather supernatural or demonic.

    A Melon with a Human Face

    An elderly farmer from Baoyang, whose profession is growing sweet melons, has several acres of melons that he waters himself. When the melons ripened, he picked one to inspect and found it had a shape resembling a human face, with a mouth, eyes, nose, and ears all present. Its expression was mostly grim and rarely relaxed. Believing it to be an omen, he threw it into the water. That year, one of his son and one of his nephew, who were guests in Shandong, were killed by bandits.

    151

    醉茶子曰：昔有人自南来，忘其何县，携有人面豆数十枚，视同奇珍。每出以示人，共相惊异。闻产豆之处，亦遭兵燹。大抵反常者为妖，此类是也，奚足为贵？
    僧蛊
    　　予舅氏郭公苇堂，客豫省。偶掘土，得一肉块，状如人首，有双耳而无口鼻。识者曰：“此太岁也，宜祭而瘗之，否则祸至。”公不听，弃之。后自豫归，途遇一僧，谓曰：“君神色青黯，腹中已有蛔虫，病发则吐泻交作，不治则死。”公曰：“可为奈何？”曰：“是不难，贫衲颇有小术，能以十金相酬，略为砭针，可保无恙。”公叱其妄。僧勃然曰：“病发休悔，勿视财如性命也。”悻悻而去。晚，公投逆旅，便觉不快。夜半，吐泻大作，出虫无算，状如小蛇。大骇，急遣人寻僧，不可得矣，遂亡。或云僧之蛊术也，贿之则免。

    Explanation

    151

    Zui Chazǐ said: Once upon a time, there was a person who came from the south, but I forgot which county he was from. He carried with him dozens of beans that resembled human faces, treating them as precious treasures. Whenever he showed them to others, they were all amazed. It is said that the place where these beans were produced also suffered from war and destruction. Generally speaking, anything abnormal is considered strange or supernatural, and this is an example of such a thing. What is there to be valued in it?

    Monk's Poison

    My maternal uncle, Guo Gong Weitang, was traveling in the province of Yu. One day, he dug into the ground and found a piece of meat shaped like a human head, with two ears but no mouth or nose. A knowledgeable person said, "This is the Tai Sui. It should be worshipped and buried, otherwise disaster will come." My uncle did not listen and discarded it. Later, when he returned from the province of Yu, he met a monk on the road. The monk said to him, "Your complexion is pale and dark, and there are already worms in your stomach. When the illness strikes, vomiting and diarrhea will occur simultaneously, and if not treated, you will die." My uncle said, "What can I do?" The monk replied, "It is not difficult. I have a small skill and can accept ten gold pieces as payment. I will give you a slight acupuncture treatment, and I can guarantee you will be fine." My uncle dismissed his words as nonsense. The monk angrily said, "Do not regret it when the illness strikes. Do not treat money as if it were your life." He left angrily. Later that evening, my uncle checked into an inn and immediately felt unwell. At midnight, vomiting and diarrhea became severe, and countless worms came out, shaped like small snakes. He was greatly frightened and quickly sent someone to find the monk, but he could not be found. My uncle died. Some say it was the monk's poison, and if you paid him, you would be saved.

    152

    金鱼
    　　邑张木匠父死，瘗诸义冢。后家暴富，乃购新茔，迁父开圹。柩前一小池，水清见底，有金鱼三尾，游泳其中。张悔之无及，乃以碗盛鱼，将瘗诸新阡。半路鱼俱死，木匠家亦贫如昔矣。
    　　醉茶子曰：风鉴之说，信有之与？既富，委亲于义地，为孝子者当不如是。而因迁葬遂贫，岂可不尽孝乎？不知非也。人惟行乎心之所安而已。彼富贵听天可也，地何能为哉！
    天门
    　　予曾祖母杨太夫人，夜坐窗前玩月。见天际忽裂一罅，俄而洞启如门，二甲士立门左右，门中霞光万道，人影憧憧，顷刻复合，盖天门也。
    黄叶村
    　　西沽旧名黄叶村，老人犹有知者，近日莫传也。道光年间有乩仙诗云：“僧归黄叶村中寺，人唤斜阳渡口船。”自注云：“黄叶村即西沽。”按：诗与明诗稍异。

    152

    Translation

    Goldfish
    The woodworker from the town, Zhang, buried his father in a public cemetery. Later, his family became very wealthy, and he bought a new burial plot to relocate his father's remains. Before the coffin, there was a small pool of clear water, in which three goldfish were swimming. Zhang regretted his decision but could not do anything about it. He then put the fish in a bowl and intended to bury them in the new burial plot. Halfway there, the fish all died, and the woodworker's family returned to their previous state of poverty.

    The Drunken Tea Master Says:
    Is there any truth to fortune-telling? Even after becoming wealthy, abandoning one's parents to a public cemetery is not what a filial son should do. Because of the relocation, the family became poor again. Is it not better to be filial? This is not necessarily the case. One should only act according to what their heart finds right. Wealth and poverty are matters of fate, and the land cannot be blamed for one's misfortune.

    Heavenly Gate
    My great-grandmother, Lady Yang, once sat by the window enjoying the moon. She saw a crack appear in the sky, and suddenly a heavenly gate opened like a door. Two guards stood on either side of the gate, and inside, there were rays of light and people moving about. After a moment, the gate closed again, revealing the heavenly gate.

    Yellow Leaf Village
    Xigu (West Ferry) was formerly known as Yellow Leaf Village, and older people still remember it. Recently, the name has fallen out of use. During the Daoguang era, a spirit medium wrote a poem that said, "The monk returns to the temple in the Yellow Leaf Village, and people call for the ferry at the sunset." The note explained, "Yellow Leaf Village is now known as West Ferry." However, the poem differs slightly from the Ming dynasty version.

    153

    产龙
    　　曲逆妇人雨夜独寝，有朱虫如线落臂上，蠕蠕而动。旋绕三匝，倏长如带。拂之，破壁飞去。未几，妇腹渐大，产一小龙，鳞角俱备。其家恶而杀之。
    祈雨
    　　山左张太守幕客某，善驱遣之术。时宾客咸集，众求观其术，某不许。固强之，不得已，设坛檐际，揲指作诀，口喃喃作诵咒。俄而云集庭除，细雨如霰。出视墙外，则赤日曈昽。片刻，复烧符诵咒，即雨止云散矣。公子悦之，苦求其术。某靳勿传。伺其他出，窃搜竹簏，得其符箓，试之果验，第自晨至日昃，雨卒不晴。始大恐，下坛欲遁。忽云开雨霁，有大蛙毒蛇数十自空坠落。公子急奔，物集足下，大肆螫噬，踝骨血流，几乎毙命。某归而见之，急设坛作法，物始不见。深怼公子好事，遂辞馆去。公子因惊成疾，数月寻亡。

    Explanation

    153

    Translation

    Giving Birth to a Dragon
    In the rain, a woman from Qu Ni slept alone. A red insect, like a thread, fell onto her arm and wriggled slowly. It wound around her arm three times and suddenly grew long like a belt. When she brushed it, it broke through the wall and flew away. Soon, her belly grew larger, and she gave birth to a small dragon with fully developed scales and horns. Her family hated it and killed it.

    Praying for Rain
    A certain guest in the office of Zhang, the governor of Shandong, was skilled in the art of exorcism. At the time, all the guests gathered, and they all asked to see his art. He refused. They insisted, and he had no choice but to set up an altar at the eaves, manipulating his fingers and making gestures, muttering incantations. Soon, clouds gathered in the courtyard, and light rain fell like hail. When he looked outside the wall, the sun was bright and clear. After a moment, he burned talismans and recited incantations again, and the rain stopped and the clouds dispersed. The young master was pleased and desperately sought the art. The guest refused to pass it on. The young master waited for him to leave and secretly searched the bamboo basket, finding the talismans. He tried them, and they worked. From morning until midday, the rain never cleared. He began to be greatly afraid, descended the altar, and wanted to flee. Suddenly, the clouds parted and the rain stopped, and dozens of large frogs and poisonous snakes fell from the sky. The young master rushed, and the creatures gathered around his feet, bit and stung him, causing blood to flow from his ankles, nearly killing him. The guest hurriedly set up an altar and performed a ritual, and the creatures disappeared. He deeply resented the young master's curiosity and left the school. The young master became ill from the shock and died after several months.

    154

    雷殛
    　　甲与乙俱通州人，寓邑之河北客店，日以唱曲糊口。有河乡人某亦居此店。一日暴雨，甲乙同归，途遇乡人，因邀入内肆避雨。时雷电奔腾，檐溜如泻，甲谓乙曰：“去店不远，宜冒雨前行。不然天暝泥泞，步履实难。”乙以为然。二人甫出肆门数武，霹雳一声，二人骈受雷击。方知一路不遽诛者，有某同行故也。众视之，一胸有小孔，一背上孔亦如之，其乡人告众曰：“此二人者，阳以度曲为名，暗诱人家子女，卖银以供赌博。昨自通州拐二女，已鬻诸青楼矣。其遭天诛也，不亦宜哉！”
    飞人
    　　郡人乘海舶，失风，至一处，沙滩寥阔，数里外暂有山岛，一树高数丈，枒槎枯立。有人结巢而居，如猱而小，黑黝无衣，背生双翼，在地上或拾蛤蜃，或抱木枝，纷纷不计其数；语音如鸮，不可辨。见人至，群相惊飞上树。后遇顺风船归，询诸洋人，云飞人国也。

    Explanation

    154

    Lightning Strike
    　　Jia and Yi were both from Tongzhou, staying at a guesthouse in the north of the city, making a living by singing. A man from the river village also stayed at this inn. One day during a heavy rain, Jia and Yi returned together, met the man from the river village on the way, and invited him inside to take shelter from the rain. At that time, the lightning was surging, and the rainwater was pouring down like a waterfall. Jia said to Yi, “The inn is not far away, we should brave the rain and go ahead. Otherwise, it will be dark and muddy, making it very difficult to walk.” Yi agreed. Just as the two stepped out of the inn and took a few steps, there was a loud thunderclap, and both were struck by lightning simultaneously. It was then understood that they were not immediately punished along the way because someone was traveling with them. The crowd looked at them and saw that one had a small hole in his chest, and the other had a similar hole on his back. The man from the river village told the crowd, “These two pretended to be singers by profession, but secretly lured people’s children, selling silver to fund gambling. Last night, they stole two girls from Tongzhou and sold them to brothels. Their punishment by heaven, is it not fitting?”

    Flying People
    　　A man from a certain region was traveling by sea when he lost control of his ship due to a storm and arrived at a place with a vast sandy beach. Several miles away, there was a temporary mountain island, with a tall tree standing there, its branches and twigs withered and dry. There were people living in nests, small like monkeys, black and naked, with wings on their backs. They were picking up clams and snails or holding branches of trees, in great numbers, too many to count. Their voices sounded like owls, and could not be understood. When they saw people, they all flew up to the trees in fright. Later, they encountered a ship with favorable winds returning, and asked the foreigners about it. The foreigners said it was the country of the flying people.

    155

    徐标
    　　徐标者，葛沽裱画工也。适野遇一叟，曳杖斜行，步履蹇缓，携一童子，约十三四岁，容貌娟秀，依其肘下。至野寺前，顾谓徐曰：“烦君为我儿觅一佳妇，定有厚酬。”徐言：“我画工也，非执斧者，何处为令郎觅妇？”叟笑云：“先有薄赠，庶不疑老夫食言。”扪囊中金一锭置石砌上，袖出短斧，斫其翘约二两许，赠之。徐受而妄许之曰：“城中大姓，予戚也，有女美而待字，与公子年齿相若，予怂恿之，事无不谐，试为君家谋之。”叟大悦，曰：“果尔，勿忧贫也。”邀徐至寺后，见累累然一望无际，皆朱提也。徐长跪而哀之，叟云：“子归急备车乘，任尔运载。明日四更，予候于途，过时则不可得。请勿悔。”徐归，遍求亲友，仅得一车。至家时，已二鼓，奔波倦甚，伏枕稍息，辄酣睡。醒则东方既白，急驾车出村外。遇叟，叟责其负约。徐善言相抚。叟引徐行数里，则浊雾满天，失叟所在。及至寺后，则断碑残瓦而已，悔恨而返。

    Explanation

    155

    Xu Biao, a bookbinder from Gezhuang, was walking in the wild when he encountered an old man dragging a cane and moving slowly. The old man was accompanied by a young boy, about thirteen or fourteen years old, who had a delicate and beautiful appearance and clung to his arm. When they reached a wild temple, the old man turned to Xu and said, "I ask you to find a good wife for my son, and I will certainly give you a generous reward." Xu replied, "I am a bookbinder, not a woodcutter, so where would I find a wife for your son?" The old man smiled and said, "First, I will give you a small gift, so you won't doubt that I will keep my word." He took a gold coin from his bag and placed it on a stone path, then pulled out a short axe and cut off a piece of gold weighing about two taels, giving it to Xu. Xu accepted the gold and rashly promised, "In the city, there is a prominent family, a relative of mine, who has a beautiful daughter still unmarried. She is the same age as your son. I will persuade her, and there will be no problem. Try to arrange this for you." The old man was very pleased and said, "If so, don't worry about poverty." He invited Xu to the back of the temple, where they saw a vast expanse of gold coins. Xu knelt down and begged the old man, who said, "Go home quickly and prepare a vehicle. You can load as much as you want. Tomorrow at the fourth watch, I will wait on the road. If you miss the time, you won't get it. Please don't regret it." Xu returned home and asked his friends and relatives for help, but only managed to get one cart. By the time he got home, it was already the second drumbeat (around 2:00 AM), and he was very tired from the journey. He lay down to rest and fell into a deep sleep. When he woke up, the east was already bright, and he hurriedly drove out of the village. He met the old man, who scolded him for breaking the agreement. Xu tried to soothe him with kind words. The old man led Xu to walk several miles, but the sky was filled with thick fog, and he lost sight of the old man. When they reached the back of the temple, all they saw were broken stones and tiles, and Xu returned in regret.

    156

    醉茶子曰：《郁离子》云：“舟必漏也，而后水入焉；土必湿也，而后苔在焉。”徐利欲薰心，故叟得而揶揄之。观其诡词相许，其心术尚可问耶？非叟戏徐，徐自戏也，悔恨奚为？
    刘大士
    　　刘大士，完县人，善画嗜饮，每携瓶酒往山林清幽处坐饮。一日至山半，松下石平如砥，草细堪梳，四顾峰峦，耸翠在目。坐而独酌，忽见杯中一虫如线，蠕蠕然类鱼游泳。惊顾间酒净杯翻，虫一跃失所在。忽霹雳一声，黑云缭绕，一金龙拏空飞去。

    Explanation

    156

    Drunk Tea Drinker said: As Yu Li Zi states, "A boat must have a leak before water enters it; the soil must be damp before moss grows on it." Xu was consumed by greed and desire, so the elderly person mocked him. Observing his deceptive words and agreement, can his intentions still be questioned? It is not the old man who is playing with Xu; Xu is playing with himself. What is the point of regret?

    Liu Dashì

    Liu Dashì was a person from Wán Xiàn who was skilled in painting and enjoyed drinking. He often carried a bottle of wine to a quiet and secluded place in the mountains to drink alone. One day, he reached halfway up the mountain. Under a pine tree, there was a smooth stone like a grindstone, and the grass was so fine that it could be combed. Looking around, he saw towering green peaks. He sat and drank alone when suddenly he saw a worm in his cup, moving slowly like a fish swimming. In his startled glance, the wine was gone and the cup overturned. The worm jumped once and disappeared. Suddenly, a thunderclap sounded, and black clouds twisted and turned. A golden dragon grasped the air and flew away.

    157

    醉茶子曰：一斗香醪，形骸放浪，酒人大抵然也，岂龙亦好曲蘖乎？观其畅饮一杯，掉尾而去，知其醺醺腾腾半空飞舞之乐也。
    僧冤
    　　邑有僧，夜为盗杀，劙肠破腹，其死甚惨。鸣于官，捉凶未获，渐寝其事，庙遂荒废。有巡更卒四人宿于内，夜围炉煨酒。僧忽入，混身血污，手提肝肠一具，血尚淋漓，泣云：“僧死冤甚。”众惊呼，倏不见。
    刘姓
    　　邑刘姓，自南乡索债归，路经旷野，乘月独步。见半里外一物当道矗立，怪往来熟路从无碑碣，是何时创建者？踟躅间物忽飞动，势如骤雨，转瞬离身咫尺。刘急侧身一躲，便听风声飕飕，回顾已在数十步外。大恐，伏地不敢少动。俄而物旋转复回。刘扪怀中有布巾裹钱三百余，即以投之。铿然作响，遽倒于地。刘恐己起而物亦随起，遂屏息以伏道旁。天明迫视，乃败棺板也，狼狈而返。

    Explanation

    157

    The Drunken Tea Drinker said: A measure of sweet wine, unrestrained and carefree, is the way of the drunkard, so could even dragons enjoy fermented grains? Observe how it drinks a cup with gusto, wags its tail, and departs, knowing that it enjoys the joy of being drunk and flying in the air.

    The Monk's Grievance
    In the county, there was a monk who was killed by a thief at night. His intestines were cut and his abdomen was torn open, and his death was very cruel. He reported it to the authorities, but the culprit was not caught. Gradually, the matter was forgotten, and the temple became dilapidated. Four night watchmen stayed inside, gathering around a fire to warm wine. The monk suddenly appeared, covered in blood, holding a bundle of intestines. Blood was still dripping, and he cried, saying, "The monk died most unjustly." The others were shocked and cried out, but he disappeared suddenly.

    Mr. Liu
    In the county, there was a man surnamed Liu. After collecting a debt from the southern village, he returned. On the way, he passed through an open field, walking alone under the moonlight. He saw something standing upright in the middle of the road half a li away. He found it strange that there were no stone monuments on a familiar road. As he hesitated, the object suddenly flew up, moving as fast as a sudden rainstorm. In an instant, it was just a few steps away. Mr. Liu quickly turned his body to avoid it. He then heard the sound of the wind whistling. Looking back, he was already dozens of steps away. He was greatly frightened and lay on the ground, not daring to move even slightly. Soon, the object turned and returned. Mr. Liu felt in his pocket and found a cloth bundle containing more than three hundred coins. He immediately threw it at the object. It made a clanging sound and fell to the ground. Mr. Liu was afraid that if he stood up, the object would also stand up. So he held his breath and lay on the side of the road. When morning came and he looked closely, it turned out to be a broken coffin board. He returned in a state of disarray.

    158

    醉茶子曰：投之以钱，颓然而倒，岂败棺板亦好货乎？予尝见世之贪官污吏，其怒也暴，其来也猛，投钱而辄解者，与败棺板何以异哉！
    蛇精
    　　邑李氏废楼中有蛇精，能巨能小，尝蟠栏上暴鳞，皎如白虹。元宵节有戏龙灯者，蛇忽出，粗与龙等，同舞空中。人骇散，蛇亦渺。相顾众中失去一美少年。众固疑为蛇摄去，遍寻不可得也。后数载，见其人与一艳妆女子在东南城楼上凭肩眺望。或呼之，二人遽入楼内。一大蛇垂尾檐际，粗如梁焉。方悟女子乃蛇精也。

    Explanation

    158

    The tea-drinking sage says: When money is thrown at it, it collapses, how can even a broken coffin board be considered a good commodity? I have often seen the corrupt officials of the world, whose anger is fierce, whose arrival is sudden, those who collapse at the mere sight of money, what is the difference between them and a broken coffin board? In the abandoned tower of the Li family in the town, there was a snake spirit, able to grow large or small, curling on the railing and displaying its scales, as bright as a white rainbow. During the Lantern Festival, there was a dragon lantern performance, the snake suddenly emerged, as thick as the dragon, dancing together in the air. People were terrified and scattered, the snake also vanished. Looking around, they noticed one handsome young man was missing from the crowd. The crowd naturally suspected that the snake had taken him away, they searched everywhere but could not find him. After several years, they saw the man and a beautifully dressed woman leaning against each other on the southeast city wall, gazing out. Someone called out to them, the two quickly entered the building. A large snake hung its tail over the eaves, as thick as a beam. Only then did they realize that the woman was a snake spirit.

    159

    奇疴
    　　德州民某，逃难来津，寓邑城北官厂。得一疾：左手暴长。三日大几如箕。群以为妖，未几遂毙。
    神妖
    　　饥民某妇，寓邑之城隍祠。病痤，梦为鬼役勾去，同寓三人亦与焉。往见神，神云：“此妇尚循妇道，可送还。此三人好詈乡里，某曾殴翁姑，某曾拐人儿女，并欺凌妯娌，均宜正法。”即见阶下一厉鬼，以巨斧斫三人首，鲜血迸流。妇惊寤，病亦愈。同寓三妇是日俱死。
    厉鬼
    　　医士粱竹溪，寓沧州客店，夜卧吸烟。有健男子四人自外入，背后濡染殷血，问之不应，一一入复室内。梁呼从者烛之，则乌有矣。主人云：“此屋昔为贼巢，官军捕获五十余人弃市。其中尤悍暴者四首逆，往往为祟。君所见者即是。”
    女鬼
    　　邑王维庵夜闻款关，出视，乃一妇人，缟衣麻裙，面墙背立，问之不语。至王唤女仆秉烛灼之，阒其无人矣。后月余，王卒。

    Explanation

    159

    Strange Illness
    A man from Dezhou, fleeing disaster, came to Tianjin and took lodging in a government-owned factory in the northern part of the city. He contracted an illness: his left hand suddenly grew abnormally large. Within three days, it had swollen almost to the size of a winnowing basket. The crowd believed it was an omen, and soon he died.

    Divine and Malevolent Spirits
    A famine-stricken woman, lodging in the temple of the city god, fell ill with a skin disease. In her dream, she was summoned by a ghost and taken away. Three other women staying in the same place were also taken. They were brought before the god, who said, “This woman still follows the ways of a wife and can be sent back. These three women often cursed the village, one had beaten her in-laws, another had kidnapped someone’s children, and all three had bullied their sisters-in-law. They should all be punished according to the law.” Immediately, a fearsome ghost appeared at the foot of the steps, wielding a large axe to behead the three women. Blood splattered everywhere. The woman awoke in terror, and her illness also healed. That very day, the three women in the same lodging place all died.

    Malevolent Ghost
    A doctor named Liang Zhuxi, staying at an inn in Cangzhou, was smoking in bed one night when four strong men entered from outside. Their backs were stained with fresh blood. When Liang questioned them, they did not respond. One by one, they entered the inner room. Liang called his servant to light a candle, but they had vanished. The innkeeper said, “This house was once a bandit hideout. The official army captured more than fifty people and executed them in public. Among them were four particularly fierce and violent ringleaders, who often caused disturbances. What you saw were those very ghosts.”

    Female Ghost
    At night, Wang Weian of the county heard someone knocking at the door. He went out to see and found a woman dressed in white clothing and a hemp skirt, facing the wall and standing with her back to him. When he questioned her, she did not speak. When Wang called his maid to light a candle and inspect the room, it was empty. A month later, Wang died.

    160

    妖避雷
    　　邑田家庄关圣庙中古槐，二百年物也。忽阴雨，雷电绕之。树巅立一小儿，如五六岁童子，身无寸缕，手执红旗。旗一麾，雷电辄退。相持数刻，始不见，天亦开霁。庙墙上见一大蝎虎，长七尺余，意即其妖也。
    火异
    　　邑荒草坨村，去城十余里。同治癸酉秋间雨后，有火一片自北来，所着林木俱焚，庐舍焦灼。其未尽燎者，窗纸成灰烬，而窗棂梁柱如故。有村人焦头烂额四五辈，入城求医，皆燎伤也。
    水灾
    　　同治辛未，邑大水。六月十四日二更天，有白气一道，宽约半尺，自南至北，其直如绳。未几，连日阴雨，城中水深五尺许，民屋倾圮，平地砖隙出泉，涓捐不竭，地陷数处，深莫见底。蛙缘壁登屋，伏行如蜥蝎然，亦一奇也。
    鬼剪烛
    　　邑宋氏，巨富也。其家太夫人深夜独坐，呼婢剪烛。一妇揭帘入，白布裹头，身被凶服，舌长出口，发乱垂肩，对案悲号，剪烛而去。夫人惊疾，旋卒。家自此贫苦。

    Explanation

    160

    Translation

    A Ghost Avoiding Lightning

    In the Guan Temple of the ancient Chinese parasol tree in the village of Tianjia in the county, a tree that had lived for two hundred years, suddenly it began to rain, and lightning surrounded the tree. Atop the tree stood a small child, about five or six years old, naked, holding a red flag. When the child waved the flag, the lightning retreated. They confronted each other for several moments before the child disappeared, and the sky cleared up. On the temple wall, a large scorpion-like creature, about seven feet long, was seen, which was likely the ghost.

    Strange Fire

    In the village of Huangcaotuo, about ten miles from the city, during the autumn of the year Guiyou in the Tongzhi era, after the rain, a fire came from the north. All the trees it touched were burned, and the houses were scorched. Where the fire did not completely burn, the window paper turned to ashes, but the window frames and pillars remained intact. There were several villagers with burned heads and faces who went to the city to seek medical treatment, all of whom had been burned by the fire.

    Flood

    In the year Xinwei of the Tongzhi era, the county experienced a great flood. On the fourteenth day of the sixth month, at the second watch of the night, a white vapor, about half a foot wide, appeared, stretching from south to north, as straight as a rope.
