Soon after, continuous rain fell for several days, and the water in the city was about five feet deep. Houses collapsed, and springs emerged from the gaps in the bricks on flat ground, never ceasing. The ground sank in several places, with depths that could not be seen. Frogs climbed walls and onto houses, moving stealthily like lizards, which was also a strange phenomenon.

    Ghost Trimming Candles

    In the county, the Song family was very wealthy. One night, the lady-in-waiting sat alone, and she called a maid to trim the candle. A woman entered by lifting the curtain, wrapped in white cloth, wearing mourning clothes, with a tongue hanging out and hair disheveled and hanging down to the shoulders. She stood by the table, wailed, and then left after trimming the candle. The lady-in-waiting became ill with fright and soon died. From then on, the family became poor.

    161

    定州僧
    　　定州某僧，荒淫不节，每出寻娼，则云耕田去。患痔不起，医教以芝麻研敷。后渐腐烂，遂刀剪肛脱于外，秽恶殆不可言，其徒避之。未几将死，谓其徒曰：“此青莲花也，非道力深者不能有此。”越数日圆寂。予友戏编诗云：“昼出耘田夜溃麻，腚中翻出青莲花。”二句亦颇解颐。
    灶神
    　　静海曹媪，生有洁癖，事灶神尤虔。自言幼时屋经漏雨，支板宿灶前，误以不浩物置灶内，夜半朦胧间有人叱曰：“此何处，岂容汝酣睡？若再以不洁物纳我口，则击杀尔脑矣。”惊醒，见一人皂袍乌纱，手持牙笏，气象森肃，转瞬失所在。
    小无常
    　　邑郭茂才海帆，夜醉归。遇一鬼，高如十余岁童子，素衣高帽，立道左，颇似庙中土偶。郭疑童子戏为也，叱之曰：“夜深矣，何物童子犹不归寝，街前恶作剧以骇行人？将寻汝父兄切加责之。”鬼立如故，郭竟过，走数武，忽悟其为鬼，酒顿醒，两足战栗不能步，为巡更者送至其家。

    Explanation

    161

    A Monk from Dingzhou
    A certain monk from Dingzhou was wildly indulgent and unrestrained, often visiting prostitutes under the pretense of going to work the fields. He suffered from severe hemorrhoids that prevented him from sitting, and a doctor prescribed a treatment involving grinding sesame seeds into a paste and applying it. Over time, the condition worsened, and the hemorrhoids began to protrude externally, becoming so foul and disgusting that it could not be described. His disciples avoided him. Soon before his death, he told his disciples, "This is a lotus flower, and only someone with deep spiritual power could have such a condition." A few days later, he passed away peacefully. A friend of mine composed a humorous poem: "During the day he works the fields, at night he deals with hemorrhoids; a lotus flower emerges from his anus." The two lines were quite amusing.

    The Stove God
    An elderly woman from Jinghai had an obsessive aversion to dirt and was especially devoted to the stove god. She recalled that as a child, her house had leaked, and she had to sleep on a makeshift bed in front of the stove. Mistakenly, she placed impure objects inside the stove. In the middle of the night, she was startled awake by a stern voice: "Where are you? How can you sleep here? If you place impure objects in my mouth again, I will kill you!" She saw a man in a black robe and black hat, holding a ceremonial tablet, with a stern and imposing presence. He vanished in an instant.

    A Minor Death
    Deputy Magistrate Guo Haifan of the county, drunk and returning home late at night, encountered a ghost who was the height of a child around ten years old, wearing a plain white robe and a high hat, standing on the side of the road, resembling a clay statue in a temple. Guo thought the child was playing tricks and scolded him: "It's late at night. What child is still not asleep, playing tricks to scare passersby? I will find your father and brothers and severely reprimand them." The ghost stood still as before. Guo passed by, walked a few steps, and suddenly realized that the figure was a ghost. He sobered up instantly, his legs trembling so much that he could not walk, and was escorted home by a night watchman.

    162

    鱼梦
    　　安徽徐杨孝廉绪不食鲤，云其先人某公梦一少年哀泣乞救。问故，少年曰：“予白龙也。因醉归，误为老渔所获。倘鬻诸庖丁，鼎镬不免矣。明日市上有巨鳞金目者即是我也。君仁者，肯出资买放，敢忘厚德。”醒而异之，求诸市，果有一苍髯老叟，持大鲤一尾，颇昂其价。公买而释诸水，洋洋而逝。后其家科第绵延，相戒不食生鲤。
    鬼驱贼
    　　邑有妇新寡，停夫柩于窗外。夜有偷儿隔窗探物，妇觉，衣已失去。盗犹未走，窘急无策，乃拔关出，大号。盗一惊而厥。邻人咸至，见盗已死，欲鸣于官，而盗苏，哀求始免。或诸之曰：“汝敢为盗，何畏一妇人？”盗曰：“方伏棺上探物，觉有手如冰，力握予臀，惊而欲遁，忽见檐下立一大鬼，锯牙电目，口张血盆，声如霹雳，攮臂欲扑，予遂晕绝，并未见有妇人也。”

    Explanation

    162

    Fish Dream
    In Anhui, Xu Yang, a scholar named Xu, abstains from eating carp, saying that his ancestor, a certain official, once dreamed of a young man weeping and begging for help. When asked why, the young man replied, "I am a white dragon. Due to intoxication on my way back, I was mistakenly caught by an old fisherman. If sold to a butcher, I would surely face the fate of being cooked in a cauldron. Tomorrow, the one with a large scale and golden eyes in the market will be me. As a kind person, if you are willing to buy and release me, I shall never forget your great kindness." Upon waking, he found it strange and searched the market, indeed finding an elderly man with a white beard holding a large carp, demanding a high price. The official bought the carp and released it into the water, watching it swim away gracefully. Later, his family continued to excel in the imperial examinations, and they all vowed not to eat live carp.

    Ghost Drives Thieves
    In a certain village, there was a widow who had recently lost her husband and kept his coffin outside her window. One night, a thief peeked through the window to steal, and the woman noticed, finding that her clothes were missing. The thief had not yet left, in a desperate and helpless situation, so he unlocked and rushed out, making a loud cry. The thief was startled and fainted. Neighbors came running, seeing the thief lying dead, intending to report to the authorities, but the thief revived and begged for mercy, thus escaping punishment. Someone asked him, "You dare to steal, what are you afraid of, a woman?" The thief replied, "Just when I was crouching on the coffin to steal, I felt a hand like ice, gripping my buttocks with great force. Startled and trying to escape, I suddenly saw a large ghost standing under the eaves, with saw-like teeth and electric eyes, mouth wide open like a blood-filled bowl, voice like thunder, lunging with an arm to attack. I then fainted and lost consciousness, and did not see any woman."

    163

    鸟捕蝗
    　　道光二十二年秋，邑南乡飞蝗为灾。有大鸟如乌，千百成群，集田陇啄虫殆尽，始翔去。是岁尚丰。
    刘玉
    　　信都儒童刘玉，自友处醉归。出村五里许，天已二鼓。路经野寺，见寺中灯火荧煌，疑僧道设坛醮者，入而求饮。有二役捉住曰：“尔来甚妙，正遣我辈寻人矣。”挽至殿下，见堂上坐一王者，龙衮垂旒，气象威赫。刘伏砌下，王曰：“烦尔登记簿册，幸勿怖。此明岁秋事，与汝无涉。”刘唯唯。从者授以笔研，使坐阶下。旋见殿东人头如山堆集，数十人往来奔走，将人头从东运至西，纷纷如蚁。每运一次，向生报数若干，看生写毕始去。此来彼往，手不停挥，渐淅东方欲白，事亦告竣。见一人上殿跪报，共万余级，即向刘索取册去，谓曰：“子劳矣，盍小休憩？”刘伏砌下，心犹惴惴。渐闻人声寂然，开目四顾，人物俱杳，自疑为梦。次年果遭发逆之变。

    Explanation

    163
    **鸟捕蝗**
In the autumn of the twenty-second year of the Daoguang era, a locust plague struck the southern part of the county. Large birds, resembling crows, appeared in groups of hundreds and thousands. They gathered in the fields and ate almost all the locusts before flying away. Despite the plague, the year still yielded a good harvest.

**刘玉**
Xindu's young scholar Liu Yu returned home drunk from a friend's place. He was about five miles out of the village when it was already two hours past midnight. As he passed by a temple in the wilderness, he saw the temple lights flickering and suspected that monks or Taoists were setting up a ritual. He entered the temple to ask for a drink. Two officials or guards caught him and said, "You've come at just the right time; we were sent to find someone." They took him to the hall below, where they saw a king or ruler sitting on the throne, dressed in a dragon-patterned robe with a long, flowing train, exuding an imposing and majestic presence. Liu Yu knelt on the steps, and the king said, "Please register the names in the book; don't be afraid. This is about the events of next autumn, which have nothing to do with you." Liu Yu nodded. A servant gave him a pen and inkstone, and he was made to sit on the steps. Soon, he saw heads piled up like mountains in the east of the hall, with many people running back and forth, transporting the heads from the east to the west in a busy and chaotic manner, like ants. Each time they transported the heads, they reported the number to Liu Yu, and only left after he finished writing. This went on until the eastern sky began to lighten, indicating dawn, and the task was completed. One person came to the hall and knelt to report, saying there were more than ten thousand heads. The person then took the book from Liu Yu and said, "You've worked hard; why don't you take a short rest?" Liu Yu still felt anxious. Gradually, he heard no more sounds, opened his eyes and looked around, finding no one or anything, and doubted whether it was a dream. The following year, a rebellion led by the "hair rebels" (a term often used for the Taiping Heavenly Kingdom) occurred.
    164

    酵茶子曰：大劫至而玉石俱焚，遭其变者，未尝一人枉死。是知冥默之中，必有司其事者矣。丁丑岁，邑设粥厂济民，城东隅立保生所女厂，不戒于火，烧毙二千二百余人，片刻间耳。当火未起，天才黎明，众闻棚外唱名声。疑官绅之施棉衣者。出视，则无其人。已而被灾。然则点鬼薄之说，或亦有之。因思白起坑卒，虽未目睹，而烧毙之人，其惨苦已不忍侧目矣。
    无常二则
    　　邑某医，夜乘肩舆，路过城隍庙，轿夫忽停步不前。怪而隔帘视之，见二大鬼高俱盈丈，一衣白，一衣青，昂然阔步至寺前。门忽豁然自辟，揖让而入，门复自合。时月色光明，纤毫毕见。归后不数日，医与轿夫四人亡其三焉，独在轿后未见鬼者幸免。
    　　予伯祖母朱氏幼时，其姊患痘，将危。朱入室，见堂中立一大鬼，高及屋梁，白衣高冠。朱惊仆，救起。病月余。其姊于是夕遂亡。

    Explanation

    164

    The translator said: A great calamity comes, and even jade and stone are consumed; those who suffer from it, none have died unjustly. This shows that in the depths of darkness, there must be one who oversees such matters. In the year Dingchou, the county established a soup kitchen to help the people, and a female factory was set up at the eastern corner of the city, known as Baoshengsuo. Without warning, a fire broke out, burning to death over two thousand people in an instant. When the fire had not yet started, it was just before dawn, and the crowd heard singing outside the tent. They suspected it was officials distributing cotton clothes. Upon looking out, there was no one there. Soon after, they were struck by the disaster. Thus, the saying of the ledger of the dead may also have some truth to it. Thinking of Bai Qi's massacre, though I did not witness it, the suffering of those burned must have been unbearable to look upon.

    Two Tales of Impermanence

    A certain doctor from the county, traveling at night in a sedan chair, passed by the city god temple. The sedan bearers suddenly stopped and would not move forward. The doctor was surprised and looked through the curtain, seeing two large ghosts, both about ten feet tall. One wore white, and the other wore blue, striding boldly to the front of the temple. The door suddenly opened by itself, and they bowed and entered, the door then closing again. At the time, the moonlight was bright, and every detail was clearly visible. A few days later, the doctor and the four sedan bearers lost three of them. Only the one who was behind the sedan chair and did not see the ghosts escaped.

    When my great-grandmother Zhu was young, her sister fell ill with smallpox and was near death. Zhu entered the room and saw a large ghost standing in the hall, as tall as the eaves, wearing white and a high hat. Zhu was so frightened that she fainted. She was revived and fell ill for over a month. Her sister died that night.

    165

    于某
    　　于某者，孟君东垣之内兄也。病笃，孟往探视，遇于于途。怪问曰：“君抱病已久，何便健壮如斯？夜深天寒，将往何处？”于不答，匆匆遂去。孟至其家，则灵幡高挂，哭声达庭外矣。方悟所遇者，于之鬼也。
    青手印
    　　邑某甲，鬻粥为业，早出晚归。时夜雨倾盆。倦寝于室，睡中闻人连呼其名甚急，未及应，觉有人力拍其背云：“不速出，命合休矣。”惊寤，便闻壁墙咋咋作响。急曳妻子出，则屋訇然倒矣，举家得免。背上留一手印，青色黯然，终身不退，夏月袒背，人皆见之。
    天官
    　　予伯祖宜昌公，讳寿彭。幼时读书别业，闻空室中人语哝哝，疑而探窗视之，见堂中坐一人，金冠蟒袍，手持金如意钩，若世传之天官像状。细觇袍下，双跣其足。傍侍一女子，官妆长袖，衣服灿然，眉目如画。公凝睇久，拭目再视，则不见矣。后公为宜昌太守，退归时，妻子俱亡。晚年惟一女，即予郭氏姑也。识者以为仙示之豫兆。

    Explanation

    165

    Yu
    Yu was the brother-in-law of Meng Jun Dongyuan. When Yu was gravely ill, Meng went to visit him and met him on the way. Surprised, he asked, "You have been sick for a long time, how can you be so healthy and strong? It is deep into the night and cold, where are you going?" Yu did not answer and hurriedly left. When Meng arrived at his home, he saw the spirit banner hung high and heard the cries reaching outside the courtyard. Then he realized that the person he had encountered was the ghost of Yu.

    The Green Handprint
    A man from the county, who sold porridge for a living, went out early and returned late. One night, it rained heavily. Tired, he slept in the room and heard someone calling his name urgently while he was asleep. He did not have time to respond and felt someone forcefully patting his back and saying, "If you don't hurry out, your fate is sealed." Waking up in shock, he then heard the walls making strange noises. He quickly pulled his wife and children out, and then the house collapsed with a loud noise. The whole family was saved. A handprint was left on his back, the color was dark blue, and it never faded. In summer, he would expose his back, and everyone could see it.

    The Heavenly Official
    My great-grandfather, Yu Bozu Yichang Gong, whose courtesy name was Shoupeng, was reading in a separate study when he was young. He heard people talking softly in an empty room and, suspecting, he peeked through the window to see. He saw someone sitting in the hall, wearing a golden crown and a robe with a python pattern, holding a golden如意hook, like the image of a heavenly official passed down through the ages. Carefully looking under the robe, he saw that both feet were bare. A woman was standing beside him, dressed in official attire with long sleeves, her clothes shining brightly, her eyebrows and eyes as beautiful as a painting. He stared for a long time, wiped his eyes and looked again, but then he saw nothing. Later, he became the governor of Yichang. When he retired, his wife and children had all died. In his old age, he had only one daughter, who was my mother-in-law, Guo. Those who knew thought it was a divine sign of a foretelling.

    166

    西贾
    　　邑北关外烟肆中，有西贾夜在河干遗矢，闻水中人语曰：“代尔者谁耶？”答曰：“贩鸡者。”又问：“以何术诱之？”曰：“驱鸡入水，秉间曳之。”再听则寂然，知为溺鬼求代者。明日，俟于河滨。至午，果有一人担荆笼过，鸡忽窜逸，寻释担欲追。西贾力阻，告以故，邀入肆，偿其鸡价。其人感谢而去。至夜有叩门买烟者，贾从板壁孔中递出，其人力握其手云：“予冤魂也，沉溺河中，骨寒肉麋。三年之久，始得一代。汝泄我谋，使我无由复见天日。予岂甘心？虽然，汝救彼生，须替彼死。”拶其腕，如受桎梏。贾大号，群商出逐，见一黑人影循墙而去。回顾西贾，倒地死矣。

    Explanation

    166

    A Western Merchant

    In a tobacco shop outside the northern gate of a town, there was a Western merchant who, one night, left something behind by the riverbank. He heard someone speaking from the water, asking, "Who is replacing you?" He replied, "A chicken seller." The voice asked again, "By what method did you lure them?" He answered, "I drove the chickens into the water and pulled them by the neck." Upon hearing this, there was silence, and he realized it was a drowned ghost seeking a replacement.

    The next day, the merchant waited by the riverbank. At noon, indeed, a man carrying a basket of firewood passed by. The chickens suddenly escaped, and he soon put down his load and wanted to chase them. The Western merchant strongly objected, explaining the reason, and invited him into the shop, paying for the chickens. The man thanked him and left.

    That night, someone knocked on the door to buy tobacco. The merchant handed it out through a hole in the wall. The man tightly held his hand and said, "I am a wronged spirit, drowned in the river, with cold bones and rotten flesh. For three years, I finally got a replacement. You revealed my plan, preventing me from seeing the light of day again. I am not willing to accept this. Although, since you saved his life, you must take his place in death." He squeezed the merchant's wrist, as if in shackles. The merchant screamed loudly, and the other merchants came out to chase him. They saw a black figure walking along the wall and leaving. Looking back at the Western merchant, he fell to the ground and died.

    167

    醉茶子曰：溺鬼缢鬼，皆能求代，由来已久。其事卒不绝，其理终不可解。俗云：造生造死，出自冥君。其人本当溺死，则死自其分，死后当入轮回；其人不当溺死，是死于非命，当初何以注册？况乎其自溺也，祸由自取，不得归咎于人；其为人所溺也，冤各有主，更不得另寻别人。谓必求一代己者始许超生，则阴曹律例，殊属荒谬。使冥王不操其权，则自死自生，绰有余地，何必毙一人以泄其私忿？使冥王操其权，则互相倾害，法当禁止。使冥王知其事而不管，则冤冤相报，更无已时，岂不自此多事？使冥王不知其事，则生死事大，何以竟置不问？且人为善降祥，为恶降殃，彼西贾恻隐之心，遂遭其毒手，抑又何说？或云：贾本阳禄已尽，不然不能闻鬼语。而何以不死于疾，而死于鬼哉？大抵阴有厉鬼，犹世有凶人，刑罚虽严，玩法者卒不少也。

    Explanation

    167

    醉茶子曰：溺鬼缢鬼，皆能求代，由来已久。其事卒不绝，其理终不可解。俗云：造生造死，出自冥君。其人本当溺死，则死自其分，死后当入轮回；其人不当溺死，是死于非命，当初何以注册？况乎其自溺也，祸由自取，不得归咎于人；其为人所溺也，冤各有主，更不得另寻别人。谓必求一代己者始许超生，则阴曹律例，殊属荒谬。使冥王不操其权，则自死自生，绰有余地，何必毙一人以泄其私忿？使冥王操其权，则互相倾害，法当禁止。使冥王知其事而不管，则冤冤相报，更无已时，岂不自此多事？使冥王不知其事，则生死事大，何以竟置不问？且人为善降祥，为恶降殃，彼西贾恻隐之心，遂遭其毒手，抑又何说？或云：贾本阳禄已尽，不然不能闻鬼语。而何以不死于疾，而死于鬼哉？大抵阴有厉鬼，犹世有凶人，刑罚虽严，玩法者卒不少也。

    Translation:

    The Drunken Tea Master said: Drowned ghosts and hanged ghosts can all seek substitutes, a phenomenon that has existed for a long time. This practice never ceases, and its principle remains ultimately inexplicable. It is commonly said that the creation of life and death comes from the Lord of the Underworld. If a person was meant to drown, then death is their due, and after death they should enter the cycle of rebirth; if a person was not meant to drown, then dying in this manner is a wrongful death, and why was this registered in the first place? Moreover, if the person drowned themselves, the calamity was self-inflicted, and they cannot blame others; if they were drowned by someone else, each grievance has its own master, and one cannot seek another person. To say that a ghost must find a substitute to be reborn is particularly absurd according to the laws of the underworld. If the Lord of the Underworld does not exercise his power, then there is ample room for life and death, and why kill one person to vent personal anger? If the Lord of the Underworld exercises his power, then mutual harm would occur, and the law should prohibit it. If the Lord of the Underworld knows about the matter but does not intervene, then grievances begetting grievances would continue without end, leading to more problems. If the Lord of the Underworld does not know about the matter, then the matter of life and death is significant, why is it ignored altogether? Furthermore, doing good brings blessings, and doing evil brings misfortune. That compassionate merchant, with his feelings of empathy, ended up suffering at the hands of a ghost—what explanation is there for that? Some say: The merchant's earthly good fortune had run out, otherwise he could not have heard the ghost's words. But why did he not die of illness, but from a ghost instead? Generally speaking, there are malevolent ghosts in the underworld, just as there are wicked people in the world, and despite strict punishments, those who evade the law are still numerous.

    168

    王媪
    　　王媪，沧州人，病臌胀死，停尸榻上。适逢阴雨，巨霆一震，腹破如鼓，其声訇然，骨肉糜烂如泥，衣衾灰烬。腹出金蛇无算，皆长尺许。床边墙下，随处蜿蜒，扫除半日始净。
    返魂
    　　菜甲将入都，晚投杨村客店。主人引入后院，窗下停一柩，乃主人之子妇新殓者。甲惧，请迁他所。时客舍填溢，不得已止宿焉。甫就枕，梦一少妇二十许，举止端凝，衣裳整肃，向甲再拜云：“妾棺中人，命合不死，阎君放还。请为白诸阿翁，速剖棺出我，否则不可活也。”甲惊寤，少顷又梦如前。怪而呼主人，告以故。启棺，女果苏。客视其人，宛如梦中所见。因诘其何以求救于客，并冥中何所见，则茫然不解。是知人当不死，鬼神呵护，不必其魂有灵也。

    Explanation

    168

    Final Translation

    Wang Ao

    Wang Ao was a woman from Cangzhou who died of ascites. Her body was laid on a bed. Coincidentally, it was a rainy day, and a sudden, powerful thunderclap shattered her abdomen like a drum, producing a loud, booming sound. Her flesh and bones were reduced to a mushy, mud-like state, and her clothes and bedding were burned to ashes. Countless golden snakes emerged from her abdomen, each about a foot long. They wound around the bed and walls in various places, and it took half a day to clean them up.

    Revival

    Cai Jia was traveling to the capital and, late in the evening, checked into a guesthouse in Yang Village. The owner led him to the back courtyard, where a coffin was placed near a window. It contained his son's wife, who had just been recently buried. Cai Jia was frightened and asked to be moved to another room. At the time, the guesthouse was full, so he had no choice but to stay there. As soon as he lay down to sleep, he dreamed of a young woman in her twenties, who was composed and dignified, dressed neatly. She bowed twice to Cai Jia and said, "I am the woman in the coffin, destined not to die. Yama has allowed me to return. Please tell my father-in-law to quickly cut open the coffin and take me out; otherwise, I cannot survive." Cai Jia woke up in shock, and shortly after, he had the same dream again. Surprised, he called the owner and explained the situation. They opened the coffin, and the woman indeed revived. Cai Jia looked at her and found her to be exactly as he had seen in his dream. He then asked her why she sought help from him and what she had seen in the afterlife, but she could not understand his questions. Thus, it became clear that when a person is not meant to die, they are protected by the spirits, and it is not necessary for their soul to be particularly powerful.

    169

    鬼市
    　　庚午乡试后，与二三友人结伴同行。至通州，买舟旋里。舱中先有一客，卧枕行囊，见我辈入，肃然起揖。询其姓宇里居，云“沧州孙姓”，礼貌撝谦，词旨爽迈。自言在营多年，以功授游击，因入都营干，指日即获实缺。袖中出金钱三枚，代人卜易。于是众友集问休咎，孙对答如流，大快人意。予素厌占卜而最喜异闻，言次相与谈鬼。孙云：“金兵惨死，未闻鬼出祟人。然目睹一事，甚为怪异。昔从军荆楚时，尝扎营半山，俯视平野，千里在目。远近村落，俱为兵燹荡平，以故人烟断绝。夜与数人岭头步月，见山下半里许一大庄院，树术庐舍历历可指。旋闻犬吠鸡鸣，杂以柝声繁碎，宛然成一世界，相与骇异。久之，中一人曰：『此冤气所钟，殆鬼市也。试一喝之即变矣。』乃即山头高声大呼贼至，柝声果截然而止。但见明云惨淡，寒月凄清，楼阁亭台一时都杳，隐隐万声叫苦，青磷滚滚，断垣破壁而已。”

    Explanation

    169

    Ghost Market

    After the provincial examination in the Jiazi year, I traveled home with a few friends. Upon arriving in Tongzhou, we bought a boat to return. There was already a passenger in the cabin, lying down with his luggage. He stood up respectfully when he saw us enter and bowed. When we asked about his surname, hometown, and residence, he replied, "I am from Cangzhou and my surname is Sun." He was courteous and humble, and his words were clear and straightforward. He said he had been in the camp for many years and was granted the rank of guizhu due to his merits. He had come to the capital camp to work and was expected to get a real position soon. He took out three coins from his sleeve to tell fortunes for others. My friends gathered to ask about auspicious and ominous signs, and Sun answered fluently, greatly pleasing us. I usually dislike fortune-telling but love strange stories. While talking, we discussed ghosts. Sun said, "I have never heard that ghosts harm people after soldiers die. But I witnessed something very strange. When I served in the army in Jingchu, I once camped on a hillside. Looking down at the plain, thousands of miles were visible. Villages near and far were all destroyed by war, so there were no signs of human habitation. One night, several of us walked on the mountain ridge under the moonlight. We saw a large manor about half a li below the mountain. Trees, buildings, and houses were clearly visible. Soon we heard barking dogs, crowing roosters, and the sound of a watchman's bell, mixed together. It seemed like a whole world, and we were all startled. After a while, one of us said, 'This is probably a place where the spirits of the wronged gather, it must be a ghost market. Let's try shouting "enemy approaching" and see if it changes.' So we shouted loudly from the mountain top that the enemy was coming. The sound of the watchman's bell stopped abruptly. But we saw the bright clouds become gloomy, and the cold moon appear clear and desolate. The pavilions and buildings disappeared suddenly. We heard thousands of faint cries of pain. Green phosphorescent light rolled around, and only ruined walls and broken tiles remained."

    170

    汤海
    　　邑有汤海者，捕蝗南郊。时禾黍方秀，翠色染人。西顾残阳，颓然欲坠。忽睹丛绿中露一巨第，沤钉兽环，门闳壮丽。徘徊间，有古衣冠人导入室中，珠玑缇绣，陈设满堂。一妇人年四十许，亦古装，气象华贵，问曰：“尔汤某耶？今存大帑若干，尽行付汝。”乃命侍蜱二人，挑莲炬引视窖所，白镪累累，高如山岳。监守者俱金铠执钺，排列两行。见汤至，森然起敬。汤留连既久，有一道人手持麈尾，劝汤速行。汤捡三锭纳怀中，出则东方既白。入市秤之，约百五十两。乃易衣乘舆再往，不识其处矣。浩叹而返，遂成颠疾，每对人辄言其窖藏。人见其鹑结，咸笑其痴，不知其实有所遇也。

    Explanation

    170

    In a certain town, there lived a man named Tang Hai, who was engaged in capturing locusts in the southern suburbs. At that time, the millet and sorghum were just beginning to grow, and their lush green color seemed to dye the people. Looking westward, he saw the setting sun, which appeared to be sinking. Suddenly, he noticed a large mansion emerging from the greenery, adorned with ornate nails and animal-shaped rings, and its gate was grand and magnificent. As he lingered, an anciently dressed man led him into the hall, where the room was filled with treasures of pearls, jade, and embroidered tapestries. A woman in her forties, also dressed in ancient attire, appeared splendid and noble. She asked, "Are you Tang Hai? Today, there is a large sum of money in the treasury, and it is all entrusted to you." She then ordered two attendants to carry torches and show him the location of the underground vault. The silver ingots were piled up in great quantities, reaching as high as mountains. The guards, wearing golden armor and holding axes, stood in two rows. When Tang Hai arrived, they stood upright and showed respect. Tang Hai lingered for a long time, and then a Taoist, holding a fly whisk, urged him to leave quickly. Tang Hai took three ingots and placed them in his chest. As he left, the east was already bright with dawn. He weighed the silver in the market, and it was about one hundred and fifty taels. He then changed his clothes and went back to the place, but he could not find it again. He returned home with a deep sigh, and soon he became mentally unstable. Whenever he spoke to others, he would talk about the hidden treasure. People saw him dressed in tattered clothes and laughed at his foolishness, not knowing that he had indeed experienced something extraordinary.

    171

    陈翁
    　　遵化陈翁，为人诚朴善饮。夜归，离村三里许，月明如昼，过大树下。有数人聚饮，见陈，遽邀入座。肴馔精奇，味穷水陆。痛饮辞归，大作吐泻。次日往视其处，有破陶器盛马矢数枚而已，殆狐鬼所化也。
    　　醉茶子曰：幻术诳人，狐鬼抑何巧哉！不知同席饮啄，自视夫复何如？岂诳人亦自诳耶？奇矣。
    木妖
    　　杨村孀妇刘氏，年四十许，素贞静，终岁不出户。庭院中有老木一段，形质朽蠹。妇暑月乘凉，每坐其上，觉有人道之感，遂有孕。将近弥月，妇泣谓其子曰：“予贞洁自持，不图有此奇丑。尔为我招亲党，所产妖物，请众观视，以明我心。”子如其教。及期产一男，体甚肥白。众皆匿笑。妇惭忿，剖之，儿肾内一木条，弯曲至脊，解视肢体，骨节皆如朽木，殆感木妖之气而成也。众疑始释。然则楚姬产铁，事或不诬也。

    Explanation

    171

    Chen the Elderly Man
    Chen the elderly man of Zunhua was sincere and simple, and enjoyed drinking. One night, as he returned home, about three miles from the village, the moonlight was as bright as day, and he passed by a large tree. There were several people gathered for a drink, who saw Chen and hastily invited him to sit down. The dishes were exquisite and rare, with flavors that encompassed all tastes from water and land. After drinking heartily, he left, and experienced severe vomiting and diarrhea. The next day, he went back to the place, and found only a broken clay pot containing a few horse droppings. It was likely transformed by fox spirits or ghosts.

    The drunken tea master said: Magical tricks deceive people, how clever are fox spirits and ghosts! They do not know how they see themselves when sitting at the same table and eating together. Are they also deceiving themselves? Strange indeed.

    Wood Demon
    A widow named Liu from Yang Village, aged around forty, was usually chaste and quiet, and never left the house all year round. In the courtyard, there was an old piece of wood, whose form and substance were rotten and decayed. During the hot summer months, she would sit on it to cool off, and felt a sense of human interaction, and then became pregnant. Almost full term, the woman wept and said to her son, "I have maintained my chastity, but did not expect such an unusual and ugly thing. You should gather my relatives, and let everyone see the monster I gave birth to, to prove my innocence." The son followed her instructions. At the expected time, she gave birth to a boy, whose body was very fat and white. Everyone secretly laughed. The woman was ashamed and angry, and cut it open. Inside the child's kidneys, there was a piece of wood, curved to the spine. Examining the limbs, all the bones and joints were like rotten wood, likely formed by the influence of the wood demon. The doubts of the crowd were finally resolved. Thus, the legend of the woman giving birth to iron may not be false after all.

    172

    丐人子
    　　丐人领一子，约十余岁，背上近肩处生一手，覆以破衣，有解视者，索一钱。予视之，五指虽具，爪甲宛然。然松软不能屈伸，犹如赘瘤。是即纪文达公所谓“感天地之杂气而生者”也。夫牛生五足，鹅育双头，谁谓斯民遂无异象？
    衣怪
    　　张公衣涛，将聘女。所作嫁衣置床上，忽自起坐如人状。女惊走，衣随之。女大号，家人至，衣始倒地。女未及出阁而亡，盖衰气所感，鬼物凭之也。
    　　又予表弟郭式如，在都中肆上购得宫绸袍一件，置杌上，衣忽如人坐。视领上微有血痕，细如密雨，盖受刑者之衣也。弃之。

    172

    Final Translation

    Beggar's Child

    A beggar had a son, about ten or eleven years old, with an extra hand growing near his shoulder blade, covered in tattered clothing. For those who wanted to see it, he demanded one coin. I observed it and found that the five fingers were present, and the nails were intact. However, the hand was soft and could not bend or stretch, much like a tumor. This is precisely what the Grand Master Ji Wendu referred to as "those born from the mixed qi of heaven and earth." Cows give birth to five legs, and geese hatch with two heads; who would say that ordinary people do not have unusual appearances?

    Clothing Ghost

    Mr. Zhang Yi, who was about to marry a woman, placed the wedding dress he had made on the bed. Suddenly, the dress rose up and sat like a person. The girl ran away in fright, and the dress followed her. She screamed loudly, and when her family arrived, the dress finally fell to the ground. The girl did not get married before she died, likely due to a decline in energy and a spirit possessing it.

    My cousin Guo Shiri, while shopping in a store in the capital, bought a court silk robe. He placed it on a stool, and the robe suddenly sat up like a person. Upon closer inspection, there were faint bloodstains on the collar, fine as a drizzle, indicating that it was likely the clothing of someone who had been tortured. He discarded it.

    173

    投胎
    　　邑朱某昼寝，梦至一处，庐舍逼侧，门上春联倒粘，才一瞻望，身忽入其门内，烦闷殆不可耐。倏觉肢体暴缩，身冷如被冰水。开目四顾，见一蓬发女郎，颦眉袒坐。怪欲致诘，女遽以手扼其喉，一惊而寤。醒而异之，寻至其处，景物俨如梦中。有老媪以蒲席裹物，遮以衣襟，方自门内出也。
    鼓楼二则
    　　邑城中鼓楼上铜钟，古器也，每夜叩之，其声訇然，远闻数十里。楼初建时，谋悬是钟，数十健夫莫能舁。俄一老人在旁笑云：“我咒之，当有验。”向钟喃喃有词。复使四人再舁，则轾而易举。回顾老人，失其所在。道光戊戌四月间，白昼钟鸣。镇宪某公怪其非时，遣营卒往视。楼固封锁如故，启钥登楼，遥见东门楼上一人，向西长跪焚香，手持红盖，旋转不休，钟则噌吰自响。急往擒之，白诸镇宪，宪讯之，供云：“钟锁日久成龙，以术取去，瘗诸墓地，则子孙出显贵焉。”公恶其妖异，重惩之。

    Explanation

    173

    Translation:

    Reincarnation

    A man from a certain town named Zhu took a nap during the day and dreamed he arrived at a place where houses were closely packed together. The couplets on the door were pasted upside down. As soon as he looked around, he suddenly found himself inside the door, feeling extremely annoyed and uncomfortable. Suddenly, he felt his limbs shrink drastically, and his body became as cold as if drenched in ice water. Opening his eyes and looking around, he saw a young woman with disheveled hair sitting with a frowning expression, her chest bare. Surprised, he wanted to ask her something, but the woman quickly grabbed his throat. He was startled awake. Upon waking, he found it strange and went to the place. The scenery was exactly as in his dream. An old woman was carrying something wrapped in a mat, covering it with the edge of her garment, just coming out of the door.

    Two Stories of the Drum Tower

    On the drum tower in the town, there was an ancient bronze bell. Every night, when struck, it produced a loud sound that could be heard for dozens of miles. When the tower was first built, plans were made to hang this bell, but dozens of strong men could not lift it. Suddenly, an old man standing nearby laughed and said, "I will chant a spell; it will work." He muttered some words toward the bell. Then, four men were sent to lift it again, and it was easily raised. Looking back at the old man, he was gone. During the fourth month of the Wu Xu year of Daoguang, the bell rang during the day. A high-ranking official, Governor Zhu, found it strange that it rang at an unusual time and sent soldiers to investigate. The tower was still locked as usual. Opening the lock and climbing up, they saw a person on the east gate tower from a distance, prostrated facing west while burning incense, holding a red canopy, spinning continuously. The bell rang on its own. They hurried to capture him, reported to the governor, who interrogated him. The prisoner confessed, saying, "The bell, locked for a long time, has turned into a dragon. Using magic, I took it away and buried it in a tomb. Then, the descendants would become prominent and noble." The governor was disgusted by the supernatural behavior and severely punished him.

    174

    邑王叟，监守鼓楼匙钥。一夕，登楼击钟，有黑狐醉眠钟侧，叟缚而要之曰：“赠我一裘则释汝，否则不汝活也。”佯欲加刃。狐作人语曰：“尔无福命，不敢妄赐财贿。如必欲强求，得钱十千，当受十杖，裘更难望也。”叟苦哀求。狐曰：“明日以轻裘相赠。”释其缚，转盼不见。次日于钟畔得貂裘一領，毛毳蒙茸，欣然披服。忽为捕役所擒，执送镇署。是夕，镇宪失一貂褂，即叟所得者也。宪怒，责以十杖。叟哀诉其情，宪信之。盖宪之失裘也，衣笥封锁，余物犹存，固疑非盗，赐叟十千而释之。

    Explanation

    174

    The Old Man of the Town, responsible for guarding the key to the drum tower, one night climbed the tower to strike the bell. There, a black fox, drunk and sleeping beside the bell, was bound by the old man, who demanded, "Give me a fur coat and I will release you, otherwise I will not let you live." He pretended to draw a knife. The fox spoke in human words, saying, "You have no good fortune or destiny, so I dare not give you wealth casually. If you insist on demanding, you will get ten thousand coins, but you will receive ten lashes, and the fur coat will be even harder to obtain." The old man pleaded desperately. The fox said, "I will give you a light fur coat tomorrow." It released the old man's bindings, and in a moment, it was gone. The next day, the old man found a fur coat beside the bell. The fur was soft and fluffy, and he joyfully wore it. Suddenly, he was caught by the patrol and taken to the town office. That night, the magistrate lost a fur coat, which was the one the old man had found. The magistrate was angry and ordered ten lashes. The old man pleaded his case, and the magistrate believed him. Because the magistrate lost his fur coat, the wardrobe was locked, and the other items were still there, so he suspected it was not theft and gave the old man ten thousand coins and released him.

    175

    杜生
    　　河间杜生，家饶裕，独居精室，自奉殊丰，一仆供其驱使。仆右目如常人，左目能见鬼怪，生而然也。暑月宿廊下，见阶前二狐语曰：“肉食生脑满肠肥，盍往采补？”仆伪睡以观其变。相将至寝门，各以白髑髅戴头上，徐揭帘入，则成二好女，一可二十余，一可十七八，服饰容光，并皆佳妙，至案前夺生所读书。生握其腕曰：“尔辈何人，得毋妖魅？”长者曰：“诚然。将噬尔狂生。”生曰：“予有长矛，何畏妖魅？”女掩口笑曰：“尔有矛，侬有盾。”以袖拂生面，兰麝喷溢。生无语，似情动。少者曳裾曰：“姊姊可速行，勿令人厌弃也。”长者唾曰：“小蹄子，尔要去便去，休缠人。”少者笑曰：“我去我去，勿阻隔汝等云雨。”乃翻身退去。至帘外，手提髑髅，化金光而没。长者偎生坐，以红巾拭坐。生曰：“偎偎傍傍，勿谓狂生无三寸藐具。

    Explanation

    175

    Resident of Hejian
    Du Sheng, a resident of Hejian, lived in a wealthy household. He resided alone in a refined room, providing himself with extraordinary abundance, and had a servant who served him. The servant's right eye was like that of a normal person, but his left eye could see ghosts and monsters, a condition he was born with. During the hot months, he stayed under the veranda and saw two foxes in front of the steps talking: "The meat-eater's brain is full and his intestines are fat, why not go to gather and supplement?" The servant pretended to sleep to observe the changes. Together, they came to the bedroom door, each wearing a white skull on their head, and slowly lifted the curtain and entered. They then became two beautiful women, one around twenty years old and the other around seventeen or eighteen. Their attire and appearance were all wonderful. They went to the desk and took the books Du Sheng was reading. Du Sheng grasped their wrists and said, "Who are you, are you not some kind of demon or ghost?" The older woman replied, "Indeed. We will devour this mad youth." Du Sheng said, "I have a long spear, what do I fear from demons or ghosts?" The woman covered her mouth and laughed, "You have a spear, I have a shield." She brushed Du Sheng's face with her sleeve, and the fragrance of orchids and musk overflowed. Du Sheng remained silent, as if his emotions were stirred. The younger woman pulled her skirt and said, "Sister, please hurry, don't let the man grow tired of you." The older woman spat, "Little thing, if you want to go, go, stop bothering people." The younger woman laughed, "I'm going, I'm going, don't block your intimate moments." She then turned around and left. At the curtain, she held the skull in her hand and transformed into golden light and disappeared. The older woman sat close to Du Sheng, wiping the seat with a red handkerchief. Du Sheng said, "Closely embracing, don't think the mad youth lacks a three-inch thing."

    176

    ”女一笑，登榻解罗衫，卧生枕上。生狂喜，遽近。女拒之，生不听，遂相欢合。仆视以左目，则髑髅横陈榻上，狐以口含生下体，不觉毛发俱悚。次日，仆以情告，劝其速绝，生不从。于是二女此往彼来，从无虚夕。生渐羸瘦。仆曰：“奴不忍坐视主死于妖。”乃入市购利刃。二女忽至，颜色凄楚，泣谓生曰：“我姊妹日奉君子，自谓不恶，何故遣仆谋害？”生白其无。女曰：“郎不之知。仆将试利匕首矣。”生曰：“卿等仙人，何惧一仆？”女曰：“我辈隐形，彼都能见，固知必遭其毒手。请从此绝。'生把袂泣不能抑。二女曰：“速逐仆，我姊妹仍愿相从。”生唯唯。明日见仆辄詈骂，仆言无过，生力遣之。仆曰：“不听奴言，必有奇祸。主人无故逐我，必误信狐子之言也。奴誓杀此二魅而甘心焉。”生益怒，即时逐之。仆浩叹曰：“奴报主无日矣。”乃忿而去。越半载余，生卒。生名恕，字心如。

    Explanation

    176

    A woman smiled and climbed onto the bed, removing her silk robe and lying down beside Xinru. Xinru was overjoyed and hurriedly approached her. She resisted him, but he did not listen and they became intimate. The servant, looking with his left eye, saw skulls lying on the bed, and the fox was holding Xinru's genitalia with its mouth, causing him to feel an involuntary shudder. The next day, the servant told Xinru about what he had seen and advised him to quickly break off the relationship, but Xinru refused. From then on, the two women alternated their visits, and there was never a night without one of them. Xinru gradually became weak and emaciated. The servant said, "I cannot sit idly by while my master dies at the hands of a demon." He went to the market and bought a sharp knife. The two women suddenly arrived, looking sad and crying, and said to Xinru, "We sisters have been serving you faithfully, believing ourselves to be not unworthy. Why have you sent the servant to plot against us?" Xinru denied it. The women said, "You don't know. The servant is going to try out his sharp knife on us." Xinru said, "You are all immortals, why should you fear one servant?" The women replied, "We can become invisible, but he can see us, so we know for certain that he will harm us. Please break off this relationship now." Xinru held their sleeves and cried, unable to hold back his tears. The two women said, "Quickly drive away the servant, and we will still be willing to stay with you." Xinru agreed. The next day, whenever he saw the servant, he would scold and curse him. The servant said he had done nothing wrong, but Xinru forcibly dismissed him. The servant said, "If you do not listen to me, there will be an extraordinary disaster. Your master has no reason to dismiss me; he must have been misled by the fox's words. I swear to kill these two demons and find satisfaction." Xinru became even angrier and immediately dismissed him. The servant sighed heavily and said, "There will be no day when I can repay my master." He left in anger. After half a year, Xinru died. His name was Su, and his courtesy name was Xinru.

    177

    醉茶子曰：色之陷人，溺其情者死而不悔，所难堪者，冷眼旁观之人耳。苟能打破尘关，则搓酥傅粉之流，安在非头戴髑髅之怪哉！

    Explanation

    177

    Drunken Tea Child said: "Appearance traps people; those who drown in emotion die without regret. What is hard to bear is the cold eyes of onlookers. If one can break through the material world, then those who are obsessed with beauty are no different from strange beings wearing skulls."

    178

    鬼馔
    　　孟君东垣往清华，其处适遭贼变，林中多悬人首，远近累累然。未几，暮色昏黄，渺无人迹，道旁村舍皆成墟丘。忽有草屋数椽，阖廠窗扉，四无院落。屋中灯光煌煌，有数人环坐饮饫，心异之。又数里，始投客寓。备言途中所见。主人惊曰：“贼过后乌得有人？明日请与君共探之。”至则破屋内积尸重迭，血腥殆不可迩，相与骇异而返。
    冥报
    　　邑某诸生，武断乡里，人畏之如虎。每唆人兴讼，生为捉刀，事起又为调停，殆两败俱伤，从中渔利自肥。邻里有田产，辄劝人贱售，又能钳制买主出重赀，两受其制，财充己囊。家小康，犹不改行。一日构讼归，晚立池上乘凉，其邻人某缓步过，见生返身狂奔。追之，已不可及。次日，诣邻问故，邻曰：“予昨晚几乎毙命。见池上立一大鬼，面铜青色，鹘睛炯炯，狼牙森森，头大直如麦斗。今日思之，犹胆寒也。”生无语而退。自此遂抱奇疾而亡。

    Explanation

    178

    Ghost Feast

    Shen Jun, traveling to Qinghua, encountered a sudden bandit raid. Many heads hung from the trees, scattered far and wide. Soon, the dusk grew dim, and there was no trace of human activity. Villages along the road had turned into ruins. Suddenly, a small, isolated thatched cottage appeared, with its windows and doors tightly shut, and no courtyard in sight. Inside, the light shone brightly, and several people were seated in a circle, drinking and feasting. Shen Jun found this strange. After traveling several more miles, he finally reached an inn. He recounted what he had seen on the road. The innkeeper was shocked and said, "How could there be people after the bandits passed? Tomorrow, let us go together to investigate." Upon arriving, they found the broken house filled with stacked corpses, the stench of blood almost unbearable. They returned, both horrified and astonished.

    Retribution in the Afterlife

    A scholar in the county was domineering in his village, feared by all like a tiger. He often incited lawsuits, writing on behalf of others, and when disputes arose, he would mediate, often leaving both parties equally harmed, profiting from the situation to enrich himself. Whenever neighbors had land or property, he would persuade them to sell at a low price, and he could also manipulate buyers to pay a high price, controlling both parties and filling his own pockets. Despite his modest wealth, he did not change his ways. One day, after returning from a lawsuit, he stood by the pond in the evening to cool off. A neighbor, walking slowly, saw the scholar turn around and run wildly. The neighbor chased after him but could not catch up. The next day, the neighbor asked the scholar why he had run. The neighbor replied, "I almost died last night. I saw a large ghost standing by the pond, with a copper-green face, sharp, piercing eyes, and sharp, menacing teeth, a head as large as a wheat measure. Even now, I am still terrified." The scholar said nothing and left. From that day on, he fell ill with a strange disease and died.

    179

    邑城南，有老妪善走无常，每与人谈休咎，有奇应。生之子偕友往寻，妪曰：“予正欲访子矣。”问何事见访，妪曰：“昨在冥间，见尊大人向予泣诉曰：『我一世英雄，不图此间一败涂地，归烦寄语家人，速为我多焚冥镪，以备官衙之费。群冤尽不我容。予屋内东南隅藏有白金五十两，可用之。”子疑妪言之诬，妪曰：“父受毒刑，犹不镂心。尚谈笑置之耶？昨见翁锁枷狼狈，臀肉尽脱，可悯极矣。归请掘验室中，果有藏金，可证我言非伪造。”子归试之，果得金，喜甚，纵其淫赌，数日囊罄，更不知冥中何如也。

    Explanation

    179

    In the south of the city wall, there was an old woman who walked without a fixed pattern. She often talked about good or bad omens with people, and her predictions were often accurate. The son of the man in question, accompanied by a friend, went to seek her out. The old woman said, "I was just about to visit you." When asked why she was visiting, she replied, "Last night in the underworld, I saw your father weeping and telling me: 'I was a hero all my life, but I never expected to be in such a state of ruin here. Please ask your family to send a message when I return, and quickly burn a lot of paper money for me to prepare for the expenses of the official court. All the grievances will not tolerate me. In the southeast corner of my house, there is hidden fifty taels of white gold, which you can use.'" The son doubted that the old woman was lying. The old woman said, "Your father suffered cruel punishment, yet he still has no resentment. Are you still laughing it off? I saw your father in chains, looking pitiful, with his buttocks completely torn. It was very sad. Go back and dig in the southeast corner of the room to find the hidden gold. If you find it, it will prove that I am not lying." The son returned and tried it, and indeed found the gold. He was very happy and indulged in gambling and debauchery, depleting his purse within a few days. He did not know what it was like in the afterlife.

    180

    醉茶子曰：攫人之财，强入己橐，而犹掉三寸之舌，颠倒是非，是与御人国门者何以异？惨受阴刑，不亦宜哉！而不肖之儿，见金辄思游荡，其视父如陌路矣。呜呼，豺虎之流，焉有干蛊子哉！
    猫怪
    　　宁河某甲，寓邑之旅店。夜思小解，探身床边，俯取溺器。忽一巨猫人立而舞，以前两足力夺其器。甲骇，以器投之。猫跃登甲背毒啮其腰，齿牙锋利如锥。甲负痛大号，主人秉烛至，猫不知何往。甲惊疾，寻卒。
    张车夫
    　　车夫张姓，驱车野外，见一红衣妇人坐井上，抱儿悲泣，已而投儿于井，己亦匍匐将堕。张急下车奔救，蹶然而倒。车轮压胫折矣。同行者扶起，载于后车。众怪其下车之猛，张告以故。众视道旁，不但无妇，并井亦乌有。众皆愕然。

    Explanation

    180

    醉茶子曰：攫人之财，强入己橐，而犹掉三寸之舌，颠倒是非，是与御人国门者何以异？惨受阴刑，不亦宜哉！而不肖之儿，见金辄思游荡，其视父如陌路矣。呜呼，豺虎之流，焉有干蛊子哉！

    醉茶子说：那些夺取他人财物，强行放入自己口袋的人，还善于用三寸不烂之舌颠倒黑白，混淆是非，这与守卫城门的人有何不同？他们最终会受到阴间的严酷惩罚，这难道不是理所当然的吗！那些不成器的孩子，一见到钱财就想挥霍，甚至视自己的父亲如同陌生人。唉，如此凶残的人，又怎会有能够继承家业的后代呢！

    猫怪

    宁河某甲，寓邑之旅店。夜思小解，探身床边，俯取溺器。忽一巨猫人立而舞，以前两足力夺其器。甲骇，以器投之。猫跃登甲背毒啮其腰，齿牙锋利如锥。甲负痛大号，主人秉烛至，猫不知何往。甲惊疾，寻卒。

    宁河有个旅店的客人，夜晚想要小便，探身到床边，弯腰去拿便器。突然一只巨大的猫站起来跳舞，用前两脚抢夺他的便器。客人吓得用便器砸向猫。猫跳到客人背上，狠狠咬住他的腰部，牙齿锋利如同锥子。客人痛得大声呼喊，店主提着灯赶来，猫不知去了哪里。客人惊恐万分，不久后就去世了。

    张车夫

    车夫张姓，驱车野外，见一红衣妇人坐井上，抱儿悲泣，已而投儿于井，己亦匍匐将堕。张急下车奔救，蹶然而倒。车轮压胫折矣。同行者扶起，载于后车。众怪其下车之猛，张告以故。众视道旁，不但无妇，并井亦乌有。众皆愕然。

    一个姓张的车夫驾车在野外行驶，看到一位穿红衣服的妇人坐在井边，抱着孩子哭泣，随后将孩子扔进井里，自己也爬着要跳下去。张车夫急忙下车去救，却突然倒在地上。车轮压断了他的小腿。同行的人把他扶起来，放在后面的车上。大家对张车夫下车时的举动感到奇怪，张车夫告诉了他们原因。大家环顾四周，不仅没有看到那位妇人，连井也不见了。大家都感到非常惊讶。

    181

    火鸽
    　　己巳夏，邑城中某家不戒于火。烈焰中有群鸽飞舞，片刻，翩翩向南翔去，每鸽翼端皆有火焰，宛如炬然。
    碌碡
    　　杨青驿某家场院，置碌碡一具。有闽人指谓村人曰：“此良药也，宜宝藏之。数年后，此地当有大疫，研服可以活人。”村人均未之深信。壬戌岁，邑患霍乱，传染辄死。巫医佥穷于术。或取碌碡研而试之，奇效，于是全活甚伙。金石入药，亦理之常，而是人能预知将患大疫，不亦神哉！
    常州役
    　　赵少尉言：常州差役某甲，性方鲠。有犯妇，将收禁，众役欲逼淫之。甲知之，力阻，亲视众役送妇入禁而后返。甫至家门，见邻叟领一女子，狼狈而至。近视，则甲之女也。叟云：“适老夫往视秫田，遇两恶少逼是儿将施无礼，予奋老拳，两人惊遁。幸无玷也。”甲拜谢，而叟已渺，始恍然悟其为鬼也。盖叟没已久，甲仓卒而忘之，询其女，果如叟言。囔，己不使人淫人之妇，鬼神即不使人淫己之女，彼苍之意可知矣。

    Explanation

    181

    Fire Doves

    In the summer of the year of the Horse, a fire accidentally broke out in a house in the city. Amid the intense flames, a flock of pigeons danced, and in a moment, they gracefully flew south, each pigeon's wingtips adorned with flames as if they were holding torches.

    Stone Roller

    In the courtyard of a certain family in Yangqing Post Station, there was placed a stone roller. A man from Fujian pointed to it and told the villagers, "This is good medicine; it should be treasured. In a few years, this place will suffer a great epidemic, and grinding and taking it can save lives." The villagers did not deeply believe him. In the year of the Dog, the town suffered from cholera, which was highly contagious and often fatal. Both sorcerers and doctors were at a loss in their methods. Someone took the stone roller, ground it up, and tried it, with remarkable results, and many were saved. It is also common for minerals and stones to be used as medicine, but that this man could predict the impending epidemic, is not divine?

    A Servant in Changzhou

    Lieutenant Zhao said: In Changzhou, there was a servant named Jia, who was upright and honest. There was a woman who had committed a crime and was to be arrested. The other servants wanted to force her into sexual misconduct. Jia knew about this and strongly objected, personally overseeing the other servants as they took the woman to jail before returning home. Just as he arrived at his door, he saw an elderly neighbor leading a young woman, who was in a state of distress. Upon closer inspection, it was his daughter. The old man said, "Just now, I went to check on my millet field and encountered two young men who were trying to force my granddaughter to do something indecent. I struck them with my fist, and they fled in fear. Fortunately, she was not dishonored." Jia bowed in gratitude, but the old man had already disappeared, and he finally realized that the old man was a ghost. It turned out that the old man had been dead for a long time, and Jia had forgotten him in a hurry. He asked his daughter, and she confirmed what the old man had said. Indeed, if one does not allow others to commit immoral acts against their wives, then the spirits will not allow others to commit immoral acts against one's daughters. The will of heaven is clear.

    182

    金佛
    　　邑夏氏家有金佛，高寸许，每示灵异，故贫而未肯售也。有邻家童子戏于其室，窃纳于袖中，出门便迷惘。盘桓半日，夕阳已沉。夏归见其状而询之，童云：“眼前昏暗不识路耳。”夏云：“可随予来。”握其手得金佛，童始豁然，抱惭而去。
    泥髑髅
    　　仆人张明言：其村人自市归，适遇暴雨。道旁有古墓，乃匿身于碑楼下。见土中一骷髅，捡出，戏以湿泥抟其面，捏作五官，复以所买之枣与蒜纳诸其口，置墙窟中，雨晴遂去。越数载，邻村有怪，每夜出，红如灯球，飞进村外，呼曰：“枣甚好吃，蒜太辣。”逐人辄病，共患之。会村人闻之，惊曰：“得毋骷髅之怪乎？”寻至故处，见物仍在窟中，绕颊丛生红毛，蓬蓬如乱发。毁之，嘤嘤有声。怪绝。

    Explanation

    182

    Golden Buddha
    In the household of a man named Xia in the county, there was a golden Buddha, about an inch tall, which often displayed miraculous phenomena. Because of this, the owner, though poor, was unwilling to sell it. One day, a neighbor's child played in the room where the Buddha was kept and secretly put it in his sleeve. When he went out, he became disoriented. He lingered for half a day, and the sun had already set. When Xia returned home and saw the boy's state, he asked him what had happened. The boy said, "Everything around me was dark, and I couldn't find my way." Xia replied, "Follow me." Taking the boy's hand, he found the golden Buddha. The boy suddenly became clear-minded and left with shame.

    Clay Skull
    According to the servant Zhang Ming, a villager returned from the market and happened to encounter a heavy rain. There was an ancient tomb by the roadside, so he hid under a stone tablet. He saw a skull in the soil, picked it up, and played by molding wet clay onto its face, forming facial features. He then placed a bought date and a clove of garlic in its mouth and put it in a wall crevice. After the rain stopped, he left. Several years later, there was a strange phenomenon in the neighboring village. Every night, something red like a lamp ball would fly into the village and call out, "The date is very tasty, but the garlic is too spicy." Anyone who encountered it would become ill, and everyone suffered from it. When the villagers heard about it, they were shocked and asked, "Could it be the ghost of the skull?" They went to the original place and found the object still in the crevice, with red hair growing around its cheeks, fluffy like disheveled hair. When they destroyed it, it made a faint sound. The ghost disappeared.

    183

    黄孝廉
    　　邑黄孝廉，与友共车北上。先生坐车中，友在辕上，相与谈笑而往。忽问语不应，回顾先生，失其所在，张皇四寻，杳无踪迹。前行二三里，遥见一人踆踞林间，就之，果黄也。问其何以至此，则茫然莫解其故。
    古瓶
    　　杨青驿何氏家有古磁瓶，置案头。一夕，雷电入室，龙攫于地，瓶无少损，化为金色。每天阴晦，则出云气缕缕然，可以验雨。插花则落后成实。何氏宝之。
    火灾
    　　道光十二年，邑城北火灾，延烧街市数里，片瓦不存。先是有颠妇赤身披发往来于市，歌哭无常，见人辄云：“一人两眼。”轻薄者或揶揄之。估衣商某见而叹曰：“彼亦犹人，颠狂不自知羞耻，情可悯也。”出布裤衣之。妇著而去。至夜，烈焰冲霄，街巷灰烬，惟商一家得免。登屋则布裤横置屋上，方悟妇乃神人。一人两眼者，火字隐谜也。

    Explanation

    183

    Yellow Scholar
    A scholar-official named Huang from the county was traveling north with a friend, sharing a carriage. The scholar sat inside the carriage, while his friend was on the front, and they talked and laughed as they went. Suddenly, when asked a question, his friend did not respond. Looking back, the scholar realized he had disappeared. Frantically searching all around, there was no trace of him. After traveling two or three miles, they saw a person squatting in the woods. Approaching, they found it was indeed Huang. When asked how he came to be there, he was completely confused and unable to understand the reason.

    Ancient Porcelain Bottle
    In the home of the He family in Yangqing Station, there was an ancient porcelain bottle placed on the desk. One night, a bolt of lightning struck the room, and a dragon grabbed the bottle from the ground, but the bottle suffered no damage and transformed into gold. Every time the weather was overcast, it would emit thin clouds of vapor, which could be used to predict rain. If flowers were inserted, they would wither and bear fruit. The He family treasured it greatly.

    Fire
    In the twelfth year of the Daoguang era, a fire broke out in the northern part of the city, burning down several miles of the market, leaving not a single tile standing. Before this, there was a madwoman who roamed the streets naked and with disheveled hair, singing and crying without regularity. Whenever she saw someone, she would say, "One person with two eyes." Some disrespectful people would mock her. A cloth merchant named Mr. Gusu saw her and sighed, saying, "She is just like any other person, but she is mad and does not know shame; her situation is pitiful." He gave her a pair of cloth pants and a shirt. The woman put them on and left. At night, a fierce flame shot up into the sky, and the streets and alleys were reduced to ashes, but only the merchant's family remained unharmed. When they climbed onto the roof, they found the cloth pants lying there, and they realized that the woman was a divine being. "One person with two eyes" was a riddle hinting at the word "fire."

    184

    醉茶子曰：赠衣而遽免于灾，神非贪其惠，重其义也。谁谓冥漠中无皂白之分哉！
    蝶蛛
    　　静海草米店村古坟中，有蝴蝶与蜘蛛二物，变形奇异，蝶每自穴出，与常蝶无异。渐飞渐大，至云端，则如纸鸢飘逸。遇踏青女儿，则飞穿裙底，翔舞髻端，扑之终不可获。邑朱氏有别业在此村，皓壁朱门，廊舍华丽。蝶入其厅，展翼则墙为之满。翅上花草云霞，五色炫烂，虽工于画者不能描。好隔窗以喙吸人口鼻，血流不止乃死。村人患之，伏军器于穴外，待其出，弩箭齐发，而蝶已飞起。蜘蛛夜出，如火团，结网林间，坚如弦索，木即枯槁，尚不为人害。数载后，村中来两道士，一服饰鲜华，仪质洒脱；一褐衣博带，神采魁梧。揖村人曰：“吾兄弟托庇多年，今将别矣。”或询其姓氏，一胡姓，一朱姓，飘然而去。二怪自此不见。或云仙去也。此道光初年事，有徐媪曾亲见之。

    Explanation

    184

    The Drunken Tea-Seer said: Giving clothes and immediately avoiding disaster, the gods did not covet the gift but valued the righteousness. Who would say there is no distinction between black and white in the unknown!

    The Butterfly and the Spider
    In an ancient tomb in the village of Cao Midian, Jinhai, there were two strange creatures: a butterfly and a spider. The butterfly would emerge from its hole each time, no different from ordinary butterflies. Gradually, it grew larger and flew gracefully into the clouds, like a kite. When it encountered young women on outings, it would fly under their skirts, dance at the ends of their hair, and could not be caught.

    The wealthy family of Zhu in the village had a beautiful estate with white walls, red gates, and ornate halls. The butterfly entered the hall, spreading its wings to fill the entire wall. Its wings were covered with colorful flowers, clouds, and rainbows, so vivid that even skilled painters could not capture them. It liked to peck through windows to suck blood from people's noses and mouths, causing them to bleed to death.

    The villagers were troubled by this, and they set up military weapons outside the butterfly's hole, waiting for it to emerge. When it did, they fired crossbows and arrows, but the butterfly had already flown away. The spider would emerge at night, looking like a ball of fire, weaving strong webs in the forest that could cause trees to wither, though it did not harm people.

    Several years later, two Taoist priests arrived in the village. One was dressed in bright colors and had a carefree demeanor; the other wore simple clothing and had a dignified appearance. They bowed to the villagers and said, "We have been sheltered here for many years, but we will now leave." When asked about their names, one was surnamed Hu and the other was surnamed Zhu. They left mysteriously.

    The two strange creatures were never seen again. Some say they ascended to heaven. This was during the early years of the Daoguang era, and an elderly woman named Xu had witnessed these events.

    185

    二竖
    　　孟君东垣，患病夜卧不寐，灯故未息。时近四更，有小人揭帘入，高四尺许，瘦若刍灵，蹑步潜行，藏匿灯后。俄又来一人，高与前等，亦往依之。灯下微露半面，白如敷粉，而冷峭无光。指床上云：“长须者，即是也。”声细如蚊，字清可辨。越数刻，联袂而出，至帘外云：“姑俟明日。”再听，则寂然矣。自以为非祥，而竟无恙。又数日，病痊。
    夙债
    　　孔孝廉广益，曲阜圣裔也。甲戌会试，入闱后，一人至孔号，口操南音，自云浙人张姓。周旋数语，向孔索债。孔思自幼生长于鲁，何得负欠于南人，力辩其诬。其人逼索益急，孔怒与哗。张云：“予实鬼也。尔前世欠予银若干，今日不偿，请对质于阎君。”言毕，攮臂欲搏。孔怖甚，许以场后核算。张不可，欲碎其卷。孔大窘，泣请宽宥。张云：“速缴尔卷，即不相扰，否则不尔活也。”孔不得已从其言，鬼随之。缴卷后，鬼始去。

    Explanation

    185

    Two Spirits
    Shen Jun Dongyuan was ill and could not sleep at night, so the lamp was not extinguished. It was nearly four o'clock when a small man pulled aside the curtain and entered, about four feet tall, thin like a straw doll, walking stealthily and hiding behind the lamp. Soon another person came, about the same height as the first, also going to hide behind the lamp. The light revealed half of his face, pale as if powdered, but cold and without luster. He pointed to the bed and said, "The one with long hair is the one." His voice was as fine as a mosquito, clear and distinct. After several hours, they left together, holding hands, and said outside the curtain, "Let's wait until tomorrow." When they heard nothing more, they were silent. Shen Jun believed this was not a good omen, but he remained unharmed. A few days later, his illness was cured.

    Old Debt
    Kong Xiaolian Guangyi, a descendant of Confucius, was taking the imperial examination in the year Jia Xu. One night, a man came to Kong's examination room, speaking in a southern dialect. They exchanged a few words, and the man demanded payment from Kong. Kong thought he had grown up in Lu and could not have owed money to a southerner, so he vigorously denied the accusation. The man demanded more urgently, and Kong became angry and argued with him. The man said, "I am a ghost. You owed me a certain amount of silver in a past life. Today, if you do not pay, I will take you to the God of Hell." Saying this, he raised his arm and wanted to fight. Kong was terrified and promised to settle the matter after the exam. The man refused, wanting to destroy Kong's scroll. Kong was in a great dilemma and pleaded for leniency with tears. The man said, "Quickly hand in your scroll, and I won't bother you. Otherwise, I won't let you live." Kong had no choice but to agree, and the ghost left with him. After handing in the scroll, the ghost finally went away.

    186

    邹某
    　　邑邹某，大病初痊，晚行隘巷，忽蹶。旁一人扶起，视之，王某，邹之亡舅也。惊曰：“舅已物故，何得来？”王曰：“我来寻汝也。”邹骇而问故，王曰：“予今充冥吏，事颇繁，欲烦尔稍分微劳，愿勿推却。”邹哀以母老子幼，泣泗乞免。王曰：“我固知其不可，然尔外更无干才，不得不奉劳耳。”邹哀求再三，王意稍转，曰：“尔归俟之，过三月十五不来相招，是我已觅得人矣。尚烦寄语吾家，靴不堪著，为我易之。”言毕而杳。及期，邹无恙。想其已得人矣。

    Explanation

    186

    Zhang, a man from the county, had just recovered from a serious illness. One evening, he was walking in a narrow alley when he suddenly fell. Someone nearby helped him up and looked at him. It was Wang, his late uncle. Startled, Zhang said, "Uncle has passed away, how can you be here?" Wang replied, "I came to find you." Zhang was startled and asked the reason. Wang said, "I am now serving as a ghost official. The duties are quite numerous, and I wish to trouble you to share some of the minor tasks. Please do not refuse." Zhang pleaded that his mother was old and his children were young, and cried and begged to be excused. Wang said, "I already know it is not possible, but there is no one else outside you who can handle it. I cannot help but trouble you." Zhang pleaded repeatedly, and Wang's mind slightly changed. He said, "You go back and wait. If I do not come to call you by the 15th of March, it means I have found someone else. Still trouble you to tell my family that the boots are no longer suitable for wearing, change them for me." After speaking, he disappeared. By the time, Zhang was unharmed. He thought that he must have found someone else.

    187

    醉茶子曰：生死大事，吏岂能操其权？然则阴司之弊，更甚于阳世耶？不然，何操纵自如若此。
    泥龙
    　　武遂某甲，葬亲开圹，得硬泥一段，长三尺，形如龙，尾、鳞、须无一不备，第后足为锸误断。葬后亦无他异·
    瘟神
    　　同治壬戌，大疫流行，传染辄毙。邑有甲与乙者，自城外夜归。时已四鼓。忽见灯烛辉煌，仪仗甚伙。数人舁一肩舆，舆中一人，头巨如斗，赤发云拥，金目电飞，状甚奇异。二人惊避道旁，众纷纷向西而去，殆疫神也。未几，甲乙俱亡。

    Explanation

    187

    醉茶子曰：生死大事，吏岂能操其权？然则阴司之弊，更甚于阳世耶？不然，何操纵自如若此。

    Translation:
    The Drunken Tea Scholar said: "Life and death are great matters; can officials really control them? Yet, is the corruption of the underworld even worse than in the world of the living? If not, why is it so easily controlled?"

    泥龙
    武遂某甲，葬亲开圹，得硬泥一段，长三尺，形如龙，尾、鳞、须无一不备，第后足为锸误断。葬后亦无他异。

    Translation:
    A Clay Dragon
    A man from Wu Shui, while digging a grave for his parent, unearthed a piece of hard clay, three feet long, shaped like a dragon, with tail, scales, and whiskers all present, except that the hind legs had been accidentally cut off by a spade. After burial, there were no other unusual occurrences.

    瘟神
    同治壬戌，大疫流行，传染辄毙。邑有甲与乙者，自城外夜归。时已四鼓。忽见灯烛辉煌，仪仗甚伙。数人舁一肩舆，舆中一人，头巨如斗，赤发云拥，金目电飞，状甚奇异。二人惊避道旁，众纷纷向西而去，殆疫神也。未几，甲乙俱亡。

    Translation:
    The Plague God
    During the year of Ren Xu in the Tongzhi era, a great epidemic spread, and those who contracted it died immediately. In the village, there were two individuals, Jia and Yi, who returned from the outskirts of the city at night. It was already the fourth watch of the night. Suddenly, they saw a brilliant display of lights and candles, with a large entourage. Several people carried a sedan chair, in which sat a person with a head as large as a winnowing basket, red hair like clouds, and golden eyes that flashed like lightning, with a very strange appearance. The two men were startled and stepped aside from the road, while the crowd hurriedly went westward. It was probably the plague god. Soon after, both Jia and Yi died.

    188

    杨瞽
    　　杨瞽，善永性。时吴艘泊岸，有舟人落金于水，遣杨求之，许以瓜分。杨入水，摸得，诡言乌有，藏金石磴下，详记层次而出。潮落，金露于外，为担水者拾去。午后瞽至，觅金不得，知潮落，忿詈而去。
    朱广文
    　　邑朱广文煦，名下士也。捐馆时，停户室内，家人聚哭其侧。先生之女孙自室出，将到灵所。见其窗外黑气如烟，有鬼五六辈团坐地上，头如麦斗，面皆漆黑。张吻掀舌，攒首摇动，意似欢舞。大惊而号，物俱不见。
    宅仙
    　　予故居赁住邵姓时，常见怪异。一夜，院中唧唧有声，邵隔窗窥视，月色皎皎，有六七女郎，艳妆华服，容并妖冶，围坐一大几。酒炙纷陈，馔似精美。持牡丹一朵，飞觞传令，谈笑不休。惜邵中馁，拥衾而眠，倘近之，则名花倾国，自必称快一时。

    Explanation

    188

    杨瞽

    Yang Gu, a blind man with a good and enduring nature. At that time, a ship from Wu was docked at the shore, and a sailor dropped gold into the water. He sent Yang to retrieve it, promising to divide it equally. Yang entered the water, found the gold, but deceitfully said there was none, hiding it beneath a stone step and carefully remembering its location before returning. When the tide receded, the gold was exposed, and a water carrier picked it up. Later in the afternoon, Yang returned, unable to find the gold, and upon realizing the tide had receded, he cursed angrily and left.

    朱广文

    Zhu Guangwen Xu, a renowned scholar of the county. When he passed away, his body was placed in the inner room, and his family gathered around him, crying. His granddaughter, coming out of the room, was about to reach the spirit hall when she saw a dark, smoky atmosphere outside her window. There were five or six ghosts sitting in a circle on the ground, their heads as large as a measure of wheat, and their faces as black as lacquer. Their mouths were wide open, tongues protruding, and their heads were shaking, as if they were dancing joyfully. She was greatly startled and cried out, and all the supernatural beings disappeared.

    宅仙

    When I rented my former home to the surname Zhao, I often witnessed strange phenomena. One night, there was a faint, continuous sound in the courtyard. Zhao peered through the window and saw the moonlight bright and clear. There were six or seven young ladies dressed in beautiful and elegant attire, their appearances all charming and alluring. They were seated around a large table, with wine and roasted meat set out, the food seeming exquisite. One of them held a single peony flower, passing cups and giving commands, talking and laughing without ceasing. Regrettably, Zhao was hungry and wrapped in a quilt, fell asleep. If one were to approach them, the famous flowers and beautiful women would surely be a delightful experience for a time.

    189

    仆幼时，随乳母张氏游戏花园中。张坐阶上，予独入空室，见一矮妇人，头梳时妆元宝髻，脑后燕尾高纵三尺许，循墙摇飐，不知何作。急呼张至，则杳。
    蜥蜴
    　　中山农夫新婚三日，出耕，使新妇饷。妇羞与夫见，遥望夫耘陇畔，即近处有老槐垂阴数亩，意夫当憩于此，遂置馔树下，不告而返。日卓午，农饥，不见来馌，焦急。寻至林间，见篮盛陶器，黎黍蒸蒸，因会意，乃取食焉。食已，腹痛如割，罢耕归家，暴卒。农夫父母疑妇毒之，忿鸣于官。拘妇审讯，妇泣曰：“三日夫妇，有何怨仇？顾食既吾饷，谓非吾所毒也，百舌何能辩？妇知罪矣，请执之，夫死又何乐于生！”宰囚妇。既而悔之，乃提妇复讯。妇以情告。宰往验其处，见古槐阴翳，其心半空，令役以瓶粥置树下，遥坐以观。有蜥蜴长数尺，蜿蜒自孔中出，探首瓶中嗅粥。须臾，登树而遁。隶白宰，宰以粥饲犬，犬毙。因悟蜥蜴毒涎，农误食而死之也。妇冤乃白，官焚其树。

    Explanation

    189

    When I was young, I played in the garden with my wet nurse, Zhang. As she sat on the steps, I entered an empty room alone and saw a short woman with her hair styled in a modern treasure chest bun, with a swallow-tail hairstyle at the back of her head, towering about three feet high, swaying along the wall, and I did not know what she was doing. I urgently called out to Zhang, but she disappeared.

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    In all writing, it is like riding a swift steed — though it may possess a wild and unrestrained spirit, one must control it with bit and bridle, and must not let it run wild, straying from the proper path or indulging in aimless wanderings.

    From ancient times, wise kings and sage emperors still required diligent study; how much more so, then, for common people! This matter is found throughout the classics and histories. I cannot elaborate further, but I will briefly mention a few essential points of recent times in order to enlighten you.

    As for writings, their origin lies in the Five Classics: edicts, memorials, and military proclamations arise from the Shujing (Book of Documents); expository and argumentative writings arise from the Yijing (Book of Changes); songs, odes, and praises arise from the Shijing (Book of Poetry); ritual sacrifices, mourning, and elegies arise from the Liji (Book of Rites); memorials, admonitions, and inscriptions arise from the Chunqiu (Spring and Autumn Annals). Court regulations, military oaths and decrees, the proclamation of benevolence and righteousness, the elucidation of virtue and merit, governing the people and establishing the state—all are indispensable. As for cultivating the mind and spirit, offering gentle admonition in a relaxed manner, and savoring the flavor of such writings, these too are pleasant pursuits. When one has spare energy, then one may study them.

    When I was young, I played in the garden with my wet nurse, Zhang. As she sat on the steps, I entered an empty room alone and saw a short woman with her hair styled in a modern treasure chest bun, with a swallow-tail hairstyle at the back of her head, towering about three feet high, swaying along the wall, and I did not know what she was doing. I urgently called out to Zhang, but she disappeared.

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    In all writing, it is like riding a swift steed — though it may possess a wild and unrestrained spirit, one must control it with bit and bridle, and must not let it run wild, straying from the proper path or indulging in aimless wanderings.

    From ancient times, wise kings and sage emperors still required diligent study; how much more so, then, for common people! This matter is found throughout the classics and histories. I cannot elaborate further, but I will briefly mention a few essential points of recent times in order to enlighten you.

    As for writings, their origin lies in the Five Classics: edicts, memorials, and military proclamations arise from the Shujing (Book of Documents); expository and argumentative writings arise from the Yijing (Book of Changes); songs, odes, and praises arise from the Shijing (Book of Poetry); ritual sacrifices, mourning, and elegies arise from the Liji (Book of Rites); memorials, admonitions, and inscriptions arise from the Chunqiu (Spring and Autumn Annals). Court regulations, military oaths and decrees, the proclamation of benevolence and righteousness, the elucidation of virtue and merit, governing the people and establishing the state—all are indispensable. As for cultivating the mind and spirit, offering gentle admonition in a relaxed manner, and savoring the flavor of such writings, these too are pleasant pursuits. When one has spare energy, then one may study them.

    When I was young, I played in the garden with my wet nurse, Zhang. As she sat on the steps, I entered an empty room alone and saw a short woman with her hair styled in a modern treasure chest bun, with a swallow-tail hairstyle at the back of her head, towering about three feet high, swaying along the wall, and I did not know what she was doing. I urgently called out to Zhang, but she disappeared.

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    In all writing, it is like riding a swift

    190

    铁佛
    　　东光县铁佛，旁配黄铜罗汉十八尊，灿烂如金。夜有群盗窃取一尊，载以小车。将行，忽迷道路，极力推挽，奔波颇远。及天明，则仍在庙中，为僧见，执送有司。
    雷报某甲，都中富室仆也。主人死，遗产甚多。家中惟主母与少主，甫四五岁，一切家事俱付仆经纪。甲欺其孤寡，盗卖田产，怀赀遁去，与其党往关外贩猪。途遇暴雨，趋避林麓。忽巨雷作响，声振山谷，猪惊窜千百头，一时星散，无一存者。甲等力莫能追，恼恨而返，赤贫如昔。闻者快之。

    Explanation

    190

    The Iron Buddha
    In Dongguang County, there stands an iron Buddha statue, accompanied by eighteen brass statues of arhats, shining like gold. One night, a group of thieves stole one of the statues and carried it on a small cart. As they were about to leave, they suddenly lost their way, pushing and pulling with all their might, traveling quite a distance. By morning, the statue was still in the temple, and the monks saw it, taking it to the authorities.

    The story of the lightning retribution of someone named Jia, who was a servant in a wealthy household in the capital. After the master died, there was a large inheritance. In the household, there was only the master's wife and a young master, just four or five years old. All household affairs were entrusted to the servant. Jia took advantage of the widow and the young master, selling the land, took the money and fled, going to the border regions with his gang to trade pigs. On the way, they encountered a heavy rain and sought shelter in the woods. Suddenly, a huge thunderclap sounded, shaking the mountains and valleys. The pigs were startled and scattered, none remaining. Jia and his gang could not catch the pigs, returning in anger and poverty as before. Those who heard about it were glad.

    191

    魅戏
    　　邑西南有小村共四十余户。每晚，村中小儿群戏月下，来聚甚伙。一村叟怪之，曰：“吾村小儿历历可数，何群儿多也？”次夕留意察阅，相识者十九人。再视诸童，顿杳。
    怪风
    　　武强张某，耕于长堤。忽旋风蔽天，尘沙中有黑白二巨蟒，粗皆如筒，夭矫相缠。张惊欲遁，风已骤至，卷身风内，颠倒随之。地上荆棘刺肤，痛不可耐。忽摸得一树，急抱之，风遂过，惊定审视，去堤里许。
    蓝衣媪
    　　予故居赁与夏姓。夏将迁，移物新第，留一老仆看守余物。夜坐息灯，月朗如昼，见堂中立一蓝衣老妪，白发椎髻，相顾而笑。问为谁，不答，笑如故。身顿矮，渐渐缩入地中，仅露其首，笑容犹可掬也。仆毛发俱悚。未几，金光如电，而媪乌有矣。

    Explanation

    191

    Translation

    魅戏
    In the southwest of the town, there is a small village with about forty households. Every evening, the children of the village gather to play under the moonlight, and many of them come together. An old villager found this strange and said, "The children in our village can be counted one by one, why are there so many children here?" The next evening, he paid close attention and recognized nineteen of the children. When he looked again, the children suddenly disappeared.

    怪风
    Zhang, from Wuqiang, was farming on a long dike. Suddenly, a whirlwind covered the sky, and in the dust and sand, two large black and white pythons appeared, both as thick as tubes, twisting and entwining each other. Zhang was so scared that he wanted to run away, but the wind suddenly arrived, catching him up and tossing him about. The ground was covered with thorns that pricked his skin, causing unbearable pain. Suddenly, he felt a tree and quickly grabbed onto it. The wind then passed, and he calmed down, looking around carefully. He found himself about a li (about 500 meters) away from the dike.

    蓝衣媪
    My former residence was rented to someone surnamed Xia. Xia was about to move and was transferring things to a new house, leaving one old servant to guard the remaining items. One night, after turning off the lights, the moonlight was as bright as daylight. The servant saw an old woman dressed in blue standing in the hall, with white hair in a bun. They looked at each other and smiled. When the servant asked who she was, she did not answer and continued to smile. Her body suddenly became shorter, and she gradually sank into the ground, leaving only her head visible. Her smile was still visible. The servant was very frightened. Shortly after, a golden light like lightning appeared, and the old woman had disappeared.

    192

    申某
    　　申某，燕人，游幕于闽。夜与友斗叶子，局散，各归寝室。申至己斋，门扃闭，室中灯影煌煌。怪而自窗窥视，有一无首妇人，置首案上，双手理发。急返故处，见三友仍在灯前赌戏，骇述其异。邀众往视。众笑曰：“君何少见多怪，我等尽能之。”于是以手承颊，各摘其头，置几上。申惊丧魂魄，飞出衙中。天明贼至，阖署遇难，申独免。
    疑案
    　　山左某县妇，传者忘其姓氏。薄暮自母家归，跨黑卫，弟负幞从诸后。路经山中，妇思小解，乃遣弟牵驴俟诸道左，自往丛树深处溺焉。行数武，见老松怪石，环一荒冢，地甚幽僻。溺毕，束衣，忽失其裤，遍觅殊无踪迹。幸长衫蔽体，尚不尽露庐山。匆匆跨卫而归，私告诸夫。夫惊颜如土，云：“予知之，予知之。”摇手戒其勿宣。妇不敢言，终莫解其何故。及夜，扃户同寝。夫酣睡，鼻息如雷，妇恐怖不寐。忽有物震床作响，如坠巨石。

    Explanation

    192

    Shen
    　　Shen, a man from Yan, was traveling as a guest official in Fujian. One night, he played a card game with his friends, and after the game ended, they each returned to their own rooms. Shen arrived at his own room to find the door locked and the bright light of the lamp shining inside. He found it strange and peeked through the window, seeing a woman without a head, placing her head on the table, and combing her hair with both hands. He rushed back to his original place, only to find his three friends still gambling at the lamp. He was shocked and told them about the strange sight. He invited his friends to see it. The friends laughed and said, "How easily surprised you are, we can all do it." Then they placed their hands on their cheeks, removed their heads, and placed them on the table. Shen was so shocked that he fled out of the government office. The next morning, bandits arrived, and all the officials in the office perished, but Shen was the only one to survive.
    Suspicious Case
    　　A woman from a certain county in the eastern mountains, whose name is forgotten, returned from her mother's house at dusk, riding a black donkey, with her younger brother following behind carrying her luggage. As she traveled through the mountains, she thought she needed to relieve herself and sent her younger brother to hold the donkey on the side of the road while she went deep into the dense forest. After walking a few steps, she saw old pines, strange stones, and a desolate grave, in a very secluded and remote place. After finishing relieving herself, she put on her clothes, and suddenly lost her pants, searching everywhere but finding no trace. Fortunately, her long robe covered her body, still not fully exposing her body. She rushed back on the donkey and secretly told her husband. Her husband looked terrified and pale, saying he knew, and warned her not to tell anyone. The woman dared not speak, still not understanding why. That night, they locked the door and went to bed together. Her husband was sound asleep, snoring like thunder, while the woman was terrified and could not sleep. Suddenly, something shook the bed and made a loud noise, like a huge stone falling.

    193

    急呼夫，莫应，烛之，见利刃如霜，贯夫胸上，牢不可拔。骇极大号，家人奔至，妇为启扃。众视门窗并无盗迹，疑妇所杀，鸣于官。拘妇讯质，妇以失裤事对。官诣验其处，垒垒高冢，封树俨然。拘墓主问故，云有弱女夭亡，瘗此已近十年。每春秋遣人扫墓，他不知也。官告以故，欲发其冢，墓主争辩不许，强而后可。既破棺，棺中并无女尸，一少年和尚，赤身仰卧，身覆红裤，即妇所失之物也。胸上插一利匕首，血迹殷湿如新刺者。众皆愣然，细访远近禅院，并云无是僧徒。而亦无报告者。重重冤苦，事殊怪异，遂悬为疑案。

    Explanation

    193

    He shouted urgently for his husband, but there was no response. He lit a candle to see, and saw a sharp blade like frost piercing his husband's chest, firmly stuck and unable to be pulled out. Terrified, he let out a loud cry, and the family members rushed to the scene. The wife opened the door. Everyone checked the windows and doors but found no signs of a thief. They suspected the wife had killed him and reported it to the authorities. The wife was arrested and interrogated, and she responded with the incident of losing her skirt. The official went to inspect the place, where he found a high mound of graves with neatly arranged tombstones. He arrested the tomb owner and asked about the reason. The tomb owner said there was a young girl who died young and was buried there for nearly ten years. Every spring and autumn, someone was sent to sweep the tomb, but the tomb owner did not know about the recent events. The official told him about the case and wanted to open the tomb. The tomb owner argued against it, but only after being forced did he allow it. After breaking open the coffin, there was no female corpse inside; instead, there was a young monk, naked and lying on his back, wearing a red skirt, which was the skirt the wife had lost. A sharp knife was stuck in his chest, and the blood was fresh and wet like it had just been stabbed. Everyone was stunned, and they carefully investigated nearby and distant temples, but all said there was no such monk. There were also no reports of any such incident. Layer upon layer of injustice and suffering, the matter was extremely strange and unusual, so it remained an unsolved mystery.

    194

    树妖
    　　赵魁者，武强县学门斗也。夜自小范归，御骡车，云月朦胧，微可辨物。忽林中出一物，状如牛而白色，奔突车前。辕骡骇伏。物直冲车而舞，魁击以鞭，鞭辗转不能着。相持既久，鸡鸣始遁入林中而杳，殆树精也。
    金目怪
    　　孟君东园，客冀州，夜休旅馆。忽屋中旋风骤起，一物长如素练，随风旋舞不休。半晌，自窗棂出，风亦飕然随之。俄又从窗左棂中钻入，双目金色，明朗如灯。直逼卧榻。孟投以枕，物惊而遁。

    Explanation

    194

    Tree Spirit

    Zhao Kui, a gatekeeper at the school in Wuchang County, returned home one night from Xiaofan riding in a mule-drawn carriage. The moon and clouds were hazy, barely allowing him to discern objects. Suddenly, a creature emerged from the forest, resembling a white cow, and charged directly in front of the carriage. The mule at the front of the carriage was startled and cowered. The creature charged at the carriage and danced around it, while Zhao struck it with his whip, which circled around but could not hit the creature. They held their ground for a long time, until the rooster crowed, and the creature fled into the forest and disappeared, likely a tree spirit.

    Golden-Eyed Monster

    Meng Jun, a guest at the eastern garden, stayed in an inn in Jizhou one night. Suddenly, a whirlwind arose in the room, and a creature as long and white as a piece of silk danced and spun with the wind without stopping. After a while, it emerged from the window lattice, and the wind followed it with a whistling sound. Soon, it re-entered through the left window lattice, with its eyes bright and clear like a lamp. It approached the bed directly. Meng threw a pillow at it, and the creature was startled and fled.

    195

    狐仙
    　　沧州王氏，家多狐仙，平昔供奉甚谨。有客寄其厅，主人谆戒备至。客笑不信，诃颇狎虐。主人摇手有怯色，使馆僮来伴寝。客以胆气自矜，力辞去。主人归，客甫就枕，便听堂中作响。忽寝门自辟，有健男四人猝入，曰：“毁谤我辈，聊惩创之。”相与登床，各执客一体，曳至庭中，向空力掷，高过屋脊，飘然若驾云雾。将及地，四人以臂承之，得不坠。如是三四作，虽未跌损，然心胆惊落矣。忽阶上立一叟，白须垂胸，曰：“且勿且勿。够彼消受矣。”置客地上，相与鼓掌而散。客呕吐昏晕，殆不知人。晓为仆人救苏，备言其故。
    鼠技
    　　予友人家多鼠，厨间食物多为所啖。有鸡子数枚，亦失去。疑仆食之，仆辩其无。因复以数卵置案上，夜假寐以观之。有二鼠登案，一鼠抱卵仰卧，护以四足，一鼠衔其尾而倒曳之，从案落杌，从杌落地，卵无少损，旋曳之入穴而去。物之智亦巧矣哉！

    Explanation

    195

    Translation:

    Fox Spirits
    The Wang family of Cangzhou had many fox spirits, and they had always served and worshipped them with great respect. A guest stayed in their hall, and the host warned him thoroughly and prepared carefully. The guest laughed and did not believe it, treating the matter rather roughly. The host shook his head with a look of fear and sent a young servant to accompany him to sleep. The guest boasted of his own courage and strongly refused. After the host left, the guest just lay down to sleep when he suddenly heard noises in the hall. Suddenly, the bedroom door opened by itself, and four strong men rushed in, saying, "You have defamed us; we will punish and correct you." They climbed onto the bed, each grabbing one part of the guest, and dragged him to the courtyard, throwing him into the air with great force, higher than the roof ridge, as if riding on clouds and mist. As he was about to hit the ground, the four men caught him with their arms, preventing him from falling. This happened three or four times, and although he was not injured, his heart and spirit were terrified. Suddenly, an old man stood on the steps, with white hair hanging down to his chest, and said, "Do not do it, do not do it. That is enough for him to bear." He placed the guest on the ground, and they all clapped their hands and left. The guest vomited and fainted, nearly losing consciousness. In the morning, he was saved by the servant and told the whole story.

    Mouse Skill
    My friend’s house had many mice, and many of the foods in the kitchen were eaten by them. There were several eggs that also went missing. My friend suspected the servant had eaten them, but the servant denied it. My friend then placed a few more eggs on the table and pretended to sleep to watch them. Two mice climbed onto the table, one mouse held the egg and lay on its back, protecting it with its four feet, while the other mouse grabbed its tail and pulled it down, causing the egg to fall from the table to a stool, and then from the stool to the ground. The egg was not damaged at all, and the mice quickly dragged it into their hole and left. How clever and skillful these creatures are!

    196

    庞氏
