    　　常熟赵抚厅妻庞氏，通诗书，精武技。赵宠任之。性奇悍，反目辄便痛楚，于是畏如虎狼。有四五僚友，不愤其事，欲舌战而折服之。赵苦劝不听，乃结伴至其家。刺入，庞问何事，赵不敢隐，以实对。速客甫入其庭，妻自室出，雄健胜于伟男，指客曰：“有屁快放！”众皆愕然。一豪客曰：“因何日挞尊夫？”曰：“结交懦汉，理合重惩。”客曰：“我何尝懦？”庞遽持白梃逐客，众如鸟散。一年迈马姓者，走稍迟，为妇所擒，裂裤痛撞其后庭，叫苦乞免。妇释之，大笑曰：“老马反为驹，不顾其后矣。”自此无敢作说客者。又数年，暴虐益甚。一日，谓其夫曰：“汝前生虐我，我今生报之，怨已解矣。”登床坦卧。众视妇顶上如烟，绕床三匝，至门，化为青面夜叉，腾空飞去。试扪妇体，已冷如冰，而目瞑矣。

    Explanation

    196

    Pang Shi

    Pang Shi, the wife of Zhao Fu Ting from Changshu, was well-versed in poetry and books and skilled in martial arts. Zhao Fu Ting indulged her. She was unusually fierce, and whenever they turned against each other, it would cause pain, so he feared her like a tiger or wolf. Four or five colleagues, dissatisfied with the situation, wanted to defeat her in argument and persuade her. Zhao Fu Ting tried to dissuade them, but they did not listen, so they formed a group and went to her house. As soon as they entered, Pang Shi asked what they were there for. Zhao Fu Ting did not hide anything and told her the truth. As soon as the guests entered her courtyard, Pang Shi came out of her room, strong and robust, surpassing even great men. She pointed at the guests and said, "Get to the point quickly!" They were all astonished. One bold guest said, "Why did you beat your husband?" She replied, "Associating with weaklings, which deserves severe punishment." The guest said, "I am not weak!" Pang Shi immediately grabbed a white stick and chased the guest away, and the crowd scattered like birds. An elderly man surnamed Ma, who walked slower, was caught by her, and she tore off his pants and hit him on the buttocks, making him cry out in pain, begging for mercy. She released him and laughed, saying, "An old horse acts like a young one, not looking behind." From then on, no one dared to act as a mediator. After several more years, her brutality and cruelty increased even more. One day, she told her husband, "You mistreated me in a past life, and I am repaying you in this life. Our grudge is now resolved." She climbed onto the bed and lay down openly. Everyone saw that her head was like smoke, circling the bed three times, until it reached the door, where she transformed into a blue-faced demon and flew away. When they touched her body, it was already cold as ice, and her eyes were closed.

    197

    冷香堂
    　　都中某官，好狭斜游。偶与友饮于酒楼，席终，乘醉独往。街市弯环，迥非熟路。至一处，门悬巨灯，署“冷香堂”，欣然独入，一短躯人前导，暗中莫辨颜色。及入室，床上灯火荧然，一女子面壁卧。某曰：“有人来，卧榻尚酣睡耶？”女欠呻起，面貌黄瘦，类久病人，谓某曰：“姑少坐，即唤姊妹辈来。”旋听屟响，一女子搴帘入，身高及床，头大如斗，双目炯炯，光焰四射。俄又来一女，身高如竿，头小如盏，向床并立。某惊晕去。方某之离酒楼也，其仆随之，转盼失所在。寻访几遍，不可得。天晓，见某卧丛冢中，气如丝，灌以姜汁始苏。言之历历。

    Explanation

    197

    Cold Fragrance Hall

    An official in the capital had a penchant for seedy encounters. One day, he happened to drink with a friend at a tavern. After the meal, he became drunk and went off alone. The streets were winding and unfamiliar, far from any familiar path. He arrived at a place where a large lantern hung outside the door, inscribed with the words "Cold Fragrance Hall." He entered eagerly, with a short man leading the way, though he could not discern his features in the darkness. Upon entering the room, he saw a dim light on the bed, and a woman lying face down. He said, "Someone has come, and you're still sleeping soundly?" The woman groaned and sat up, her face pale and emaciated, looking like a long-term patient. She told him, "Please wait a moment, I will call my sisters." Soon, the sound of footsteps was heard, and a woman pulled back the curtain and entered. She was tall enough to reach the bed, with a head as large as a basket, and her eyes gleamed brightly, radiating light. Shortly after, another woman came in, standing by the bed. She was as tall as a pole, with a head as small as a cup. The official was so shocked that he fainted. When he left the tavern, his servant followed him, but he soon disappeared. They searched everywhere but could not find him. The next morning, they found him lying in a cluster of graves, his breath as thin as a thread. They revived him by pouring ginger juice into his mouth. He recounted everything in detail.

    198

    醉茶子曰：柳巷花街，罕觏佳丽，闻者皆以吾言为矫也。不知冶态妖容，其去鬼正不相远，况鬼而为妓者乎？惊焉致死，犹胜溺焉致死多矣。
    猬怪
    　　吾乡士人，春夜读书，闻窗外簌簌叶响。视之，二猬旋转作风，入后院去。士随之，转过墙，化为二老叟，须发苍然，身躯短矮，相顾而笑。士骇问其谁，忽失所在。

    Explanation

    198

    Zuicha Zi said: In the red-light district, beautiful women are rarely seen, and those who hear this all think my words are exaggerated. But they do not know that the seductive and beautiful appearance of these women is not far from being a ghost, how much more so if it were a ghost turned into a prostitute? Dying from surprise is still better than dying from drowning.

    A Strange Event

    A scholar from my hometown was reading at night in spring when he heard the sound of leaves rustling outside the window. He looked out and saw two hedgehogs spinning like a wind, entering the back garden. The scholar followed them, turned the corner of the wall, and they transformed into two old men. They had white beards and hair, short and stocky bodies, and looked at each other and laughed. The scholar was startled and asked who they were, but they suddenly disappeared.

    199

    蛇卵
    　　曲逆农夫，于野田拾一巨卵，如鹅子而五色，备爱而使鸡伏之，壳破，产一小赤蛇，蜿蜒间，即长数尺，健与鸡斗。急杀之，血碧而腥。或云蛟之属也。
    金龟
    　　予友赵印昙幼时，掘地得一物，如龟，方厚四五寸许，遍体金色，炫灿有光，四足齐动。惊顾已杳，或云是太岁也。
    四川女
    　　妇人有阴梃阴茄之说，奇而不奇也。梅军门言蜀中某甲，新婚三日，与妇交，势截然断，血流不止而毙。请官检验，仵云：“伤非刀剪，一似口嚼之者。”宰比妇，妇泣云：“交欢之际，因龁断之。”宰忍笑而问曰：“岂含以口乎？”妇忸怩曰：“非也。”再问不答。遣官媪验其阴，则唇包白齿，左右嶙嶙。怪而探之以指，牙陡然合，指几啮而为两。急白于官，官重笞而释之。人妖之奇，乃至如此，即使善战者御之，当亦一败涂地。
    　　醉茶子曰：韩诗云“羡君齿牙牢且洁，大肉硬饼如刀截。”试使喂以饼肉，定如老饕之大嚼也。一笑。

    Explanation

    199

    Snake Egg

    A farmer from Guicai found a large, multicolored egg in a field, about the size of a goose egg. He cherished it and had a hen sit on it. When the shell broke, a small red snake emerged, winding and twisting, and quickly grew to several feet long, fiercely fighting with the hen. They killed it immediately; its blood was greenish-blue and pungent. Some said it was of the species of the dragon.

    Golden Turtle

    When my friend Zhao Yintan was young, he dug up an object from the ground that resembled a turtle, about four to five inches wide and thick, entirely golden, dazzling and radiant, with all four limbs moving together. It disappeared in an instant when they looked at it, and some said it was the mythical creature tai sui.

    Woman from Sichuan

    There is a folk belief that women have a penis-like organ, which is both strange and not so strange. According to General Mei, a man from Sichuan married a woman three days after their wedding. During their intimate encounter, her genitalia suddenly severed, causing him to bleed profusely and die. The authorities examined the case, and the forensic expert said, "The wound was not made by a knife or scissors; it looked as if it were bitten." The magistrate questioned the woman, who wept and said, "During our intimate moment, it was bitten off." The magistrate tried to suppress his laughter and asked, "Did you bite it with your mouth?" The woman blushed and said, "No." When asked again, she remained silent. The magistrate then sent an official woman to examine her, and she found that the woman's genitalia had white teeth embedded in the lips, which were bumpy and uneven. The official woman was startled and probed with her finger, and the teeth suddenly closed, almost biting her finger in two. She quickly reported this to the magistrate, who severely flogged the woman and released her. The extraordinariness of this case, combining human and supernatural elements, is such that even a skilled warrior would be defeated by it.

    Tea-Drinking Scholar Said: According to the Han Shih (Han Poetry), "I envy your strong and clean teeth, and how you cut through large pieces of meat and hard bread with ease." If one were to feed such a person with bread and meat, they would certainly eat voraciously like a glutton. A smile.

    200

    豕舞
    　　邑双忠庙，有穿心阁临街，其下可通往来。陈某者，夜醉归，远见阁上灯烛辉煌，即近则仍黑暗，知为狐仙，欲观其异，乘醉登阁。既上，寂无一物，顿悔欲下，忽楼门中灯光射入，乃屏息伏暗陬。俄有俊仆十余人，挑莲炬入，展席设座，肴酒纷陈。主人导十余客，揖座欢饮，谓仆曰：“招朱家僮来作剧。”仆应诺下楼，引二狡童，婉媚皆如好女，袱出朱袍乌纱二，装饰颇雅，至座前。旁二客吹笙鼓簧，童舞蹈应节。有客善琵琶者，令朱歌而愿和之。二童若不解。又一客笑曰：“如此蠢动，纵勉强能舞，何能歌也。”主人惭，以拳击二童，嗥然长号而仆，化为二豕，冠落地上，硕腹彭彭，犹着朱袍。陈视之不觉失笑，一响，则人物俱杳。细视，东方既白，身踆栏外瓦上，术檐咋咋作响欲折，转侧即堕。狂叫僧至，缘梯下之。尝谓人曰：“虽惊怖欲死，然狐之作剧，亦可观也。”

    Explanation

    200

    A Pig Dance

    At the Double Loyalty Temple in the town, there is a pavilion called the Through-the-Middle Pavilion that spans the street, with space beneath it for passage. One night, a man named Chen returned home drunk and saw from a distance that the pavilion was illuminated by bright lights. As he approached, however, it remained dark. Knowing this was the work of a fox spirit, he wanted to see the strange spectacle and, still drunk, climbed up to the pavilion. Once inside, he found nothing but silence and regretted his decision to descend. Suddenly, a beam of light shot through the door, and he held his breath, crouching in the darkest corner. Soon, more than ten handsome servants entered, carrying lotus torches, and set up tables and chairs. The host guided more than ten guests to their seats and began a joyful drinking session. He told the servants, "Call the young boys from the Zhu family to perform a trick." The servants nodded and went downstairs, leading two mischievous boys who were charming and beautiful like good girls. They brought out two red robes and black hats, which were elegantly decorated, and placed them before the guests. Two other guests played the sheng and huqin, and the boys danced in time to the music. One of the guests, skilled at the pipa, asked the boys to sing, promising to accompany them. The boys seemed confused. Another guest laughed and said, "Such clumsy movements, even if they force themselves to dance, how could they possibly sing?" The host felt ashamed and hit the boys with his fist. They let out a loud cry and collapsed, transforming into two pigs. Their hats fell to the ground, and they had large, swollen bellies, still wearing the red robes. Chen couldn't help but laugh, and in an instant, all the people and objects disappeared. When he looked closely, the eastern sky was already bright. He found himself standing on the tiles outside the railing, with the eaves creaking and threatening to collapse. He was on the verge of falling. A panicked monk arrived and climbed down the ladder to rescue him. Later, Chen told others, "Although I was terrified and felt like I was about to die, the fox's trick was quite amusing."

    201

    醉茶子曰：携妓偕优，狂饮拇战，当局者乐此不疲，而旁观者早见哂矣。况客之雅谑，犹不如狐妓之貌，艺更不如猪哉？洵可笑也。
    金鸡
    　　长随某，淡泊寡营，不类长安道人。自言昔年寓保定客店，困苦异常，主人每欲逐之。婉言相求，使居后院空室。月明鉴物，愁思未眠，闻窗外蹴践声。视之，一五彩金鸡，雄健无比，以为店中所畜也，不之怪。既而每夕必出，及晓则隐。遂待其来，拔刀逐之，至墙下而没。掘其下，得黄金一锭重五十两，藏诸衣囊，负装而归。觉左手微痛，乃逐鸡时刀所误伤，至家疮大作，几至陨命。卖金调理，金尽疾僡，始信财有定数，不可妄求也。
    狐祟
    　　信都刘生，家多狐祟，器物往往自失去。一夜有巨豕二头，奔突院中。刘家固无是畜，怪而击之以砖，遂倒，其一逸去。细视，非豕，乃苇萎，实之以麦，即仓中物也。再察仓中，先二麦萎。皆狐之幻术也。

    Explanation

    201

    The Drunken Tea Master said: Bringing courtesans and entertainers together, drunkenly drinking and thumb-fighting, those involved find it endlessly enjoyable, while those watching have long been ridiculed. As for the refined jests of guests, they are even less impressive than the appearance of a fox courtesan, and their artistry is even worse than a pig. Indeed, it is laughable.

    Golden Rooster
    A retainer of a certain household, simple and unambitious, was unlike a person from Chang'an. He said that in the past he stayed at a guesthouse in Baoding, where he endured extreme hardship. The owner often wanted to drive him away. He pleaded gently, and was allowed to stay in a vacant room in the back courtyard. Under the moonlight, which revealed objects, he was unable to sleep due to his sorrowful thoughts. He heard a sound of trampling outside the window. Looking out, he saw a five-colored golden rooster, unmatched in strength and vigor, and assumed it was something kept by the inn, finding it not strange. Afterward, it would surely appear every night, and would disappear by dawn. So he waited for it to come, drew a knife to chase it, and it disappeared at the wall. He dug underneath, found a golden ingot weighing fifty taels, hid it in his clothes, and carried his belongings back. He felt a slight pain in his left hand, which was an accidental injury from the knife when chasing the rooster. Upon returning home, the wound became severe, almost leading to death. He sold the gold to treat the wound, but the gold was spent, and the illness worsened. Finally, he believed that wealth has its limits and cannot be recklessly sought.

    Fox Haunting
    Mr. Liu from Xindu, whose home was often haunted by foxes, found that objects often disappeared on their own. One night, two large pigs appeared, rushing through the courtyard. Mr. Liu's family did not have such animals, so he found it strange and hit it with a brick, causing it to fall down, with one escaping. Upon closer inspection, it was not a pig but a reed bundle, filled with wheat, which was from the granary. Upon further inspection of the granary, there were two more wheat bundles. All were foxes' illusions.

    202

    蛛怪
    　　汤阴某氏女，晚坐庭中，见一火球从檐落，辗转不见。旋有一美少年，神采俊逸，向女调笑，遂与私合。既久，秘不告人。一夕，少年谓女曰：“与卿交好，不图竟遭天怒。明午暴雨，有大蜘蛛伏窗上者，即我是也。如不忘旧好，急以溺器掷空际，可免此劫。”言毕而去。女告母，母衔恨之，豫将诸器藏于秘所。至午，雷雨大作，果有蜘蛛如盘，从空堕伏窗棂上，痴若木鸡，不敢少动。女急寻秽物，猝不可得。忽震雷直击，物毙于地，天亦顿晴。
    黄老
    　　邑乔君书年，字鹭汀，为山左县丞，御贼战殁，灵车归里。至静邑唐官屯镇，有仆梦一青衣人，仿佛官隶。询之，云：“予名黄老，天津郡署役也。奉命迎大人柩。”醒而异之。及至津，谒城隍祠，见山门中站像，酷似梦中所见，询诸道士，果名黄老。盖黄固府隶，生时有德，死而为神者也。

    Explanation

    202

    Translation

    蛛怪

    A woman from a family in Tangyin was sitting in the courtyard late in the evening when she saw a fireball fall from the eaves, which then disappeared after rolling around. Soon after, a handsome young man appeared, with an excellent spirit and graceful demeanor. He flirted with the girl, and they had a secret relationship. Over time, they kept it a secret from others. One night, the young man told the girl, "I have been your friend, but I did not expect to suffer the wrath of heaven. Tomorrow at noon, there will be a heavy rainstorm, and a large spider will sit on the window. That will be me. If you do not forget our past friendship, quickly throw a urinal into the air, and you can avoid this calamity." After saying this, he left. The girl told her mother, who harbored resentment against him and secretly hid all the urinals in a hidden place. By noon, a heavy rainstorm broke out, and indeed, a large spider, as big as a tray, fell from the sky and sat motionless on the window, like a wooden chicken, not daring to move. The girl hurriedly searched for something dirty but could not find it in a hurry. Suddenly, a thunderbolt struck, and the spider was killed on the ground, and the sky cleared up immediately.

    黄老

    In the town of Tangguantun in Jing County, there was a county magistrate named Qiao Junshu, with the courtesy name Lutin, who served as a county magistrate in Shandong Province. He died in battle against bandits, and his funeral carriage returned to his hometown. On their way to Tangguantun Town, one of his servants dreamed of a man in green clothes, who resembled an official's servant. When asked, the man said, "My name is Huang Lao, and I am a servant in the government office in Tianjin. I was sent to escort the coffin of the deceased." The servant found this strange upon waking. Upon reaching Tianjin, they visited the City God Temple and saw a statue in the mountain gate that looked very similar to what the servant had seen in his dream. They asked the Taoist priests, and it turned out that the statue was indeed named Huang Lao. Huang was originally a servant in the government office, had virtue in life, and became a god after death.

    203

    醉茶子曰：有善可称，死且不没。况士大夫生为河岳，殁为日星者哉!人生斯世，宜自勉矣。
    泥魃
    　　七里海边有魅曰泥魃，状如婴孩，高二尺许，通体红色。每以湿泥投入，中之辄病。畏金铁，闻声即退，亦水鬼之类也。
    　　又有羊魃，状如小羊，长数寸，夜出水边寻食，不为人害。乃羊骨浸水多年，感天地之精气而成者也。
    东光女
    　　东光某甲，与村中女子有私，两情欢悦，订以婚娶。甲父为聘邻村女，亦少好，甲与女绝。一日遇于隘巷，女曰：“得新忘旧，君何太忍！”捽至秘所，怨詈不休。甲婉言再四，女怒稍解，诱与交合。女酸泪盈盈，搦其阳曰：“侬之至宝，他人抿之，殊可恼也。”阴以刀藏枕底，事讫，猝握而奄割之。鸡飞卵落，甲负痛而遁。女获禽，如得拱璧，藏诸荷囊，常佩于身，暇时取出玩视，持其柄而摇之，则两旁耳环自击。

    Explanation

    203

    醉茶子曰：有善可称，死且不没。况士大夫生为河岳，殁为日星者哉!人生斯世，宜自勉矣。
    The sage said: If one has good deeds to be praised, even death cannot erase them. How much more so for scholars and officials, who in life are like rivers and mountains, and after death are like the sun and stars! In this world, one should strive hard.

    泥魃
    Seven Li's seaside has a demon called Mud Demon. It looks like a baby, about two feet tall, entirely red. It often throws wet mud, causing illness upon contact. It fears metal, retreating at the sound, and is also a type of water ghost.

    又有羊魃，状如小羊，长数寸，夜出水边寻食，不为人害。乃羊骨浸水多年，感天地之精气而成者也。
    There is also a Sheep Demon, which looks like a small sheep, a few inches long. It comes out at night to the water's edge to find food, does not harm people. It is formed by a sheep's bone soaking in water for many years, absorbing the essence of heaven and earth.

    东光女
    A man from Dongguang named Jia had an affair with a village woman. They were both happy and agreed to marry. His father arranged a marriage with a girl from a neighboring village, who was also young and good-looking. Jia broke off the relationship with her. One day, they met in a narrow alley. The woman said, "Having a new one, you forget the old one, how cruel are you!" She dragged him to a secret place, kept cursing him. Jia pleaded with her repeatedly, and her anger gradually subsided. She enticed him to have sexual intercourse. She shed bitter tears, holding his penis and saying, "Your most precious thing, if others touch it, it's very annoying." She secretly hid a knife under the pillow. After the act, she suddenly grasped it and cut it off. Like a chicken flying away and an egg falling, he fled in pain. She got what she wanted, like obtaining a precious jade, she kept it in a lotus bag, always wearing it. When she had free time, she took it out to play with, holding the handle and shaking it, the earrings on both sides would strike each other.

    204

    初，甲狼狈归家，卧床不起，妻问之，不答。血殷席褥，寻毙。翁讼于官，捉凶未获，常比役。役有至戚某，卖饧村巷，至一家，门前有三四女郎游戏。中一少长者艳无比，众呼为姊，向女索钱买饴。女言其无，众云：“荷囊充物如许，何得云无耶？”乃掣其肘而强搜之。既探出，则强强之鹊，臭味已差池矣。众不识，骇曰：“留此败肉，尚堪食耶？”委之于地。女红晕于颊，急拾而藏之，众乃散去。某窥其状，述诸役，役禀于官。拘去一讯，尽得其实，置之于法。
    　　醉茶子曰：爱其阳而割之，与爱其花而折之者无异。当操刀一试，未必非深于情也。然花折而树固无伤，阳亡则人即寻毙。女子之痴且妒，殊可怜而可恨也。独是谋杀戏杀，罪有轻重，我不知为之宰者，以何法处之。
    黑妖
    　　邑刘生，忘其名字。夜自塾归，遇一物如巨猿，鹰目炫金，牙粲白，周身黑毛垂三寸许。见刘奋爪欲攫。刘返奔，物追之。街犬惊吠，物越垣登屋而去。

    Explanation

    204

    Initial Translation

    At first, Jia returned home in a disarrayed state, lying in bed unable to get up. His wife asked him about it, but he did not respond. The blood was so profuse that it stained the bedding, and soon he died. The old man filed a complaint with the authorities, but the culprit was not caught, and the case was often compared to similar ones. One of the official's close relatives, who sold sweets in the village lanes, came to a house where there were several young ladies playing. Among them, one who was slightly older was incomparably beautiful, and the others called her "sister." They asked the girl for money to buy candy. The girl said she had none, and the others said, "Your bag is so full, how can you say you have nothing?" They grabbed her arm and forcibly searched her. When they pulled out the object, it was a dead bird with a strange odor. The crowd did not recognize it and exclaimed, "Is this rotten meat still edible?" They left it on the ground. The girl blushed and quickly picked it up and hid it. The crowd then dispersed. The relative observed the situation, reported it to the official, and the official reported it to the authorities. They detained and interrogated the girl, obtained the truth, and punished her according to the law.

    The Drunken Tea Master said: Loving and then cutting it off, it is no different from loving and then breaking off a flower. To take a knife and try, it may not be a deep expression of emotion. But breaking a flower does not harm the tree, but losing one's manhood leads to immediate death. The woman's foolishness and jealousy are both pitiable and hateful. The crimes of premeditated murder and playful killing have different degrees of severity, but I do not know how the judge will punish them.

    Black Monster

    A man named Liu from the county, whose name is forgotten, returned from school at night and encountered a creature like a giant ape, with golden eyes, white teeth, and black fur hanging down three inches. It saw Liu and lunged at him with its claws. Liu ran away, and the creature chased him. The street dogs barked in alarm, and the creature leaped over the wall and climbed onto the roof, disappearing.

    Final Translation

    At first, Jia returned home in a disarrayed state, lying in bed unable to get up. His wife asked him about it, but he did not respond. The blood was so profuse that it stained the bedding, and soon he died. The old man filed a complaint with the authorities, but the culprit was not caught, and the case was often compared to similar ones. One of the official's close relatives, who sold sweets in the village lanes, came to a house where there were several young ladies playing. Among them, one who was slightly older was incomparably beautiful, and the others called her "sister." They asked the girl for money to buy candy. The girl said she had none, and the others said, "Your bag is so full, how can you say you have nothing?" They grabbed her arm and forcibly searched her. When they pulled out the object, it was a dead bird with a strange odor. The crowd did not recognize it and exclaimed, "Is this rotten meat still edible?" They left it on the ground. The girl blushed and quickly picked it up and hid it. The crowd then dispersed. The relative observed the situation, reported it to the official, and the official reported it to the authorities. They detained and interrogated the girl, obtained the truth, and punished her according to the law.

    The Drunken Tea Master said: Loving and then cutting it off, it is no different from loving and then breaking off a flower. To take a knife and try, it may not be a deep expression of emotion. But breaking a flower does not harm the tree, but losing one's manhood leads to immediate death. The woman's foolishness and jealousy are both pitiable and hateful. The crimes of premeditated murder and playful killing have different degrees of severity, but I do not know how the judge will punish them.

    Black Monster

    A man named Liu from the county, whose name is forgotten, returned from school at night and encountered a creature like a giant ape, with golden eyes, white teeth, and black fur hanging down three inches. It saw Liu and lunged at him with its claws. Liu ran away, and the creature chased him. The street dogs barked in alarm, and the creature leaped over the wall and climbed onto the roof, disappearing.

    205

    毛某山左毛某，侨居津门。夜行，见一家门外立二差役。一云：“此其时矣。”一云：“渠乃命妇，姑俟彼整束衣裳。”其一忿云：“如此因循，归当受责矣。我自去勾之。”旋从门内引出一妇人，朱袍霞帔，钮犹未结，匆匆随役俱去。毛问夜拘妇女是何公事？三人不答。追至巷外，则杳。乃回款关问故，老仆云：“太夫人才逝世矣。”毛述所见，哀求寄宿。不得已，白诸主人，使二仆送至其家。半月，毛寻卒。
    疫鬼
    　　邑城隍祠每四月赛会，邑人戴假面具彩衣持叉，装作魑魅魍魉，即乡傩之遗意也。壬戌岁，大疫流行，五六月犹甚。有宋姓者，夜起街前遗秽，见灯火自西来，有厉鬼数十，状皆奇丑，持叉而过。宋疑为会也，观其去远而返。归述诸友，友惊曰：“此非奏会之时，乌得有是？”宋亦愕然，陡觉身起寒战，吐泻大作，及晓而亡。

    Explanation

    205

    A man from Shandong, living temporarily in Tianjin, was walking at night when he saw two officials standing outside a house. One said, "It's time." The other replied, "She is a woman of high status; let her dress properly first." The first official angrily said, "Such delays will get us blamed. I'll go and summon her myself." Soon, they led out a woman from inside, dressed in a red robe and a colorful ceremonial cap, her button still unfastened. She hurriedly followed the officials away. The man asked what business it was to arrest a woman at night, but the three officials did not answer. He followed them to the end of the alley, where they disappeared. Returning to inquire, an old servant told him, "Her husband just passed away." The man recounted what he had seen and pleaded to stay the night. Reluctantly, the owners agreed and sent two servants to accompany him. Half a month later, the man died.

    Epidemic Spirits

    The local City God Temple holds a festival every April, during which the townspeople wear masks, colorful clothes, and carry spears, dressing up as evil spirits or monsters, a tradition that harks back to the ancient ritual of "xuán." In the year of the Water Dog, a major epidemic spread, particularly severe in the months of May and June. A man named Song was defecating in the street at night when he saw lights approaching from the west, accompanied by dozens of ugly, terrifying spirits, each holding a spear. Song thought it was the festival procession and watched them go far away. Upon returning, he told his friends, who were shocked and asked how this could happen outside of the festival. Song was also shocked, suddenly felt a chill, and suffered severe vomiting and diarrhea, dying by morning.

    206

    狐妻
    　　邑徐某，娶狐为妻，举止如常人，人不知其为狐也。事能前知。徐性喜赌，每出，妻付一囊，局终偿负，探钱恰符其数。生二子，家亦小康，辞徐欲行。留之不可，赠香一炷，裹以重纸，嘱云：“有急难事焚之，则我立至。”出门遽去。越数年，徐遇一人哭泣觅死，询之，云：“某富家之仆也，主人遣予往质金钏，渡河时失之，惧无以偿，惟有一死。”徐怜之，乃云：“随我来，当知钏之所在。”引至其家，出香焚于炉。忽檐际一物，堕如飞鸟，视之，乃其妻也。问：“何事见召？”徐告以故。妻曰：“我以为有急难也，乃细事耳，可令彼于浮桥舱中求之。但此香一焚，他日即不可复至。是亦数也。”浩叹而去。徐告诸其人，果于舱中得之。又数载，徐病危，巫医无术。取余香焚之，不至，遂亡。

    Explanation

    206

    Fox Wife

    In the town of Xu, there was a man who married a fox as his wife. She behaved like a normal person, and no one knew she was a fox. She could predict future events. Xu liked gambling. Every time he went out, his wife gave him a bag. When the game ended, the amount of money he owed was exactly the amount he found in the bag. He had two sons, and his family was also moderately prosperous. One day, she bid farewell to Xu and wanted to leave. He could not keep her. She gave him a stick of incense wrapped in thick paper and said, "If you have an emergency, burn it, and I will come immediately." She left hurriedly.

    After several years, Xu met a man who was crying and looking for death. Xu asked him what was wrong. The man said, "I am a servant of a wealthy family. The master sent me to pawn a gold bracelet, but I lost it while crossing the river. Afraid that I cannot repay it, I have no choice but to die." Xu pitied him and said, "Follow me, and I will tell you where the bracelet is." He led him to his home and burned the incense in the stove. Suddenly, something fell from the eaves like a flying bird. Xu looked up, and it was his wife. Xu asked, "Why did you come?" His wife said, "I thought there was an emergency, but it's just a small matter. You can find the bracelet in the cabin of the floating bridge. But once this incense is burned, I will not be able to come again. This is also my fate." She sighed and left. Xu told the man, and the man found the bracelet in the cabin of the floating bridge.

    After several more years, Xu fell seriously ill, and the doctors and magicians could do nothing. He burned the remaining incense, but she did not come, and he died.

    207

    涞水盗
    　　涞水某家少妇新死，殓物丰侈。有盗夜发其冢，出尸以带系脑后如环，己亦纳首带中，与尸对立，以便颠倒衣裳。方扶尸起，尸遽伸一拳，其坚如铁，直贯盗胸。盗惊而毙，以项后有带故，相将俱僵立。晓为人见，报官。已而盗苏，备吐其实，重惩系狱焉。
    粥厂鬼
    　　丁丑岁暮，饥民流离，官设数十厂施粥留养。时城北广患疫者众。有役夫周德者，除夜侍病人汤水，见一巨鬼高二三丈，头如栲栳，瞋目四顾，焰闪金辉。惊骇欲倒，旋不见。次日，役夫周廷喜执其事，夜又见之，二人皆大病，几乎毙命。是知大劫大难，必有神鬼监察，故每厂动死千余人，非细事也。

    Explanation

    207

    A Thief from Liaozhui
    In Liaozhui, a young woman from a certain household had recently passed away, and her funeral arrangements were lavish. A thief dug up her grave at night, taking out the corpse and tying a belt around the back of her head like a ring. He then tied his own head to the belt and stood opposite the corpse to dress it. Just as he was lifting the corpse, the corpse suddenly extended a fist, which was as hard as iron, and directly pierced through the thief's chest. The thief died of shock, but because his neck was tied to the belt, both he and the corpse stood stiff together. In the morning, someone discovered the scene and reported it to the authorities. Later, the thief revived and detailed the entire incident, leading to his severe punishment and imprisonment.

    The Ghost of the Soup Kitchen
    At the end of the year in the Dingchou cycle, famine-stricken people were wandering homeless, and the government set up dozens of soup kitchens to provide relief. At the time, many people in the northern part of the city were suffering from an epidemic. A servant named Zhou De was serving patients soup and water on New Year's Eve when he saw a huge ghost about two to three zhang tall. The ghost had a head like a winnowing basket, glaring and looking around, with flames flickering with golden light. Zhou De was shocked and almost fell, but the ghost soon disappeared. The next day, another servant named Zhou Tingxi, who was responsible for this matter, saw the ghost again at night. Both of them fell seriously ill and almost died. Thus, it is known that during times of great calamity, there must be divine beings watching over, which is why each soup kitchen often resulted in the deaths of over a thousand people, not a trivial matter.

    208

    大蛇
    　　邑赵翰林世曾，自山左归，晚休旅店，大雨倾盆。忽屋角一砖堕落，有大蛇粗如筒，自砖缺处下，蟠据几上，几察察作响。赵急奔至堂中，呼从人，皆莫之应。雷电绕窗，訇訇不绝。既而雨止，蛇亦不见。视几案倾侧欲折矣。究不知其为龙为蛇也。
    小黄人
    　　邑梅某，客开州。馆中孤坐，见砖隙中出一物，长寸许，如人，黄色。转瞬高如人等，遽前相搏，梅即昏不知人。惧迁他室，复见如前，日二三次，不堪其扰。或教以削桃木剑，乘其不意而暴击之。次日，物出，骤以剑击，嘎然而倒，化为黄鼠。杀之，怪绝。后半载，梅方夜卧，忽布帘掀动，一蓝手大如箕，自门外探入。梅大号，手缩去。急治装归里焉。

    Explanation

    208

    Big Snake
    A scholar named Zhao from the town, who had traveled from the east of the mountains, returned late at night and stayed at an inn. A heavy rainstorm was pouring down. Suddenly, a brick fell from the corner of the roof, and a big snake as thick as a tube came down from the gap in the brick, coiled and sat on the table, making a creaking sound. Zhao rushed to the hall, called the servants, but none responded. Lightning and thunder surrounded the window, roaring continuously. Afterwards, the rain stopped, and the snake was also gone. Looking at the table and desk, they were leaning and about to break. Ultimately, it was unknown whether it was a dragon or a snake.

    Small Yellow Person
    A man named Mei from the town, staying in Kaizhou, was sitting alone in the study. He saw something coming out of a crack in the brick, about an inch long, like a person, yellow in color. In a moment, it grew as tall as a person, suddenly attacked him, and Mei immediately fainted and lost consciousness. Afraid, he moved to another room, but again saw it as before, two or three times a day, unable to bear the disturbance. Someone taught him to carve a peach wood sword and to strike it unexpectedly. The next day, the creature appeared, and he suddenly struck with the sword, causing it to suddenly fall over and transform into a yellow rat. He killed it, and the strangeness was extreme. After half a year, Mei was lying down at night, and suddenly, the curtain was lifted. A blue hand as big as a winnowing basket reached in from outside the door. Mei cried out loudly, and the hand retreated. He quickly prepared and returned home.

    209

    棺怪
    　　邑王某，将之汤阴。车过旷野，有旋风蔽天而来。中一巨蟒，身粗如梁，尾击车顶，爆然作响，牢几倾覆。风过，蟒亦不见。车辙前有一物高尺许，形略似人，其行踯躅。车夫追近，以脚踢之，物急奔而没。晚至旅邸，车夫脚暴肿，痛如被杖，医治半载始瘥。询之土人，云：“是处有棺怪，幻形不一，常出为祟焉。”
    鬼哭
    　　河间宋某，妻死，停柩于室。遗一幼女，无人养育，乃寄居于戚家。宋独出贸易，烦二乡人守其空宅。夜间闻室中泣声，不以为怪。无何，每夜皆然，遂寻宋而告以故。宋归闻泣声，颇类亡妇，细辩之，乃呼其女之小字而哭也。夜静无人，声益悲楚。宋感恸，如割肺肝，明日思剖其棺，冀妇苏重为夫妇。友谏曰：“鬼神之德无常，焉有死经半载而生者？是必妖物所凭。君不可冒昧轻信，恐为所祸，悔难追也。

    Explanation

    209

    棺怪

    In a certain town, there was a man named Wang who was traveling to Tangyin. As his carriage passed through an open field, a whirlwind obscured the sky. Amidst the wind, there appeared a giant python, its body as thick as a beam, its tail striking the carriage roof with a loud bang, nearly causing the carriage to overturn. When the wind passed, the python disappeared. Ahead of the carriage tracks, there was an object about a foot tall, somewhat resembling a human, moving with a limp. The driver chased after it, kicking it with his foot, and the object fled quickly. By evening, they arrived at an inn, and the driver's foot swelled up violently, causing him pain as if he had been beaten, and it took half a year of medical treatment for the swelling to subside. When he asked the locals, they said, "There is a coffin spirit in this area that can take on various forms and often causes mischief."

    鬼哭

    In Hejian, there was a man named Song whose wife had died, and her coffin was kept in the room. She left behind a young daughter, who was left without anyone to care for, so she was sent to live with relatives. Song went out alone to trade, and he asked two villagers to guard his empty house. One night, he heard crying coming from the room where the coffin was kept, but he did not think much of it. Soon after, every night the crying continued, so he went to find Song and told him about it. Upon returning home, Song heard the crying and thought it sounded like his deceased wife. Upon closer examination, he realized it was his daughter calling out to him by her pet name. When the night was quiet and no one was around, the crying became even more sorrowful. Song was deeply moved and felt as if his heart and liver were being torn apart. The next day, he thought of opening the coffin to try to revive his wife and be with her again. A friend advised him, "The virtue of ghosts and spirits is unpredictable. How could there be someone who dies and then comes back to life after half a year? This must be due to some evil spirit. You should not rashly believe it, for fear of being harmed by it, and you may regret it later."

    210

    ”因循数日，声倍高朗，居然从室而哭于庭。邻里共闻，佥畏恶之，劝宋速葬，宋不忍也。众鸣于官，官遣二役逻守，察其虚实。役操兵而往，伏于停灵之复室。夜闻声自棺出，绕屋而走，无何，出堂门而步于庭。二人破窗私窥，星光下仿佛妇人。及走至窗前，则遍体毛毳，状如巨犬，人立而行，仰面犹号泣也。急发火枪，一击而中，嗥嗥长鸣，越垣而遁。时村人恐二役有失，早聚众人持械于门外。众闻枪响，俱惊顾，见一物自墙跃下。甫及地，众刀剑齐下，毙之，乃白狐也。怪遂绝焉。

    Explanation

    210

    For several days, the sound grew even louder and clearer, and the creature suddenly began to cry in the courtyard from the room. The neighbors all heard it and were all afraid and hated it, advising Song to bury it quickly. Song could not bear to do so. The crowd reported to the official, who sent two guards to watch and investigate its truthfulness. The guards armed themselves and went, crouching in the room where the coffin was placed. At night, they heard the sound coming from the coffin, circling the house and walking, and eventually came out of the hall door and walked in the courtyard. The two men broke the window to secretly look, and in the starlight, it seemed like a woman. When it walked to the window, it was covered in fur, like a large dog, standing upright and walking, facing upwards and still crying. They quickly fired a gun, hitting it with one shot, and it roared loudly, jumped over the wall and fled. At that time, the villagers feared the guards might be lost, and early gathered people with weapons outside the door. When they heard the gunshots, they were all startled and looked around, seeing something leap down from the wall. As soon as it touched the ground, the crowd attacked with swords and knives, killing it. It turned out to be a white fox. The strange phenomenon ended.

    211

    树哭
    　　邑韩氏庭中有古树，植已百年，每家有丧事，树预于前数日枝柯摇动，洒洒滴水，有如暴雨，人谓之树哭。后迁他姓，恶欲伐之，树忽訇訇雷吼，三日不休，慎而止焉。其家少妇产一子，无首，腹中有弯骨。细视，乃一铜钩。众莫知其故。初生时，四肢举动，越一夕始亡。或以为树妖所感也。
    画妖
    　　邑王姓，居室精洁，床头悬美人画一轴，笔墨精巧，粉黛如生。一夕，王他出，其妻对灯独坐，见系帐长绠影拖美人颈下，状如投缳。惊疑间，美人自纸下，颔悬绳上，旋转不休。惧而大号，其夫适归，备言其状。王投画于火。后数日，梦美人怒谓王曰：“我偶戏秋千，何于尔事，而毁我之形！此惨毒之仇，誓必相报。”遽以手扼其喉。惊寤，自此患病而亡。

    Explanation

    211

    Tree Weeping
    In a certain town, the Han family had an ancient tree in their courtyard that had been there for over a hundred years. Whenever there was a death in the family, the tree would show signs of distress several days before, with its branches shaking and water dripping from them, as if it were raining heavily. People called this "tree weeping." Later, when the house was sold to another family, the new owners disliked the tree and wanted to cut it down. Suddenly, the tree let out a loud, thunderous roar, which continued for three days without stopping. They were so terrified that they stopped cutting it down. One day, a young woman in the family gave birth to a child with no head, but inside the child's abdomen was a bent piece of copper. Upon closer inspection, it turned out to be a copper hook. No one knew the reason for this. The child, despite having no head, moved its limbs as if it were alive, but it died the next night. Some believed it was a sign of the tree's supernatural influence.

    Painting Demon
    In the same town, there was a family named Wang. Their home was very clean, and on the wall near their bed hung a painting of a beautiful woman. The painting was very well done, and the colors were so vivid that it looked almost real. One night, while the husband was away, his wife was sitting alone by the light of a lamp. She saw a long rope hanging from the curtain, trailing down to the neck of the woman in the painting, as if the woman were about to hang herself. She was so shocked and frightened that she screamed loudly. Her husband happened to return just then, and she told him what she had seen. The husband threw the painting into the fire. A few days later, he had a dream in which the woman appeared and angrily said to him, "I was just playing on a swing, what do you have to do with it? Why did you destroy my image? This is a terrible and unforgivable grudge, and I swear I will take revenge on you!" She then grabbed his throat with her hands. He woke up in a panic, and from that day on, he fell ill and eventually died.

    212

    醉茶子曰，粉黛如生，呼之欲下，真令人夸丹青笔妙，而想念真真也。奈何红颜为厉，作怪骇人，其遭焚身之祸不亦宜哉！而犹挟私仇以逞报复，其不能自咎也甚矣。
    张孝子
    　　张孝子，未详其郡邑名字。其父卒于津，瘗诸城西义阡。后数载，张至，寻其父柩。义阡中司事者稽旧册，得知瘗所。开圹见棺，棺前字迹磨灭，未敢认也。张伏地哀求。司事曰：“土蚀剥落，予将奈何？”张计穷，焚香哭拜于城隍神前，三昼夜不倦。旋到柩前，哭拜如前状。义阡中司事遣二人侍其侧，防豺狗之伤人也。如是三昼夜，二人颇烦。忽一紫衣童子，神采俊逸，谓二人曰：“彼因何事而哭拜乃尔？”二人为述颠末。童子指柩曰：“尔等皆呆耶？分明字迹，何使云无？”乃高声朗诵之。三人拭目细视，果如童言。回顾童子，已失所在。张迁父柩归里焉。是知童子神人，缘张纯孝所感，故指迷也。

    Explanation

    212

    The Teahouse Drunkard said, "The powdered and painted ladies are as if alive, one could almost call them down. Truly, it makes one praise the skill of the brush and think of the real ones." How unfortunate that the beauty has become a menace, causing strange and frightening things. Is it not fitting that she should suffer the calamity of being burned? Yet she still harbors private grudges and seeks revenge, showing how incapable she is of self-blame.

    Zhang the Filial Son

    Zhang the Filial Son, whose place of origin and name are not known. His father died in the city of Jin and was buried in the western outskirts of the city in a public cemetery. After several years, Zhang came to search for his father's coffin. The person in charge of the cemetery consulted the old records and found out the burial place. When the tomb was opened, the coffin was seen, but the inscriptions on the front were worn away, and he dared not recognize it. Zhang knelt on the ground and pleaded. The person in charge said, "The soil has eroded and worn away, what can I do?" Zhang was at a loss, so he burned incense and wept and bowed before the city god for three days and nights without tiring. He then went to the coffin and wept and bowed as before. The person in charge of the cemetery sent two people to guard him, to prevent wolves and dogs from harming him. For three days and nights, the two people were quite tired. Suddenly, a purple-robed boy appeared, with a graceful and spirited appearance, and said to the two people, "Why is he crying and bowing so?" The two people recounted the entire story. The boy pointed to the coffin and said, "Are you all foolish? The inscriptions are clearly visible, why do you say they are not there?" He then loudly recited the inscriptions. The three people wiped their eyes and looked closely, and indeed, it was as the boy had said. When they turned to look at the boy, he had already disappeared. Zhang then moved his father's coffin back to his hometown. It is known that the purple-robed boy was a divine being, moved by Zhang's pure filial piety, and thus guided him.

    213

    刘晖
    　　亳州刘晖，食鱼骨鲠于喉，咯出一物，状如鱼目，莹洁而圆。拾置几上，倏然分为两，又分为数十块，宛转俱化为人，长寸许，坐者、卧者、行者，来往纷纷。刘急捕之，皆遁去，仅获其一。置砚池中，日饭米一颗，爱如奇珍，作绿纱小帐以护之。欲为之置床榻、制衣履，而小人毙。刘甚怏怏，举置笔床上。忽见前者数人，素衣而至，向尸饮泣甚悲。刘无言，以觇其变。旋有四人舁一小棺，朱漆明净，纳小人于其中，合棺，众人拥簇而去。至几下，遂失所在。

    Explanation

    213

    Liu Hui, a man from Bozhou, had a fish bone stuck in his throat. He spat out an object that looked like a fish eye, clear and round. He picked it up and placed it on the table, and suddenly it split into two, then into dozens of pieces, all of which turned into people in a graceful manner, each about an inch long, sitting, lying, and walking, coming and going in a flurry. Liu hurried to catch them, but they all fled, and he managed to catch only one. He placed it in the inkstone pool, feeding it a grain of rice each day, treating it like a rare treasure, and made a green silk screen to protect it. He wanted to provide it with a bed, clothes, and shoes, but the small person died. Liu was very disappointed and lifted the body and placed it on the writing desk. Suddenly, he saw several people in white clothes approach, weeping bitterly over the corpse. Liu said nothing, watching to see what would happen. Soon, four people carried a small coffin, bright and clean with red lacquer, and placed the small person inside, closing the coffin. The crowd gathered around and left. When they reached the table, they suddenly disappeared from their place.

    214

    吴某
    　　邑宋氏仆吴某，骑行旷野。忽见一妇人飞行空际，袒臂露胸，肤白如粉，颈上横插霜刃，血痕殷湿，髻发蓬松，红裙拖曳。吴大惊，几乎堕马。转瞬飞入云端矣。其亦夜叉之类欤？
    旱魃房山亢旱，有术人云：“西山冢中，有僵尸变为旱魃。”为乡人指其处。议共发之，坟主不许，众鸣于官。官不能禁，谓术人曰：“众惑汝言，牢不可破。若无旱魑，坐汝以盗坟罪。”术人力白其不诬。乃开圹，则一空棺，板有巨孔。棺旁卧一物如人，遍体绿毛，长寸许，双目赤如灯火，见人起立欲遁。众缚而焚之。未几大雪。土人云：“每阴云四布，辄有白气自坟中出，即时晴朗。”固不必因术人之言而始信也。

    214

    Wu, a servant of the Song family in a town, was riding alone in the wilderness. Suddenly, he saw a woman flying in the air, bare-armed and bared-breasted, with skin as white as powder. A frosty blade was horizontally inserted in her neck, and bloodstains were dark and wet. Her hair was in a loose bun, and her red skirt trailed behind her. Wu was so shocked that he almost fell off his horse. In an instant, she flew into the clouds. Perhaps she was a type of night spirit?

    In Fangshan, there was a severe drought. A fortune-teller said, "In the tomb in Xishan, there is a zombie that has turned into a drought demon." He pointed out the location to the villagers, who decided to open the tomb together. The tomb owner did not allow it, and the people reported to the authorities. The officials could not stop them and told the fortune-teller, "The people are confused by your words, and it is hard to break through their beliefs. If there is no drought demon, we will accuse you of tomb robbery." The fortune-teller insisted that it was not false, and they then opened the tomb. They found an empty coffin with a large hole in the lid. Next to the coffin lay something like a person, covered with green fur, about an inch long, with eyes red like lanterns. It saw people and stood up to escape. The crowd bound and burned it. Soon after, it snowed heavily.

    The locals said, "Whenever dark clouds gather, there would be white vapor coming out of the tomb, and immediately the sky would clear." This legend about the tomb is a testament to the supernatural forces at play in the region.

    215

    醉茶志怪卷三
    张公
    　　邑先达张公虎拜，字啸崖，乾隆己丑进士。性至孝，母死，庐墓三年，乡里推重焉。将终时，梦二役延至冥府。冥官降阶迎揖，让公坐案侧。方欲致辞，忽闻呼冤声。视之，一王后长袖宫妆，背立墀下。俄而诸鬼绾绁提锤，拥一金甲将军，伏跪案前。二人质辩多词，官略诘数语，旋命俱退。公询其情，官曰：“此前朝事，公来自知，勿劳诘问。”公曰：“辱蒙见招，不知有何驱遣？”官云：“仆之冥差期满，例应交卸，公当荣任此职。”公闻之甚骇，托辞推却。官曰：“此上帝所命，仆不敢专。请归，速理后事，至期遣役相迓。”公霍然遂寤。未几，无疾而逝。或云：后乃杨妃，将军则陈元礼也。玉环其含冤乎？

    Explanation

    215

    Strange Tales of Intoxicated Tea, Volume Three
    Zhang Gong

    Respected scholar Zhang Gong Hubai, courtesy name Xiao Yi, was a jinshi (imperial scholar) in the year Jichou of the Qianlong reign. He was extremely filial; after his mother's death, he lived in a hut by her grave for three years, and was highly regarded by his hometown. On his deathbed, he dreamed of two officials inviting him to the underworld. The underworld official descended from the steps to greet him with a bow, inviting him to sit beside the desk. Just as he was about to speak, he suddenly heard a cry of injustice. Looking around, he saw a queen in long-sleeved palace attire standing with her back to the steps. Soon after, various ghosts bound and held hammers, surrounding a golden-armored general who knelt in front of the desk. The two people debated with many words. The official briefly questioned them a few times, then ordered them both to withdraw. Zhang Gong inquired about the matter, and the official said, "This is a matter from the previous dynasty. You will know it yourself. Do not bother to ask." Zhang Gong replied, "Honored to be invited, I do not know what is required." The official said, "My term as an underworld official has ended. By custom, I should hand over, and you should take on this honorable position." Zhang Gong was greatly startled and found an excuse to decline. The official said, "This is the command of the Supreme Deity. I dare not act alone. Please return, and quickly arrange your affairs. At the appointed time, officials will come to fetch you." Zhang Gong suddenly woke up. Shortly afterward, he died without illness. Some say that the queen was Yang Guifei, and the general was Chen Yuanli. Did Yuhuan suffer injustice?

    216

    娄某邑娄某，暑夜纳凉，其窗半开，一夜叉探身入，锯牙钩爪，竟体纯青，跃近榻前，以巨爪扼其肩，深入肌理。娄惊踣床下，昏不知人。及晓，为人救醒，血涔涔遍背矣。其弟贸易都中，寓室逼侧。夜息灯独寝，忽有光荧然自墙隅出，乃一巨蟒，头大如斗，目灼灼类双炬，肋下有翅如鱼，逼近榻前，势将吞噬。某急，拔关而遁，回顾室中，犹如燃烛。惊病，遂归。

    Explanation

    216

    A man from a certain town in Lou was cooling off on a hot summer night. His window was half open. One night, a creature with forked body and saw-like teeth and hooked claws, entirely green, leapt close to the bed and clutched his shoulder with huge claws, deeply into the flesh. The man was frightened and fell off the bed, losing consciousness and being unaware. By dawn, he was saved and woken up, with blood dripping all over his back. His younger brother, who traded in the capital, lived in a cramped guest room. One night, he extinguished the lamp and slept alone. Suddenly, light flickered out from the corner of the wall, revealing a huge python with a head as large as a winnowing basket, eyes bright like twin torches, and wings under the ribs like fish fins. The python approached close to the bed, about to swallow him. The brother panicked and pulled the door and fled, looking back into the room, as if a candle was burning. Frightened and falling ill, he returned home.

    217

    冯君三异
    　　冯君熙，邑之奇人，勇敢有力，慷慨轻财，家产尽让弟，己惟取一金簪，余物不问，故群呼为“冯大簪”云。嗜远游，生平履迹所到，凡十三省。每出游，跨一驴，买薄货异地售之，仅足糊口。夜不投逆旅，择山林坟墓僻静处展被偃息，枕下一短刀，防虎狼暴客。偶至一墓所，有新圹未下棺者，喜其坦平，襆被卧焉。月明未寝，见圹边立一小人，高尺许，探首下视。俄去而复返，又引数小人来，咸俯圹边，彼此指划，似相告语。冯抽刀跃上，见圹外尚有十余辈，纷纷星散。冯仍卧寝，至晓而去。
    　　尝行山中，远见二人对搏，近视，乃虎与人对立。人以斧斫虎顶，虎以爪攫人肩，相持并死，其尸俱僵。盖非柴夫，即猎人也。
    　　冯晚年小康，不事远游，与妻子别院居。晚散步庭中，有风门一扇，在身后随之行。约十余行，冯回顾不惊，门至故处不动。盖狐鬼戏之也。奇人多遇奇事，斯亦奇矣。

    Explanation

    217

    Feng Xiyi: Three Strange Encounters

    Feng Xiyi was a remarkable person of the town, known for his bravery and strength, his generosity, and his disregard for material wealth. He gave away all of his family's property to his younger brother, taking only a single gold hairpin for himself and showing no interest in the rest of the possessions. As a result, the people of the town called him "Feng the Big Hairpin." He had a passion for traveling far and wide, and in his lifetime, he had visited all thirteen provinces. Whenever he went on a trip, he would ride a donkey, buy cheap goods in different places, and sell them just enough to make a living. He preferred not to stay in inns at night, choosing instead to sleep in quiet and secluded places in the mountains and among tombs. He would keep a short knife under his pillow to protect himself from tigers, wolves, and bandits.

    Once, while traveling, he came upon a newly dug grave that had not yet been used. He was pleased with its flatness and decided to sleep there. Under the bright moonlight, he did not fall asleep immediately and noticed a small person standing by the grave, about a foot tall, peering down into the grave. The small person went away and then returned, bringing several more small people with him. They all crouched by the grave, pointing and gesturing as if communicating with each other. Feng Xiyi drew his knife and leaped up, seeing more than ten more small people outside the grave, scattering like stars. He returned to sleep and left at dawn.

    On another occasion, while walking in the mountains, he saw two people fighting from a distance. Upon closer inspection, it turned out to be a tiger and a man facing each other. The man was chopping at the tiger's head with an axe, and the tiger was clawing the man's shoulder. They fought until both died, and their bodies lay stiff. It was either a woodcutter or a hunter, but Feng Xiyi did not know for sure.

    In his later years, Feng Xiyi lived comfortably and no longer traveled far and wide. He lived separately from his wife and children. One evening, while taking a walk in the courtyard, he noticed a door following him behind. After walking about ten steps, he turned back without being startled, and the door returned to its original place. It was likely a fox spirit or ghost playing a trick on him. Remarkable people often encounter strange events, and this was indeed a remarkable event.

    218

    白塔寺
    　　扬青驿河干，有积柴如丘，相传仙居其内，旁建一祠曰白塔寺。乡人祈祷辄灵。邻村有收生妪，夜半有人叩扉延请，云：“已为姆备肩舆，敢奉劳也。”未暇详问，即扶入舆中，舁之而去。至一第，门仅如窦，入则楼阁连亘，服物奢侈。内室锦帐绣褥，坐一佳人，年二十许，美丽无比，绿蛾双蹙，红粉凝娇，似将分娩。旁立数婢，亦皆妖艳。妪使一婢登床抱产妇，使柳腰细弯，莲足高举。女产殊不艰涩，一举四男，体俱肥茁。惟尻际有小尾，不时摇动，啼声啾啾。一婢奔出送喜。有美妇四五人，入室欢贺，邀妪至他室，盥以金盆，啖以肴酒，出黄豆升许赠之，云：“将去，一生吃著不尽。”妪大失所望，方欲致辞，遽使人导出。回顾并无屋宇，乃麻楷垛也。深怨仙人吝啬，举袖中豆洒诸河干。及至家，袖底得珠二粒，始悔。再往寻之，乌有矣。

    Explanation

    218

    White Pagoda Temple

    Near the riverbank of Yangqing Post Station, there was a pile of firewood as high as a hill, and it was said that a deity resided within it. A temple was built nearby, called the White Pagoda Temple. Local people's prayers were often answered. A neighboring village had a midwife, who at midnight was knocked at the door and invited to attend a birth. The caller said, "I have prepared a sedan chair for you, and I dare to trouble you with the journey." Without time to ask more questions, she was helped into the sedan chair and taken away. They arrived at a mansion, with a small entrance like a hole, and upon entering, they saw a series of connected buildings with luxurious furnishings. Inside a room, there was a beautiful woman in her twenties, incredibly beautiful, with furrowed eyebrows and a rosy complexion, as if she were about to give birth. Several maids stood by, all of them stunningly beautiful. The midwife instructed one maid to climb onto the bed and support the woman in labor, bending her slender waist and lifting her feet. The woman gave birth without difficulty, delivering four boys, all healthy and well-formed. However, they each had a small tail at the base of their buttocks, which moved occasionally, and their cries were faint. One maid rushed out to announce the good news. Several beautiful women entered the room to celebrate, inviting the midwife to another room, where they washed her hands in a gold basin, fed her food and wine, and gave her a measure of soybeans, saying, "When you leave, you will have enough to eat for a lifetime." The midwife was greatly disappointed and wanted to speak, but was quickly escorted out. Looking back, she saw no buildings, only a pile of straw. She deeply resented the deity for being stingy and threw the soybeans into the river. Upon returning home, she found two pearls in her sleeve and regretted her actions. When she returned to the riverbank, there was nothing there.

    219

    李志青
    　　李志青，豫之茂才。馆于汤阴，去家七十里，遇乱奔归。将近家，暴雨骤至。道旁有茅舍，一老叟剃发于其中，就之，乃其素识。言其情，叟款李共饮。更深雨歇，李辞叟欲行，止之不可。出门半里许，至一村寺，前横溪水，一人从寺出，圆目凿齿，黄面赤眉，要路奋击。李与格斗，将胜，忽又来三人，状皆奇丑，裸李衣攒殴致毙，弃尸水中，置衣帽于庙台上，始散。次日，李弟经此，见其尸，访知从叟处出，入城将讼叟，止于戚家。其家婢忽作李言曰：“切勿控叟，不干彼事。予自彼处归，酒兴正浓，遇四人攒殴而死。此四人名某某，皆饿鬼也。予即讼于阎君，汝勿妄告。”弟闻言，疑信参半。方踌躇间，家人追至，言其兄附家中人，所言与婢同，弟乃罢讼。葬李后，又附人云：“冥君以四人凶暴，皆下狱受炮烙刑，予冤雪矣。”遂退。其弟访近处新鬼故鬼，皆无其名，不知是何处厉魄。

    Explanation

    219

    Li Zhiqing, a talented individual from the state of Yu, was staying in Tangyin, seventy miles from his home, when he encountered chaos and fled back. As he neared his home, a sudden heavy rain began. Along the road, there was a thatched cottage where an old man was shaving. Li approached him, and it turned out to be an old acquaintance. The old man told him the situation and invited Li to drink together. Deep into the night, the rain stopped, and Li tried to leave, but the old man wouldn't let him. After going out for about half a mile, Li arrived at a village temple, with a stream flowing in front. Suddenly, a person emerged from the temple, with round eyes, sharp teeth, a yellow face, and red eyebrows, blocking the path and attacking fiercely. Li fought back, nearly winning, but three more people suddenly appeared, all of whom were strangely ugly, stripped Li's clothes, and attacked him in a group, causing his death. They threw his body into the water and placed his clothes and hat on the temple platform before dispersing. The next day, Li's younger brother passed by the spot and found the body. Upon inquiry, he learned that Li had come from the old man's place and was about to sue the old man, stopping at a relative's house. Suddenly, a maid in the household spoke as if Li were saying, "Do not sue the old man; it has nothing to do with him. I was returning from there, and my spirits were high when I encountered four people who attacked me and killed me. These four people are named so-and-so, and they are all hungry ghosts. I have already reported this to the Lord of Yama; you should not make false accusations." The brother heard this and was half-believing and half-doubting. As he hesitated, his family arrived and said that Li's spirit had communicated through a family member, and what was said matched the maid's words. The brother then stopped the lawsuit. After Li's burial, his spirit appeared again and said, "The Lord of Yama has punished the four vicious people, sentencing them to the cruel punishment of being burned alive in prison; my wrong has been cleared." Then it disappeared. The brother searched for the new and old spirits in the vicinity but found no one with those names, unsure of where the malevolent spirits came from.

    220

    化犬
    　　邑朱某昼寝，梦里人丁姓者造其庐，青衣白带，神色张皇，谓朱曰：“官府命予寄养君家，祈善顾也。”语毕入内去。朱骇追问，遂寤。乃步入后院，见犬生数子。中一小犬，腰间白毛，周匝如带，始悟为丁之后身。稍长，送于长生院焉。

    Explanation

    220

    Transformed into a Dog

    Mr. Zhu from the village slept during the day and dreamed that a man surnamed Ding came to his house. The man was wearing a blue robe and a white belt, with a frightened and agitated appearance. He told Zhu, "The authorities have ordered me to live in your family, so please kindly take care of me." After saying this, he went inside. Zhu was startled and asked more questions, but then woke up. He then went to the backyard and saw that his dog had given birth to several puppies. One of the puppies had white fur around its waist, all around like a belt. Zhu realized that this was the reincarnation of the man from the dream. When the puppy grew a bit older, he sent it to the Longsheng Temple.

    221

    醉茶子曰：闻丁某贪暴霸产，无所不为，死而化犬，固所宜也。冥罚可畏已。
    陶生
    　　平阳陶生，武技绝伦。予邑盐贾某，喜其勇，延至家，使往来护送银信。偶投旅馆，掩扉独坐，忽几案察察作响，喝之即止。已而复然。陶以胆气自矜，无少怯。俄而声愈大，陶抽刀大吼。木案拍簸一声，响震四壁，梁尘飞落，仆仆如烟，一长绳直拖顶上。大惊奔出。殆缢鬼也。
    鬼吟诗
    　　邑陶某，清明扫墓，路出西郊。牢上盹睡，迷离间见道旁一矮屋，环以疏篱。有女子倚闾立，缟衣素裙，妆束淡雅。陶就之，女子掩扉入，低声吟曰：“清明雨，湿纸灰，良人一去不复回。尘埋玉色，酒涸金杯。清明雨，湿孤坟。家家春色不开门。红垂树杪，绿拥篱根。”声词凄楚，节短音长。陶方惊叹，爽然而醒。视道旁则三尺荒坟，几株衰柳而已。驱车而过。

    Explanation

    221

    醉茶子曰：闻丁某贪暴霸产，无所不为，死而化犬，固所宜也。冥罚可畏已。
    The Drunken Tea Master said: "I have heard of Mr. Ding, who was greedy, violent, and seized others' property, doing whatever he pleased. After his death, he was transformed into a dog, which is indeed his due. The punishment of the underworld is truly to be feared."

    陶生
    In Pingyang, there was a man named Tao, whose martial skills were unparalleled. A salt merchant from my hometown, admiring his courage, invited him to his home and employed him to escort silver and letters. One day, Tao stayed at an inn, closing the door and sitting alone. Suddenly, he heard a faint sound from the table, which ceased when he shouted at it. Soon after, the sound returned. Tao, proud of his courage and daring, was not the least bit afraid. As the noise grew louder, Tao drew his sword and roared. With a loud thud, the wooden table shook violently, causing the walls to tremble, dust to fly from the beams like smoke, and a long rope to hang from the ceiling. Frightened, Tao rushed out, realizing it was the ghost of a hanged man.

    鬼吟诗
    In the county, there was a man named Tao. During the Qingming Festival, he went to sweep the graves of his ancestors, traveling through the western suburbs. While dozing off in a stable, he half-awake saw a small cottage by the roadside, surrounded by sparse fences. A woman stood leaning on the gate, dressed in white, with a simple and elegant appearance. Tao approached her, and she closed the door and entered, softly reciting: "The rain of Qingming wets the ashes of paper, the beloved one gone never to return. Dust buries the beauty, wine dries the golden cup. The rain of Qingming wets the lonely grave. Every household's spring colors do not open their doors. Red fruits hang from the tips of trees, green plants surround the base of the fence." Her voice was sorrowful, her words short but her melody long. Tao was just amazed when he suddenly woke up. Looking around, he saw only a three-foot-high mound and a few withered willows by the roadside. He drove his carriage away.

    222

    捉鬼
    　　邑黄公，夜醉归，路经水边，遇一矮人力牵其裾，将曳入水。黄与撑拒，闻人呼曰：“此鬼也，勿为所迷。”视之，乃其亡友。醉中忘其已死，亟求相助，遂共擒之。相将至家门，其友别去。再视所擒之鬼，则乱发一团耳。
    　　醉茶子曰：发，血余耳。岂所余之血能为厉耶？抑人死，魂魄或寄于是耶？人有擒鬼者，往往所得者皆乱发，又何说欤？
    冥报
    　　某甲以贪暴起家，死时停尸于床，顶上血流不止，如被杖，家人佥以为怪。其邻高某亦于是日死，已而复苏，谓家人曰：“予本不应死，因与甲同名，鬼役误勾之耳。适于阴曹见甲朝衣朝冠立殿下，冥王稽其册，大怒日；『尔有何职，敢被皇家公服？如某事某事，当赴油鼎。』令鬼卒褫其衣，以大杖力挞其顶，砰然血溅数步之外，旋令牵下。见予泣日。『烦君寄语家人，勿以衣冠殓我，嘱后人好自为之，冥责不可逃也。』高唤其子秘告之，其家深讳此事。不数日，传遍乡里。

    Explanation

    222

    Translation

    Catching Ghosts

    One day, a man named Huang from the county, after a night of heavy drinking, returned home and passed by the water's edge. He encountered a short figure who was pulling his robe with force, trying to drag him into the water. Huang struggled against the force and heard someone call out, "This is a ghost! Do not be deceived by it." Upon looking, he saw that it was his deceased friend. In his drunken state, he had forgotten that his friend was dead and urgently sought help. Together, they managed to catch the ghost. They brought it to the door of Huang's home, and his friend left. When they looked again at the captured ghost, it was just a clump of tangled hair.

    The drunken tea enthusiast remarked: "Hair, the residue of blood. Can the remaining blood cause harm? Or does the soul of a deceased person sometimes reside in it? Those who catch ghosts often find only a clump of hair, what does this mean?"

    Retribution in the Afterlife

    A man named Jia rose to prominence through greed and violence. When he died, his body was placed on the bed, and blood flowed continuously from the top of his head, as if he had been beaten with a stick. His family all found this strange. His neighbor, Gao, also died on that day but later revived and told his family, "I should not have died. Because I share the same name as Jia, the ghost officials mistakenly summoned me. Just now, in the underworld, I saw Jia standing before the throne in official attire, and the King of Hell examined his record and became furious, saying, 'What position do you hold to dare to wear the imperial robes? For such and such deeds, you should be sent to the oil cauldron.' He ordered the ghost soldiers to strip him of his clothes and beat him on the top of his head with a large stick, causing blood to splash several steps away. Then, he ordered him to be led down. He saw me and said, 'Please trouble you to convey a message to my family, do not dress me in official attire when you bury me, and advise my descendants to behave well. Punishment in the afterlife cannot be avoided.' Gao called his son to secretly tell him, and his family deeply concealed this matter. Within a few days, the story spread throughout the village.

    223

    定兴城隍
    　　定兴城隍最灵，两庑下塑城隍像，大小不计其数。询诸居民，知每元宵节各村迎一像去，节后送还。计村之大小，取像之大小，乡俗也。有偷儿盗神袍服银脏，僧鸣于官。差役捕盗未获，屡受刑比，泣诉于神，祈示梦兆。梦神告曰：“玩十签可知。”醒而取签占视，有云：“缺墙之外，寸木之中，柳阴路曲，锦绣飞红。”不解。寻至城，隅有小村，见地上碎锦飘零，细审，乃神袍也。盖盗从城缺处出，至此村遂迷惘，自碎所盗之袍弃于地，旋为村人所获。役至，搜其怀，得银脏，牵盗赴公庭焉。

    Explanation

    223

    The City God of Dingxing
    The City God of Dingxing is the most powerful, with images of him carved in the two side wings of the temple, too many to count in size. When asked about this, the residents explained that each village takes one image to their village during the Lantern Festival and returns it after the festival. The size of the image taken by each village corresponds to the size of the village, a local custom. Once, a thief stole the robe of the city god and the silver it contained. A monk reported this to the authorities. The officials tried to catch the thief but failed, and the thief was repeatedly punished. Desperate, he appealed to the city god for help, asking for a sign in a dream. In his dream, the city god told him, "Play with ten signs to know." Upon waking, the thief consulted the signs and saw the message: "Beyond the broken wall, in the midst of a small tree, under the willow tree in a winding road, the brocade flying red." He did not understand. He searched the city and found a small village where he saw fragments of brocade scattered on the ground. Upon closer inspection, he realized it was the robe of the city god. It seemed the thief had escaped from a broken wall in the city and, lost, had torn the robe he had stolen and left it on the ground. Soon, the villagers found him. The officials arrived, searched his pockets, and found the stolen silver. They took the thief to the public court.

    224

    冤妇
    　　孙翘江，闽人，为肃宁县令。接篆入署，尚未理事，夜有白衣妇人，形容惨淡，云：“予北直人，本未失节，汝前生为此地县尹，误以失节拟罪，使我抱不白之冤。予已请于帝矣，许我报复索命。”言毕，遽以手扼其喉，孙便昏如中恶。既醒，见妇立床前，眈眈怒视。或投以绳，则奔赴悬鬟；投以刃，则撑持自刎。诸态百作，狼狈难堪，公事多半废弛。一夕，妇捽之往见城隍，便觉魂魄飘飘然随之行，恍至神案下。神朱袍金冠，气象严厉，谓孙曰：“查尔今生尚知孝道，不犯淫行，为尔留薄禄养亲，归宜改教，不许贪禄恋栈。尔贞妇亦应遵命，听其辞官而去，毋得再扰。”旋命俱退。

    Explanation

    224

    Translation:

    The Vindicated Woman

    Sun Qiaojiang, a native of Fujian, served as the magistrate of Suxian County. After assuming office and entering the government office, he had not yet begun to handle any official duties. One night, a woman in white appeared, her appearance pitiful. She said, "I am from the northern part of the direct jurisdiction of the emperor. I was never unchaste, but in your past life, you were the magistrate of this county and wrongly judged me for losing my chastity, causing me to bear an unjust and unexplained grudge. I have already petitioned the emperor, and he has allowed me to seek revenge and claim my life." After she finished speaking, she quickly grabbed his throat with her hand, and Sun fainted as if struck by a sudden illness. When he woke up, he saw the woman standing by his bed, glaring at him angrily. If he tried to strangle her with a rope, she would rush to hang herself; if he tried to stab her, she would hold up her neck to be cut. She displayed various states and actions, greatly distressing and uncomfortable, causing most of his official duties to be neglected. One night, the woman dragged him to see the city god. He felt his spirits floating along with her, and they suddenly arrived at the city god's altar. The city god, wearing a red robe and a golden crown, had a stern appearance. He said to Sun, "I have investigated that in this life you still know filial piety and do not commit adultery. I have left you with a small salary to support your parents. Upon returning, you should change your ways and not allow greed for salary or attachment to your position. The virtuous woman should also obey the command, allow him to resign and leave, and do not disturb again." Soon after, they were both dismissed.

    225

    忽如梦寤，视室中灯火无光，妇立于前，怒谓曰：“明日亟为禀辞，不然，仍不汝活。”孙唯唯。次日修禀上请。河间守熊下札慰留，妇复至骂曰：“狗奴子！汝不去耶？誓毙尔命。”力提其发，发簌簌落如乱丝。孙泣云：“昨已请命大人，当即亲交印绶，何敢再迟。请留姓字，熊大人将为请旌延僧超度，以慰贞魂，且为天下后世听讼不慎者戒。”妇抚首云：“我事已昭彰，毋劳大人表扬，且我并非求名求利，何须请旌与超度。第汝前生以不白之事误我，我今日以不白之事误尔。尔不在此为官，予又何求？”言毕，悻悻而去。次日，亲诣府署交纳印绶。熊准其请，遂旋里。妇不再扰。

    Explanation

    225

    Suddenly, as if waking from a dream, he looked around the dimly lit room and saw the woman standing before him, her face filled with anger. She said sternly, "Tomorrow, you must urgently present a formal request to resign; otherwise, I will still not let you live." He nodded in agreement. The next day, he prepared and submitted his resignation. Governor Xiong of Hejian issued an edict to comfort and retain him, but the woman returned and scolded, "You worthless slave! Are you not going to resign? I swear I will kill you!" She forcefully pulled his hair, causing it to fall out like tangled threads. He wept and said, "I have already requested permission from the great man yesterday, and I will personally hand over the seal and official seal immediately. How dare I delay further? Please leave your name, and Governor Xiong will request a posthumous honor and invite monks to perform a ceremony to liberate the soul, to console the pure spirit and to warn future generations who listen to cases carelessly." The woman touched her head and said, "My matter is already clear; do not trouble the great man to praise me. Moreover, I am not seeking fame or profit, so why need a posthumous honor and a ceremony to liberate the soul? But in your previous life, you wronged me with an unjust matter, and today I wrong you with an unjust matter. If you do not serve as an official here, what do I want?" After saying this, she left angrily. The next day, he personally went to the government office to hand over the seal and official seal. Governor Xiong approved his request, and he then returned home. The woman no longer troubled him.

    226

    醉茶子曰：甚矣！听讼不可不慎也。污人名节，报施至于再生不忘，良由一时之误也。况有明知其冤，更贪贿而威制之，则蒙冤者虽当时无如何，其惨毒之报岂能免耶！
    鼠怪
    　　邑章氏，家有空室三楹，多年扃闭，云其中多怪。有武夫寓其家，欲觇其异，每隔窗窥视，俱无所睹。一夕，月明鉴物，见堂中有男妇数人，皆衣锦绣，身高尺许。视案上立二人，高如前状，苇帽袍褂，相对揖。武夫以铁丸击中其一，蹶然倒案上。余俱不见。入室视之，乃一巨鼠，苇帽则鸡子壳，袍褂则青兰纸耳。

    Explanation

    226

    醉茶子曰：甚矣！听讼不可不慎也。污人名节，报施至于再生不忘，良由一时之误也。况有明知其冤，更贪贿而威制之，则蒙冤者虽当时无如何，其惨毒之报岂能免耶！
    The Drunken Tea Master said: How grave indeed! One must be very careful in hearing lawsuits. Defaming someone's reputation and integrity, the retribution can be so severe that it lingers even in their next life, truly due to a momentary mistake. How much more so when one knows they are innocent but still takes bribes and uses force to control them? Those who are wronged, though they may be helpless at the time, how could they escape a cruel and severe retribution?

    鼠怪
    　　邑章氏，家有空室三楹，多年扃闭，云其中多怪。有武夫寓其家，欲觇其异，每隔窗窥视，俱无所睹。一夕，月明鉴物，见堂中有男妇数人，皆衣锦绣，身高尺许。视案上立二人，高如前状，苇帽袍褂，相对揖。武夫以铁丸击中其一，蹶然倒案上。余俱不见。入室视之，乃一巨鼠，苇帽则鸡子壳，袍褂则青兰纸耳。
    Mouse Monster
    　　In the town of Zhang, there was a family whose house had three empty rooms that had been closed and locked for many years, with rumors of many strange occurrences inside. A martial man stayed in their house, wanting to see if there were any strange things, and looked through the window every now and then, but saw nothing at all. One night, the moon was bright enough to illuminate objects, and he saw several men and women in the hall, all dressed in brocade and embroidery, about a foot tall. He saw two people standing on the table, as tall as before, wearing reed hats and coats, bowing to each other. The martial man hit one of them with an iron ball, and it fell over and collapsed on the table. The rest disappeared. He went into the room to look at it, and it was a large rat. The reed hat was made of a chicken eggshell, and the coat was made of blue paper.

    227

    醉茶子曰：据人之室为己之宅，是与鹊巢鸠居者无异。初不料有好事之武生奋博浪之技也。虽然，揖让雍容，犹胜于世之强霸。真人而无礼，不知鼠之有礼矣。吾愿以鸠自居者，勉为鼠焉可也。
    梦诗
    　　邑高茂才，雨后昼寝，梦二女入室，艳妆绝代，芳馥袭人，谓高曰：“高郎高郎，何乃抑郁？”高询其姓氏，二女笑云：“尚未蟒玉，何竟目不识人？”高熟视，似曾相识。问其何为，二女握其腕云：“子好游乎？吾与子游。”高喜，随行。再顾并非己斋，翰墨图书，罔不古雅。俄一小鬟唤二女出。高视案头一卷，乃《闺秀集》，中多佳句。读未竟，为家人唤醒。曾记有句云：“远水有情招客渡，岸花含笑向人开。”

    Explanation

    227

    The Drunken Tea Seed said: "Taking someone else's house as one's own home is no different from a magpie occupying a crow's nest." At first, he did not expect to encounter a skilled martial artist. However, even if one is a true person without manners, they do not know that a mouse can have manners. I wish those who occupy others' places would strive to be like mice, and that would be sufficient.

    Dream Poem

    The talented scholar from the town of Gao, after a rainstorm, slept during the day and dreamed that two women entered his room. They were adorned in exquisite makeup, unparalleled in beauty, and their fragrance filled the air. They called out to Gao, saying, "Gao郎, Gao郎, why are you so depressed?" Gao inquired about their names, and the two women laughed and said, "We have not yet acquired something precious, yet you do not even recognize us?" Gao looked at them closely and felt as though he had seen them before. He asked them what they wanted, and the two women took his wrist and said, "Do you enjoy traveling? We will travel with you." Gao was delighted and followed them. Upon looking back, he realized it was not his own study, but the room was filled with ancient and elegant books and writings. Soon, a young maid called out for the two women to leave. Gao glanced at a book on the table, which was a collection of poems by women, containing many beautiful verses. Before he could finish reading, he was awakened by his family. He remembered a line from the poem: "The distant water has feelings and invites guests to cross, while the flowers by the shore smile and open to people."

    228

    鬼戏
    　　昌黎某甲，贸易山海关外，每往集镇负贩，辄骑驴夜行。一夕，途次失道，窜山谷间，遥闻钲鼓喧阗，洋洋盈耳。关外固多影戏，未之怪也。转思野旷人稀，何得有此？下骑四顾，忽眼前楼阁壮丽，身立其庭中。堂前纸帧布帷，将演影戏。中设一几，旁列数座。几上茶铛茗盏，器具都精，一赤瑙盘，满堆瓜果。俄有二少女扶一老妪，白发龙钟，居中对座。旁虚二座，似尚有眷客未来者。未几，傀儡登场，舞蹈应节。妪观之喜，取盘中餐与二女共啖，颐颊鼓动如老猿。

    Explanation

    228

    Ghost Play
    A certain person from Changli, who traded outside Shanhaiguan, would often travel to market towns at night on a donkey. One evening, he lost his way on the journey and hid in the mountains and valleys. From afar, he heard the sound of gongs and drums, filling his ears with a grand noise. There are many shadow plays in the area outside the pass, so he did not find it strange. Then, thinking of the wilderness with few people, he wondered how such a thing could exist here. He dismounted and looked around, suddenly seeing magnificent buildings in front of him, finding himself standing in the courtyard. In front of the hall, there were paper screens and cloth curtains, about to perform a shadow play. A table was set in the middle, with several seats arranged on the sides. On the table, there were a kettle and cups for tea, all the utensils were exquisite, and a red jade dish filled with fruits and melons. Soon, two young girls supported an old woman, with white hair and frail, sitting facing each other in the center. Two seats were empty on the side, suggesting that some guests were still to come. Soon after, the puppets took the stage, dancing in time with the music. The old woman watched happily, taking food from the dish and eating with the two girls, her cheeks and jaws moving like an old monkey.

    229

    某怪其非人，心益恐，乃敲石取火吸烟。伫立以视。烟烬叩筒，击地一响，妪与二女头并落几上，然牙齿震震，啮物如故。某急敲双镫，见景物如烟云，飘然退去里许，而灯影乐声，犹历历在望。跨卫急奔，前途一车当路，车中连呼救人。骇而问故，车夫言迷路遇鬼，所见与己同。询其姓氏，知车中乃富商某氏之妻。甲与商素识，遂同行。乃晓至关别去。好事者诬妇与车夫夜遁，商父子贸易于外，闻其事以为丑，先遣子归出妇。至家遇甲，备言其异，疑谤均息焉。

    Explanation

    229

    A strange thing occurred to Jia, who found it strange that it was not a human, and his heart grew more fearful. He struck a stone to light a fire and smoked. Standing still to observe, he knocked the ash of the cigarette into the tube, hit the ground with a sound, and the old woman and two girls' heads fell onto the table. However, their teeth chattered, and they bit objects as usual. Jia quickly struck the double stirrups, and the scene vanished like smoke and clouds, flying away about a li, while the lights and music were still clearly visible. Leaping onto the guard and rushing, he encountered a carriage blocking the road ahead. The person in the carriage repeatedly called for help. Startled and asking the reason, the driver said he had gotten lost and met a ghost, and what he saw was the same as Jia's. Asking for their surname, Jia knew that the person in the carriage was the wife of a wealthy merchant. Jia and the merchant were acquainted, so they traveled together. They then reached the pass and parted ways. Some people spread rumors that the woman and the driver fled at night, and the merchant and his father were trading outside. Hearing of the incident, they considered it disgraceful and sent their son back to send the wife away. When they arrived home, they met Jia, and he told all about the strange events. All doubts and slander were dispelled.

    230

    黑山大王
    　　邑李氏家有蟒仙，曰“黑山大王”，能隐见其形，细则如蚓，巨则如瓮。其家有吉凶事，则预示之兆。故孝氏供奉甚虔。有索债者，横施无礼，偃息厅间，肆口辱詈。主人隐忍，无如之何。夜有一武士推扉入，铁铠黑甲，手持利剑，叱云：“吾为主人除此患。”忿欲加刃，某惧逸去。后数载，主人梦一黑甲将军拜别而去。自此家遂落。予友戏谓予曰：“仆负债既多，受侮不少，安得黑山辱临，为之一泄积忿哉！”
    龙眠穴乡人陈氏，卜葬新阡，掘地尺许，术人戒勿掘，云其中有生龙，主后昆发越。工人嗤其诬妄，窥其他去，窃发视之，有二鲤鱼跃出。惧而瘗诸其侧，陈与术士均不知也。及开故坟，棺下多芦苇，有绿蛇盘据无算。亦发祥地也。后陈氏零落焉。

    Explanation

    230

    The Black Mountain Lord

    In the Li family of a certain town, there was a python spirit known as the "Black Mountain Lord," which could hide or reveal its form, being as small as a worm or as large as a jar. Whenever there were auspicious or ominous events in the family, it would foretell them. Therefore, the Li family worshiped it with great reverence. There was a creditor who acted rudely and unreasonably, lying down in the hall and speaking insultingly. The master endured in silence, unable to do anything about it. One night, a warrior pushed open the door and entered, wearing iron armor and a black helmet, holding a sharp sword. He shouted, "I am here to rid the master of this trouble." Angered to strike with the sword, I was afraid and fled. After several years, the master dreamed of a black-armored general bowing and leaving. From then on, the family declined. My friend jokingly said to me, "I have many debts and have suffered much humiliation; how I wish the Black Mountain could come and relieve my accumulated anger!"

    In a village near a dragon's lair, there was a man named Chen. He chose a new burial site and began digging. A diviner warned against digging, saying there was a living dragon inside, which would ensure the prosperity of future generations. The workers laughed at it as false and absurd, watched him leave, and secretly dug to see. Two carp leaped out. Afraid, they buried them beside the site. Chen and the diviner were both unaware of what happened. When they opened the old grave, there were many reeds under the coffin, and countless green snakes were coiled. It was also a place of good fortune. Later, the Chen family declined.

    231

    醉茶子曰：宋倪父云：“坟地好不如心地好，墓田好不如心田好。”洵良言也。先人既安窀穸，百余年后复颠倒之，纵使子孙荣贵，彼化者不安于夜合，忍乎哉！奉劝世人，无惑于堪舆之说而妄迁先茔，则福地只在方寸中矣。昔有瞽僧，云游于上谷，善医术，投剂辄效，而不以术自鸣。有晋人高建明，拜求其术。僧云：“明医亦死人，风鉴亦受贫。”亦见到语也。
    冯氏仆
    　　人之梦中呓语，各有不同。如心有所思，梦中脱口而出者，由于神气之亏；白昼所作，夜间信口而言者，由于气质之惰。又有或哭或歌，种种无足奇也。大兴冯氏仆，愚蠢人也。生平目不识丁，不晓书为何物。每夜熟睡，则高声朗诵《学》《庸》全部，或读《毛诗》。自始至末，不脱只字，胜于开卷。唤醒询之，则茫然不解。主人谓其有夙根，试教之读，则终日不能识数字。是盖灵鬼凭之，借其口以为戏，又不可以梦魇同日语也。岂真如吴下阿蒙，呓语通《周易》哉？

    Explanation

    231

    醉茶子曰：宋倪父云：“坟地好不如心地好，墓田好不如心田好。”洵良言也。先人既安窀穸，百余年后复颠倒之，纵使子孙荣贵，彼化者不安于夜合，忍乎哉！奉劝世人，无惑于堪舆之说而妄迁先茔，则福地只在方寸中矣。昔有瞽僧，云游于上谷，善医术，投剂辄效，而不以术自鸣。有晋人高建明，拜求其术。僧云：“明医亦死人，风鉴亦受贫。”亦见到语也。

    In the words of the Drunken Tea Master: Ni of the Song dynasty once said, "A good burial site is not as important as a good heart, and a good grave is not as important as a good mind." Indeed, these are wise words. Once the ancestors have been laid to rest in their graves, even after a hundred years, to disturb them again would be to cause them distress in the afterlife, even if the descendants become prosperous and honored. I advise all people not to be misled by the teachings of geomancy and to move ancestral graves without reason, for the blessed land lies within one's heart. In the past, there was a blind monk who wandered in Shanggu, skilled in medicine, and his remedies always worked, yet he did not boast of his abilities. A man from the Jin dynasty named Gao Jianming sought to learn his skills. The monk replied, "A skilled physician can still die, and fortune-telling can still lead to poverty." These are also true words.

    冯氏仆
    　　人之梦中呓语，各有不同。如心有所思，梦中脱口而出者，由于神气之亏；白昼所作，夜间信口而言者，由于气质之惰。又有或哭或歌，种种无足奇也。大兴冯氏仆，愚蠢人也。生平目不识丁，不晓书为何物。每夜熟睡，则高声朗诵《学》《庸》全部，或读《毛诗》。自始至末，不脱只字，胜于开卷。唤醒询之，则茫然不解。主人谓其有夙根，试教之读，则终日不能识数字。是盖灵鬼凭之，借其口以为戏，又不可以梦魇同日语也。岂真如吴下阿蒙，呓语通《周易》哉？

    As for the servant of the Feng family from Daxing, people's rambling speech in their dreams varies. If one has something on their mind, it may be spoken aloud in a dream due to a deficiency in spirit. If one does things during the day, they may be spoken casually at night due to a lack of discipline in one's nature. There are also those who cry or sing in their sleep, but such things are not worth being surprised about. The servant of the Feng family was a foolish man. Throughout his life, he could not read a single character and did not know what books were. Every night when he was deeply asleep, he would loudly recite the entire Xue and Yong or read the Mao Shi. From beginning to end, he did not miss a single word, surpassing those who merely open a book. When he was awakened and asked about it, he was completely unaware. His master believed he had inborn talent and tried to teach him to read, but he could not recognize even a few characters all day. This was likely because a spirit possessed him, using his mouth for play, and it cannot be compared to a nightmare. Is it truly like Wu Xia Ameng, who can understand the Yijing in his rambling speech?

    232

    於菟大鬼
    　　金安人，邑王君小亭之妻，夫亡，绝粒死。初，王就武遂盐馆，嫌其居室隘陋。后院有楼三楹，颇高爽，久无人居，相传有怪异。王喜其静，扫除尘埃，设帐居楼下，夏月乘凉，殊觉快意。居数日，梦与人交，初以为偶然耳，不之怪。继则连夜皆然，乃假寐以俟之。有少女姗姗而来，启衾共卧。王抱而睇之，貌绝艳，心知其妖，而恋其美，戏订来夕约。女笑许之。于是无夕不至，王觉困疲。已而疾作，医药罔效，奄奄待毙。乃谓女曰：“情好如我两人，固非怨偶，予羸弱如此，不堪旦旦而伐，倘欲竭我脂膏，请从此绝。”女忿然曰：“汝尚欲生乎？吾实告尔，奔波固非所愿，为上仙驱使，不敢不奉命耳。

    Explanation

    232

    A Tiger Ghost

    Jin Anren was the wife of Wang Xiangting, a local official. After her husband's death, she starved herself to death. Initially, Wang took up residence at the Wu Su Saltworks, finding the living quarters narrow and shabby. The back courtyard had a three-story building that was quite high and cool, though it had been unoccupied for a long time and was rumored to be haunted. Wang liked the quietness and cleaned the dust, setting up his bed under the eaves. In the summer, he enjoyed the coolness and felt very content. After a few days, he dreamed of having relations with someone, initially thinking it was just a coincidence and not finding it strange. Soon, however, it happened every night, so he pretended to sleep and waited for it. A young girl came gracefully and opened the quilt to lie down with him. Wang held her and looked at her, finding her extremely beautiful. He knew she was a ghost but was captivated by her beauty and playfully made an appointment for the next night.
