The girl smiled and agreed. From then on, she came every night, and Wang became exhausted. Soon, he fell ill, and despite medical treatment, his condition worsened, and he lay on the brink of death. He then told the girl, "Our affection for each other is not that of a resentful couple. I am so weak, I cannot endure such frequent exertion. If you wish to drain my vitality, please leave me alone." The girl angrily said, "Do you still want to live? I will tell you the truth: I do not want to go on like this, but I am being driven by the Heavenly Immortals and dare not disobey their commands."

    233

    ”王唏嘘无言，女强与交合而去。王大惧，遣二仆伴寝。女来如故，遂卒于鹾馆。妻闻王死，悲悼不食，梦王谓曰：“卿之贞节，足光吾门户。顾有仇未复，奈何？予所居之楼上，有老魅曰於菟大鬼，性极悍暴，日遣数妖婢四出采精，供其补炼。予实死于是。卿之正气足以相敌，请雪此恨。”醒而异之，于是死志益坚。姊妹戚党，苦劝勿听。体渐惫不能起，呕吐勿止，满室生异香。乃谓众曰：“予魂适至武遂盐馆，见楼上一厉鬼青面朱髯，旁立数妖婢，见予俱奔避。予擒其一婢诘之，即杀吾夫者。今捉之来，汝辈试嗅之。”众觉血腥扑鼻，相与骇异。问擒来如何，曰：“将赴阎君。”越数日，唇焦口枯，整裳而逝。
    　　醉茶子赞曰：卓哉金氏，至刚至仁！殉夫泉壤，重义轻身。生为烈女，没作贞魂。彼大厉胡为哉，宜乎朝廷旌表，血食千古，俎豆常新。

    Explanation

    233

    Wang was moved to tears and speechless, and the woman forced him to have intercourse and then left. Wang was greatly afraid, so he sent two servants to accompany him to bed. The woman came as before, and he died in the salt shop. Upon hearing of Wang's death, his wife mourned deeply and refused to eat. She dreamed that Wang told her, "Your chastity is sufficient to honor our family. But there is still an enemy to be avenged, what shall we do? On the upper floor of where I lived, there is an old demon called the Great Ghost of the Tiger, who is extremely fierce and violent. He sends out several demon maids every day to gather essence, to supply his alchemy. I actually died because of this. Your righteous spirit is enough to match him. Please avenge this grudge." Upon waking, she found it strange, and her resolve to die became even stronger. Her sisters and relatives tried hard to dissuade her, but she would not listen. Her body gradually grew weak and she could not get up, and she continued to vomit without stopping. A strange fragrance filled the room. She then told the others, "My soul just arrived at the salt shop in Wusu, where I saw an evil ghost with a green face and red beard on the upper floor. Several demon maids stood beside him, and they all fled upon seeing me. I captured one of the maids and questioned her, and she is the one who killed my husband. Now I have brought her here, you all can smell her." Everyone felt a strong smell of blood, and they were all startled. They asked what happened to the one she caught, and she said, "She will be taken to the Lord of the Underworld." After several days, her lips were parched, her mouth dry, and she dressed herself before passing away.

    醉茶子赞曰：卓哉金氏，至刚至仁！殉夫泉壤，重义轻身。生为烈女，没作贞魂。彼大厉胡为哉，宜乎朝廷旌表，血食千古，俎豆常新。

    Zui Chazǐ praised, "Outstanding is Jin, who is both extremely strong and benevolent! She sacrificed her life with her husband in the grave, valuing righteousness over her own body. In life, she was a valiant woman; in death, she became a chaste spirit. What great evil is this? It is fitting that the court honored her, and her spirit will be venerated for a thousand years, with her name always fresh in the minds of people."

    234

    稻田鬼
    　　盘沽李姓家，稻田旁临墟墓，雇佣三人，一黄姓，一王姓，一张姓，与李同操作。夜则四人换班作息。一夜，王灌田，黄开渠，行至田畔，见冢上坐一女子，缞麻凶服，对月悲泣。黄大惊，冷汗如雨，急曳锸而返。王询故，黄诡言身倦，遣王往，己代王转辘轳。王去半晌，亦奔归，深怨黄之讹己，诟谇久之。黄与王议共归庄，使李、张来。二人见亦如前。于是四人一同往，持械助寻，则乌有矣。
    疟鬼
    　　赵某，平阳人。夏月昼寝，朦胧间见一妇人搴帘入，白衣麻裙，面貌黄肿，眉目戚戚然，神色可畏。逼近床榻，以手按其胸，便觉气闷如噎，寒热交作。及晚患疟，越日稍愈。妇复来，疟又作。如是月余，形骸骨立，盛暑常着重绵。或教以桃木剑钉床四隅，更粘符于壁。妇至，瞋目怒视，指划不敢近前。赵急狂呼，妇取锡檠掷于地，悻悻而去，后不复来，病亦渐瘳。

    Explanation

    234

    The Ghost of the Rice Fields
    In the household of a man surnamed Li in the village of Panggu, the rice fields were adjacent to a burial ground. They hired three men: one surnamed Huang, one surnamed Wang, and one surnamed Zhang, who worked alongside Li. At night, the four took turns working and resting. One night, Wang was irrigating the fields while Huang was digging a canal. As they approached the edge of the field, they saw a woman sitting on a grave, dressed in mourning attire, weeping mournfully under the moon. Huang was greatly startled, sweating coldly, and quickly pulled his spade and fled. Wang asked why, and Huang lied that he was tired, sending Wang to continue the work while he took over turning the waterwheel. After a while, Wang also ran back, deeply resenting Huang for deceiving him, and they argued for a long time. Huang and Wang decided to return to the village together, sending Li and Zhang to continue the work. When Li and Zhang arrived, they saw the same woman as before. The four then went together, armed with tools to search for her, but found nothing.

    The Malaria Ghost
    Zhao, a man from Pingyang, was resting during the day in the summer when, in a daze, he saw a woman pull aside the curtain and enter. She wore a white hemp dress, her face swollen and yellow, with a sorrowful expression in her eyes and brows, and a terrifying demeanor. She approached the bed and placed her hand on his chest, causing him to feel suffocated as if choking, with alternating chills and fever. By evening, he fell ill with malaria, and the next day he was slightly better. The woman returned, and the malaria recurred. For a month, his body became skin and bones, and in the height of summer, he always wore thick wool. Someone taught him to nail a peachwood sword to the four corners of his bed and to attach charms to the walls. When the woman returned, she glared angrily, pointing and gesturing, not daring to come closer. Zhao shouted wildly, and the woman took a tin candlestick and threw it to the ground, leaving angrily. She did not return, and his illness gradually subsided.

    235

    醉茶子曰：疟之有鬼，信然乎？肝胆藏魄，人之妄见，责之此经有邪，固不可以有鬼论也。
    李茂才
    　　河间李佛桐茂才，边公巨峰之戚。边公司铎吾邑时，李寄宿于学官之乡贤祠，夜诵金刚经，帘外鬼影幢幢，往来不绝。李坦然，鬼亦不扰。仆居别室，鬼屡揶揄，移居李屋则安。
    花果楼
    　　邑担夫夏某，卤莽多力。妻患邪祟，发时则大声言曰：“吾居花果楼甚潇洒，胡为以茅塞径，致我闷藏欲死？”言毕，毁物无算。夏不堪其扰，延术士驱遣无效。一日，于窑室中柴后得花竹筐，中有大猬二头，始悟此物为妖也，杀之。

    Explanation

    235

    The Wine-Tea Sage said: Is it indeed true that malaria is caused by ghosts? The liver and gallbladder house the soul, and human delusions are blamed on evil in these organs, so it is certainly not something that can be attributed to ghosts.

    Li Maocai
    Li Maocai of Hejian was related to Bian Gongjufeng. When Bian Gongjufeng was stationed in our county, Li Maocai stayed at the Hall of the Virtuous in the schoolhouse, where he recited the Diamond Sutra at night. Outside the curtain, ghostly shadows appeared and disappeared without end. Li Maocai remained calm, and the ghosts did not disturb him. His servant lived in a separate room and was frequently teased by the ghosts, but when he moved into Li Maocai's room, he found peace.

    Flower and Fruit Tower
    Xia Some, a shoulder pole bearer from our county, was rough and strong. His wife suffered from supernatural disturbances, and when she was afflicted, she would loudly say, "I lived in the Flower and Fruit Tower, which was very elegant, but why did you block the path with grass, causing me to feel suffocated and want to die?" After she finished speaking, she destroyed countless objects. Xia Some could no longer bear the disturbance and invited a sorcerer to drive the spirits away, but it was ineffective. One day, he found a basket made of flower and bamboo in a kiln room behind some firewood, containing two large hedgehogs. He then realized that these creatures were the source of the evil, and he killed them.

    236

    龚姓
    　　邑有鱼贩龚叟，将曙，至南郊。闻隔沟有人呼其名，翁视之，乃其邻女。然旧岁物故，已瘗之矣。龚问何为，女云：“求伯负儿渡水，则感德无既。”翁涉水过，负女于背，问：“尔在此，曾思家否？若思家，予负汝入城，寻汝父母。”女力辞不愿。翁不听，负至城门。女挣欲遁，翁力持之，急呼门人。众视之，乃棺板也，斧之。
    陈氏怪
    　　太原陈氏，侨居于津。每夜闻复室中有声隆隆然，如转碌碡。以灯烛之，即亦暂止。又半载，白昼亦然。窥之，有老叟长须彩服，高仅二尺，身圆几如小瓮，绕地旋转，其声随之。闻人语即遁去。细穷其处，似在柜后。移柜视之，有纸糊不倒翁，酷似所见。毁之，怪绝。盖物大肖人形，感天地之精气，即足为妖。故作俑者，圣人所不取也。

    Explanation

    236

    Translation

    The Gong Family

    In the town, there was a fish vendor named Old Gong. As dawn approached, he went to the southern suburbs. He heard someone calling his name from across the ditch. The old man looked and saw it was his neighbor's daughter. But last year, she had passed away and was already buried. Old Gong asked what she wanted. The girl said, "I want you to carry me across the water, and I will be grateful forever." The old man waded through the water and carried the girl on his back. He asked, "Have you thought about your family? If you miss them, I will carry you into the city to find your parents." The girl strongly refused. The old man did not listen and carried her to the city gate. The girl struggled to escape, but the old man held her tightly and called for the gate guards. When the guards looked, they saw it was a coffin board, and they chopped it open.

    The Teng Family's Strange Occurrence

    The Teng family from Taiyuan had settled in Jin. Every night, they heard a loud, rumbling sound in the back room, as if a large stone roller were being turned. When they lit a lamp to investigate, the sound would temporarily stop. After half a year, the sound also occurred during the day. Upon closer inspection, they saw an old man with long beard and colorful clothing, barely two feet tall, with a round body almost like a small jar, spinning around the floor. The sound followed him. When they spoke to him, he would vanish. Upon closer examination, it seemed to be behind the cabinet. They moved the cabinet and found a paper-mache toppling doll that closely resembled the old man. After destroying the doll, the strange occurrences ceased. It is said that objects that greatly resemble human form and are influenced by the essence of heaven and earth can become spirits. Therefore, those who create such objects are not approved by the sages.

    237

    医术
    　　邑吴某，落魄奇穷，友为谋范阳鹾馆。行起程矣，忽疽发于背，红肿坟起，痛彻心髓。有华姓医与之善，延视之，云：“此搭背也，发则必死。”吴哀求不已，华慨然曰：“怜君一贫彻骨，初有生路而获此奇疴，八口何以全活？吾欲巧夺天功，以挽回君命。但今日病愈，明春仍作。不拘何处，见有微疮起者，即此证也。医识为搭背，则令治之，否则速归，吾为施治。幸勿轻忽，性命有关焉。”吴唯唯。华为投剂，次日疽消，欣然就道。至范阳数月，时暮春，觉肢体不快，开襟见肘上生一疮，形如黍粒，不甚痛楚。

    Explanation

    237

    Medical Skill

    In a certain county, there was a man named Wu who was extremely destitute and without prospects. A friend secured him a position at the salt administration office in Fan Yang. Just as he was about to start his journey, he suddenly developed a severe boil on his back, which became red, swollen, and painful, piercing his very marrow. A doctor named Hua, who was acquainted with him, was called to examine him. The doctor said, "This is a case of daba (a severe form of boils), and if it develops, it will be fatal." Wu pleaded incessantly, and Hua, moved by his plight, said, "I pity you for being so utterly poor. You have just found a way to make a living, yet you have contracted this strange illness. How will your eight family members survive? I wish to perform a miracle to save your life. But today, your illness will be cured, but next spring, it will recur. No matter where you are, if you see a small sore appear, it is this condition. If a doctor recognizes it as daba, they should treat it. Otherwise, return quickly, and I will treat you. Do not neglect it, for it concerns your life." Wu nodded in agreement. Hua prescribed medicine, and the next day, the boil disappeared. Wu started his journey with joy. After several months in Fan Yang, it was late spring, and he began to feel discomfort in his body. When he opened his clothes, he saw a sore on his elbow, shaped like a grain of millet, not very painful.

    238

    然忆华言，惴惴然郑重之。迭延数医，佥云无害。末延一铃医视之，惊曰：“肤紫肉凸，非寻常症！何形是而部位非耶？岂我学有未逮欤？”坚辞欲行。吴请言其故，医云：“酷似搭背，然生于肘上，予故不敢知也。”吴赞叹不已，告其故而求医之。医惊曰：“贵乡有此国手，高我一筹矣。”出药治之，月余而愈。于是结为至交，酬钱不受，曰：“予足迹半天下，愿觏奇人。君乡人，良工也，吾愿识荆焉。请赐荐书，胜厚贶矣。”吴为修书，备赆以往。及至津，而华已故。盖医看证之日，即华捐馆之日也，浩叹而返。
    　　醉茶子曰：人生得一知己，足以无憾。铃医乃华之知己也，神乎技矣，可称一时瑜亮。而人定胜天，遂遭造物之忌，岂医术固不贵乎活人耶？无怪乎草菅人命者，皆寿如龟鹤矣。

    Explanation

    238

    Translation:

    Recalling what Hua had said, I felt a deep sense of anxiety and took it very seriously. I consulted several doctors in succession, all of whom said it was not harmful. Finally, I called upon a traveling doctor to examine him, and he was surprised and said, "The skin is purple and the flesh is protruding—it is not a common illness! How is the shape right but the location wrong? Is it that my learning is incomplete?" He firmly declined and wanted to leave. Wu asked for the reason, and the doctor said, "It is very similar to a boil on the back, but it is on the elbow; I therefore dare not know." Wu was greatly impressed and told him the reason, asking for treatment. The doctor was surprised and said, "Your hometown has such a master, one step ahead of me." He prescribed medicine for it, and he recovered after a month. They then became close friends, and the doctor refused payment, saying, "My travels have covered half the world; I wish to meet extraordinary people. You are from your hometown, a skilled craftsman; I wish to make your acquaintance. Please give me a letter of recommendation, which is more valuable than a large gift." Wu wrote a letter and prepared a gift to send. When he arrived at the ferry, Hua had already passed away. It was on the day the doctor examined him that Hua passed away, and he sighed deeply and returned.

    The Drunk Tea Scholar said: "To have one true friend in life is enough to have no regrets." The traveling doctor was Hua's true friend, his skill was divine, and he can be called a contemporary of equal talent. But man can conquer nature, and thus was envied by fate. Is it that medical skill is not valued for saving lives? It is no wonder that those who trample on human life live as long as turtles and cranes.

    239

    妖术
    　　道光丙午秋，邑针市街洋货栈房，忽有男妇五六人登门乞钱。内一人将柜上珠盘略为拨弄，随即散去。铺中人均不在意。至晚间开柜取银，封锁如故，而银乌有矣。殆行乞者之妖术也。或云银袱中置米一掬，则其术不行矣。
    娇娥
    　　商辂，字子车，汉阳人。偶游郊外，见白鹅泛泳水滨，呼之，缓缓而来，集足下。商抱归斋中，颇驯，饲以稻粱，如获珍宝。一夕，友人招饮，返扃而去。及归，鹅已失去，遍询邻里，皆云未见。夜宿斋中，觉有人同寝处。烛之，美人也，肌理细腻，肤如凝脂，遂相燕好。晓失所在，夜复来。疑其狐仙，苦诘之。女云：“妾名娇娥，天府中司夜之宫人也。偶有小过，谪向人间，与君有夙分，故此缱绻。”商曰：“池上鹅儿得非卿耶？”女笑而不答。

    Explanation

    239

    A Trick of the Beggar
    In the autumn of Daoguang's third year of the Bingwu cycle, at a foreign goods warehouse on the needle market street in the town, suddenly several men and women appeared at the door asking for money. One of them slightly manipulated the pearl tray on the counter and then left. The merchants in the shop did not pay much attention. That evening, when they opened the cabinet to retrieve the silver, it was still sealed as before, but the silver was nowhere to be found. It was likely a trick of the beggars. Someone suggested that placing a handful of rice in the silver cloth could prevent such a trick from working.

    The Beauty Jiao'e
    Shang Lu, courtesy name Ziche, was from Hanyang. Once, while strolling in the outskirts, he saw a white goose swimming leisurely by the water's edge. He called it, and it slowly came to him, gathering at his feet. Shang took it back to his study, where it was quite tame. He fed it rice and grains, treating it as a precious treasure. One evening, a friend invited him for a drink, and upon returning, he locked himself in his room. When he came back, the goose had disappeared. He asked around the neighborhood, but no one had seen it. That night, as he slept in his study, he felt someone sleeping beside him. When he lit a candle, he saw a beautiful woman with smooth and delicate skin, like congealed fat. They spent the night together. The next morning, she was gone, but she returned that night. Shang suspected she might be a fox spirit and tried to question her. She said, "My name is Jiao'e, and I am a night attendant in the heavenly palace. I was demoted to the human world due to a minor offense, and I have a predestined connection with you, which is why we are so close." Shang asked, "Is the white goose by the pond not you?" The woman smiled but did not answer.

    240

    商曰：“卿是仙人，苟冒然而来，仆敢不纳，乌用是貌貌者为哉！”女曰：“我辈灵气所钟，必凭生物以游人世，不必身即是鹅也。”商曰：“世之灵物，修炼成仙，抑又何说？”女曰：“是非君所知也，请勿多疑，当为君生贵子而后去。”绸缪年余，忽谓商曰：“妾谪限已满，行与君别。”乃盛妆出门。有彩霞万道，从空堕地，层层如丹梯。女踏之，冉冉入云端而没。归见床头一巨卵如瓜，俄而自破，有婴儿攒抱其中。视之，男也，方面大耳，貌殊不凡。遣乳媪哺之。及长，智虑过人，善贸易。时中青犯禁，私货之。不数载，富雄一乡，以捐纳例授头品焉。

    Explanation

    240

    The Immortal Woman

    The merchant said, "You are an immortal, if you come boldly, I dare not refuse, but what use is there in having someone who looks ordinary?" The woman replied, "We are blessed with spiritual energy and must rely on living beings to wander among humans, not necessarily being a goose." The merchant asked, "If spiritual beings become immortals through cultivation, what else can be said?" The woman said, "This is beyond your understanding. Please do not doubt, I will give you a noble son before I leave." They were intimate for over a year, and suddenly she told the merchant, "My punishment period is over, I will leave you now." She then dressed in fine clothes and departed. There were thousands of rainbow-colored clouds falling from the sky, layer by layer like a red ladder. The woman stepped on them and gradually disappeared into the clouds. Upon returning, she found a huge egg by the bed, resembling a melon. Soon after, it broke spontaneously, revealing a baby gathered in the arms inside. Upon seeing him, it was a boy with a broad face, large ears, and an extraordinary appearance. She sent a nurse to feed him. When he grew up, he was exceptionally wise and skilled in trade. During a time of prohibition, he smuggled goods. In a short while, he became wealthy and powerful in one village or town, and was bestowed the highest rank through donation and recommendation.

    241

    乩仙
    　　邑邵某，偶因小事与妻反目，妻愤欲授缳。及晚，邵出，有美女子立灯下，靓丽如仙，呼邵妻为姊，邀与同游。乃掷绳于壁，便见壁豁然空洞如月门，其间奇花异草，迥异凡境。邵妻辞不愿往，女强曳之。正撑拒间，屋隅出一老妇，椎髻葛衫，雄健如伟男子，手持木杖，横击女臀。女扑地而灭。邵妻一惊，再视，人物俱杳，而邵自外归矣。方邵之出也，在友人处扶乩，乩仙云：“君室有急难，已遣拙荆往救矣。”邢骇问故，仙云：“君归自知。”至家询妻，备言其故，盖缢鬼求代，逢仙拯也。

    Explanation

    241

    A Spirit's Intervention

    In a certain village, there was a man named Shao who, due to a trivial matter, fell out with his wife. His wife, filled with anger, wanted to hang herself. That evening, as Shao left the house, a beautiful woman stood by the lamp, radiant like a fairy. She called Shao's wife "sister" and invited her to go on a journey together. The woman then threw a rope onto the wall, and suddenly, the wall opened up like a moon-like arch, revealing a realm filled with exotic flowers and plants, completely different from the mundane world. Shao's wife declined to go, but the woman forcibly dragged her. Just as they were struggling, an old woman appeared from the corner of the room. She had a topknot and wore a coarse cloth shirt, appearing strong and robust like a great man. She held a wooden staff and struck the woman's buttocks horizontally. The woman fell to the ground and disappeared. Shao's wife was startled and looked again, but all people and things had vanished. Meanwhile, Shao had returned home. While he was out, he was doing spirit-writing at a friend's place. The spirit said, "There is an urgent trouble in your home, and I have sent your wife to save it." Shao was startled and asked why, and the spirit replied, "You will know when you return." When he returned home and asked his wife, she told him everything. It turned out that the spirit of the hanged person was seeking a substitute and met and was saved by a fairy.

    242

    醉茶子曰：世之扶乩召仙，大半皆人之腕力，视为真仙，则惑之甚矣。顾扶乩一事，偶尔游戏则可，若藉以占吉凶、治疾病，鲜有不误事者，君子审之。
    矢魔
    　　蒲阴有怪曰“矢魔”，状如布囊，恒夜出，远闻臭气，即知魔至。急避之，物自过，不为人害也。或猝不及避，则粪汁污衣，臭秽不可耐。居人不以为怪，然亦奇矣，岂粪壤而亦为妖乎？友人戏曰：“是必精于墨艺者，没后为此怪。不然遗臭之外，更无他长，安能谓腐朽为神奇哉？”

    Explanation

    242

    醉茶子曰：世之扶乩召仙，大半皆人之腕力，视为真仙，则惑之甚矣。顾扶乩一事，偶尔游戏则可，若藉以占吉凶、治疾病，鲜有不误事者，君子审之。

    醉茶子说：世上那些通过扶乩来召唤神仙的行为，大多都是依靠人的手腕之力，如果把这些当作真正的神仙，则是被大大地欺骗了。然而，扶乩作为一种活动，偶尔当作游戏是可以的，但如果用来占卜吉凶、治疗疾病，几乎没有什么不失误的，君子应当审慎对待。

    矢魔
    　　蒲阴有怪曰“矢魔”，状如布囊，恒夜出，远闻臭气，即知魔至。急避之，物自过，不为人害也。或猝不及避，则粪汁污衣，臭秽不可耐。居人不以为怪，然亦奇矣，岂粪壤而亦为妖乎？友人戏曰：“是必精于墨艺者，没后为此怪。不然遗臭之外，更无他长，安能谓腐朽为神奇哉？”

    在蒲阴这个地方有一种怪异的现象被称为“矢魔”，它的形状像一个布袋，常常在夜晚出现，远远就能闻到它散发出的恶臭，就知道“矢魔”来了。人们急忙躲避，它就会自行离去，不会伤害人。有时来不及躲避，就会被它的粪便弄脏衣服，那气味让人难以忍受。当地居民对此并不感到奇怪，但这也确实是一件奇特的事情，难道连粪土也能成为妖怪吗？一位朋友开玩笑地说：“这一定是某个擅长书法的人死后变成的怪异现象。否则除了留下恶臭之外，再没有别的优点，怎么能说腐朽的东西能变得神奇呢？”

    243

    鼠媪
    　　邑费茂才，客宜安时，与友人饮于王氏别业，主人留与憾戏。夜有老妪推扉入，白发兰衫，形貌枯瘦，双目瞠瞠然。立几前，遍视赌具。费疑其眷属，欲与周旋。主人急摇手止之，似欲客勿顾也者。众见其神色惨淡，皆生疑惧。中一客问其为谁妪，不答，从容出门去。主人曰：“此鼠精也，居此园中二百余年矣。每见有赌局辄至，人习见，不之惧也。仆偶未虑此，致使怪来惊客，获罪多矣。然此物虽常出没，从不祸人，故相安之。”次日费辞归。
    　　醉茶子曰：鼠之为物，蠢然耳，乃物老而精，公然为怪。予乡有供五仙像者，其神为胡、黄、白、柳、灰。胡，狐也；黄，黄鼠也；白，猬也；柳，蛇也；灰，鼠也。予谓此五者何以分五色，客曰：“白黄是其本色，灰为黑而柳为青，然胡可为赤乎？”予曰：“可，《诗》云：莫赤匪狐。”客亦为之粲然。

    Explanation

    243

    Mouse Spirit

    In a certain town, there was a talented scholar named Fei, who visited the village of Yi'an. While staying at Wang's villa, he drank with his friends, and the host invited them to play a game. That night, an old woman pushed open the door and entered. She had white hair and wore a blue robe, and her appearance was gaunt and emaciated. Her eyes stared blankly. She stood in front of the table and looked around at the gambling equipment. Fei suspected that she was a relative and wanted to engage with her. The host quickly shook his hand to stop him, as if wanting the guest not to pay attention to her. Everyone saw her grim expression and all felt suspicious and fearful. One of the guests asked who she was, but she did not answer and left calmly. The host said, "This is a mouse spirit, who has lived in this garden for over two hundred years. Whenever there is a gambling session, she always appears. People are familiar with her and do not fear her. I did not think of this possibility, and thus the spirit came and scared the guests, causing me much guilt. However, this spirit, although it often appears, never harms people, so we live together peacefully." The next day, Fei bid farewell and returned home.

    The tea-drinking scholar said: Mice are just stupid creatures, but when they become old and wise, they openly become monsters. In my hometown, there are offerings to images of five immortals, whose deities are the fox, yellow rat, hedgehog, snake, and mouse. I wondered why these five are divided into five colors. The guest replied that white and yellow are their natural colors, gray is black, willow is green, but can the fox be red? I said yes, because the Book of Songs says that nothing is red but a fox. The guest also laughed heartily.

    244

    倭某
    　　倭某，邑县署之禁卒也。衙前盹睡，梦二役唤之去，路甚生疏。至一署，役引至堂下，报勾倭某到。旋见二囚负锁带械而至，审视，即前日狱中新毙之犯也。骇问二役，疑身已死。役云：“死否尚不可知，不过对质数言耳。”俄而官谓倭曰：“为何虐遇二因，致彼枉死于狱？是非自有官法，尔辈何敢尔？”对曰：“小人充当禁卒，惟管上退镣铐，并送犯入禁中，他不知也。”官使二囚试认。二囚云：“虐我者固非他，然彼未尝不知其人。”官诘倭，倭云：“县署差役数百人，轮流充当营务处差，不实指其为谁，小人不敢知也。”官还问二囚，不能对。官曰：“汝辈不能指实，我安能遍勾众役？”令退，遣倭还阳，一惊而寤。后半载，倭始卒。

    Explanation

    244

    Wōmǒu
    Wōmǒu was a guard at the county yamen. While dozing in front of the yamen, he dreamed that two officials called him to follow. The path was very unfamiliar. They arrived at an office, and the officials led him to the hall, reporting that Wōmǒu had arrived. Soon, they saw two prisoners bound and shackled arrive. Upon inspection, they were the new prisoners who had died in the jail the previous day. Wōmǒu asked the officials in shock, suspecting he was already dead. The officials said, "Whether you are dead or not is still unknown, but it's just a few words of questioning." Soon after, the official said to Wōmǒu, "Why did you mistreat the two, causing them to die wrongfully in the jail? Right and wrong have official laws, how dare you do such things?" Wōmǒu replied, "I am just a guard, responsible only for removing shackles and sending prisoners into the jail, I don't know anything else." The official asked the two prisoners to identify. The prisoners said, "The one who mistreated us is not him, but he certainly knows the person." The official questioned Wōmǒu further, and Wōmǒu said, "There are hundreds of officials in the county yamen, taking turns to serve in the camp office, if I don't point out exactly who it is, I cannot know." The official asked the two prisoners again, but they could not answer. The official said, "If you cannot point out exactly, how can I summon all the officials?" He ordered them to leave, sent Wōmǒu back to the living world, and Wōmǒu woke up in a shock. Half a year later, Wōmǒu died.

    245

    擒风
    　　陈姓与其友将往北村索债，行至丁沽，歇息道左。有二人亦与并坐，语次，见旋风蔽天而来。中一人曰：“诸君看我擒风中之魅。”乃默默诵咒，风至前，旋转不能去。倏于风中落一巨鼬鼠，大几如犬，背负黄袱，殆仙家之公差者。众劝释之，鼠驾风而去。片刻，风复至，尘沙漠漠，昏不见人，将前作法者卷入空中，飘然堕下而气绝。身旁一巨鼬鼠头，盖因误公被诛矣。术可不慎哉！失妖不扰人，人反扰妖，宜其获祸也。况一知半解，逞才害事，势不至一败涂地而不止，是可为好事者戒。
    古瓦罐
    　　良乡农人，掘地得古瓦罐，中实以五铢钱盈器。持归，日市其钱，人争购之，数日而罄。有涿鹿士人，好古者也，闻其事，谓人曰：“钱为五铢，器必汉窑，予将买其器。”乃之良乡，访农人。农曰：“钱已贷尽，以瓦器无用，凿破其底，作为烟突，今置诸檐端矣。”引士视之，浩叹而返。

    Explanation

    245

    Final Translation

    Capturing the Wind

    Chen and his friend were on their way to the north village to collect a debt. They reached Dinggu and rested on the side of the road. Two other men joined them and sat down together. As they talked, they saw a whirlwind covering the sky. One of the men said, "Look, I will capture the demon in the wind." He then silently recited a spell. The wind stopped in front of them, unable to move on. Suddenly, a large weasel-like creature fell from the wind, about the size of a dog, carrying a yellow bundle. It was probably a messenger from the realm of immortals. The others urged him to release it, and the weasel flew away on the wind. Moments later, the wind returned, bringing up a dusty storm that obscured everything, and the man who had performed the spell was lifted into the air, falling down dead. Next to him was a giant weasel's head, likely because it had been mistakenly punished. One must be cautious with magic! If the demon does not trouble people, people should not trouble the demon; it is appropriate for them to suffer misfortune. Moreover, half-understanding and showing off one's talent can harm things, and the situation will not stop until it reaches total ruin. This can serve as a warning to those who are enthusiastic about such things.

    The Ancient Clay Jar

    A farmer from Liangxiang dug up an ancient clay jar, which was filled with Wuzhu coins. He took it home and sold the coins daily, and people competed to buy them, selling out within a few days. A scholar from Zhuolu, who loved antiquities, heard about it and told others, "The coins are Wuzhu, so the jar must be from the Han dynasty. I will buy the jar." He went to Liangxiang to visit the farmer. The farmer said, "The coins have been lent out. Since the clay jar was useless, I broke the bottom and used it as a chimney, now it is placed at the eaves." The scholar showed him the jar and sighed deeply before returning.

    246

    醉茶子曰：器埋没数千年，一旦复见天日，则瓦缶真胜金玉，与商彝夏鼎何殊？虽置诸案头不为过也。而无知之俗物，竟尔摧残，殊属可惜。此器之不幸，正好古者之不幸也。千里马虽有，而伯乐不常有，是真遭际之难矣。彼伪宣炉，假铜瓦，无怪乎人间恒见也。
    铁叉
    　　邑南乡渔人，于沆中网得铁叉头一具，重七斤许，上铸“朱温”二字，其为梁时物非梁时物，不可知也。携归，置诸瓜圃中。守圃者用以逐豕逐猬，则火光迸出，无不应手辄殪，于是宝之。传播里中，来视者接踵而至。主人厌之，乃藏诸寝室。适有产妇临盆，叉戛然作响而倒，自此遂如顽铁。

    Explanation

    246

    Drunken Tea Child said: Objects buried and forgotten for thousands of years, once they are seen again in the light of day, are truly superior to gold and jade, how different are they from the ritual vessels of Shang and the bronze tripods of Xia? Placing them on the desk is not excessive. The ignorant common folk, who destroy them, truly a pity. The misfortune of these objects is the misfortune of antiquity itself. Even if there are thousand-mile horses, there are not always Baoles, truly the difficulty of finding true appreciation. Those fake Xuanshu, false bronze tiles, it is no wonder they are always seen in the world.

    Iron Fork

    Fishermen from the southern village caught an iron fork head from the marsh, weighing about seven catties, with the characters "Zhu Wen" cast on it. Whether it is a relic of the Later Liang period or not, it is unknown. They carried it back and placed it in the melon garden. The gardener used it to chase pigs and hedgehogs, and flames burst out, and no one could touch it without being killed. Thus, they valued it. It spread in the village, and visitors came one after another. The owner grew tired of it and hid it in the bedroom. Just as a woman was about to give birth, the fork made a sudden sound and fell over, and from then on, it became like ordinary iron.

    247

    醉茶子曰：或有闻而惜之者。予曰：“暴主之凶器，纵使有灵，亦不足贵，污之恨其晚也，何惜为？”
    岳某
    　　岳某者，葛沽卖药贾也，独居药坊，夜将寝，出锁肆门。归，见床头坐一少女，蓬发韶颜，姿容媚妩。询其为谁，则羞涩如新妇。岳惊疑，未敢穷诘。女亦无语，相与对坐。至晓，从容下床自去，夜复来。如是数夕，遂成伉俪。越半载，岳容貌憔悴。众诘之，初犹臆讳，久而尽吐其实。众谋为驱遣，而女不再至矣。不知其为鬼为狐，然可谓善于见机也。又有梅生者，读书斋中，忽有美女子随之坐，夜与之寝，他人皆不见，惟梅独见之，缠绵半载余，女自去。又有边某者，亦得狐妻，年余始别去。方知此等事，世固不少，奇而不奇也。若加以粉饰润色，则不无文人之笔耳。

    Explanation

    247

    Drunken Tea Child said: "Some may pity her." I replied, "A tyrant's weapon, even if it has spirit, is not worthy of respect. Regret that it was soiled too late, what is there to pity?"

    Yue, a medicine seller from Gezhu, lived alone in a pharmacy. As night approached, he locked the shop door and returned home. He saw a young girl sitting by the bed, with disheveled hair and a beautiful face, graceful and charming. He asked who she was, and she was shy as a newlywed bride. Yue was surprised and suspicious, but did not dare to press further. The girl remained silent, and they sat facing each other. Until dawn, she calmly got up and left, coming back at night. This happened several nights, and they became husband and wife. After half a year, Yue's appearance became gaunt. Others questioned him, and initially he was hesitant to reveal the truth, but eventually he told everything. Others planned to drive her away, but the girl never came back. It is unknown whether she was a ghost or a fox spirit, but one could say she was good at seeing the signs.

    There was also a person named Mei Sheng, who was studying in a study. Suddenly, a beautiful woman sat beside him, and at night, he slept with her. Others could not see her, but only Mei could see her. They were together for more than half a year, and then the woman left on her own. There was also a person named Bian, who also had a fox wife. After more than a year, they parted ways. One only then realized that such things are not uncommon in the world, strange but not really strange. If embellished and polished, it would not be without the touch of a writer.

    248

    乩示题
    　　壬戌科，有士人请仙豫问闱题。乩书云：“马上相逢无纸笔，凭君传语报平安。”士不解。及试，题乃《吾斯之未能信子说》，方悟仙示以谜语也，而思亦巧。
    古剑
    　　静邑张氏家，灶后每出白气如云，以为烟也。既而不爨亦然。穷其处，自砖隙中出，乃掘地深五六尺，得小石匣，中藏小宝剑一柄，长三四寸，锋利无比。置诸几上，时露光怪。张本农人，不知为宝。秋稔后，夜有群盗越垣入，举家惶恐，莫知所措。忽剑自跃出，旋闻庭中窸窣有声，群盗纷纷逃遁。剑如白虹，仍归故处。次日断发飘零，遗地无算，盖盗俱为其髡矣。张氏始知宝之。后年余，有老尼入室化斋，语多不解。张急赠以粮，尼不争而去，口中喃喃诵咒，剑从室中腾出，似随尼去。遣人追尼，不知何往，而剑自此亡矣。或云，尼，剑侠也，故以术取剑焉。

    Explanation

    248

    Divination Questions

    In the year of Rén Xū, a scholar sought a divination from a spirit to predict the examination questions. The spirit wrote: "Meeting on horseback without paper or pen, rely on you to convey a message of safety." The scholar did not understand the meaning. When the examination came, the question was "I have not yet been able to believe your words," and he realized that the spirit had given him a riddle, and the thought behind it was ingenious.

    The Ancient Sword

    In the home of the Zhang family in Jing County, a white vapor like clouds would often emerge behind the stove. They thought it was smoke. Even when no cooking was being done, the vapor still appeared. Investigating the source, they found it coming from a crack in the brick. They dug deep into the ground, about five or six feet, and found a small stone box containing a small treasure sword, about three or four inches long, unmatched in sharpness. They placed it on the table, and it sometimes revealed strange light. The Zhangs were originally farmers and did not recognize its value. After the harvest, one night, a group of thieves climbed over the wall to enter, causing the entire family to be in a state of fear, not knowing what to do. Suddenly, the sword leapt out by itself, and soon rustling sounds were heard in the courtyard. The thieves fled in disorder. The sword appeared like a white rainbow and returned to its original place. The next day, there were countless strands of hair scattered on the ground, as the thieves had all been shaved by the sword. The Zhangs then realized the sword's value. After a year or so, an old nun entered the house to beg for alms, speaking in a language that was mostly incomprehensible. The Zhangs quickly gave her food, and the nun left without argument, muttering incantations under her breath. The sword suddenly flew out of the room, as if following the nun. They sent someone to pursue the nun, but they did not know where she went, and the sword was never seen again. Some say that the nun was a swordswoman, and thus used magic to take the sword.

    249

    磁鹤
    　　邑北关外磁器店中，有磁鹤二，白质黑章，高尺半，每于子午时则呦呦自鸣，腹中并无机器，乃烧窑时天然成之，亦物之不可以理解者也。主人爱同赵璧，虽千金不能易，珍藏于秘室，不肯轻易示人。有欲观其异者，必再三求之而后可。主人引客至其室，以湿布巾搭鹤背，则鸣一二声，真奇物也。每南北负贩，则重绵包裹，载于车，主人自守护之，其珍重如是。
    茔中怪
    　　邑朱氏茔地，每夜静，有小人高三尺许，身披铠甲，自冢中出，牵白马大如犬，至道边呼曰：“顶盔擐甲，将军上马。”语毕，策马加鞭，飞奔而去。甲声淅淅，风声飕飕，转瞬不见，俄而复返。守墓者怪之，乃暗伏矰缴于林中，机发，人马并获。视之，一大黄鼠骑白兔，盔则髑髅，甲则以麻索联络人指甲而已。或劝放之，然自此不出为怪矣。

    Explanation

    249

    Magnetic Cranes
    In the porcelain shop outside the northern gate of the city, there were two magnetic cranes, white with black markings, each about half a foot tall. They would make a soft, bird-like sound at the hours of Zi and Wu. There was no mechanism inside them; they were naturally formed during the firing of the kiln, and thus are things that cannot be understood. The owner loved them as much as the Heirloom Jade of Zhao, and no matter how much gold was offered, he would not exchange them. He treasured them in a secret room and would not show them lightly to others. Those who wished to see their wonders had to request repeatedly before they could be shown. The owner would lead visitors to the room, place a damp cloth on the crane's back, and it would make one or two sounds, truly a wondrous object. Whenever he traveled north or south to trade, he would carefully wrap them in thick cotton and carry them in a cart, guarding them himself, valuing them so highly.

    Strange Occurrence in the Cemetery
    In the cemetery of the Zhu family, every quiet night, a small figure about three feet tall, wearing armor, would emerge from a tomb, leading a white horse as large as a dog. It would reach the side of the road and call out, "Put on your helmet and armor, General, mount your horse." After speaking, it would urge the horse on with a whip and gallop away. The sound of the armor was rustling, and the wind was rushing, and it vanished in an instant, only to return shortly after. The gravekeeper found this strange and secretly set up snares in the forest. When the trap was triggered, both the figure and the horse were caught. Upon inspection, it turned out to be a large yellow rat riding a white rabbit, with a skull for a helmet and armor made of hemp rope and human fingernails. Some suggested releasing them, but from then on, they no longer appeared as strange occurrences.

    250

    山神
    　　屠秀才，楚北人，名越。岁暮，撤帐归家。忽一武士，皮冠战裙，气象猛厉，岸然造其庐，自云庄姓，问屠明年设帐何处。屠云：“尚无定所。”庄云：“仆有两豚儿，敢劳先生教诲，岁赠三十金。”屠少其数。庄云：“予村中尚有邻家子二三人，同来请业，先生可安砚矣。”屠犹踌躇，庄遽以鹿脯置案上，云：“以此为贽，先生勿却。”出门遂去。屠欲询其里居，而庄去已远。时山中绿林啸聚，屠疑其党，心窃惴惴，未敢更就他聘。未几，元宵节过，庄忽夜来叩扉，云：“车乘已备，请先生就道。”屠请明日，庄不可，遂曳之登车而去。山路拗折甚远，至一村，皆架术为居，状甚奇古。时已将曙，乃共入室，四壁槎枒如巢。二童子出拜，长者十四五，少者十二，貌皆猿目鸢眉，率如鹗獍。教之读，驽钝无才，而性殊暴烈，数日，即将伴读童子击伤额角。

    Explanation

    250

    The Mountain God

    Scholar Tu, a man from the northern part of the state of Chu, named Yue, dismantled his teaching setup and returned home at the end of the year. Suddenly, a warrior appeared at his door, wearing a leather cap and a battle skirt, with a fierce and intimidating presence. He introduced himself as surnamed Zhuang and asked where Tu would set up his teaching the following year. Tu replied, "I still have no fixed place." Zhuang said, "I have two young boys, dare to trouble the teacher with teaching, and I will give an annual gift of thirty gold pieces." Tu thought the amount was too low. Zhuang said, "In my village, there are still two or three children from neighboring families who will come together to ask for instruction, so the teacher can settle down to teach." Tu hesitated, but Zhuang hurriedly placed deer jerky on the table and said, "Take this as a token, please do not refuse." He then left, and Tu could not ask about his hometown as Zhuang had already gone far away. At the time, bandits and outlaws gathered in the mountains, and Tu suspected that Zhuang might be one of them, feeling secretly anxious and did not dare to accept other offers.

    Not long after, during the Lantern Festival, Zhuang suddenly came at night and knocked on the door, saying, "The carriage is ready, please let the teacher get on the vehicle." Tu asked to wait until the next day, but Zhuang did not agree, and he dragged Tu onto the vehicle and took him away. The mountain road was very winding, and they arrived at a village far away. The houses were all built with wood, looking very strange and ancient. By then, it was almost dawn, and they entered a room. The walls were rough and uneven like a bird's nest. Two boys, one around fourteen or fifteen years old and the other twelve, bowed to Tu. They looked rough and wild, like fierce birds and beasts. Tu taught them to read, but they were stupid and lacked talent, and their nature was particularly violent. After a few days, they hit and injured the forehead of one of the students who was accompanying them.

    251

    盖邻家子来同学者三人，而庄姓子均陵压之。先生责之不服，反颜相向。庄出，谓先生袒护邻子，语忿且詈。屠大怒，辞欲行。庄曰：“汝欲何往？恐不能由汝。”悻悻而出。屠随出，闻室中群儿争号。归视，见二子擘邻子胸探食肺脏。屠大惊而逃。既出门，不识路径，二童追来而要遮之，屠奋身野窜。日暮途穷，鸮鸣狐叫，返顾二童，不知何往。道旁一荒窑，欲伏其内，忽有二狼从荆棘中跃出，倒衔屠衣，曳行数武，惊骇欲绝。俄一老人褐衣高冠，须长过胸，持杖击，二狼并毙。询屠何以至此，屠告以故。老人曰：“此豺虎之乡，何可朝夕也！幸遇老夫，不然危矣。”示之途。出则重峦迭岭，不复有道路。归家数日，梦老人提人头挂于庭村，曰：“庄某不法，予已枭其首矣。”屠惊问姓名，老人曰：“老夫此山之主也。”屠遂寤，起视树上，悬一虎头，血犹殷湿，方悟老人为山神也。

    Explanation

    251

    As for three neighbors' children who came to study together, Zhang, a boy surnamed Zhang, bullied them all. The teacher scolded him, but he refused to admit his wrong, instead turning his face in anger. Zhang left, accusing the teacher of favoring the neighbors' children, speaking angrily and cursing. The butcher, greatly angered, refused to continue and left. Zhang said, "Where do you want to go? You can't just leave as you please." He left in a huff. The butcher followed him out and heard the children inside the room fighting. Upon returning, he saw two boys tearing open the chest of a neighbor's child to eat his lungs. The butcher was greatly shocked and fled. After leaving the door, he did not recognize the path. The two children came chasing and blocked him, and the butcher fled wildly. At dusk, at the end of the road, owls were calling and foxes were howling. Looking back, the two children were nowhere to be seen. By the side of the road was a deserted kiln, where the butcher wanted to hide. Suddenly, two wolves leaped out from among the thorns, grabbing his clothes and dragging him several steps, causing him to be terrified almost to death. Suddenly, an old man appeared, dressed in a brown robe and wearing a high hat, with a beard longer than his chest, holding a staff and striking, killing both wolves. He asked the butcher why he was there, and the butcher told him the reason. The old man said, "This is a place of tigers and wolves; how can you stay here! Fortunately, I met you, otherwise it would have been dangerous." He showed him the way. Outside, there were mountains and ridges piled up, with no more paths. After returning home a few days later, the butcher dreamed of the old man carrying a head hung in the village, saying, "Zhang did not follow the law, and I have already hanged his head." The butcher was shocked and asked for his name. The old man said, "I am the master of this mountain." The butcher woke up, looked up at the tree, and saw a tiger's head hanging, the blood still fresh, finally realizing that the old man was a mountain god.

    252

    醉茶子曰：农人之子恒为农，则恶兽之子终是兽矣，又何必延求良师，以期其化气质哉！顾居停如虎，及门如狼，诚为罕闻。然世之轻慢师者，更虎狼之不若矣，可胜慨哉！
    铜骡
    　　都中齐化门东岳庙，有铜骡一匹，高如真者，周身色暗，惟胯间势梃然下竖，光明如鉴。予甚怪之。或告予曰：“凡妇女入庙焚香，有艰于嗣者，则窃把而扪搎之。以故日久若磋以炉钖焉。，夫铜骡何幸，而得纤手摩挚。若岳庙前之铁人，则千人唾詈，抑何其不幸也。铜铁无知，任人位置。而惑于俗例者，遂相习成风，牢不可破，亦可笑甚矣。

    Explanation

    252

    The Tea Drinker said: "The son of a farmer will always be a farmer; the offspring of a vicious beast will always be a beast. Why then should one seek out good teachers in hopes of transforming one's character? Yet, the host is like a tiger, and the guests are like wolves; this is indeed rare to hear. However, those who treat their teachers lightly are even worse than tigers and wolves; how can one not be moved to sorrow!"

    Bronze Horse
    In the capital, at the East Gate of Qihua, the Temple of Dongyue, there is a bronze horse statue, as tall as a real one, with a dark body all over, except for the crotch, which is erect and downward, bright as a mirror. I found it very strange. Someone told me: "All women who enter the temple to burn incense, those who have difficulty in having descendants, secretly hold and touch it. Therefore, over time, it has been polished as if with a furnace and tin. What good fortune for the bronze horse! To be touched by delicate hands. Like the iron men in front of the Temple of Dongyue, they are spat at and cursed by a thousand people; how unfortunate! Bronze and iron have no knowledge, are placed by people. But those who are misled by customs, thus they become a habit and a custom, unbreakable; how laughable indeed!"

    253

    猪异
    　　庚午夏迷拐幼童一案，野外暴尸，多剥目剖心而死者。惨亦甚矣。邑城中杨叟，贩猪为业。午间盹睡，闻有人叩门。出视，则瞽目童子六人，流血满面，皆裸无寸缕，胸际刀迹宛然。骇问何为？泣去：“予六人俱被冤狱，尚无结期，祈暂寄居翁家几日，亦不敢久留也。”翁大恐，坚拒不纳。童云：“请翁勿畏，不累翁也。”杨辞以家无闲舍，难容尔辈多人。童云：“豚栅即可息止，敢升堂入室，作嘉宾耶？”语毕，纷纷并入。杨力阻之，而豁然遂醒。怪而往视，栅间见豕产六子，目尽无睛，始悟童之托生也。惊叹不已，乃益糟粕而豢养之。不数日，六小豭相继而毙。吁！冤气所钟，遂有是异，岂轮回之说，信有之与？

    Explanation

    253

    The Strange Affair of the Pigs

    In the summer of the year Gengwu, a case of child abduction occurred in the countryside, where the abducted children were brutally murdered, often with their eyes gouged out and their hearts cut out. The cruelty was appalling. In the county town, there was an elderly man named Yang, who made a living by selling pigs. One afternoon, he fell asleep and heard someone knocking on his door. When he went out to see, he found six blind children standing there, their faces covered in blood, completely naked, with clear knife marks on their chests. Startled, he asked what they were doing there. They wept and said, "We six were all wrongfully imprisoned, and our cases have yet to be resolved. We beg you to allow us to stay in your home for a few days. We dare not stay long." The old man was terrified and refused to let them in. The children said, "Please do not be afraid; we will not trouble you." Yang tried to explain that he had no spare room to accommodate so many of them. The children replied, "A pigpen can suffice for us. Do you think we would dare to enter your house as honored guests?" With that, they all entered the pigpen. Yang tried to stop them, but when he woke up, he found six piglets in the pen, all of them blind. He realized that the children had been reborn as pigs. He was amazed and began to feed them with more care. However, within a few days, all six piglets died. Ah! The accumulated冤气 (wrongful suffering) had led to this strange phenomenon. Is the concept of轮回 (reincarnation) truly real?

    254

    蛤佛
    　　扬州北乡民人，于水滨得一蛤，长八寸许，壳中有生成佛像。两片共罗汉十八尊，须眉飒爽，英气如生。时城中兴教寺千佛塔被火，诸善士捐资修葺，金碧一新，乡民献此蛤于寺，置诸浮图之内，宛若天成，亦奇观也。
    鬼影
    　　陇西士人夜卧，见墙上立一小人影，高尺许，依稀如画。未几，款款前行，后随数十辈，自墙隅出，持旛者、持戟者，骑者、步者，憧憧不绝。末后一肩舆，四人舁之，大仅如筒，从东至西，隐隐而没。后数夕皆然，惧迁他室，不知何怪。
    泥娃
    　　津中风俗，妇人乏嗣者，向寺中抱一泥娃归，令塑工捏成小像如婴儿，谓之压子。有某家妇获一泥娃，日供飧馔，如生人等。妇偶归宁，闭娃于室，缺其供给，辄闻室中儿啼声甚厉。穴窗窥之，乃泥娃也。或以为灵爽，益信之。不知顽泥何知？其必有凭焉者。故祭非其鬼，圣人以为谄，良有以也。

    Explanation

    254

    A Conch Shell Buddha
    In the northern countryside of Yangzhou, a villager found a conch shell by the water's edge. The shell was about eight inches long and contained a naturally formed Buddha image. The two halves of the shell together featured eighteen Arhats, their beards and eyebrows lifelike and spirited, as if they were alive. At the time, the city's Xingjiao Temple had its Thousand-Buddha Pagoda damaged by fire. Various virtuous individuals donated funds to repair and renovate it, making it shine with new splendor. The villager presented this conch shell to the temple, placing it inside the pagoda, where it appeared as if naturally formed, a truly wondrous sight.

    A Ghostly Shadow
    A scholar from Longxi was sleeping one night when he saw a small shadow standing on the wall, about a foot tall, vaguely resembling a painting. Soon after, the shadow slowly moved forward, followed by dozens of others. They emerged from the corner of the wall, some carrying banners, others holding spears, some riding and some walking, moving continuously. At the end came a sedan chair, carried by four people, large enough to resemble a tube. It moved from east to west, gradually disappearing. For several nights afterward, the same thing occurred, and the scholar, fearing the unknown, moved to another room, unaware of what strange phenomenon this was.

    A Clay Doll
    In Tianjin, it was a custom for women who were unable to bear children to take a clay doll from the temple and have a sculptor mold it into the likeness of an infant, called a "yazǐ" (substitute child). One woman from a certain household obtained such a clay doll and treated it as if it were a real child, providing it with daily meals. One day, when the woman went to visit her family, she locked the doll in the room and neglected to provide it with food. Soon, she heard a loud child's cry coming from the room. Peering through a hole in the window, she saw that it was the clay doll. Some believed it to be a manifestation of a spirit, and the woman came to believe it even more. But how could a lump of clay have any awareness? There must be something behind it. Therefore, to worship the wrong spirit is considered flattery by the sages, and there is good reason for it.

    255

    产叟
    　　献县某村，有农妇怀妊三载，未产而夫亡，后又怀三载余，生一子，须眉皓白，皤皤然一老翁也。其姑恶而欲杀之，儿张目四顾，呼曰：“幸勿戕害，予非妖也。”语未毕，而刃下头落矣。众以农死已久，佥讥诮之，妇无以自明，遂忿而死。后阅邸抄，载苏州胥门外白马涧，有妇生子满口于思，不啼而笑，投之雪中而毙。是知生而有须，古今恒有，如李耳、周灵王辈，亦不足奇。人之血脉偏胜，妇女亦有生须者，史载李光弼母有须。概目之为妖，亦少见多怪耳。
    产异
    　　邑有潘氏妇，生平三产皆有异。初生一女，头耸双角，口列密牙，弃之。又数载生一儿，两胁毛茸茸如犬，亦弃之。后又生一男，形无他奇，但生而能言，亦杀之。

    Explanation

    255

    An Elderly Man Born

    In a certain village in Xian County, there was a peasant woman who was pregnant for three years but did not give birth before her husband died. After another three years and more, she gave birth to a son whose beard and eyebrows were white, looking like an old man. Her grandmother, displeased, wanted to kill him. The child looked around with wide eyes and called out, "Please do not harm me; I am not a demon." Before he finished speaking, the blade fell on his head. The villagers, thinking the farmer had been dead for a long time, mocked and ridiculed him. The mother, unable to defend herself, died out of anger. Later, reading an official bulletin, she learned that in Suzhou, outside the White Horse Gorge, there was a woman who gave birth to a child who smiled instead of crying and was thrown into the snow where he died. This shows that being born with a beard is not uncommon throughout history, as seen in figures like Laozi and King Ling of Zhou, and it is not remarkable. People whose bloodlines are imbalanced may also be born with beards, as recorded in the case of Li Guangbi's mother. Generally regarding such phenomena as demons is merely a sign of少见多怪 (seeing something rare and being surprised by it).

    Unusual Births

    In the county, there was a woman from the Pan family who had three unusual births. First, she gave birth to a girl with a double-headed horn on her head and closely spaced teeth, whom she abandoned. Several years later, she gave birth to a boy with fur on his sides like a dog, whom she also abandoned. Later, she gave birth to another boy who was not otherwise unusual, but who could speak at birth, and he too was killed.

    256

    古磁器邑谢氏修屋掘地，得二磁器，其一破碎。其未破者如磁礅而小，式如东瓜，色黝而光，四无微隙，中似空洞，倾之即闻水声淅淅然。博物者不能名，亦不晓其何用。主人置诸床头，夏月作枕，其凉沁骨，至今宝之。
    徽商
    　　安徽巨商于某，携眷入京，赁一第。居数月，恒见怪异。未几，婢死，妻又病，睡中喃喃，似与人辩。醒而询之，云有一女子披发索命，如是频频。商惧，另租他所。将迁前一夕，灯下倦卧，仰见梁上倒悬一女子，红裳跣足，发如飞蓬，怪而叱之，旋失所在。朦胧中，闻人语曰：“妾某官之姬，过蒙主人宠爱，遭大妇虐遇，缚而鞭之殒命，瘗诸此屋之东隅，人不知也。埋冤已久，屈莫能伸。长者能为我延高僧，瑜伽超度，拔此沉沦，九泉感德矣。”商云：“汝胡为杀我之婢？”女云：“彼命应死，非妾为也。

    Explanation

    256

    A Family in Ancient Ceramic Town

    In the ancient town of Ceramic, the Xie family was renovating their house and digging the ground when they unearthed two ceramic objects. One was broken, but the other, though small like a magnetic stone, had the shape of a winter melon. It was dark and glossy, with no visible seams, and inside it seemed hollow. When tilted, a sound like water trickling could be heard. Scholars of natural history could not name it, nor did they know what it was used for. The owner placed it on the bedhead, using it as a pillow in summer. Its coolness penetrated the bones, and it is still treasured to this day.

    The Anhui Merchant

    A wealthy merchant from Anhui, Mr. Yu, took his family to Beijing and rented a mansion. After living there for several months, he frequently saw strange occurrences. Soon after, his maid died, and his wife fell ill, muttering in sleep as if arguing with someone. When he awoke and asked her about it, she said there was a woman with loose hair demanding death, and this happened repeatedly. Fearing for his family's safety, the merchant decided to rent another place. The night before moving, he was tiredly lying down by the lamp when he looked up and saw a woman hanging upside down from the beam. She wore a red skirt and was barefoot, with hair like flying thistles. Finding it strange, he shouted at her, and she soon disappeared. In a drowsy state, he heard someone say, "I am the concubine of a certain official. I was overly favored by the master and suffered cruel treatment by the principal wife, who tied me up and beat me to death. I was buried in the eastern corner of this house, and no one knows about it. The grievance has been buried for a long time, and the injustice cannot be redressed. If a wise person could invite a high monk to perform yoga and deliverance, to rescue me from this state of suffering, I will be grateful in the netherworld." The merchant asked, "Why did you kill my maid?" The woman replied, "It was her fate to die, not because of me."

    257

    ”商诺之。次日迁居，遂置不理。移居新第，数月，女忽夜至，立灯下，谓商曰：“既许以超度，何竟失信？妾果恶作，岂避地所能免耶？”商曰：“予与汝无仇，且冤各有主，尔不扰仇人，而扰我耶？”女曰：“惟其无仇，故仅尔尔，不然，岂铙鼓诵经所能已也？”商曰：“世乏高僧，虚文何益？”女求商自诵《金刚经》一藏，即能顿除苦恼。商曰：“我非僧，何能效力？”女云：“心诚非僧亦可，不诚僧亦徒然，岂拘拘于有发无发乎？”商不得已，诵满其数。是日炉烟结如华盖，数刻不散。或者女果得超升欤？

    Explanation

    257

    "Merchant agreed to her request. The next day, they moved to a new residence, and he left her alone. After several months, the woman suddenly appeared at night, standing by the lamp, and said to the merchant, 'Since you promised to help me attain liberation, why have you ultimately failed to keep your promise? If I truly committed evil deeds, how could moving to a new place exempt me from my karma?' The merchant replied, 'I have no enmity with you, and each person's suffering has its own cause. Why do you trouble me instead of troubling your true adversaries?' The woman said, 'Since there is no enmity, I am only causing this much trouble. Otherwise, how could merely sounding the bell, beating the drum, and chanting scriptures suffice?' The merchant said, 'The world lacks virtuous monks, and empty rituals are of no benefit.' The woman asked the merchant to recite the Diamond Sutra once, and she would be instantly freed from her suffering. The merchant said, 'I am not a monk, how could I be of any help?' The woman said, 'If the heart is sincere, even if one is not a monk, it is possible; if the heart is not sincere, being a monk is also in vain. Is it not about having or not having hair?' The merchant, having no choice, recited the sutra as requested. On that day, the incense smoke formed like a canopy and did not disperse for several moments. Perhaps the woman truly attained liberation?"

    258

    狐帽
    　　敝先茔在邑城北刘园村，古墓崔巍，狐狸穴处。有童子刈草于其处，见二人狼狈而来，至墓前，凭肩而立。一人曰：“酒气醺人，令人不耐。”其一人怒，以手捽其帽，掷地上。俄俱不见。童拾视之，乃败凉帽一具，乱缨蓬蓬然，携归。夜，村外有人呼云：“何人拾我帽去，速为还我，不然，祸及!”如是数夕，其家惧而置诸村外，明晨失所在，而夜亦不呼矣。或言帽乃狐之蔽身物，然欤否欤？

    Explanation

    258

    A Fox Hat

    My humble ancestral tomb is located in Liuyuan Village, north of the city wall. The ancient tomb stands majestically, and it is a place where foxes make their dens. One day, a young boy was cutting grass in this area when he saw two people come in a disheveled state. They reached the tomb and stood close together, leaning on each other's shoulders. One of them said, "The smell of alcohol is overwhelming and unbearable." The other person, angered by this, pulled off the hat and threw it on the ground. Soon after, both of them disappeared. The boy picked up the hat and examined it; it was a tattered cool hat with tangled ribbons that were fluffy and messy. He took the hat home. That night, someone called out from the outskirts of the village, "Who took my hat? Return it to me quickly, or disaster will come upon you!" This happened several nights in a row. Fearing the warnings, the family placed the hat outside the village. The next morning, the hat was gone, and the calls ceased. Some say that the hat was a protective item for the fox, but is this true or not?

    259

    张氏妇
    　　张某，豫人。其妻某氏，生而能言，犹记其前世事。额角生有红斑如钱，或视之云：“此儿面貌颇佳，何有微痣？”女答云：“阿姨提抱，颠踬所伤也。”时在襁褓，共惊为妖。再诘之，则不言矣。后长数岁，渐能言。或询其前世事，云：“记前生五六岁时，患痘病笃，昏昏中至一处，广厦五楹，其中童男女聚集甚伙，年齿俱相若，亦不识其谁何。长则兄之、姊之，少则弟之、妹之。有妇人日与众餐饮，众呼为娘，己亦呼之。约半载，忽来一老妪，众呼为妈，谓众日，『某某随我寻尔娘去。』乃选择五人，并己为六。将行，女曰：『两娘在此，胡更有娘？』妪云：『随我去自知，勿多问也。』遂同行。至中途，数童不知何往，怪而诘妪，妪云：『彼各归其家，问侬将安归？』日，『即在此。』骤以手拍其背，一惊而踬，则降生矣。疑而四顾，猝闻人语，则答之。后遂迷惘。及长，始复能言，然前世事则多半遗忘矣。”

    Explanation

    259

    Zhang's Wife

    Zhang, a man from Yu, had a wife named Ms. Zhang. She was born with the ability to speak and could still recall events from her previous life. A red birthmark like a coin was on her forehead. Someone who saw her said, "This child has a beautiful appearance; why does she have a small mole?" The girl replied, "My aunt held and cared for me, and I was injured by falling." At that time, she was still in her swaddling clothes, and everyone was surprised and thought she was a prodigy. When they questioned her again, she remained silent. After growing older by several years, she gradually regained her ability to speak. When someone asked her about her previous life, she said, "I remember when I was five or six years old in my past life, I was seriously ill with smallpox. In a daze, I arrived at a place with a large house of five rooms. Many boys and girls of similar ages were gathered there, and I did not recognize any of them. Those older than me were addressed as brothers or sisters, and those younger were called brothers or sisters. There was a woman who provided daily meals for the group, and they called her mother, and I also called her mother. After about half a year, an elderly woman suddenly appeared, and the group called her grandma. She told the group, 'Follow me to find your mother.' She then chose five people, including herself, making six in total. As they were about to leave, the girl said, 'There are already two mothers here; why is there another mother?' The old woman said, 'Follow me, and you will know when you get there; don't ask too many questions.' They then went together. At the halfway point, several children did not know where they were going and were surprised and asked the old woman. The old woman said, 'They each returned to their homes; ask what we will return to.' The girl said, 'Right here.' Suddenly, she slapped her back, and she was shocked and fell. Then she was reborn. She doubtfully looked around and suddenly heard human voices, and she responded to them. After that, she became confused. When she grew up, she could speak again, but most of the events from her previous life were forgotten.

    260

    蝎异
    　　邑朱氏家有斗室，主人每吸烟于其内。地板中有一大蝎，长尺半，恒夜出，其声瑟瑟，伏茶几上，几为之满，不为人害，人亦不害之也。一日，主人他出，数日始归，启钥入室，见蝎伏几上，足尾皆拳，奄奄若毙。仆欲斫之，主人云：“物老而精，不可戕其生。盍送诸野外？”乃令仆寻筐筥，己卧吸芙蓉膏。饿而蝎入跪，举动郭索而遁。始悟其闻烟气而甦也。
    江苏某乙
    　　江苏某乙，家畜一马，雄健难控，仆夫恶而痛鞭之。一日，缚于柱，毒挞无算，血汗交下。马竖睛怒视，作人语曰：“尔再痛打不休，我便诉诸主人。”仆骇而走避。马自此亦不复言。

    Explanation

    260

    The Strange Scorpion

    In the Zhu family's village, there was a small room where the master often smoked inside. In the floor, there was a large scorpion, one and a half feet long, which always came out at night. Its sound was hissing, and it rested on the tea table, making the table full of it. It did not harm people, and people also did not harm it. One day, the master went out elsewhere and did not return for several days. When he finally returned, he opened the lock and entered the room. He saw the scorpion resting on the tea table, with its legs and tail curled, seeming to be dying. The servant wanted to cut it, but the master said, "The creature is old and refined; we cannot harm its life." Why not send it to the wilderness? He then ordered the servant to find a basket or container and lay smoking tobacco. Hungry, the scorpion entered and knelt, moving cautiously and fled. Finally, he realized that it was revived by the smell of tobacco.

    A Person from Jiangsu

    In Jiangsu, there was a certain person B who kept a horse at home. The horse was strong and vigorous but difficult to control. The servant hated it and whipped it severely. One day, the horse was tied to a pillar and beaten countless times, with blood and sweat mixed and flowing down. The horse raised its eyes and glared angrily, speaking in human language: "If you continue to beat me mercilessly, I will tell the master." The servant was frightened and ran away. From then on, the horse no longer spoke.

    261

    醉茶子曰：或以为鬼神恶仆心忍，故凭马为言以骇之。又以为马前世人所托生，未饮迷魂汤故。二说皆未可信。果尔，何以平日不言，独此时言之？盖言为心声，忿怒所积，郁极必发，故受挞难忍时，猝然脱口也。声出则气泄，挞止而忿亦平。平则无奇，故无语亦如常也。鬼神与托生之说，终近于虚诞。
    山左布商
    　　布商某，山左人，客于完、满之界。野行遇雨，晚无息止。远望有草房在半里外，急趋之。至屋前，有老叟迎入，情极殷洽，而四壁萧然，似久缺烟火者。商出囊中糇粮，与叟共啖。叟自言济南人，流落于此，苦不能归：“今幸遇乡人，万生有福。务烦寄语家人，遣人来迓，则感德无穷矣。”言次，泪落如雨。商亦恻然，扪金相赠。叟固辞不受。俄一少年冒雨入，衣如悬鹑，容颜枯瘦，向客一揖，侧立垂手。

    Explanation

    261

    Final Translation:

    The Drunken Tea Master said: Some believe that the ghosts and gods are displeased with my heart's cruelty, so they make the horse speak to frighten me. Others think that the horse is the rebirth of a person from a previous life who has not yet drunk the soul-numbing potion. Both explanations are not credible. If that were true, why would they not speak on a normal day, but only at this moment? Words are the voice of the heart; accumulated anger, when it reaches its peak, must be released. Therefore, when the beating becomes unbearable, I suddenly let slip what I feel. When the words come out, the breath is released, and when the beating stops, the anger also calms down. When the anger subsides, there is nothing strange, so I remain silent as usual. The theories of ghosts and gods and rebirth are ultimately close to being fanciful.

    A Cloth Merchant from Shandong

    A certain cloth merchant from Shandong was traveling through the border regions of Wan and Man. He encountered rain while traveling in the wild and, by evening, had no place to rest. Far off, he saw a thatched cottage about half a li away, and hurried towards it. Upon reaching the front of the house, an old man welcomed him in, showing great hospitality, though the walls were bare, suggesting a long absence of warmth and fire. The merchant took out some dry food from his bag and shared it with the old man. The old man introduced himself as from Jinan, saying he had wandered here and was suffering from not being able to return home: "Now that I have met a fellow townsman, I feel blessed in ten thousand ways. Please trouble you to convey my message to my family, asking them to send someone to come and meet me, and I will be infinitely grateful." As he spoke, tears fell like rain. The merchant was also moved with pity, and offered him some money. The old man firmly refused to accept it. Soon, a young man entered in the rain, wearing tattered clothes and looking withered and emaciated, bowed to the guest, and stood aside with his hands hanging down.

    262

    商云：“何谦逊如此？”叟云：“此予之敝邻，亦有求与君，焉敢倨傲。”少年云：“仆亦落魄于此地，归里时，亦烦寄信，没齿不忘。敝庐去此不远，愧无肴酒敬客，故不敢奉屈贵趾。请与丈人谈，仆去也。”言毕，拱手而去。商询二人姓名里居，叟并详告，乞商书于簿而志之。叟语毕，面墙卧，沈沈酣睡，商坐以待旦。天明雨止，呼叟辞行，不应。撼之珊珊作响，应手而颓，则一堆白骨而已。出门回顾，乃一败棺。数步外复有一冢，始悟为少年之墓。后至济南，访其两家之人，而各告以故。

    Explanation

    262

    The merchant said, "Why be so humble?" The old man replied, "I am your humble neighbor, and I also have something to ask of you; how dare I be arrogant?" The young man said, "I am also destitute here. When I return home, please also send a letter for me, and I will never forget it. My humble abode is not far from here, but I am ashamed of not having dishes and wine to entertain guests, so I dare not trouble your noble presence. Please talk with my elder, and I will take my leave." Having spoken, he bowed and left. The merchant asked about the names and places of origin of the two people, and the old man gave a detailed account, asking the merchant to write it in the register and record it. After the old man finished speaking, he lay facing the wall, soundly asleep. The merchant sat and waited for dawn. At dawn, the rain stopped. The merchant called the old man to take leave, but he did not respond. When shaken, it made a tinkling sound and crumbled at the touch, revealing only a pile of white bones. Going out and looking back, he saw a broken coffin. A few steps away, there was another grave, and only then did he realize it was the grave of the young man. Later, when he arrived in Jinan, he visited the people from the two families and told them the reason.

    263

    冤魂
    　　丁丑岁，邑城东女粥厂被焚，烧毙多命，以后屡见怪异。有卖食物者，夜过其处，见寺墙上无数妇人影，月明之下，络绎不绝。身后犹闻履声，回顾并无一人，而墙上影如故。又某甲夜行东城隅，见二妇人联袂前行。追之，二妇面墙立。某问其为谁，二妇返身一顾，见面目血污，舌出数寸。某惊昏绝，为人救苏，后病半载。
    大人迹
    　　香河县东郊外，雨后有大人足迹，长四尺余，五指印泥，箕斗俨然，彳亍北向，每步相去约丈许，是或魍魍之类欤？夫姜嫄郊禖，临洮长翟等，史载之。但不知此辈既有踪迹，必有形质，庞然大躯，果寄居于何所？是真不可以理解矣。又予乡有华姓者，好烧丹之术，于空院闭门修炼。邻人夜闻其院中格斗声，嘈杂甚多。登梯窥之，辄无所睹。或见其庭中有大足迹，长二三尺，五指俱备，所谓魔也。第不知此辈皆赤足，何耶？

    Explanation

    263

    Translation

    Wronged Spirits

    In the Year of the Ox, a soup factory in the eastern part of the city was burned down, resulting in the deaths of many people. Afterward, strange phenomena occurred frequently. One night, a food vendor passing by saw countless shadows of women on the wall of a temple, moving continuously under the bright moonlight. He heard footsteps behind him, but when he turned around, there was no one there, yet the shadows on the wall remained unchanged. Another night, a man named Mr. A was walking in the eastern corner of the city and saw two women walking together. He followed them, and the two women faced the wall and stood still. Mr. A asked who they were, and the two women turned around and looked at him. Their faces were covered in blood, and their tongues protruded several inches. Mr. A was so shocked that he fainted and was rescued by others, after which he fell ill for half a year.

    Tracks of Great Beings

    After the rain, in the eastern outskirts of Xianghe County, large footprints appeared, each about four chi long, with five-fingered prints in the mud, clearly resembling the patterns of a winnowing basket and a dipper. They walked northward in a slow, hesitant manner, with each step about a zhang apart. Could these be of the class of ghosts or spirits? In history, there are accounts of Jiang Yuan's ritual offerings in the suburbs and the long-legged people of Lintao. However, it is unknown where these beings reside, given their huge and bulky bodies. Truly, this cannot be understood. In my hometown, there was a person surnamed Hua who enjoyed the art of alchemy and sealed himself in an empty courtyard to practice. Neighbors heard sounds of fighting in his courtyard at night, which were very loud. Climbing a ladder to look, they saw nothing. Sometimes, they saw large footprints in his courtyard, about two or three chi long, with all five fingers present, what is called a demon. But it is not known why these beings are all barefoot.

    264

    二仙
    　　玉田岳叟，有良田顷余，家道小康。为人好善乐施，每济乡里贫乏。一日薄暮，立门外，有二人箬笠布衫，肩负行李，询叟逆旅何所。叟云：“此去六里余，始有驿亭。日已西沉，客得毋倦？如不嫌茅舍逼仄，可以下榻。”二人喜出望外，随叟入其室。叟为具鸡黍，情甚周密。二客佥云李姓，豪饮高谈，酬酢欢甚。更深叟去，二客偃息在床。次晨，叟备茶汤，呼客不应。隔窗窥视，见床上横卧二大鲤，长几如人，奄奄并睡。主人惊退。未几客起，则无异常人，呼主人，酬以金。叟力辞不受，转赠以糇粮而别。叟以贱价售其田，移居而去，越半载而成巨浸。

    Explanation

    264

    Two Immortals

    Old Mr. Yue from Yutian had several good qing of farmland and lived in modest prosperity. He was a charitable man who enjoyed helping the poor in his village. One evening, as dusk approached, he stood at his door when he saw two men wearing straw hats and coarse cotton shirts, carrying their luggage. They asked Old Mr. Yue where they could find an inn. He replied, "It’s about six li away before you reach a posthouse. The sun has already set; are you not tired? If you don’t mind the humble and cramped thatched cottage, you can stay here for the night." The two men were overjoyed and followed Old Mr. Yue into his home. Old Mr. Yue prepared a simple but hospitable meal of chicken and millet, showing great care and attention. The two guests both claimed to be surnamed Li, and they drank heartily and talked grandly, exchanging drinks and words joyfully. As night deepened, Old Mr. Yue left, and the two guests lay down to rest on the bed. The next morning, Old Mr. Yue prepared tea and soup, but when he called out to the guests, there was no response. He looked through the window and saw two large carp lying horizontally on the bed, almost as long as a person, sleeping soundly together. The host was startled and retreated. Soon after, the guests woke up, and they were no different from ordinary people. They called out to Old Mr. Yue and offered him gold as payment. Old Mr. Yue firmly declined to accept it and instead gave him provisions. Old Mr. Yue sold his land at a low price, moved away to a new residence, and within half a year, the land became a vast expanse of water.

    265

    槐仙
    　　邑城外天后庙北王氏之家，有古槐二株，荫广数亩。主人欲广其宅，将伐树。时观察李公之母，梦二童子绿衣双髻，韶秀可爱，泣拜于床前，云：“小人辈无辜被执，将遭刀斧。乞太夫人言其情于大人，庶可活命。”询其为谁，二童子并云：“姓槐，寄居于王氏宅中。不图主人妄思戕害。明日大人谒天后庙时，亟求究问，则犹可及止也。”叩头而去。太夫人醒而异之，言其事于李公。公遣役往探王氏，见其庭中有二槐，麻索缩系，诸工将纵斧矣。急止之，得不伐。竖碑以志颠末，且戒后人。

    Explanation

    265

    The Ghost of the Chinese Parasol Tree

    Outside the city wall, north of the Temple of the Heavenly Empress, at Wang's house, there were two ancient Chinese parasol trees whose shade covered several acres. The owner wished to expand his residence and planned to cut down the trees. At that time, the mother of Governor Li had a dream in which two boys in green clothes with twin braids appeared. They were graceful and lovely, wept, and knelt at the bedside, saying, "We, the innocent, are being seized and will be cut down with axes. Please, respected lady, speak on our behalf to the gentleman, perhaps we can save our lives." When asked who they were, the two boys replied, "We are surnamed槐 (槐), and we reside in Wang's house. We did not expect the owner to wish to harm us. When the gentleman visits the Temple of the Heavenly Empress tomorrow, please urge him to investigate; it may still be possible to stop it." They knelt and left. The respected lady woke up and found it strange, so she told the governor about the matter. The governor sent someone to investigate Wang's house and saw two Chinese parasol trees in the courtyard, tied up with hemp ropes, with the workers about to cut them down. He stopped them urgently, and they were not cut down. A stele was erected to record the events, and a warning was given to future generations.

    266

    醉茶子曰：此事予得之父老传闻，至今过其处，则迁徙数姓，庐舍全非，并树尽归于乌有，而短碣更不可问矣。然则树果有灵与？抑无是与？吾不敢知之也。
    张千总
    　　天津镇标千总张某，从军徽省。是处新遭兵变，人烟绝稀。张奉上命传递公文，与一营卒乘马驶行。天色昏黑，无所投止，远见半里外一星灯火，奔赴之。有破屋二楹，殊无院落。推扉入，土灶前一竹筒，承以灯碗，碗中残火犹明。有二叟面目枯瘦，神色凄凄，疑张为贼，伏地乞命，言词悲楚。张告以故，且求寄宿。二叟惊定始起，伛偻甚恭。张索饮食，二人唯唯应命，一人为张秣马于阶前，一人持饼至。张更求汤饮，叟应命而出，久待不返。张与卒俱疲倦，乃倚装酣睡。及醒，已晓，视其马啮草于庭间，砌下横一尸，白发苍苍，类六十许人。转视屋后，卧一尸，身首异处，年亦如之。瞻其面貌，即夜间之二叟也，浩叹而去。

    Explanation

    266

    The Drunken Tea Master said: This matter I heard from the old people of the village. To this day, when I pass by that place, several families have moved away, the houses are all different, and the trees have all vanished into nothingness, while the small stele cannot be asked about anymore. Then, does the tree really have a spirit? Or not? I dare not know it.

    Ou Zhangqianzong
    Zhang, a Thousand-Total Commander of the Tianjin Garrison, served in the military in Huizhou Province. This place had recently suffered a military rebellion, and very few people lived there. Zhang was ordered to deliver official documents and set out with a squad of soldiers on horseback. The sky was dark, and there was nowhere to stay. He saw a single light from half a li away and rushed towards it. There was a dilapidated house with two rooms, but no courtyard. He pushed the door open and entered. In front of the earthen stove was a bamboo tube holding a lamp bowl, with the remaining fire still bright. There were two old men with gaunt faces and sorrowful expressions. They suspected Zhang of being a bandit and crouched on the ground begging for mercy, their words filled with sorrow. Zhang explained the reason and asked to stay overnight. The two old men calmed down and stood up, bending over very respectfully. Zhang asked for food and drink, and the two men replied obediently. One fed Zhang's horse at the front steps, while the other brought a cake. Zhang asked for a hot drink, and the old man went out to fetch it. After waiting for a long time without returning, Zhang and the soldiers were both tired and leaned against their luggage to fall asleep. When he woke up, it was already dawn. He saw his horse chewing grass in the courtyard, and lying on the steps was a corpse with white hair, looking about sixty years old. Turning to look behind the house, he saw another corpse, with the head and body separated, also looking about the same age. Looking at their faces, he realized they were the two old men from the night before, and he sighed deeply and left.

    267

    王金铎
    　　邑儒童王金铎，住西沽，与二友自书院肄业归。天晚，遇暴风雨，三人并踬桥下，狼狈俱上。王失一履，行数武，始觉。欲觅之，二友劝归。至家，秉烛复往，妻苦劝勿听，于是去而不返。家人寻求，殊无下落。其妻梦王谓曰：“予在浮桥下某处，宜速往寻。若过明日，予即顺流而下矣。”如其言，果得其尸，殆水鬼迷惘所至也。

    Explanation

    267

    A Young Scholar Named Wang Jinduo

    A young scholar named Wang Jinduo lived in Xigu, a town near the academy where he studied. One day, he returned home with two friends from the academy. It was late in the evening when they encountered a sudden and intense storm. All three of them stumbled and fell under the bridge, struggling to climb up in a disheveled state. Wang lost one of his shoes and only realized it after walking a few steps. He wanted to find it, but his friends advised him to return home. Upon reaching home, he lit a candle and went back again, despite his wife's strong advice to stay home. He went and did not return. His family searched extensively but found no trace of him. His wife then had a dream in which Wang told her, "I am at a certain place under the floating bridge. You should go quickly to look for me. If you wait until tomorrow, I will be carried downstream." Following her dream, they indeed found his body, likely due to the confusion caused by water spirits.

    268

    邵明
    　　邵明，山左人，幼失怙恃，依其叔婶。至十三岁时，婶以暴疾亡。叔出购材，邵独守之。年幼胆怯，呼邻人作伴，佥不肯至。叔至夜不返，已恐甚，乃以布衾蒙首卧。忽闻灵床上察察有声，掀衾窃窥，见婶尸起，立灯光下，面如淡金，目炯炯有黑焰。转身向外欲行，忽有巨手如箕，从门外探入，直批其颊，拍簸有声，尸颓然倒，己亦惊绝，不知人矣。叔归，见尸卧地上，邵昏绝，气仅如丝。急救苏，始知其故。
    鬼窃饮
    　　吕某者，邑之杂货客也。以贸易至海滨，晚与二三友人，聚饮于船头。更深月上，拇战甚欢。忽众中杂坐二人，相与饮饫。众未之识，亦醉中未暇问也。舟子知其为水鬼，劝众罢饮。众不听，饮益豪。舟子不耐，大呼曰：“短脚者在席中，尚放恣耶！”众始惊，而二人跃入水矣。

    Explanation

    268

    邵明
    　　Shao Ming was a man from the eastern part of the mountain (Shandong). As a young child, he lost his parents and relied on his uncle and aunt. When he was thirteen years old, his aunt died suddenly. His uncle went out to buy materials, leaving Shao Ming alone to guard the house. Being young and timid, he called upon neighbors to keep him company, but they all refused. His uncle did not return that night, and Shao Ming became very afraid. He then covered his head with a quilt and lay down. Suddenly, he heard a rustling sound on the spirit table. He lifted the quilt to peek and saw his aunt's body rise, standing by the lamp, her face pale as gold, her eyes bright with black flames. She turned to leave, but suddenly a huge hand like a winnowing basket reached in from outside, striking her cheek forcefully, causing a rattling sound. The body collapsed, and Shao Ming was also terrified to the point of fainting, losing consciousness. When his uncle returned, he found the body lying on the ground, and Shao Ming was unconscious, with only a thread of breath. His uncle quickly revived him, and only then did Shao Ming understand what had happened.

    鬼窃饮
    　　Lü Mou was a merchant of sundries in the county. On a trading trip to the seaside, he gathered with two or three friends in the evening to drink at the prow of the boat. As the moon rose higher, they played finger games and drank heartily. Suddenly, two people sat among them, drinking and eating heartily. The group did not recognize them, nor did they have time to ask while drunk. The boatman knew they were water ghosts and advised the group to stop drinking. The group ignored him and drank even more boldly. Unable to bear it any longer, the boatman shouted, "The short-legged ones are among us, and they are still being so unrestrained!" The group was startled, and the two people jumped into the water.

    269

    醉茶子曰：乘海舶者，讳言水鬼，每呼为短脚云。
    粤东童
    　　粤东士人，传者忘其姓氏。娶某氏，宿儒之女也。生二子，长十九，次十七，俱已教之读。未几，又生一子，年六岁，不能语。众疑其暗哑，痴视之。一日，士拟一题课二子，己则出吊于戚家。二子难其题，构思不就，相与商论作法，殊无精意。其幼子在旁，谓二兄曰：“是题扼要在某处，当何如则制胜矣。”二兄互相惊叱，幼子曰：“予非妖异，特知前生耳。请赐笔楮，代为成之。”二兄如其言，见其文思泉涌，颇称佳构。父归，读其文而奇之，谓二子曰：“文笔颖锐，颇类尔外祖某公之作，尔辈之蓝本果从何处得来？”二子不能隐，以实对。

    Explanation

    269

    醉茶子曰：乘海舶者，讳言水鬼，每呼为短脚云。

    Those who travel by sea avoid talking about water spirits and often call them "short-legged ones."

    粤东童

    A child from Guangdong.

    粤东士人，传者忘其姓氏。娶某氏，宿儒之女也。生二子，长十九，次十七，俱已教之读。未几，又生一子，年六岁，不能语。众疑其暗哑，痴视之。

    A scholar from Guangdong, whose name is forgotten by those who transmitted the story, married a woman from a family of learned scholars. She gave birth to two sons, the eldest being nineteen and the youngest seventeen, both of whom have been taught to read. Not long after, she gave birth to another son, who was six years old and could not speak. The people suspected him of being mute or deaf and looked at him with a blank stare.

    一日，士拟一题课二子，己则出吊于戚家。二子难其题，构思不就，相与商论作法，殊无精意。

    One day, the scholar set a question for the two older sons, while he himself went to mourn at a relative's house. The two sons found the question difficult, struggled to come up with a solution, and discussed it among themselves, but their efforts lacked depth.

    其幼子在旁，谓二兄曰：“是题扼要在某处，当何如则制胜矣。”

    The youngest son, standing nearby, said to his two elder brothers, "The key to this question lies in a certain place; what should be done to solve it?"

    二兄互相惊叱，幼子曰：“予非妖异，特知前生耳。请赐笔楮，代为成之。”

    The two elder brothers were startled and shouted at him, but the youngest son said, "I am not an abnormal or supernatural being; I simply know about my past life. Please give me a pen and paper, and I will write it for you."

    二兄如其言，见其文思泉涌，颇称佳构。

    The two elder brothers did as he asked, and they saw his thoughts flow like a spring, and the composition was quite well-crafted.

    父归，读其文而奇之，谓二子曰：“文笔颖锐，颇类尔外祖某公之作，尔辈之蓝本果从何处得来？”

    The father returned, read the composition, and was amazed by it. He said to the two sons, "The writing is sharp and keen, quite similar to the work of your great-grandfather; where did you get the inspiration for it?"

    二子不能隐，以实对。

    The two sons could not hide it and told the truth.

    270

    父益奇之，乃唤其幼子苦诘之。幼子泣曰：“予隐忍数年，兹不得言矣。予本汝之岳翁某人，素无疾病，午后倦卧，魂离于壳。自不知其死，信步到女家看女。不图入门而踬，即使呼人，觉肢体寒噤。自顾已成婴儿，然心目了了，口不能言，莫解其故。及见女子临褥，始恍然悟其转生，不觉失声大痛。自思生老病死，人之常理，无足为悲。所难堪者，女而母、父而子也。是天作孽耶？是自作孽耶？不得而知也。”言毕，大痛。士告其妻，妻思父没之日，即儿生之日，不觉亦悲。一堂为之怆然。儿自此绝粒数日，终不死。后削发为僧，至今三十余岁矣。

    Explanation

    270

    The father found him even more extraordinary and called his youngest son to question him sternly. The young son wept and said, "I have endured in silence for several years, but now I can no longer remain silent. I was originally your father-in-law, Mr. So-and-so, always healthy, who fell asleep in the afternoon. My soul left my body, and I did not realize I was dead. I walked leisurely to my daughter's house to see her. I did not expect to stumble upon entering, and even though I called out, I felt my limbs grow cold and shiver. I looked at myself and saw that I had become a baby, but my mind and eyes were clear, and I could not speak, unable to understand why. When I saw my daughter by the bed, I suddenly realized that I had been reborn, and I could not help but cry out in great pain. I thought about birth, old age, sickness, and death, the normal course of human life, which is not worth mourning. What is hard to bear is that my daughter became my mother, and I became my father. Was it a disaster caused by heaven? Was it a disaster caused by myself? I do not know. After speaking, I cried out in great pain." The scholar told his wife, who thought about the day her father-in-law died, which was also the day the child was born, and she too was filled with sorrow. The entire family was filled with sorrow. The child stopped eating for several days but did not die. Later, he shaved his head and became a monk, and he is now over thirty years old.

    271

    醉茶子曰：投胎托生，世或有其事。不知司其事者，果何神也？闻之聪明正直而为神，况赏善罚恶，宜出于至公至正。胡为转易之间，令其伦常颠倒，抑又何心耶？异矣。
    营弁
    　　天津营弁于某，云在楚湘从军时，署月夜息，天忽暴雨，窗蔽苇箔，电光闪耀，见窗上立一婴儿，影贴棂际。怪而出视，乃一蜥蜴，长二尺许，爪握红旗，知是妖避雷劫者。急呼同人共毙之，天遂开霁。视其旗乃寸许，败布夹以草梗而已。又一日，野处于林间，与四五人造饭共食。忽见树巅一物如灯，垂垂欲堕。中一人援弓射之，物惊鸣如鹊，飞避而去。次日，前行半里许，见地上一大蜘蛛，长尺许，横贯羽箭而已毙矣。盖林间所射者也。

    Explanation

    271

    醉茶子曰：投胎托生，世或有其事。不知司其事者，果何神也？闻之聪明正直而为神，况赏善罚恶，宜出于至公至正。胡为转易之间，令其伦常颠倒，抑又何心耶？异矣。
    营弁
    　　天津营弁于某，云在楚湘从军时，署月夜息，天忽暴雨，窗蔽苇箔，电光闪耀，见窗上立一婴儿，影贴棂际。怪而出视，乃一蜥蜴，长二尺许，爪握红旗，知是妖避雷劫者。急呼同人共毙之，天遂开霁。视其旗乃寸许，败布夹以草梗而已。又一日，野处于林间，与四五人造饭共食。忽见树巅一物如灯，垂垂欲堕。中一人援弓射之，物惊鸣如鹊，飞避而去。次日，前行半里许，见地上一大蜘蛛，长尺许，横贯羽箭而已毙矣。盖林间所射者也。

    Translation:
    The Drunken Tea Master said: Reincarnation, perhaps there are such things in the world. But who is the deity in charge of this matter? It is said that a deity is wise, upright, and just, especially when rewarding good and punishing evil, it should be absolutely fair and just. Why, then, in the process of transformation, does it cause the disruption of social norms and moral order? What kind of heart does it have? This is indeed strange.
    A Military Officer
    A certain military officer stationed in Tianjin recounted that when he was serving in the military in Hubei and Hunan, he took a rest in the office during the night. Suddenly, a heavy rainstorm broke out, and the window was covered with a screen made of reeds. Lightning flashes illuminated the room, and he saw a baby standing on the window, its shadow clinging to the edge of the window frame. He found it strange and went out to look, only to find it was a lizard, about two feet long, clutching a red flag in its claws. He realized it was a supernatural being avoiding a lightning strike. He quickly called his colleagues to kill it, and the sky cleared up. Upon closer inspection, the flag was only an inch long, made of tattered cloth with a grass stem. Another day, while camping in the forest, he and several others cooked and ate together. Suddenly, they saw an object like a lamp at the top of a tree, hanging precariously.
