One of them drew a bow and shot at it, and the object screamed like a magpie and flew away. The next day, they continued their journey for half a mile and found a large spider on the ground, about a foot long, pierced by an arrow and already dead. This was the creature they had shot in the forest.

    272

    夙冤
    　　予乡人某，新婚初，与妇交拜成礼，即反奔而走，狂叫觅死。或询其故，曰：“妇青面獠牙，双目如炬，得妇如此，不敢近也。”众视其妇，貌仅中人，然尚不至如罗刹。强之入室，则战栗不前。察其意，似真畏怯也。从此遂异室居。越五六年，父母亲戚苦劝，某意微转，乃择吉为之成礼，遂无他异。数年，生子女各一，夫妇从无反目。一日方共坐，其姑戏谓某曰：“尔今日儿女俱全，视妇尚如鬼否？”某陡然惊顾，大号失声，狂奔而遁。众追之返，汗犹浃背。自此终身与妇绝。或偶与妇遇，必惊避。是殆纪文达公所谓“夙冤”也。夫见妇而畏，人之恒情。顾他人畏之而犹乐就之，或曲意逢迎而谄媚之，此人畏之而终绝之，是真能畏妇者矣。

    Explanation

    272

    夙冤

    My fellow townsman, upon newly married, after exchanging vows with his wife, immediately turned and ran away, calling out in panic and seeking death. Someone asked him the reason, and he said, "My wife has a green face, fanged teeth, and eyes that shine like torches. How can I dare to approach such a woman?" Everyone looked at his wife, and her appearance was only average, but not as monstrous as a demon. When they forced him to enter the room, he trembled and refused to go in. Observing his demeanor, it seemed he was genuinely fearful and timid. From then on, they lived in separate rooms. After five or six years, his parents and relatives persistently urged him, and his mind slightly softened. He chose a favorable day to complete the ceremony, and there were no other unusual occurrences. Several years later, they had one son and one daughter each, and the couple never quarreled. One day, while they were sitting together, his mother-in-law playfully said to him, "Now you have both sons and daughters; do you still see your wife as a ghost?" Suddenly, he started in surprise and looked back, let out a loud cry and lost his voice, and ran away in panic. Everyone chased him back, and he was still sweating profusely. From that day on, he permanently cut ties with his wife. Whenever they met, he would always avoid her in fear. This is indeed an example of the "夙冤" described by Mr. Ji Wenda.

    The sight of his wife filled him with fear, a common human emotion. However, while others might fear her yet still enjoy her company, or even flatter and cajole her, this man feared her so much that he ultimately severed all ties. Truly, he was a man who could fear his wife.

    273

    孙某
    　　孙某者，不知如许人。在邑城南赁庑居。生平庸庸无功过，好持斋诵佛，其他善事未之见也。将死时，群鼠攒集其面，嚼耳鼻殆尽，始气绝，未知是何惨报。或云此人好剥生猫而炙食之。杀生既多，报固不爽。夫猫，鼠之敌也。彼食猫肉，鼠何致反仇之？或云食猫既久，气味盎然，将死时气浮于外，故鼠闻而啖之。其理或然也。然室中有猫，鼠嗅其味而即早避，不必待其捕捉也。抑又何欤？

    Explanation

    273

    A Man Named Sun

    A man named Sun lived in a rented house in the southern part of a town. He was an ordinary person with no notable achievements or faults. He enjoyed practicing vegetarianism and reciting Buddhist scriptures, but no other good deeds have been seen. When he was about to die, a group of mice gathered on his face and nearly chewed off his ears and nose before he finally passed away. It is unknown what terrible retribution this was. Some say that this man enjoyed skinning live cats and roasting them to eat. Having killed many living beings, the retribution was certainly not wrong. Cats are the natural enemies of mice. If he ate cat meat, why would the mice turn against him? Another explanation is that after eating cat meat for a long time, the smell became strong, and when he was about to die, the smell emanated outward, so the mice smelled it and ate him. This reasoning might be correct. However, if there was a cat in the room, the mice would smell the scent and immediately flee, without waiting to be captured. But why is this the case?

    274

    黄鼠
    　　邑陈茂才，寄居三河县之村墅。夜喜临帖，脱帽置灯后。有黄鼠盗其帽去，幻作陈形，先大声詈仆，随即入后院，云：“仆太懒，不为我备饮食，我饥甚。”其眷属即出食物奉之。食讫，匆匆遂退。每夕皆然，而陈与其仆俱不知也。其家人疑陈方馔罢，不应如此之饥。且为人素谨，亦不应如是之率然，终未之究也。一夕，其仆仓卒入曰：“请稍缓须臾，即作馎饦来，幸勿怒詈。”陈曰：“我固不急索，何乃云然？”仆曰：“才命奴取胡桃，令置窗外，又急索馎饦，何云不急耶？”陈因与共验之，而胡桃乌有矣。相与诧异。次日，令仆待其侧，俄见灯后有物弄其帽，手如婴儿。叱之，乃一巨黄鼠冲门去。方悟每夕詈仆并诈饮食者，皆此物为之也。妖之计亦巧矣哉。然再二再三，终至于败露，此其所以为禽兽之智欤？

    Explanation

    274

    A Yellow Mouse
    In a village in Sanhe County, there lived a man named Chen, a scholar who was staying temporarily in a rural villa. He enjoyed practicing calligraphy at night and would remove his hat and place it behind the lamp. One evening, a yellow mouse stole his hat and transformed into Chen's form. It first loudly cursed the servant, then immediately entered the back garden, saying, "The servant is too lazy to prepare food for me; I am very hungry." Chen's family immediately brought out food to serve it. After eating, it quickly left. Every night it was like this, but Chen and his servant were unaware of it. Chen's family suspected that Chen had just finished eating and should not be so hungry. Moreover, Chen was usually very careful and should not act so rashly, so they did not investigate further. One night, the servant rushed in and said, "Please wait a moment; I will prepare bota (noodle dish) for you. Please do not curse me again." Chen replied, "I am not in a hurry to find it; why do you say that?" The servant explained, "I was just told to fetch walnuts and place them outside the window, and then suddenly asked for bota. How can you say you are not in a hurry?" Chen and the servant checked together, and the walnuts were nowhere to be found. They were both surprised. The next day, Chen asked the servant to wait by his side. Soon, they saw something behind the lamp playing with Chen's hat, with hands like a baby. When they shouted at it, a large yellow mouse rushed out the door. They finally realized that every night the cursing and false claims of hunger were all done by this creature. The mouse's plan was indeed clever. However, after repeated attempts, it eventually failed, suggesting that this is the limit of animal intelligence.

    275

    粥厂灾异
    　　杨氏妇，交河人。与其娣女四人，寄食西沽粥厂。夜梦妇女数人，彩衣华楚，云“此间不足糊口，城东南保生所女厂，食有粱肉，并给青蚨，盍速往投，过三日则不得入矣。”醒而述诸其娣与女。娣女之梦亦相同。于是决意往投。甫入厂一夜，次晨即遇灾，四人俱被焚矣。

    Explanation

    275

    粥厂灾异
    　　杨氏妇，交河人。与其娣女四人，寄食西沽粥厂。夜梦妇女数人，彩衣华楚，云“此间不足糊口，城东南保生所女厂，食有粱肉，并给青蚨，盍速往投，过三日则不得入矣。”醒而述诸其娣与女。娣女之梦亦相同。于是决意往投。甫入厂一夜，次晨即遇灾，四人俱被焚矣。

    A Disaster at the Porridge Factory

    The woman surnamed Yang was from Jiaohe. She and her four sisters-in-law lived by receiving charity at the Xigu Porridge Factory. One night, she dreamed of several women dressed in colorful and elegant attire, who said, "There is not enough food here to sustain us. At the women's factory in the southeast of the city, known as Bosheng, there is plenty of rice and meat, and they also provide money. Why not go there quickly? After three days, you will no longer be allowed to enter." Upon waking, she told her sisters-in-law about the dream. They also had the same dream. Thus, they decided to go to the Bosheng factory. Just one night after arriving, the next morning they encountered a disaster, and all four of them were burned to death.

    276

    醉茶子曰：甚矣，贪之害事也！使其不贪为宝，不践妖梦，则薄粥糊口，终不致毙，又安能骈死于一烬哉？
    林承嗣
    　　林生承嗣，闽之士人，侨居于邑之古刹。夜有长须叟，自称“胡髯”，与之谈，言词渊奥，遂订为忘年交。每见翁来，重门未启辄自入，归去亦然。固诘之，始知为有道之狐仙。莫逆既久，尽倾胸膈。生以贫故，屡求周急，翁不许，生有愠色。翁曰：“凡人之享厚福者，大半皆有前因，君前生为士人，无功过于世。今生饥躯奔走，强欲富贵，不能也。”林殊睥睨，不以为然。翁云：“明日请与仆偕往，洞观其故，当信仆言不诬。”乃出元巾一顶，眼镜一具授生。次日，引至一处，生识其为富室某家。

    Explanation

    276

    醉茶子曰：甚矣，贪之害事也！使其不贪为宝，不践妖梦，则薄粥糊口，终不致毙，又安能骈死于一烬哉？

    Lin Chengsi said: How great is the harm caused by greed! If he had valued contentment over wealth and not followed strange dreams, he could have lived on thin porridge and never come to his end, how could he have died together in a single flame?

    林承嗣
    　　林生承嗣，闽之士人，侨居于邑之古刹。夜有长须叟，自称“胡髯”，与之谈，言词渊奥，遂订为忘年交。每见翁来，重门未启辄自入，归去亦然。固诘之，始知为有道之狐仙。莫逆既久，尽倾胸膈。生以贫故，屡求周急，翁不许，生有愠色。翁曰：“凡人之享厚福者，大半皆有前因，君前生为士人，无功过于世。今生饥躯奔走，强欲富贵，不能也。”林殊睥睨，不以为然。翁云：“明日请与仆偕往，洞观其故，当信仆言不诬。”乃出元巾一顶，眼镜一具授生。次日，引至一处，生识其为富室某家。

    Lin Chengsi, a scholar from Fujian, lived temporarily in an ancient temple in the town. At night, an old man with a long beard appeared, calling himself "Hu Ran." They talked, and his words were profound, so they became close friends despite their age difference. Whenever the old man came, he would enter through closed doors and leave in the same way. Lin questioned him, and only then did he learn that the old man was a virtuous fox spirit. After becoming close, they shared everything. Because of his poverty, Lin often asked the old man for help, but the old man refused, causing Lin to become angry. The old man said, "Those who enjoy great fortune in this life usually have a reason for it. In your past life, you were a scholar who did not perform any great deeds in the world. Now, you are starving and running around, but you cannot force yourself into wealth and honor." Lin looked at him with disdain and did not believe him. The old man said, "Tomorrow, please accompany me. We will go to see the truth, and you will believe my words are not false." He then gave Lin a top hat and a pair of glasses. The next day, he led Lin to a place where Lin recognized it as a wealthy household.

    277

    某尝为人作中保，写借券调停于两间，得分余利，以故小康。登其堂，见有数十人，狐裘貂袖，容貌富丽，相与团坐斗叶子戏。已而具馔，珍羞杂陈，举爵饮饫，气焰骄豪。旁立数俊仆，奔走奉馔。翁使林戴眼镜视之，见坐者食者，率皆狼豺犬兔牛羊辈，殊无一类人者。又偕翁深入一院，见屋内教小豭坐椅上，捧书咿晤，一乞丐坐摊皋比，手持界方，指挥督责。生自怪小豬焉能读书，摘镜审视，则数纨裤儿与一塾师而已。与翁归，骇而问哉。翁曰：“予所假君之元巾，隐身宝也；眼镜，阅世宝也。世事如此，君固欲富乎？”乃索其巾与镜，一笑而去，后不复来。
    猪龙
    　　辛未岁，河水涨发，有渔人于潞河中央见一物，首大如丘，其形类豕，头浮水面，顺流而下。或云是猪龙也，见之主一邑大水。纪文达公笔记曾载水中巨羊头浮于波面，云亦是龙。设此二物相遇，不知自以为何如也。

    Explanation

    277

    Once upon a time, I served as a mediator for a loan agreement, earning a surplus profit and thus achieving moderate prosperity. Upon entering the hall, I saw several dozen people wearing expensive fur coats and sleeves, all of whom were wealthy and handsome. They were sitting together, gambling in a game called "leaves." Afterward, a lavish feast was prepared, with an abundance of delicacies laid out. They drank toasts and feasted, displaying arrogance and pride. Several handsome servants ran around serving the food. The old man made me put on a pair of glasses to look at the scene. Through the glasses, I saw that the people sitting and eating were all non-human, resembling wolves, jackals, dogs, rabbits, sheep, and other creatures, and none of them looked human. The old man then led me deeper into the courtyard, where I saw inside a house a young pig sitting on a chair, holding a book and making reading sounds. A beggar sat on a mat, holding a ruler, giving orders and scolding. I wondered how a pig could read, so I took off the glasses and looked carefully, and saw several young nobles and a private tutor. I returned with the old man, shocked and asked him what was going on. The old man said, "The hat I lent you is a magical hat that allows you to see through illusions; the glasses are a treasure that allows you to see the true nature of the world. The world is like this. Are you really seeking wealth?" He then took back his hat and glasses, laughed, and left, never to return.

    Pig-Dragon

    In the year Xinwei, the river flooded, and a fisherman on the Lu River saw a creature with a head as large as a hill, its shape resembling a pig. The head floated on the water, drifting downstream. Some said it was a pig-dragon, and seeing it would bring a great flood to a town. The notes of Ji Wenda once recorded a giant sheep's head floating on the water, also said to be a dragon. If these two creatures met, who knows what they would think of each other?

    278

    二鼠
    　　邑海河滨土地祠神座下，有一巨鼠状如小犬，常出没不畏人。僧徒呼为鼠爷鼠奶，恒饲以饭。凡僦居者必先致祭，不然恶作。有闽人赁其东厢，僧告之，不听。一夕，闽人出贩货，及归，见所蓄货物，俱为颠倒。呼僧询之，僧云：“此必鼠怪所为。”闽人不信。未几，二鼠率其族类以千百计，嗥叫跳跃。闽人大为所窘，乃亟市香楮果品，如法祭之，遂寂然不扰。
    蝶仙
    　　太常寺仙蝶，人尽知之，无足奇也。武遂鹾馆后院楼上西北隅，恒设仙位，每朔望致祭，不知始于何人。相沿已久，故馆主屡易而祭祀不废。温东川藩司未第时，曾就是处外席。会试前祝于仙曰：“如果得中，祈示警兆。”至夜，温独酌，忽有黑蝶翼大如扇，栩栩席前，嗅其杯酒而去。是科高捷南官。盖精诚所感，仙亦预贺也。不然，世之邀福祈祷者不少，仙不曾示之兆，岂贵贱异致，仙亦有世态之见存耶？

    Explanation

    278

    Two Rats
    Under the seat of the local land god temple by the sea, there was a large rat that looked like a small dog, often appearing without fear of people. The monks called it "Mouse Lord" and "Mouse Mother," always feeding it with food. Anyone who rented the place had to first offer a sacrifice, or else bad things would happen. There was a man from Fujian who rented the eastern wing. The monks warned him, but he did not listen. One night, the Fujianese man went out to trade goods, and upon returning, he found all his goods had been turned upside down. He called the monks to ask about it, and they said, "This must be the work of a mouse spirit." The Fujianese man did not believe them. Soon after, two rats led their clan, numbering in the hundreds, howling and jumping. The Fujianese man was greatly troubled by them and hurriedly bought incense, paper money, and fruits, offering a sacrifice according to the proper method. Then, they became quiet and no longer disturbed him.

    Butterfly Deity
    The divine butterfly in the Imperial Music Office is well-known, and there is nothing surprising about it. In the northwest corner of the upper floor of the back courtyard of the salt shop in Wusu, a shrine for the deity was always set up, with sacrifices offered every first day of the month and every full moon. It is unknown who started this tradition, but it has been passed down for a long time, so even though the owner has changed many times, the sacrifices have not been abandoned. Wen Dongchuan, a provincial official who had not yet passed the imperial examinations, once stayed at this place. Before the metropolitan examination, he prayed to the deity, saying, "If I pass, please show a sign of good fortune." That night, Wen drank alone, and suddenly, a black butterfly with wings as large as a fan appeared, flying gracefully in front of his seat, smelling his wine and then flying away. That year, he passed the metropolitan examination and became a scholar in the Southern Palace. It is said that the deity was moved by his sincere heart and also congratulated him in advance. Otherwise, there are many people in the world who pray for good fortune, but the deity does not always show signs. Is it because of different social statuses, or does the deity also have a bias towards social status?

    279

    冥狱
    　　陈典史，传者忘其郡邑名字，为阴曹掾属。每沉睡，数日始醒，家人习见，不以为怪。或询其阴司何状，云：“每至一廊舍，与十余人同办案牍，有二三鬼卒为之服役，他不知也。”或劝其穷探胜境，某以为然。一日，求鬼卒引窥冥狱，卒不许。恳之再三，卒曰：“随我去，万勿任意邀游，不然，累我非浅。”乃引至一处，有巨井，俯视别有洞天，其中动植飞潜，无物不有。珍禽异兽，纷往沓来，景殊奇绝。俯瞰既久，忽目眩，身倾倒，跌井底。荡荡然如堕万仞深渊，骇极大号，陡觉肌体冰冷。开目四顾，则已婴儿卧襁褓中矣。始悟托生人世，焦极无策。俄帘外一人招之以手，奋身一跃，魂始离壳。更见一童子登床往投，已遂随其人出。其人怼曰：“无故至此，几乎偾事！”乃指以路，令归。醒后身脱薄皮殆遍，从此遂无冥差之事，而典史亦被革。

    Explanation

    279

    冥狱
    　　陈典史，传者忘其郡邑名字，为阴曹掾属。每沉睡，数日始醒，家人习见，不以为怪。或询其阴司何状，云：“每至一廊舍，与十余人同办案牍，有二三鬼卒为之服役，他不知也。”或劝其穷探胜境，某以为然。一日，求鬼卒引窥冥狱，卒不许。恳之再三，卒曰：“随我去，万勿任意邀游，不然，累我非浅。”乃引至一处，有巨井，俯视别有洞天，其中动植飞潜，无物不有。珍禽异兽，纷往沓来，景殊奇绝。俯瞰既久，忽目眩，身倾倒，跌井底。荡荡然如堕万仞深渊，骇极大号，陡觉肌体冰冷。开目四顾，则已婴儿卧襁褓中矣。始悟托生人世，焦极无策。俄帘外一人招之以手，奋身一跃，魂始离壳。更见一童子登床往投，已遂随其人出。其人怼曰：“无故至此，几乎偾事！”乃指以路，令归。醒后身脱薄皮殆遍，从此遂无冥差之事，而典史亦被革。

    冥狱
    　　The magistrate, Chen, whose county and city names were forgotten by the reporter, served as an official in the underworld. He often fell into a deep sleep that lasted several days, and his family became accustomed to this and did not find it strange. Someone asked him what the underworld was like, and he said, "Every day, I visit a hall where I work with over ten others on official documents, and a few ghost servants assist me. I don't know much else." Someone suggested that he explore the more beautiful parts of the underworld, and he agreed. One day, he asked a ghost servant to show him the underworld, but the servant refused. After pleading repeatedly, the servant said, "Come with me, and do not wander aimlessly, or else I will be greatly troubled." The servant then led him to a place where there was a large well. Looking down, he saw a different world, where all kinds of living things, from plants to animals, existed. Rare birds and exotic beasts came and went, creating a scene of extraordinary beauty. After looking down for a long time, he suddenly felt dizzy, his body tilted, and he fell into the well. He felt as if he was falling into a deep abyss, and he shouted in great fear. Suddenly, he felt his body become cold. When he opened his eyes and looked around, he found himself as a baby in a cradle. He realized he had been reborn into the human world and was extremely anxious with no solution. Suddenly, a person outside the curtain signaled to him with a hand gesture, and he leaped out of the cradle, and his soul left his body. He saw a young boy climbing onto the bed and following him, and they both left with that person. The person angrily said, "You came here for no reason, and almost everything went wrong!" The person then pointed the way back and told him to return. After waking up, he found his body covered in thin, peeling skin. From then on, he never again received tasks from the underworld, and the magistrate was also dismissed from his position.

    280

    观花爆
    　　予邑五方杂处，人烟稠密。城中无隙地，惟四隅各有坑坎，为注水也。辛巳春，东风冻解，池水枯涸。好事者于东南隅夜放花爆，以硝黄作诸戏具，火发机动，则触处成彩。洵属雅剧。于是来观者男妇老幼数万众，舆马填衢。宵分人声如雷，灯光类星。地窄人稠，聚而难溃。俄而花爆放毕，互相拥挤，以致蹂践死者片刻十余人。次晨捡其尸于淤泥中，衣垢骸折，其惨已甚。中有一少年尸，于其怀中得女舄三钩，盖因贪窃此物，遂致毙也。呜呼！采莲之乐未终，就木之凶立至。报应之速，斯亦奇矣。

    Explanation

    280

    Watching Fireworks

    My hometown is a place where people from various regions live together, and the population is dense. There is no empty space in the city, except for the ditches and depressions in each corner, which are used for draining water. In the spring of the year Xin Si, the east wind melted the ice, and the pond water dried up. Enthusiasts set off fireworks at night in the southeastern corner of the city, using saltpeter and sulfur to make various toys. When the fire ignited and the mechanisms moved, they created colorful effects everywhere. This was truly a refined entertainment. As a result, tens of thousands of men, women, old and young came to watch, and carriages and horses filled the streets. At midnight, the sound of people was like thunder, and the lights were like stars. The land was narrow and the population dense, making it difficult to disperse the crowd. Soon after the fireworks finished, people pushed and shoved each other, resulting in dozens of deaths in a short time. The next morning, the bodies were found in the mud, with dirty clothes and broken bones, the scene being very tragic. Among the bodies was that of a young man, and in his possession were three hooks of a woman's shoe. It seems that he died because he was greedy for this item. Alas! The joy of picking lotus flowers had not yet ended, and the misfortune of death came immediately. The speed of retribution, this is indeed strange.

    281

    醉茶子曰：乐板生悲，至言也。夫游戏之事，至使举国若狂。轻挑者遂从中多事，其弊直不可胜言。死于非命，天之警人深矣。彼玩物丧志，犹细焉耳。
    蜂异房邑王某，能以咒取蜂房，众蜂冥然类蛰，任其摘取而去。日恒以此为戏。或劝其鬻诸药市，俾得微利。于是广搜博取，蜂房堆积盈千。其咒词鄙俚可笑，而灵应如响。一日，王袒卧柳阴昼寝，梦一赤衣丈人，巨目广颡，气象狞恶，谓王曰：“与尔何怨何仇？既毁我室，复取我子。若不严加惩创，我辈无噍类矣。”随呼其群，有千百健男蜂拥而至，各以利矛狠刺。其痛彻于心府，大号而寤。乃无数蜇蜂，集体毒螫，挥之不去，肩背都肿，因是成痈。医治半载始瘥，遂罢其戏。

    Explanation

    281

    The Tea Drinker said: "Joy turns into sorrow, a profound saying indeed." As for the matter of play, it can lead to the whole country going wild. Those who are frivolous thus stir up trouble, and its drawbacks cannot be fully described. To die an untimely death, the heavens have deeply warned people. To be distracted by trifles and lose one's purpose, it is still a minor matter.

    In the town of Fangyi, there was a man named Wang who could use spells to take honeycombs. The bees seemed to be asleep and resembled stingers, allowing him to take them away. He always did this as a game. Someone advised him to sell them in the medicine market to gain a small profit. He then widely collected and gathered, and honeycombs piled up by the thousands. His spells were crude and laughable, but they responded like echoes. One day, Wang lay naked under the willow shade during the day for a nap. He dreamed of an old man in red clothes, with large eyes and a broad forehead, who had a fierce and evil appearance. The old man said to Wang, "What have you done to me? You have destroyed my home and taken my children. If you do not severely punish me, we will have no survivors." The old man then called his swarm, and a thousand strong men swarmed in, each with sharp spears, viciously stabbing. The pain penetrated to the core of his heart, and he screamed and woke up. There were countless bees stinging him, their collective venom refusing to go away, causing his shoulders and back to swell up, and thus forming abscesses. He was treated for half a year before recovering, and he then stopped the game.

    282

    醉茶子曰：蜂之为物，渺乎微矣。乃能报怨如是，是固罕闻。然日毁无数蜂房，日毙无限性命，天长日久，积怨垒毒，譬如涓滴之永，积久成河海矣。设无显报，伊于胡底哉！古人云：“匆以善小而不为，忽以恶小而为之。”信然。
    白郎
    　　邑人某家咸齑瓮后，有白猬大如盎。某欲杀而妻止之。至夜，妇似梦非梦，见一少年入室，白面丰肌，褐衣宽袖，周身垂穗，累累然如蓑，谓妇曰：“感卿缓颊，得免遭渠毒手。恩实身受，敢不竭衔环之报。”妇询其为谁，曰：“我白郎也。”言次语涉调谑，解履登床，遽前亲吻。妇欲撑拒，而肢体顽痹如瘘，任其快意而去。如是觑某他出，辄来信宿。半载，妇已怀孕，而腹中奇痛，如万针攒刺，每一鼓动，则呼号欲死。妇哀求乞免，白郎曰：“予之德报犹未已也。”妇泣云：“微子之故，腹痛尚不致如此。”白悻悻含愠而去。其家延师驱逐，焚香设坛。歘坛上风吹烛灭，几案尽倾，视妇气绝于室。

    Explanation

    282

    The Tea Drunkard says: Bees, as creatures, are tiny and insignificant. Yet they can repay grievances so thoroughly, which is indeed rarely heard. However, they destroy countless hives and kill countless lives every day. Over time, grievances accumulate and toxins build up, like a drop of water that, over time, accumulates to form a river or sea. If there is no clear retribution, where will it end? The ancients say: "Do not consider good deeds too small to do, nor evil deeds too small to avoid." Indeed.

    Bai Lang

    In a certain household in the village, behind the salted vegetable jar, there was a white weasel as large as a jar. The man wanted to kill it, but his wife stopped him. At night, the woman, half-dreaming, saw a young man enter the room. He had a white face and plump cheeks, wore a brown robe with wide sleeves, and had long, hanging tassels that hung down like a straw raincoat. He said to the woman, "I am grateful to you for delaying his action, which saved me from his deadly hands. The real gratitude I feel, I dare not fail to repay fully." The woman asked who he was, and he replied, "I am Bai Lang." As they spoke, his words were playful, and he took off his shoes and climbed onto the bed, suddenly kissing her. The woman tried to resist, but her limbs were paralyzed like a paralytic, allowing him to satisfy himself and leave. Thus, whenever the man was away, he would come and stay for two nights. After half a year, the woman was pregnant, and her abdomen was in severe pain, like being pricked by ten thousand needles. Every time it moved, she would cry out in agony, as if she wanted to die. The woman pleaded with him to stop, and Bai Lang said, "My gratitude is not yet fully repaid." The woman cried, "Without your fault, my abdominal pain would not be so severe." Bai Lang left angrily, filled with resentment. The family invited a master to expel him, burning incense and setting up an altar. Suddenly, a wind blew out the candles on the altar, and the tables and chairs were overturned. They saw the woman lying dead in the room.

    283

    醉茶子曰：再生之恩，报之以此，此其所以为妖也。而妇人之仁，往往害事，多半类此。
    鬼结婚
    　　静海王某，旧为某宦长随。适寓于保阳逆旅，患病，自知不起，将其积蓄私瘗于榻下，人不知也。无何，病益笃，唤店主至，给以五十金，嘱其为己棺殓，所余衣物，悉烦登记一册，分作两分，自留一半，其余俱赠店主。数日果死。店主如其教，停柩窗外，将待其家人至而交付焉。有津门郭姓，往保定谋事，寓于其室。夜卧帐中吸烟，忽一人揭帘入，衣服修洁，礼貌甚恭。询其姓氏，云静海王某。以同乡故，款接益亲，让其吸烟，王一吸而尽，口鼻中并无烟缕，屡试皆然。郭心怪之，慨询其故。王云：“予两人合有夙缘，幸勿惊怖。仆实鬼也，不为君祸，且有求于君。

    Explanation

    283

    Final Translation

    The Drunken Tea Master said: The kindness of being reborn is repaid in this way, which is why it is considered an evil or supernatural act. The kindness of women often causes harm, and many situations are like this.

    Ghost Marriage

    Wang from Jinghai, who was formerly a long-term attendant of a certain official, was temporarily staying at an inn in Baoyang. He fell ill and knew he would not recover, so he secretly buried his savings under his bed, unbeknownst to others. Soon, his illness worsened. He called the innkeeper and gave him fifty gold coins, asking him to prepare his own coffin and burial. He also asked the innkeeper to register all his remaining clothes in a book, divide them into two parts, keep half for himself, and give the rest to the innkeeper. A few days later, he died. The innkeeper followed his instructions, left the coffin outside the window, and waited for his family to arrive to hand it over. There was a man surnamed Guo from Jinnan who was traveling to Baoding to seek employment and was staying in the same room. One night, while lying in bed and smoking, he suddenly saw a person enter by lifting the curtain. The person was dressed neatly and very polite. When Guo asked about his name, the person said he was Wang from Jinghai. Because they were from the same hometown, they became even more friendly. Wang offered him a smoke, and he smoked it all in one puff. There was no smoke coming from his mouth or nose, and this happened every time. Guo found it strange and asked him why. Wang said, "We have a predestined connection. Please do not be afraid. I am actually a ghost. I will not harm you, but I have something to ask of you."

    284

    予前月没于此屋，藏有金若干，芙蓉膏若干，俱瘗于榻下，君可取之。仆家息女尚不甚陋，与令郎年齿相若，愿奉箕帚，万勿推拒。瘗金处并有衣物清册一札，明日唤店主至，云我两人旧为姻娅，按册索物。如某负心，还请兴讼追索。如其慨然交付，即求将敝柩送回原籍。所存之金，以百两为儿女完婚，其余掷交拙荆可也。”言毕三揖，云：“仆不能再来。所恳者切记勿忘，则枯骸感德矣。”飘然遂去。郭发榻下，果如其言。唤主人至，询之不诬。乃赁舆载柩装物以归，寻至其家，备述颠末。王妻某氏叱其妄，并疑郭昧其金。鄣冤忿莫伸，乃哭诉诸王柩。氏夜梦王来，备言其故。氏知悔，结为婚姻，至今犹往来不断焉。

    Explanation

    284

    I died in this house last month, and I have hidden some gold and lotus oil here. Both are buried under the bed, and you may take them. My daughter, though not very beautiful, is not unattractive, and she is of similar age to your son. I wish for her to serve as a wife, and please do not refuse. The place where the gold is buried also has a list of clothes. Call the innkeeper tomorrow and say that we were once related by marriage, and follow the list to claim the items. If someone is unfaithful, please file a lawsuit to recover them. If he generously hands them over, then ask to send my coffin back to my hometown. The remaining gold, use a hundred taels to marry off my children, and the rest can be given to my wife. After finishing speaking, I bowed three times and said, "I cannot come again. What I ask is to remember and not forget, then my dead body will be grateful." He left gracefully. Guo dug under the bed, and just as he said, there were the hidden items. He called the innkeeper, and when asked, it was not false. Then he hired a carriage to carry the coffin and belongings back, and went to his home, detailing the entire process. Wang's wife, Lady Mou, scolded him for being unreasonable and suspected that Guo had hidden the gold. Her anger and injustice were not resolved, so she cried and told her story to Wang's coffin. Lady Mou dreamed that Wang came and explained the reason in detail. She realized her mistake and they became married, and they still visit each other regularly.

    285

    醉茶子曰：萍水之交，遽托以重任，附为婚姻。盖其笃信之衷，必有可以对幽独而无憾者。不然，诡谲之行，人且避之，而况于鬼！
    三世
    　　予乡沈观察维敬，将往山右，栖于逆旅。见一人左手俨然豚蹄，骇而问故。其人对曰：“此前生孽也。初一世为缙绅，多行不义。既死，冥王罚作牛。主人豢养臻至，驱使亦无所苦。常有邻人借去旋磨，言磨麦三斗，午后送还。及至其家，磨至五斗犹不止，终日不饲刍荛。牛饥甚，食其麦。邻人执梃痛挞。主人怪其爽约，诣邻视之，邻言牛食我麦，责主人偿。生人虽知其诈，无可分辩，乃挞牛以辱之。牛忿作人语，尽以情告。于是群惊为妖，咸趋避。牛饱餐余面而归，谓主人日。『日蒙主人恩养，敢不结草图报，牛本非妖，主人其勿恐。』从此驯良胜于常牛。

    Explanation

    285

    Drunk Tea Master said: An acquaintance made by chance, suddenly entrusted with a heavy responsibility, and allied through marriage. His sincere belief must have something that can be without regret before the silent and unseen. Otherwise, deceitful actions would be avoided by people, let alone ghosts!

    Three Generations

    My fellow townsman, Shen Guan Jian, was about to go to Shanxi and stayed at an inn. He saw a person holding a pig's trotter in his left hand and asked in surprise why. The person replied, "This is a karmic debt from a past life. Originally a member of the gentry, he committed many unjust acts. After death, he was punished by the underworld king to be reborn as a cow. The owner raised and cared for him to perfection, and using him for work caused no distress. Often borrowed by a neighbor to grind grain, he was told to grind three dou of wheat and return it after noon. When he arrived at the neighbor's house, he ground five dou and still didn't stop, all day without feeding hay and straw. The cow was very hungry and ate the wheat. The neighbor took a stick and beat him severely. The owner was surprised by his breach of promise and went to the neighbor to see what happened. The neighbor said the cow ate his wheat and demanded compensation from the owner. The living person knew it was a trick but had no way to argue, then beat the cow to humiliate it. The cow, angry, spoke in human language and told everything. Everyone was shocked and thought it was a ghost, and all fled. The cow ate the leftover food and returned, saying to the owner, 'Daily, I receive your kindness and care, how could I not repay you with grass and leaves? I am not a ghost, please do not be afraid.' From then on, he was more gentle and obedient than a regular cow.

    286

    数年将死，谓主人曰：『冥罚限满，牛之报主无日矣。牛死后，请剥而鬻诸市。不然，冥贵恐未已也。』主云；『汝一生勤苦，何忍令残鼎镬？』卒不听，目视厮仆，以敝帷埋于园内。牛至阴曹，冥王命鬼卒脱其皮，自顾依然人也，而裸无寸缕，叱令自去。出署不知所之，见有华衣置搭栏上，诸人争著，似无有禁阻者。己亦取一青衣被之，随众坐舆上。行不数里，车忽倾。惊视，则身伏木椓中，已化为小豭矣。不数月，躯竟肥腯，为人所斩。归复见王，王遣人剥其衣，粘著肌理，痛彻肺肝，不使剥净遽逃，而左袖未脱。展转间，复落褥为人。今之豚蹄，即左袖也。”

    Explanation

    286

    After several years, the cow was nearing death and said to its owner, "The punishment in the afterlife has reached its limit, and my time to repay you is soon. After I die, please skin me and sell me in the market. Otherwise, my punishment in the afterlife may not end." The owner replied, "You have worked hard all your life. How can I bear to let you be destroyed in a cauldron?" Despite the owner's refusal, the cow looked at the servants and requested to be buried with a tattered curtain in the garden. Upon arriving in the underworld, the king of the underworld ordered the underworld attendants to skin the cow. The cow looked at itself and realized it was still a person, but it was naked without a single thread. It was shouted at to go away. Leaving the office, the cow did not know where to go and saw fine clothes placed on a stand. Many people competed to wear them, as if there were no restrictions. The cow also took one green robe and put it on, following the crowd onto a carriage. They had not traveled far when the carriage suddenly tipped over. The cow was startled and saw its body fall into a wooden stake, having already transformed into a small pig. Within a few months, the pig's body became fat and plump and was killed by a person. Returning to see the king, the king ordered someone to skin the pig. The skin stuck to the flesh, causing intense pain that penetrated the lungs and liver. The pig was not allowed to escape before being completely skinned, but the left sleeve had not been removed. In the process of turning around, the pig fell back into a bed and became a person again. The pig's trotters today are the result of the left sleeve.

    287

    醉茶子曰：牛知报主，其良不没，宜其牛而豕，豕而人也。夫颠倒众生，罚不稍恕，则昭昭之报，悉冥冥之刑。然则士大夫可不兢兢自爱欤！
    妖诈食
    　　有邑人夜行垄畔，误践一物，腻然肥软，疑其猬也，祝曰：“愚人夜行无炬，误践贵体，深自悔惧，敢祈上仙万勿加罪。”祝毕而归。甫至家门，闻堂中掷器声铮铮然，其响类磁。入室则破瓯残盏，狼藉盈前。方欲致问，妻忽瞋目云：“无端被凶人作践，使我胁痛骨伤。此怨誓必相报。”家人焚冥镪无算，其怒稍解，云：“明日亟以旨酒嘉肴祀我，则涣然冰释。否则不尔活也。”邑人泯首自赎，妇昏睡不醒。次晨，携酒榼香楮至旧处设祭，视昨所践者，乃一大瓜也。恍然悟悔，痛诋，妇陡然寒战而愈矣。

    Explanation

    287

    The Drunken Tea Master said: The ox knows to repay its master; its virtue is not forgotten. It is appropriate for it to be an ox, then a pig, then a human. To invert the world, punishment is not slightly lenient; clear rewards are all dark punishments. Then can the scholars and officials not be cautious and self-loving?

    The Tale of Deceitful Food

    There was a commoner who was walking at night along a field path and mistakenly stepped on something. It was greasy, fatty, and soft, and he suspected it was a hedgehog. He prayed: "A foolish person walking at night without a torch has mistakenly stepped on your noble body. I deeply repent and fear. I dare to pray that the immortal will not impose punishment." After finishing his prayer, he returned home. Just as he arrived at the door, he heard the sound of objects being thrown in the hall, clanging like metal. Upon entering the room, he found broken bowls and cups scattered everywhere. He was about to ask what had happened when his wife suddenly glared at him and said, "Unjustly mistreated by a wicked person, it has made my ribs hurt and bones ache. This grudge will surely be avenged." The family burned countless paper money, and her anger slightly subsided. She said, "Tomorrow, quickly offer fine wine and delicious food to me, and then it will be resolved like ice melting. Otherwise, I will not let you live." The townsman bowed in self-redemption, and his wife slept deeply and did not wake. The next morning, he carried wine, incense, and paper money to the old place to offer a sacrifice. Looking at what was stepped on yesterday, he realized it was a large melon. Suddenly realizing and regretting, he severely criticized the situation. The wife suddenly shivered and then recovered.

    288

    缢致富
    　　邑有某氏者，落魄奇穷，无以卒岁。会除日家家酒脯，而自顾其室则嗷嗷众口，坐以待毙。自思身为男子，不能致富，使妻孥困惫至此，不如死之为佳。浩叹而出，信步至教军厂，见其东厢内僻静无人，思于窗上投缳，俾得死所。踌躇已定，归求助于亲友，凡历数家，井无有假一文者。于是心灰志丧，而念愈果。入夜，嗒然竟往，至东厢，漆漆然黑不见物。急趋窗下，解带将悬，忽有物触手。扪之，人也，高挂棂际，盖缢死矣。

    Explanation

    288

    A Man Who Hanged Himself to Gain Wealth

    In a certain town, there was a man who was utterly destitute and could not make it through the year. On the eve of the New Year, every household was celebrating with wine and meat, but when he looked at his own home, he saw many mouths to feed, crying out in hunger, and he felt as if he were sitting and waiting for death. He thought to himself that as a man, he was unable to become wealthy, and he had caused his wife and children to suffer to this extent; it would be better to die. He sighed deeply and went out, walking aimlessly to the military training ground. Seeing that the eastern wing was quiet and deserted, he thought about hanging himself from a window to find a place to die. After hesitating, he made up his mind and returned home to seek help from friends and relatives. He visited several households, but no one lent him even a single coin. His spirit was crushed and his will broken, but his resolve became firmer. At night, he went there silently and resolutely. He arrived at the eastern wing, which was pitch black and empty. He hurried to the window, loosened his belt to hang himself, and suddenly something touched his hand. He felt it and found it was a person, hanging high between the window frames. It was someone who had hanged themselves.

    289

    细摸其衣，狐裘貂褂，被体茸茸，大骇，曰：“仆之觅死，不得已也，公如此富豪，何为亦寻短见？仆若有此服饰，断不至此。”心中怔忡，欻生一计，祝曰：“今幸遇公，缘即非浅。请暂借公物，入局一博，其胜也返君完璧，其负也与汝偕亡。”遂解其袍褂衬衣置地上。复扪其内裳，亦皆华丽，不忍再解。乃俯取所脱之物，赴质库得钱百千。诣局纵赌，呼卢唤雉，应念而来，大获全胜。未至终夜，已钱钞千万计。核算而归，见家人犹环泣也。急市饼饵，举室饱餐。随即赎衣，令子负袱同往。至故处秉炬四照，不但其尸乌有，视窗间蛛网，棂上败尘，并无踪迹，诧异而返。从此经营贸葛，不数载，富雄一乡。遂藏其袍于雕龛，春秋致祭焉。

    Explanation

    289

    He carefully examined his clothes, finding them to be luxurious fur coats made of fox and sable, which felt soft and plush against his body. Startled, he said, "I was seeking death out of necessity, but you are so wealthy. Why would you also seek to end your life? If I had such clothing, I would never have come to this." His heart was agitated, and suddenly he came up with a plan. He prayed, "Now that I have met you, our fate is not shallow. Please lend me your clothes temporarily, and I will go to the gambling house to try my luck. If I win, I will return everything to you intact. If I lose, I will die with you." He then took off his robes, overcoat, and underclothes and placed them on the ground. He then touched his inner garments, which were also splendid, and could not bear to take them off. He picked up the clothes he had taken off and went to a pawnshop to get money. He went to the gambling house, called out the numbers, and won everything he bet. Before the night ended, he had won millions in money. He calculated his winnings and returned home, seeing his family still weeping around him. He quickly bought bread and snacks, and the whole family had a feast. He immediately redeemed the clothes and sent his son with the bundle to return them. He returned to the original place with a torch and looked all around. Not only was there no body, but looking at the spider webs in the window and the dust on the lattice, there was no trace. He returned in astonishment. From then on, he engaged in business, and in a few years, he became wealthy and powerful in the region. He then stored the robe in a carved niche and offered sacrifices to it in spring and autumn.

    290

    醉茶子曰：颠倒衣裳，尚不忍令其赤体。则中怀坦白，因与穿窬之盗不同也。由是致富，宜哉！
    卵怪
    　　邑人某，移居新第。夜闻室中哝哝人语，骇而静听，声在炕间。发砖视之，得二鸡卵，怪而置诸窗外，其语犹刺剌不休。第不辩其何辞耳。碎之则坚如铁石，并无少损。急斫以斧，始破。血色殷红，纹如石理，未知何怪。
    溺簿
    　　某姓将赴上洋，甫登海舶，舱中假寐，闻二人语曰：“人数足否？”答曰：“尚欠二人。”某猛醒，暗思乘海舶者，向无定额，焉有点数之理，得毋神稽溺簿乎？”遂决意负装登岸。及舟扬帆入海，遇风覆溺，一舟无有存者。光绪七年九月事。
    产怪
    　　赵某妇产一子，头耸双角，额生一目，面平坦，惟有口鼻。脑后垂一膜如帘，揭视俨然人面，眉目口鼻俱备。骇而杀之。

    Explanation

    290

    The Drunken Tea Master said: To wear clothes inside out, still cannot bear to let it be naked. Therefore, having a sincere heart, it is different from petty thieves. Thus, to become wealthy is appropriate.

    The Egg Monster
    A certain person from the town moved to a new residence. At night, they heard murmuring human voices in the room. Startled and listened quietly, the sound was coming from the kang. Broke the bricks to look inside, found two chicken eggs. Found it strange and placed them outside the window. Their voices continued to murmur incessantly. But could not understand what they were saying. When broken, they were as hard as iron and stone, with no small damage. Quickly chopped with an axe, only then did they break. A deep red blood color, with patterns like stone texture. It is unknown what kind of monster it is.

    The Drowning Register
    A certain person was about to go to Shanghai, just boarded the sea ship, took a nap in the cabin, heard two people say, "Is the number of people complete?" Answered, "Still lacking two people." The person woke up, thought that those who travel by sea have no fixed quota, how could there be a counting system? Perhaps the gods are checking the drowning records? Decided to carry his belongings and disembark. When the boat set sail into the sea, encountered a storm and capsized. No one survived on the boat. An event in September of the seventh year of Guangxu.

    The Birth of a Monster
    A woman named Zhao gave birth to a son, with two horns on the head, one eye on the forehead, a flat face, only with a mouth and nose. A membrane hanging like a curtain behind the head, when lifted, it appeared as a human face, with eyebrows, eyes, mouth, and nose all present. Startled and killed it.

    291

    守官
    　　邑藏经阁上有大守宫，伏椽下，状如小犬。阁上旧畜群鸽。物仰吸之，辄投于日，日戕数鸽，果腹而去。好事者自下击以火铳，物掉其尾，铅丸不能中。后不知所往。
    青蛙精
    　　邑城北村刘孀妇，有子尚幼，就外塾读。值天暴雨，儿未归，倚闾以待。有小鬟年约十三四，姿容秀美，绿衣光泽，冒雨而入，曰：“请暂借姆庐避雨，晴即速行。”妇爱其慧，纳之。鬟应对如流，甚惬妇意。适空际雷声訇腾，鬟惊变色，遽投姆怀，妇抱之。一炊时许，雨霁，鬟始起，向妇稽首，顿失所在。儿自塾归，见女冲门出，方一凝眸，遽化为大蛙如车轮，跳跃而去。奔入告母，相与惊异。后数载，因捻变，母子避地舟行。淀中波浪陡作，舟人大骇。有巨物自水出，跃登其舟。舟平稳，直抵彼岸。众负装下，见船头一大蛙，赴水而逝。

    Explanation

    291

    Guardian of the Official Position
    On the upper level of the city hall's storeroom, there was a creature that crouched under the rafters, resembling a small dog. The upper level of the city hall used to house a flock of pigeons. The creature would suck upwards, then throw itself into the sun, where it would kill several pigeons and depart satiated. Once, a curious person shot at it from below with a firelock, but the creature swung its tail, and the lead pellets could not hit it. Later, it was unknown where it went.

    The Frog Spirit
    In a village north of the city, there lived a widowed woman named Liu, whose son was still young and attended an external school for his studies. On a day of heavy rain, the boy did not return home, and his mother waited anxiously by the door. Suddenly, a young girl, about thirteen or fourteen years old, entered, her appearance graceful and her green dress shining despite the rain. She said, "Please allow me to temporarily shelter in the wet-nurse's hut from the rain, and I will leave quickly once it clears." The woman, pleased by the girl's cleverness, welcomed her. The girl responded fluently, greatly pleasing the woman. Just then, a loud thunderclap echoed in the sky, causing the girl to turn pale with fear and rush into the wet-nurse's arms, whom the woman held tightly. After about a cooking session's time, the rain stopped, and the girl rose, bowed to the woman, and suddenly disappeared. When the boy returned from school, he saw the girl rushing out the door. As he watched her, she suddenly transformed into a large frog, like a cartwheel, and leaped away. He rushed inside to tell his mother, and they were both astonished. Several years later, during the chaos of the Nian rebellion, the mother and son fled by boat. On the lake, the waves suddenly surged, and the crew was terrified. A huge creature emerged from the water and leaped onto the boat. The boat remained steady and reached the other shore. The crew unloaded their belongings and saw a large frog at the bow of the boat, which dove into the water and disappeared.

    292

    钱龙
    　　山左诸生陶某，训蒙于邑之葛沽，恂恂谨抑，良士也。忽购一利刃，朝夕磨砺，藏谱荐底。一日，键户谓其徒曰：“吾平日诲尔谆谆，尔辈竟仇药石。今尽杀却而甘心焉!”急捉其徒之年长者，忿欲加刃。徒年十六岁，黠而多谋，跪而请曰：“弟子敢不奉教。杂不见父母，终觉抱憾九泉。请归辞双亲，回塾一听先生所为。”生曰：“孺子尚知孝道，可暂去速回。”乃拔关放出，复闭户而倚以石。童子归家诉诸其父，父叱曰：“先生何致杀人？是尔托辞逃塾。宜速归，否则加鞭责矣。

    Explanation

    292

    Money Dragon
    A student named Tao from Shandong was teaching young children in the village of Geju. He was cautious, respectful, and restrained, a virtuous man. Suddenly, he bought a sharp knife, sharpened it day and night, and hid it under his pillow. One day, he locked the door and told his students, "I have been teaching you earnestly, but you all resent my advice. Now I will kill all of you to satisfy my anger!" He quickly grabbed the oldest student and was about to strike him with the knife. The student was sixteen years old, clever and resourceful. He knelt down and pleaded, "I dare not disobey your teachings. If I do not see my parents, I will always feel regret in the afterlife. Please allow me to go back home to bid farewell to my parents and return to the school to follow your instructions." The teacher said, "You still know the way of filial piety; you may leave for now and return quickly." He then unlocked the door and let the student go, but he locked the door again and placed a stone against it. The student returned home and told his father, who scolded him, "What made the teacher want to kill you? You are just making an excuse to escape from school. You should return immediately; otherwise, I will beat you severely."

    293

    ”童子无奈，哭诉诸居停赵姓者，且曰：“阿伯不速往救，令郎与二三窗友成齑粉矣。”赵大惊，乃集众至其塾，隔墙闻童子号泣声甚惨，扣其扉牢不可开。众缘梯过，见生裸缚一童子，跨其身而剖其股，已露筋骨，声嘶嘶几不能号。其余二三人有惊踣气绝者，有匿诸案下者，匿诸柴后者。众夺其刀，问何仇害？生曰：“吾教诲子弟，不如是不足以有成。”众中有好事者，怒欲痛殴。赵急止之。乃厚赠遣人送之归。生语多颠倒，至河干谓送者曰：“吾非为阿堵物，亦无须汝远送。”出居停所赠之钱，尽弃诸水，悻悻而去。至家颠狂益甚。父母佥以为忧，闭诸室而给饮食。年余，歌笑怒骂无常。

    Explanation

    293

    The boy, helpless, cried to the host, Mr. Zhao, and said, "Uncle, why didn’t you come to save us quickly? Your son and my two or three classmates have been turned into powder." Mr. Zhao was greatly alarmed and gathered the crowd to the boy’s study. Through the wall, they heard the boy’s cries of despair, which were very pitiful. They knocked on the door, but it was tightly locked and could not be opened. The crowd climbed over using a ladder and saw the man naked, binding a boy, straddling him, and cutting into his thigh. The bones were exposed, and the boy’s voice was hoarse, barely able to cry. The other two or three people were either so shocked that they fell and lost consciousness, or they hid under the desk, or hid behind the stove. The crowd seized his knife and asked what the grudge or harm was. The man said, "I teach my disciples, and without this, they cannot achieve anything." Among the crowd, there were some who were eager to beat him severely. Mr. Zhao hurriedly stopped them. They then gave him generous gifts and sent him back home. The man spoke in a confused and illogical manner. When he reached the riverbank, he said to the person who was seeing him off, "I did not do this for the money, and I do not need you to see me off so far." He took out the money that Mr. Zhao had given him and threw it all into the water, then left angrily. When he returned home, his madness became even more severe. His parents, all of them, were worried, and they locked him in the room and provided him with food and drink. After more than a year, he sang, laughed, shouted, and cursed without any regularity.

    294

    有老道士托钵于门，自言能相宅。陶翁延入，至庭中，喟然叹曰：“妖气太恶，宝眷未必安也。”翁奇其言，急求拯救。道士谢以不能。翁五体投地，恳求益切。许之，令出病者。生见道士，大言曰：“吾奉天命败其家，尔勿干预！”道士曰：“此儿他日功名显达，未可戕害。吾劝翁改行，汝勿恶作也。”生曰：“一乡饥饿，彼坐拥厚粮，视死不救，此复焉有人理？”乃趋避登楼而闭其门。道人追及，破扉而入。生怒且骂。道士亦怒曰：“吾本不为己甚，顽梗若此，不得不下毒手。”乃出铜针长二尺许，直刺其胸，颓然遂毙。翁顿足痛曰：“不图竟杀吾儿。

    Explanation

    294

    An elderly Taoist monk, begging at the door, claimed to be able to inspect the dwelling. Mr. Tao invited him in, and they went to the courtyard, where the monk sighed and said, "The malevolent energy is too strong; your beloved ones may not be safe." Mr. Tao was startled by his words and urgently sought salvation. The monk apologized, saying he could not help. Mr. Tao prostrated himself completely, pleading even more earnestly. The monk agreed and asked for the ill person to come out. The son saw the monk and loudly declared, "I act under divine mandate to ruin this family; you must not interfere!" The monk replied, "This boy will one day achieve great fame and success; he should not be harmed. I advise you to change your ways, and you must not do evil." The son retorted, "The entire village is starving, yet he sits with ample grain, refusing to save lives. How can this be humane?" He then rushed to climb the stairs and closed the door. The monk pursued him, breaking the door open to enter. The son was angry and cursed. The monk, too, became angry and said, "I did not intend to do anything extreme, but with such stubbornness, I cannot help but resort to drastic measures." He then took out a copper needle, about two feet long, and stabbed it straight into the son's chest. The son collapsed and died. Mr. Tao stomped his feet in pain, saying, "I never thought he would kill my son."

    295

    ”道士笑曰：“翁请勿忧，公子固无恙也。”禹步作法，生果苏，嘱其调养百日当愈。翁骇问故，道士曰：“此妖乃翁之钱龙也。先辈富而好善，彼暗中司会计，故家道日兴。今翁吝啬，不如先人，彼欲破产而作祟，公子颠疾皆彼所致。君如不信，请验之，今已毙于空室乱木下。”翁督众仆移木，众嚏其妄。固强之，木尽，有巨蟒长数丈，粗如柱，盘踞而毙。众始大惊。道士曰：“此静应运而来，罪不应死。可于麦场上作坛，吾咒而生之。”乃埋于坛下，作法三昼夜，发视蟒已渺。道士劝翁改行，翁知悔，厚酬道士，不受而去，翁自此恤邻睦宗，靡善不为。又教年，生领乡荐。

    Explanation

    295

    The Taoist priest smiled and said, "Grandfather, please do not be worried; the young master is certainly fine." He performed the ritual steps, and the young master indeed revived. The priest advised him to rest and recover for a hundred days, and he would be cured. The old man was startled and asked the reason. The Taoist priest said, "This demon is your money dragon. Your ancestors were wealthy and kind-hearted, and it secretly managed the accounts, so the family's fortunes grew day by day. Now you are stingy, not as generous as your ancestors, and it wants to ruin the family and cause trouble. The young master's madness is all caused by it. If you do not believe me, please verify it; it is now dead under a pile of broken wood in an empty room." The old man ordered his servants to move the wood, and everyone sneezed in disbelief. Insisting strongly, they moved the wood, and there was a giant python several meters long, as thick as a pillar, lying coiled and dead. Everyone was greatly surprised. The Taoist priest said, "This demon came quietly and should not be punished to death. You can build an altar on the wheat field, and I will chant and revive it." It was then buried under the altar, and the ritual lasted for three days and nights. When they checked, the python had vanished. The Taoist priest advised the old man to change his ways. The old man realized his mistake, offered a generous reward, but the Taoist priest refused and left. From then on, the old man cared for his neighbors and kin, and did no good deed too small. After another year, the young master passed the local examination.

    296

    醉茶子曰：狐仙妖魅，每视人之运气为转移。运旺而家兴也，暗为增益财，几不知何自来；运衰而家败也，暗为消耗财，又不知何自去。裒多益寡，妖不能均匀之，此理之不可解也。然不难解也，不贪意外之财，财不伤于意外。尝见祖宗刻薄成家，子孙游荡败产。此妖之兴，不尽在妖也。人能于财非义不取，财可以处贫赡，可以守富贵。虽属老生常谈，而狷洁自爱，修德自能胜妖。识者当不以予言为河汉也。

    Explanation

    296

    The Tea-Drunk One said: Fox spirits and other supernatural beings often shift their influence based on a person’s fortune. When fortune is strong, the family prospers, and wealth is secretly increased, almost as if it comes from nowhere; when fortune declines, the family falls, and wealth is secretly depleted, again without knowing where it has gone. Taking from the many to give to the few, the spirits cannot distribute it evenly, and this principle seems inexplicable. Yet, it is not difficult to understand: do not covet unexpected wealth, and your wealth will not be harmed by unforeseen events. I have often seen that ancestors who were frugal in building their family had descendants who squandered their property through idleness. The rise and fall of fortune is not solely in the hands of the spirits. If one does not take wealth that is not righteous, wealth can be maintained in poverty with dignity and preserved in prosperity. Though this is a familiar saying, by being chaste and self-loving, and by cultivating virtue, one can overcome the influence of the spirits. Those who understand will not regard my words as empty or meaningless.

    297

    石珠
    　　武弁某，从军湘楚。夜行山内，见石隙中有光透露，照澈数步外，明朗如昼。凿之，得一石丸，大如指顶，持归，置诸暗室，如烧蜡炬。每燕会悬于中堂，数十席微芒悉见。某大僚欲夺之，弁不与，因是免官。益以为宝，藏诸椟，秘不示人。又数载，光渐敛，后仅尺许，今竟全暗如顽石焉。或云石之精气未足，故光不久。若待数百年精华充足，便如照乘珠，久而勿暗也。然欤否欤？夫石果韬光匿彩，自能历久常新。乃急自炫，遂至元精败露，的然日亡，殆自取也。
    土中鱼
    　　癸末夏，静邑掘河，土中得一鲫鱼，长二寸许。土工咤异，于是环视者如堵。中有一任姓者，逼近视之，鱼跃入其口中，即失所在。任甚懊悔，而卒无恙。亦异事也。

    297

    Stone Pearl

    A military officer named Wu, serving in Xiangchu, was traveling through the mountains at night when he saw a light seeping through a crack in the stone, illuminating several steps away, bright as day. He dug into it and found a stone ball about the size of a fingertip. He took it home and placed it in a dark room, where it shone like a burning candle. At every banquet, he hung it in the main hall, and the faint light of dozens of seats was all visible. A high-ranking official wanted to take it from him, but the officer refused, and as a result, he was demoted. He came to treasure it even more, storing it in a box and keeping it secret and not showing it to others. After several years, the light gradually diminished, and later it was only about a foot in size. Now it is completely dark like a common stone. Some say that the stone's essence and energy were insufficient, so the light did not last long. If it had waited for several hundred years for its essence to be fully developed, it would be like a pearl that illuminates, lasting a long time without becoming dim. Is this true or not? The stone indeed concealed its light and color, and could naturally remain fresh and new over time. But it quickly revealed itself, thus the essence and energy were revealed, and it clearly faded away day by day, probably self-inflicted.

    Fish in the Soil

    In the summer of the year of the Water Rat (1670), in Jing County, laborers were digging a river and found a carp about two inches long in the soil. The laborers were surprised and exclaimed, and those who were looking at it were as dense as a wall. Among them was a person with the surname Ren, who approached closely to look at it. The fish jumped into his mouth and immediately disappeared from its place. Ren was very regretful, but in the end, he was unharmed. It is also a strange matter.

    298

    蝎虎尾
    　　癸未秋七月二十八日丑刻，忽大雨，霹雳甚巨。有二丐寄宿邑南门楼，见雷逐一物如猪，绕楼奔窜，忽匿宇下。雷击楼脊崩坼，物遂不见。后十余日，城下王氏修屋，于屋上得一蝎虎巨尾，长四五尺，粗如鸡蛋。始悟所震之物，即是也。予亲见之。
    焦某
    　　安徽焦某，从军驻扎于津。一日，从城西营中醉归，将往海光寺机器局。时已三鼓，路经城西甫野，见道旁一烟馆，其中灯火荧然。入视，矮几短榻，亦颇雅洁。乃向主人买芙蓉膏少许。见主人年三十许，短衣青被心，足着双靴，色亮殆如乌纸。未暇细审，卧榻上对灯烧吸。灯光青黯，烟筒塞如无隙。因出己囊中烟盒置盘中，挑烟烧试，灯火亦然。某口中喃喃怒詈，俄一无首人推扉入，呼曰：“買烟。”主人挑烟与之。

    Explanation

    298

    The Giant Scorpion's Tail

    On the 28th day of the seventh month in the year of the Water Goat (癸未), at the hour of the ox (丑刻), it suddenly rained heavily, accompanied by a massive thunderclap. Two beggars who were staying at the south gate of the city saw lightning chasing a creature resembling a pig, which circled the building and fled, then hid under the eaves. The lightning struck the roof ridge, causing it to collapse and crack, and the creature disappeared. Ten days later, the Wang family in the city repaired their house and found a giant scorpion's tail on the roof, measuring four to five feet long and as thick as an egg. They finally realized that the creature struck by lightning was indeed this scorpion's tail. I witnessed this event firsthand.

    Jiao from Anhui

    Jiao from Anhui, who was stationed in Tianjin, returned drunk from the military camp in the west of the city one day, intending to go to the Hailiang Temple Machinery Bureau. It was midnight, and as he passed through the outskirts of the west of the city, he saw a smoking den with dim, flickering lights. Upon entering, he saw a low table and short bed, which were quite elegant and clean. He bought a small amount of opium from the owner. The owner was a man in his thirties, wearing a short-sleeved shirt with a blue cloth heart and boots, with a very dark color. Jiao did not have time to examine the owner carefully and lay on the bed, lighting and smoking the opium. The lights were dim and dark, and the opium pipe was so clogged that there was no gap. He took out his opium box from his bag, placed it on the tray, picked up the opium, and burned it to test it, with the lights also dimming. Jiao muttered angrily, and soon a headless person pushed the door open and called, "Buy opium." The owner then picked up the opium and gave it to the person.

    299

    焦醉眼模糊，不知其为鬼，乃喝曰：“何故著衣盖顶，作此恶态，骇煞人也。”某人不答而去。主人曰：“尔以彼为丑，视我何如？”因对灯瞋视，口眼砰砰作响，舌出于口，目出于眶，累累然如铃下垂，血淋漓满面。焦大惊，奔避至南关外，厥然而倒，大号，及有人出救，某已昏不知人矣。灌以姜汁始苏，备言颠末。次日遣人往视其处，并无室宇，丛冢中地上有泥塑小圈，方圆数寸，中置烟盘。乃持归，即焦之物也。众为诧异。癸未十一月事。
    鬼眼
    　　有选人徐姓，赁居邑城东某家民宅。中多邪祟，衣食等物往往辄自失去。一夕，窗上破孔中有人自外窥视，目光炯炯射人，朗如明镜。疑其盗也，出户视之，窗外渺无人迹。以物投之，则遁去。次日视窗上并无微隙。至夜，窗上眼光依然，神色倍觉嗔怒。如是数日。焚以楮帛，祷祝，始不复见。

    Explanation

    299

    Jiao, in his drunken state with blurred vision, did not recognize the figure as a ghost and shouted, "Why are you dressed in such a frightening manner, covering your head like this? It’s terrifying!" The figure did not respond and left. The owner of the house said, "If you find that person ugly, how about me?" He then glared at the lamp, his mouth and eyes making loud noises, his tongue protruding, his eyes bulging out of their sockets, hanging down like bells, and blood dripping all over his face. Jiao was greatly startled, fled to the southern outskirts, collapsed, and cried out loudly. When someone came to save him, the figure had already fainted and was unconscious. He was revived by drinking ginger juice and recounted the entire incident. The next day, someone was sent to check the place, but there was no house to be found. In the midst of a cluster of graves, there was a small clay circle on the ground, about a few inches in diameter, with a smoking tray placed in the center. It was taken back, and it turned out to be Jiao’s own property. Everyone was astonished. This was an event that occurred in the eleventh month of the year Guiwei.

    Ghost Eye

    A man surnamed Xu, who was lodging in a rented house in the eastern part of the city, found that the house was often haunted by evil spirits, and his clothes and food would frequently disappear on their own. One night, through a broken hole in the window, someone was seen peering in from the outside, their eyes glowing brightly like a mirror. He suspected it was a thief and went out to look, but there was no trace of anyone outside. When he threw something at the figure, it vanished. The next day, he found no trace of any gap in the window. By night, the eyes still glowed through the window, and the expression seemed even more angry. This continued for several days. After burning paper money and praying, the ghost was no longer seen.

    300

    陈姓
    　　军械局中差官陈某，目能视鬼。有再醮妇病笃，发狂请语。或浼陈往视病者，陈辞之云：“予非巫觋，视之何益？”或再三求之，云：“他无所劳，请君视之。倘室中有鬼，嘱其急备后事，否则延医调之，庶不误也。”陈随往。见室有男子坐床头，怒目指詈。询其年貌衣履，其友人曰：“是妇之故夫也。”未几果卒。观此则知失节之咎，冥责难逃。夫妇既改适，而死者抱恨九泉，卒至索命而后已。然则丧偶者亦何必娶再醮妇哉!
    义仓怪
    　　邑城内义仓中多怪，湘军借为银钱所。或寓居其室，每月明，见东厢空室顶上，有物大如布囊，人恒见之，不以为怪也。一夕，有武弁某，寝于西廊。夜将半，有物揭其帐，面大几与帐等，目炯双炬，齿列群峰，白毛蓬松，长半尺许，满布其面。弁急以拳击之，物啮其拇，牙如利刃。弁大号，物不释口。甫脱出，又咬其臂，血涔涔遍席，晕然而绝。物自去。次日为人救苏，视其伤已见骨，调养数月而愈。

    Explanation

    300

    Chen Surname
    In the Military Arsenal, there was an officer named Chen who could see ghosts. There was a remarried woman who was seriously ill and went mad, begging to speak. Someone asked Chen to go and see the sick woman, and Chen declined, saying, "I am not a shaman or sorcerer, so what good would it do to see her?" The person persisted in asking, and Chen said, "I have no other tasks, so please let me see her. If there is a ghost in the room, instruct it to quickly prepare for the afterlife, otherwise, call a doctor to treat her, perhaps not to make a mistake." Chen went with them. He saw a man sitting at the head of the bed, glaring and cursing. When asked about his age, appearance, clothing, and shoes, his friend said, "He is the woman's former husband." Soon after, the woman indeed died. From this, we can see that the fault of losing virtue is hard to escape punishment in the afterlife. Even though the couple had remarried, the deceased bore resentment in the underworld and eventually took a life. Therefore, those who have lost a spouse, why marry a remarried woman?

    Strange Phenomena in the Granary
    There were many strange phenomena in the public granary within the city walls, which the Xiang Army temporarily used as a storage place for silver and money. Someone lived temporarily in the room, and every month when it was bright, people often saw something as large as a cloth bag on the top of the empty room in the east wing, but they did not find it strange. One night, a certain military officer slept in the west corridor. Just before midnight, something lifted his curtain. The face was almost as big as the curtain, with eyes shining like two torches, teeth arranged like mountain peaks, and white, fluffy hair about half a foot long, fully spread on his face. The officer quickly hit it with a fist, and the thing bit his thumb, with teeth like sharp blades. The officer shouted loudly, but the thing did not let go of his mouth. Just as he managed to get out, it bit his arm, causing blood to drip all over the mat, and he fainted and died. The thing left on its own. The next day, he was saved by someone and revived. Upon examination, his wound had already exposed bone, and he recuperated for several months and recovered.

    301

    秦裕
    　　秦裕，奉天人。夏夜看守瓜圃，见塍上有十余童子，往来担物，知为妖魅，乘间捉其一。童子衰泣求释，秦云：“与我金钱二担，则释汝，否则毙刃下矣。”童许以明晚将来，秦迫其矢誓而放之。及晚，有童子四人，送宝物二担，口中唯唯作用力声。黑暗视之，物圆如磨，金彩辉煌，知其为宝，不胜喜悦。童谓秦曰：“报君金宝二担，请笑纳之，我辈去矣。”言毕掷于地，骨董有声，纷然散去。秦喜极，取火灼视，乃苇管二枝，各贯铜钱一枚。大怒，再索童子，不可得矣。
    蛇异
    　　完县村夫，清晨灌溉瓜圃，见一小赤蛇蟠瓜蔓上，细裁如蚓。戏以物拨之，蛇忽跃然奋起，头如斗，身如梁焉。村人急奔。蛇行风响，瞬息即集足下。骇极而倒，蛇失所在。及视故处，蛇蟠蔓上如初。

    Explanation

    301

    Qin Yu

    Qin Yu, a man from Fengtian, was guarding a melon patch one summer night. He saw several children, about ten in number, carrying objects along the raised path of the melon patch. Realizing they were supernatural beings, Qin Yu took advantage of a moment to catch one of them. The child wept and begged for release, saying, "If you give me two loads of money, I will let you go. Otherwise, I will be killed by your sword." The child promised to bring the money the next evening, and Qin Yu forced the child to swear and then released him. That evening, four children brought two loads of treasure, speaking softly as they carried the objects. In the darkness, the objects appeared round like grinding stones, with golden brilliance. Qin Yu knew they were valuable and was overjoyed. The children told Qin Yu, "Here are two loads of gold and treasure for you. Please accept them with a smile, and we will leave." After saying this, they threw the objects to the ground, making a clattering sound, and quickly dispersed. Qin Yu was so happy that he lit a fire to examine the objects, but they turned out to be two bundles of reed pipes, each with a single copper coin. He became extremely angry and tried to catch the children again, but they were gone.

    Snake Anomaly

    A villager from Wanyuan was watering the melon patch in the early morning. He saw a small red snake coiled on the melon vine, thin as an earthworm. Playfully, he tried to move the snake with an object, but the snake suddenly leapt up, its head as large as a winnowing basket and its body as thick as a beam. The villager ran away in panic. The snake moved with a sound like the wind and, in an instant, was at the villager's feet. The villager was so terrified that he fell over, and the snake disappeared. When he looked back, the snake was still coiled on the melon vine as before.

    302

    猬火
    　　邑李氏家祠中，往往见怪异。有守祠者，夜见后院红光熠熠如燔柴，疑其火也。亟视之，一老猬立墀下，高于三岁童子，口中喃喃作人语，其双目放光类炬然。
    鸡异四则
    　　有人乘海舶行至巨洋，忽波涛翻沸，一巨鸡其大如鹤，黑色羽毛，濯濯有光彩，立船头。一炊许，始飞入波中而逝。博物者不能指其名。
    　　河南某县，雨后忽有一雄鸡，自空飞落，高二三尺，雄冠长距，文彩夺目。或捕而烹之，鸡忽自釜中飞起，火从灶出，延烧街道数里。
    　　西贾段某，寓居京都某家空室。夜见鸡雏数十，从地隙出。穷其处，在院西隅。发之，得银锭无算，上铸崇祯年号。殆前巨绅之故第也。
    　　满城农夫，于村外见一黑鸡，双睛突出，神采悍怒。云逐之，入苍莽中，卒获之。烹食则吐泄大作，膈冷如冰。识者谓此物乃新死者之煞也。

    Explanation

    302

    Hedgehog Fire
    In the clan hall of the Li family in a certain town, strange occurrences were often seen. One night, a caretaker of the hall saw a bright red light flickering like burning wood in the backyard, and he suspected it was a fire. He hurriedly went to investigate and found an old hedgehog standing below the steps, taller than a three-year-old child, speaking in human-like murmurs, with its eyes glowing like torches.

    Four Strange Incidents Involving Chickens
    A man traveling by sea to a vast ocean suddenly saw the waves boiling over. A giant chicken, as large as a crane, with black feathers shining brightly, stood on the ship's deck. After about the time it takes to cook a meal, it flew into the waves and disappeared. Scholars could not identify its name.

    In a certain county in Henan, after a rainstorm, a rooster suddenly flew down from the sky, standing about two to three feet tall, with a prominent comb and long spurs, its feathers brightly colored and striking. Some captured and cooked it. The rooster suddenly flew out of the pot, and fire came out of the stove, spreading for several miles along the street.

    A merchant named Duan from the west was staying in an empty room of someone's house in the capital. One night, he saw dozens of chicks coming out from a crack in the ground. He searched for their source and found them in the western corner of the courtyard. When he dug there, he found countless silver ingots, each stamped with the era name of Chongzhen. These were likely from the former mansion of a prominent official.

    A farmer from a village outside the city saw a black chicken with protruding eyes and a fierce, angry appearance. They chased it, and it entered a dense forest. Eventually, they caught it. When they cooked and ate it, they experienced severe vomiting and diarrhea, and their chests felt cold as ice. Knowledgeable people believed this was the spirit of a recently deceased person.

    303

    产妖
    　　邑人张某，生一子，兽首人身，其状如猫，口巨几连其耳。置之隘巷，人争视之。此光绪十一年四月八日事。
    羊怪
    　　邑人某，偶出南郊，至一处，去城十里许，芳草迷人，野花布地。徘徊间，见坟穴中出一羊首如栲栳，狰狞可畏。邑人蹀躞而返。回视其处，旋风蔽天，物驾风高入云表，向西南而没。不知何怪。
    张杰
    　　张杰者，山右人，流落津门，为逆旅佣工。一日，自杨柳驿乘船归，有妇人与共渡，年可至十许，风致嫣然，仿佛富室仆妇。心窃好之，与之语，言词便捷。既渡，各分路去。迂回数里，始至旅店。妇人先候于门。张讶其先至，惊诘之，妇云：“天色已晚，无所投止，浼君寄宿耳。”张错愕未及答，而妇已入。仓卒间，旋失所在。及夜张归，寝室逼仄，土榻仅容一人，妇已坐于榻，展裀褥焉。速其灭烛，相与共寝，绸缪备至。

    Explanation

    303

    Final Translation

    产妖
    A Man from the Town
    A man from the town named Zhang gave birth to a son with a beast's head and a human body, resembling a cat. His mouth was enormous, almost connected to his ears. When placed in a narrow alley, people gathered to watch him. This happened on the eighth day of the fourth month in the eleventh year of the Guangxu era.

    羊怪
    A Sheep Demon
    A man from the town once went out to the southern suburbs and arrived at a place about ten miles from the city. The fragrant grass was enchanting, and wildflowers covered the ground. While wandering, he saw a sheep's head emerge from a grave, the size of a large gourd, fierce and terrifying. The man hurried back. When he looked back at the place, a whirlwind covered the sky, and the object rode the wind high into the clouds, heading southwest before disappearing. It is unknown what kind of demon it was.

    张杰
    Zhang Jie
    Zhang Jie was a person from Shanxi who had wandered to Tianjin and worked as a hired worker at an inn. One day, he returned by boat from Yangliu Post Station and met a woman who shared the journey with him. She was around ten years old, graceful and charming, and seemed like a servant from a wealthy household. He secretly admired her and conversed with her; her words were quick and fluent. After crossing the river, they each went their separate ways. After wandering several miles, they finally reached the inn. The woman had already waited at the door. Zhang was surprised that she had arrived first and asked her why. She said, "It is already late, and I have nowhere to stay, so I asked you to let me stay the night." Zhang was startled and did not have time to answer when the woman had already entered. In a hurry, she suddenly disappeared. When Zhang returned at night, the bedroom was cramped, and the earth bed only fit one person. The woman was already sitting on the bed, spreading the mat and quilt. She quickly extinguished the candle, and they lay down together, their relationship being closely and thoroughly intimate.

    304

    张询其为谁，妇云：“我仙人也，与汝有缘，当秘之，不为君祸。”天明自去。每来必先灭烛，不但人不之知，即张亦未睹其容也。如是两年余，有僚仆夜闻张室人语声，疑其纳妓也，迫诘之，张以实告，其不信。至夜，坐以待之，遂不复来。张已绝望。一日，张出西郊，有妇人自后呼曰：“戒尔勿泄，何便失言？缘尽于是矣。”张回顾，殊不相识，妇曰：“两载好合，临别不会面，亦属憾事。”张恍然，顿首谢罪，既起而妇杳然矣。自此遂绝。

    Explanation

    304

    Zhang asked who she was. The woman replied, "I am a fairy, and I have a connection with you. You should keep it secret, it will not bring you harm." She left at dawn. Every time she came, she would first extinguish the candles, not only did no one know about it, but Zhang also never saw her face. For over two years, one of Zhang's servants heard voices in Zhang's room at night and suspected that Zhang was taking in a courtesan. He pressed Zhang for an explanation, and Zhang told him the truth, but he did not believe him. That night, he sat waiting for her, and she never came back. Zhang had given up hope. One day, Zhang went out to the western suburbs, and a woman called out to him from behind, "I warned you not to reveal it, how did you lose your word? Our fate ends here." Zhang looked back, but did not recognize her. The woman said, "Two years of harmony, but we won't meet face-to-face before parting, which is also regrettable." Zhang suddenly understood and bowed his head in apology. After he stood up, the woman vanished. From then on, she never came back.

    305

    醉茶子曰：两载绸缪，妍媸莫辨，此不奇于仙之幻，而奇于张之愚也。夫西子、无盐，以暗室逢之，应无所异。张之愚，正张之黠矣。彼空即色，色非即空耶？
    金化水
    　　谚有云：“时来则黄土变金，运败则黄金成土。”此虽寓言，然实有之。邑有朱某者，其先家本富，母溺爱过情，藏有白镪六罂，为之私瘗于室内，嘱曰：“毋妄奢费，汝他时乡会试，可取作川资也。”母死后，游荡不羁，呼卢唤雉，柳巷花街，无所不为。不数载，家产荡然，而妻亦亡，孑然一身，室如悬罄。因忆所瘗之金，发之，则罂固如故，启视满贮皆清水，六器皆然。朱卒以困死。噫！异矣。

    Explanation

    305

    The Drunken Tea Drinker said: For two years, he meticulously prepared, yet could not distinguish between beauty and ugliness. This is not strange in the way of the fairy's illusion, but rather strange in Zhang's foolishness. When encountering Xi Shi and Wu Yan in a dark room, one should find no difference. Zhang's foolishness, indeed, is Zhang's cleverness. That emptiness is form, and form is not emptiness, is it not?

    Gold Turning to Water

    There is a saying: "When luck comes, clay turns to gold; when luck wanes, gold turns to clay." Although this is a parable, it is indeed true. In a certain county, there was a man named Zhu. His ancestors were wealthy, and his mother loved him excessively, hiding six jars of silver in the house, instructing him: "Do not waste extravagantly; when you take the local and provincial examinations in the future, you can use this as travel money." After his mother's death, Zhu became a vagrant, gambling and indulging in brothels and pleasure districts, doing everything without restraint. Within a few years, his family's wealth was depleted, and his wife also died. He was left alone, with a room as empty as a bell. Remembering the buried gold, he dug it up, and the jars were still as before. Upon opening them, they were all filled with clear water, and all six jars were the same. Zhu eventually died of poverty. Alas! How strange!

    306

    尸起
    　　癸未七月夜，暴风怒雷，即城南震蝎虎时也。邑东城中华姓翁卒，停尸未殓。经霹雳一震，尸忽挺然起立。守灵者骇散，急掩其门。门未得闭，而尸追及。幸为扉隔，未蘧出，抱扉而僵。至晓天晴，众视尸尚亦倚门立，右手四指掐入木中，深没其甲，数人力挽始出。此亦尸变之类。夫雷能辟邪，此反因雷致变。何也？
    古磁缸
    　　邑北仓赵氏院中，有一磁缸，式圆如钵，下承以砖台，多年物也。一日阴霾，雷电绕之，有巨手自空中下，据缸空际。旋有一巨蝎虎，自砖中出，骤为雷击毙。俄而缸置故处，巨手冉冉入云而没。众视缸口有指痕五，甚巨，深入寸许，嗅之有异香。乡人抚其痕以疗疾，云颇有效。今缸仍存。

    Explanation

    306

    The Corpse Rises
    On the night of the seventh month of the Water Goat year, a violent storm with thunder and lightning swept through the city, coinciding with the time when a scorpion-like creature was struck by lightning in the southern part of the city. In the eastern part of the city, a man surnamed Zhang from the Chinese surname died, and his body was still lying in state, not yet buried. After a clap of thunder, the corpse suddenly rose to its feet. The people guarding the body were frightened and scattered, hurriedly trying to close the door. However, the door was not closed in time, and the corpse caught up with them. Fortunately, the door prevented the corpse from fully emerging, and it remained stuck to the door. By the next morning, when the sky was clear, the people saw the corpse still standing against the door, with its right hand's four fingers embedded in the wood, deeply into the nails. It took several people to pull the corpse out. This was also a case of a corpse coming back to life. Lightning is said to be able to ward off evil, but in this case, it caused a transformation. Why was that?

    Ancient Porcelain Jar
    In the courtyard of a Surname Zhao's house in the northern warehouse of the city, there was an ancient porcelain jar, shaped like a bowl and supported by a brick base. It was an old object. One day, under a gloomy sky, lightning surrounded the jar. A giant hand descended from the sky and reached out to the jar. Soon after, a large scorpion-like creature emerged from the bricks and was immediately struck by lightning and killed. The jar was then placed back in its original position, and the giant hand slowly rose into the clouds and disappeared. The people saw five large finger marks on the jar's opening, deeply embedded. The marks had a strange fragrance. Local people used the marks to treat illnesses, and it was said to be quite effective. The jar still exists today.

    307

    优伶某
    　　优伶某，夜与二人对奕。忽窗棂作响，一鬼物突入，头大如斗，面如涂朱，目光四射，咄咄逼人。几上灯火如豆，忽飘飘然悬于空。伶与二人攒抱一处，未敢作声。俄有扣门者，则鬼忽不见，视灯仍在几上。不数日，伶与二人俱卒。
    河间乙
    　　河间乙，将之豫省，晚息旅店。忽墙壁摇摇欲倒，一巨手从墙中出，五指如柱，屋为之满。骇急奔出。有二足自檐际下，胫粗如瓮。大号，店主至，物始不见。
    齐某
    　　齐某，束鹿人。与其友往山左，晚投旅店。友出买物，某挑灯坐。灯忽青暗，一披发矮妇人自几下出，面貌枯瘦，眉目愁惨，伛偻前行。某方惊愕间，又出一男子，貌亦惨淡，并肩立榻前，似有所诉。某惊号失声，其友适推扉入，物俱不见。因移居他店。

    Explanation

    307

    优伶某
    A performer named [Name], at night, played chess with two others. Suddenly, the window frame made a sound, and a ghostly figure suddenly entered. Its head was as large as a winnowing basket, its face like painted vermilion, its eyes radiating in all directions, pressing hard with a threatening manner. The lamp on the table was as small as a bean, and it suddenly floated and suspended in the air. The performer and the two others huddled together in one place, dared not make a sound. Soon, there was someone knocking on the door, and the ghost suddenly disappeared. They looked and saw the lamp still on the table. Not long after, the performer and the two others all died.

    河间乙
    A person from Hejian, named [Name], was heading to Henan Province and stayed at an inn late at night. Suddenly, the walls seemed to be about to collapse, and a giant hand emerged from the wall, its fingers as thick as pillars, filling the room. Terrified, they rushed out. Two feet descended from the eaves, thick as a jar. They shouted loudly, and the innkeeper arrived, and the objects all disappeared.

    齐某
    A person from Shulü, named [Name], went to the eastern part of Shandong Province with a friend and stayed at an inn late at night. The friend went out to buy something, and [Name] lit a lamp and sat down. The lamp suddenly turned green and dim, and a short, thin woman with disheveled hair emerged from under the table, her face gaunt and her eyes sorrowful, hunching forward. [Name] was just startled when another man emerged, his appearance also somber, standing next to the bed, as if he had something to say. [Name] screamed in fright, and just then, his friend pushed open the door, and the objects all disappeared. They then moved to another inn.

    308

    曹商
    　　曹商，山右太谷人，与其乡人贸易于津。夜归，路经南郭外。时已三鼓，四顾无人。见有小车长尺许，马大如鼠，人裁如指，戴小缨帽如钱，辘辘然循墙而走。其行甚驶，曹力追莫及，恚曰：“戋戋之物，行何速也，待我追及踏碎之！”将至郭门，车稍停，曹踵至。忽砰然一响，高与城齐，人如魍魉，马类犀象，执鞭怒视，其状狞恶。闻车中人语曰：“此西贾也，宁舍命而断不舍财。勿伤其生，搜取腰中钱钞可也。”曹惊踣大号。转瞬竟失所在。自扪囊中财，固不曾失也，狼狈而返。曹其遇仙耶？何变相之速也？索其财而不索其命，仙固因其畏者而戏之耳。夫爱财如命，岂独西贾，特仙未之遇也。

    Explanation

    308

    Cao Shang, a merchant from Taigou, Shanxi, was trading with his fellow villagers at the market. On his way home at night, he passed through the area outside the southern gate. It was already past midnight, and there was no one around. He saw a small cart about a foot long, with a horse as big as a mouse and a person as thin as a finger, wearing a small hat like a coin. The cart rolled along the wall. It moved very quickly, and Cao could not catch up. He angrily said, "This trifling thing, how fast does it move? Wait for me to catch up and crush it!" As he approached the city gate, the cart slowed down slightly, and Cao followed. Suddenly, there was a loud bang, and the cart grew as high as the city wall. The people looked like ghosts, and the horses resembled rhinos and elephants. They held whips and glared angrily, their appearance fierce and evil. Cao heard the person in the cart say, "This is a Western merchant, who would rather give up their life than their wealth. Do not harm their life, just take the money in their waistband." Cao fell down and screamed loudly. In an instant, they disappeared. He felt in his bag and found his money was still there, and he returned in a disheveled state. Did Cao meet a fairy? How quickly did the transformation occur? They took his money but not his life. The fairy certainly played with him because of his fear. Loving money as much as life, not just Western merchants, but the fairy hasn't encountered many people.

    309

    张老殿
    　　张老殿，邑人，居御河北。其院之东室，空无人居，用积零物。夏月，每晚纳凉庭中，有旋风贴地而来，入东室而没。怪而掘地，至五六尺，得一巨瓮，石板覆之。揭视，乃一老僧尸，迭膝端坐，皮骨已僵。不知何代释子，何年圆寂，惜无碑碣可考也。张畏而仍瘗之。

    醉茶志怪卷四
    爱哥
    　　杜翁，直隶人。家富于财而艰于嗣。五十余得一女，名爱哥，视同拱壁，溺爱过于常情。稍长，饰以男子装，呼为公子，人莫辨其雄与雌也。恐人见疑，使常酬酢于乡里间。而女亦自忘其为女，公然以丈夫自居。十五六岁，患痘几危，翁遍延名医调治，得无恙。既瘥，面麻削瘦，其貌不扬。而乡人羡其家，争愿妻之以女，翁俱未允。延师于别业，教之读。爱哥殊骄纵不驯，师悉含忍，遣聪明僮仆三四人伴之读，且供服役。每塾中日课，辄遣诸僮代，而翁不之知。翁期望颇切，课功甚密，又不忍拂女意，每示意于师。

    Explanation

    309

    Translation

    张老殿

    Zhang Lao Temple

    Zhang Lao Temple was a local resident who lived north of the Imperial River. His courtyard had an east room that was empty and unused, serving as a storage space for miscellaneous items. During the summer months, he would often cool off in the courtyard at night. One evening, a whirlwind skimming along the ground entered the east room and disappeared. Curious, he dug into the ground and found a large jar covered by a stone slab. Upon lifting the slab and looking inside, he discovered the mummified body of an old monk, sitting with his knees crossed, and his skin and bones were stiff. It was unclear which dynasty this monk belonged to or the year he passed away, as there were no inscriptions or markers to provide any information. Zhang, feeling frightened, buried the body again.

    醉茶志怪卷四

    Love Ge

    Du, a man from Zhili, was wealthy but had difficulty having children. In his fifties, he finally had a daughter named Ai Ge, whom he cherished as if she were a precious jade, showing more affection than usual. As she grew older, he dressed her in boys' clothing and called her "Young Master," and no one could tell whether she was a boy or a girl. To avoid suspicion, he often sent her to social gatherings in the village. Ai Ge herself forgot that she was a girl and openly identified as a man. At fifteen or sixteen, she contracted smallpox and nearly died. Her father called in many famous doctors to treat her, and she eventually recovered. After her illness, her face was pockmarked and thin, making her appearance unattractive. Despite this, the villagers admired her family and competed to offer their daughters to marry her, but her father refused all offers. He hired a teacher at a separate estate to educate her. Ai Ge was particularly spoiled and disobedient. The teacher endured her behavior, sending three or four intelligent servants to accompany her in her studies and serve her. Every day, she would send the servants to do her homework in the school, and her father was unaware of this. Her father had high expectations for her and set strict standards for her studies, but he also did not want to upset her, so he often hinted to the teacher about her behavior.

    310

    久无进益，辄怼怨，情见乎辞。师不耐，遂辞去。女益无忌，谓翁曰：“儿之学业已成，无待延师。”翁从其言。乃日处馆中，与诸僮博戏。翁询所读之书，颇能朗诵。翁益惑而溺爱之，每食必鸡舌羹。羹一杯须杀鸡数十，日以为常。翁虽殷富，而吝啬异常。爱哥任意挥霍，翁心虽痛惜，然以宠故，亦隐忍而姑纵之。诸僮皆服以鲜衣，一出入随诸其后，如锦簇花团。凡酒楼茶馆，无不任意游荡。又结纨裤子弟四五辈，订为昆仲。奢侈斗富，征遂酒食，日无宁晷，众竟莫知其雄与雌也。有优伶才官者，技貌双绝，一时名重。

    310

    A Child of Unusual Nature

    For a long time, the child showed no progress, always complaining, and his emotions were revealed through his words. The teacher, unable to tolerate this, left. The woman became even more unrestrained and told the old man, "My child's studies are complete; there is no need to hire a teacher." The old man agreed with her. The child then spent his days in the school, playing games with the other boys. When the old man asked what books he had read, the child could recite them quite well. The old man became even more confused and doted on him, insisting that he must have chicken tongue soup with every meal. One bowl of soup required killing dozens of chickens, and this became a daily habit. Although the old man was wealthy, he was extremely stingy. He let his son spend money freely, and though the old man's heart was pained by this, he also endured and tolerated it because of his affection for the child. The other boys were all dressed in fine clothes and followed him in and out, like a cluster of brocade and flowers. He wandered around freely in every tavern and teahouse. He also associated with several young nobles and formed a brotherhood with them. They competed in luxury and wealth, indulging in feasts and banquets, with no moment of peace. Everyone was unsure of his gender. There was a talented actor or official, both skill and appearance were exceptional, and he gained renown for a period.

    311
