    守使人许以金而索其尺，司理力白其无。太守疑其居奇，益恨之。时郡中有巨盗案，守阴使人言于盗，污司理为同伙，所言里居甚详。捉司理至，搒掠甚苦，终无词，陷于囹圄。盗中有任姓者，性梗直，知司理冤，乃谓曰：“予虽大盗，具有天理。明日堂上，予代白君冤而噬余某，使彼来纠缠此案，何如？”司理泥首泣请。越翌日，守理案，盗诬余，请守行文关之。齐东令某，与守有旧，差役捕余，搜其家，则铜尺在其柜中，赃证并获。余口不能辨，遂坐同党罪，以铜尺入官，司理得免。

    Explanation

    359

    A Magistrate's Ordeal and a Bandit's Redemption

    The prefect sent someone to offer gold in exchange for the location of a copper ruler, but the magistrate vigorously defended himself, stating that he did not possess it. The prefect suspected the magistrate of hoarding the ruler and grew even more resentful. At the time, there was a major case involving a notorious bandit in the prefecture. The prefect secretly instructed someone to tell the bandits that the magistrate was an accomplice, and the information provided was very detailed. The magistrate was arrested and subjected to severe torture, but he remained silent, eventually being imprisoned. Among the bandits, there was a man surnamed Ren who was straightforward and honest. Knowing the magistrate's innocence, he said to him, "Although I am a bandit, I still have a sense of right and wrong. Tomorrow in court, I will speak on your behalf and expose the prefect's treachery. What do you think?" The magistrate fell to his knees and begged. The next day, the prefect presided over the case, and the bandit falsely accused the magistrate, asking the prefect to issue an order to arrest him. The magistrate of Qidong, who had a prior relationship with the prefect, arrested the magistrate and searched his home. The copper ruler was found in his cabinet, and the evidence of guilt was discovered. The magistrate could not defend himself, so he was convicted of being an accomplice. The copper ruler was confiscated by the authorities, and the magistrate was spared.

    360

    既出，阴怪其事，而未知其由。自遭大讼，贫无立锥，至于悬釜。一日，将乞米于邻，甫出门，有老姥持五斗米求售。司理诉其窘，姥慨以米赠。司理感之，邀至其家。生视之，阿菱之外祖母也，不觉失声。司理询其故，生以实告。司理颇觉自惭，向姆谢过。姥云：“君爱子之情，老身何怪。前日铜尺，使获脏于余氏者，皆我为之。”司理深感其义，询阿菱何在，姥云：“纸上符箓，其奈我何！阿菱固无恙也。”司理亟求婚姻，姥不许。乃跪求之，始应其请。

    Explanation

    360

    After he came out, he secretly found the matter strange but did not know the reason. After being involved in a major lawsuit, he was so poor that he had no place to stand, to the point of hanging a pot for cooking. One day, just as he was about to leave the door to beg for rice from his neighbor, an elderly woman held five dou of rice to sell. The official told her of his predicament, and the woman generously gave him the rice. The official was moved by her kindness and invited her to his home. When he saw her, he realized she was the grandmother of Arling and couldn't help but cry out. The official asked her why, and the official told her the truth. The official felt quite ashamed and apologized to her. The woman said, "Your love for your son, how could I blame you? The copper ruler from the previous day, which led to the accusation against the Yu family, was all done by me." The official deeply appreciated her righteousness and asked where Arling was. The woman said, "Paper talismans can do nothing to me! Arling is certainly fine." The official urgently sought marriage, but the woman refused. He then knelt and begged, and she finally agreed to his request.

    361

    因问姥居何里，姥云：“予僦居城南王氏第，君择良辰往聘之可也。”于是以百金赠司理，使备青庐，遂出门去。司理备乘舆，至期往，见王氏第仆役纷纭，金玉锦绣，居然素封，奁物丰侈，有珊瑚镜台高三尺许，中嵌汉时透光镜，其余珍物，多不能指其名。众视阿菱，皆惊为神仙中人，比燕犹肥，较环稍瘦，才如道韫，德似孟光，虽西子、王嫱，不能擅美于前也。人见之罔不惊叹。生家自此亦小康。闻刘守终以墨败。菱循循妇道，善理家，终日欣欣无戚容。尝谓生曰：“天下事强合者终离，违天故也。今而后可百年矣。”

    Explanation

    361

    The man then asked the elderly woman where she lived. She replied, "I rent a house in the Wang family mansion in the southern part of the city. You should choose a good day to go and propose marriage." He then gave a hundred gold pieces to the official (司理) to prepare for the wedding, and the official set up a green tent (青庐). The man left to go home. The official prepared a carriage, and on the appointed day, he went to the Wang family mansion. There, he saw a bustling household filled with servants and attendants, adorned with gold, jade, silk, and brocade, truly resembling a wealthy noble household. The bridal chamber was richly furnished, with a coral mirror stand about three feet high, containing a Han dynasty translucent mirror, and many other precious items whose names could not be identified. Everyone who saw Arling (阿菱) was amazed, considering her a being from the realm of immortals. She was plumper than Yan (燕) but slightly thinner than Ring (环). Her talent was like that of Tao Yun (道韫), and her virtue like that of Meng Guang (孟光). Even Xi Shi (西子) and Wang Qiang (王嫱), the two most beautiful women in history, could not surpass her beauty. Everyone who saw her could not help but be amazed. From this marriage, the man's family also became moderately wealthy. It was heard that Liu Shou (刘守) eventually failed due to corruption. Arling followed the proper conduct of a wife and was good at managing the household, always appearing cheerful and without a trace of sadness. She once told the man, "In the world, what is forced together will eventually separate, because it goes against nature. From now on, we can live together for a hundred years."

    362

    汪某
    　　汪某者，传者忘其县邑。家中惟一老母。有妹幽娴贞静，及笄，许字于邻村管翁之子，亦务农为业。相隔十余里，素相稔熟。每遇于市廛，辄相醵饮，情益亲密。妹于归后，至旬月归宁。及归夫家，则兄御车送之，谓之送亲，乡俗然也。管翁知汪偕来，备酒肴与汪痛饮，晚即止宿。有汪村无赖子觊觎其妆奁，夜穿窬入妇室，攫衣袱去。妇惊大号，夫起追之。汪亦醒，逐诸其后。管氏子追数里，将及，贼恐，弃袱道左，管追犹不已，奔及，从后抱其腰。盗不得脱，急抽刀刺其胁，颓然而倒。

    Explanation

    362

    Wang Mou, whose specific county and district are unknown, lived with his elderly mother at home. He had a sister who was quietly virtuous and gentle. When she reached the age of marriage, she was engaged to the son of Mr. Guan from the neighboring village, who also worked as a farmer. They had been familiar with each other for a long time, and whenever they met in the market, they would drink together, and their relationship became even closer. After a short period, she returned home for a visit. When she returned to her husband's house, her brother drove the carriage to accompany her. Mr. Guan knew Wang Mou would come with her and prepared food and wine to drink heartily with him. They stayed overnight.

    There was a rascal from Wang's village who coveted her dowry and sneaked into the wife's room at night, grabbing her clothes and leaving. The wife screamed loudly in shock, and her husband got up to chase him. Wang Mou also woke up and chased the thief behind. The husband ran several miles and was about to catch up. The thief, fearing for his life, dropped the clothes on the side of the road. The husband continued to chase and eventually caught up, grabbing the thief by the waist from behind. The thief could not escape and desperately stabbed the husband in the side, causing him to fall down.

    363

    盗见其毙，归家裹粮夜遁。迨汪后至，见路旁有衣袱，知为妹物，视其处，离己家不远，拾归，待明日为妹送之，至管为盗杀，则未见也。天明，管翁以子久不归，往寻之。见尸横道旁，疑汪所杀，鸣于官。遣役将同往，诣汪家。汪出见翁，戏云：“衣物已失，谅难璧返，翁胡为乎来？”翁无语，入其室，见袱色变。汪举袱付翁云：“本欲为翁送去，今既来，请自将去。”翁让之，曰：“赃证并获，夫复何言！”汪大骇。不容分辩，捉将官里去。邑宰黄某，毒施五刑诬服，坐以因奸致杀，拟兄妹并抵。

    Explanation

    363

    The thief, seeing the body lying there, returned home to pack food and fled at night. When Wang arrived later, he saw a bundle of clothes by the roadside, recognized them as his sister's belongings, and noticed that the location was not far from his own home. He picked up the bundle and brought it back, intending to deliver it to his sister the next day. However, when it came to Guan being killed by the thief, Wang did not see it. At dawn, Guan's father, seeing his son had not returned for a long time, went to look for him. He found his body lying by the roadside and suspected that Wang had killed him, so he reported to the authorities. The officials sent some men to accompany him to Wang's home. Wang came out to meet the old man and teasingly said, "The clothes are already lost, it would be difficult to return them as good as new, why did you come?" The old man remained silent, entered his room, and saw the bundle change color. Wang picked up the bundle and handed it to the old man, saying, "I originally wanted to deliver it for you, but since you have come, please take it yourself." The old man reproached him, saying, "The evidence and the stolen goods were both found, what more can be said?" Wang was greatly alarmed. There was no room for argument, and he was taken to the authorities. The county magistrate Huang inflicted cruel punishments to force a confession, convicting him on the charge of killing due to adultery, and proposed to execute both the brother and sister.

    364

    汪有舅刘某，疑汪冤，第案已结，无可奈何。越数载，贸易关东，遇乡人，即杀管之盗也。相见甚欢，遂并入酒市饮。语次屡以管家失盗事盘问甚苦，某云：“管氏子冤已伸。”问：“何以伸？”云：“其妻与妻兄并抵。”盗惊曰：“冤哉！”刘以其言有因，苦诘之。盗似悔失言，语多支吾。刘乃亟劝以酒，盗大醉，试以言相诱，且云将烦其司会计。盗甚感，徐以言餂，尽吐肝鬲，云：“若非知交，此情不敢泄。杀管子者，实我也。前在乡时，仆落魄好赌，不得已夜盗其物，被彼穷追。

    Explanation

    364

    Wang has an uncle named Liu, who suspected that Wang was wronged, but the case had already been concluded, leaving him helpless. After several years, Liu engaged in trade in the eastern region and met a fellow villager, who was actually the thief who killed Guan. They met with great joy and then went together to drink at an inn. During their conversation, Liu repeatedly interrogated the thief about the theft from the Guan household, causing him great distress. The thief said, "The son of the Guan family's injustice has been rectified." Liu asked, "How was it rectified?" The thief replied, "His wife and her brother were both punished." The thief exclaimed, "How unfair!" Liu, sensing that his words had a reason behind them, pressed him hard for more information. The thief seemed to regret having spoken, and spoke incoherently and evasively. Liu then urged him to drink heavily, and the thief became very drunk. Liu tried to coax him with words and mentioned that he would trouble him to manage the accounts. The thief was very grateful and gradually coaxed him with words, completely revealing his innermost thoughts: "If not for a close friend, I would not dare to reveal this. The one who killed Guan was actually me. Before in the village, I was penniless and addicted to gambling. Unwillingly, I stole from him at night and was chased by him relentlessly."

    365

    予弃其袱于道左，以为得物斯已耳。不料彼尚纠缠，且云识我，将为播扬。窃想邻村本皆熟识，此后何以见人？急抽刀刺之，遂毙其命。潜逃此处，得小负贩，聊以无馁。不知冤连多命，此悔何追！”刘云：“大丈夫既往不咎，此宜缄秘，请勿谈也。”盗唯唯。乃邀入逆旅，复酌。盗醉如泥，卧眠榻上，刘遣主人坐守之，诣官鸣冤。官即差役往捕，盗犹未醒。至公堂，未刑而服，乃移文发回本县。是时黄令宰任邱县，闻其事忧闷欲死。有王姓武举，虎而冠者，居恒奔走公门，鱼肉乡里，诸多强霸事。黄喜其为鹰犬，相交甚昵。

    Explanation

    365

    I left my bundle on the side of the road, thinking that getting the object would be enough. I didn't expect him to纠缠, and he claimed to know me, and would spread it around. I secretly thought that everyone in the neighboring villages knew me, and how can I face people after this? I quickly drew my knife and stabbed him, killing him. I fled here secretly, found a small peddler, and barely managed to survive. I didn't know that this would lead to more deaths, and now there's no way to regret it!" Liu said, "A man of honor should not dwell on past mistakes. This should be kept secret, please don't talk about it." The thief nodded in agreement. He then invited him to an inn and had another drink. The thief was drunk as a mud, lying asleep on the bed. Liu sent the owner to guard him while he went to the authorities to report the injustice. The official immediately sent the constables to capture him, but the thief was still asleep. When he arrived at the courthouse, he confessed without being tortured. The case was then transferred back to the county. At that time, the magistrate Huang governed Renqiu County, and he was so distressed by the news that he wanted to die. There was a martial arts candidate named Wang, who was fierce and wore a hat, always running around in the public offices, oppressing the people, and doing many tyrannical things. Magistrate Huang was pleased with him as his hound, and they were very close.

    366

    王偶入官署，见黄不怿，询其故，黄以实告，且求筹策。王云：“是不难，某自关外来，必经某县，遣心腹人暗贿禁卒，使毙于狱，则事化乌有矣。”黄善其言，令其侄与一干仆怀千金往，其计果行。黄喜，造王申谢，倾谈甚欢。言次有旋风蓬蓬然起自座后，二人毛发俱懔。黄起告辞，王送之。及归，见从帐中出一女子。方骇怪间，女子辄往帐后。揭帐视之，阒其无人。惊而成疾，渐至不起。黄宰知之，急来问疾，王诉其所见。宰询女子年貌，王备言之，宰惊颜如土，托故辞出。盖女子即汪氏，宰知之而不敢言也。

    Explanation

    366

    Wang chanced upon entering the office and saw Huang looking unhappy. He asked why, and Huang told the truth and sought advice. Wang said, "It is not difficult. I am traveling from outside the pass and will pass through a certain county. Send a trusted person to secretly bribe the jailer, causing him to die in prison, and the matter will be as if it never happened." Huang approved of his plan and ordered his nephew and several servants to carry a thousand gold pieces to carry it out. The plan succeeded. Huang was pleased and visited Wang to express his gratitude. They engaged in a long, pleasant conversation. Suddenly, a whirlwind arose behind their seats, and both were terrified, with their hair standing on end. Huang got up to take his leave, and Wang saw him off. Upon returning home, he saw a woman emerging from behind the screen. As he was startled and bewildered, the woman quickly retreated behind the screen. When he pulled back the curtain to look, there was no one there. He was so frightened that he fell ill and gradually became gravely ill. The magistrate, learning of this, hurried to visit him and inquire about his illness. Wang recounted what he had seen. The magistrate asked about the woman's appearance, and Wang described her in detail. The magistrate was shocked and pale, made an excuse, and left. It turned out that the woman was actually Wang's wife, and the magistrate knew this but dared not speak of it.

    367

    先是，王有甥杜某，性诚笃，为富室经营钱行。王尝借贷，恃强横悍不还，数年来拖累颇多。甥索债则加诟谇，畏其势不敢与争。而富室诬其甥舅合谋，讼于官。官偏袒王，不理其词，富室益逼杜。杜冤忿填胸，而为人刚直，货田产以偿居停，不足抵十之一，家业已空，坐以待毙。闻舅病，往探之。王方袒卧昼眠，呼之醒，大怒，骂曰：“我以汝为望舅来耶，汝乃急讨债耶!银钱俱有，恐汝将不去。”杜婉言哀恳，至泣下，王词色愈厉。杜窘甚，自思进退一死，不如早先杀却，抽其枕底小刀子，猛刺之，中其腹。

    Explanation

    367

    Previously, Wang had a nephew named Du, who was sincere and honest, and managed a money shop for a wealthy family. Wang often borrowed money but, relying on his strength and behaving in a domineering manner, refused to repay it. Over the years, this caused significant trouble. When Du demanded repayment, he was subjected to insults and abuse, and out of fear of Wang's power, he dared not argue. The wealthy man falsely accused Du and his uncle of conspiring together and took the matter to court. The official favored Wang, ignored Du's claims, and the wealthy man increased his pressure on Du. Filled with unjust anger, Du, being a straightforward person, sold his land and property to repay the debt, but it was insufficient to cover even one-tenth of the debt. His family's assets were depleted, and he was left to wait for death. Hearing that his uncle was ill, Du went to visit him. Wang was lying naked and sleeping during the day. He woke up and angrily shouted, saying, "I thought you came to see your uncle; you came to collect the debt, didn't you! I have all the money; I'm afraid you won't leave until you get it." Du pleaded gently and sorrowfully, until tears came, but Wang's words and demeanor became even more severe. Du was in a very awkward situation and thought to himself that whether he advanced or retreated, it would lead to his death. It might be better to kill him first, he thought, and drew a small knife from under the pillow. He stabbed Wang fiercely, hitting him in the abdomen.

    368

    王起扑之，下床而踬，乃透腹中，血流而绝。其二子知之，杜已走出，自思罪无可逃，乃诣县自首。其子亦奔至。宰问：“汝不怕死乎？”杜云：“怕。”问：“既怕，胡以杀人？”曰：“窃闻杀人偿命，欠债还钱，有是例乎？”宰曰：“有。”曰：“小人所以怕死者，为偿命；所以不敢怕死者，为还钱也。大人果能追其二子还钱，微命所不敢惜。”宰以其言直，系之，迫令其二子急为变产偿债。二子托人关说缓追，而宰亦畏因果，不敢循私，置不理。乃具其词详提问，并无异词。

    Explanation

    368

    A man named Wang attacked him, and as he stepped off the bed, he stumbled and fell, causing his abdomen to be pierced, and he bled to death. His two sons, upon learning of this, fled. Resigned to the fact that there was no escape from his crime, he went to the county office to confess. His son also ran there. The magistrate asked, "Are you not afraid of death?" The man replied, "I am." The magistrate then asked, "If you are afraid, why did you kill him?" The man said, "I have heard that killing someone means paying with one's life, and owing money means repaying it, is this the case?" The magistrate replied, "Yes." The man continued, "I am afraid of dying because I must pay with my life; but I do not fear death because I must repay the debt. If you can truly pursue my two sons to repay the debt, I will not hesitate to give up my life." The magistrate, considering his words to be honest, detained him and forced his two sons to quickly sell their property to repay the debt. The sons asked someone to plead for a delay in the pursuit, but the magistrate was also afraid of the consequences of karma and dared not act out of favoritism, so he took no action. He then presented the man's words in detail and asked further questions, and there were no differing statements.

    369

    上亦严追王子偿债，而家资又不足以相抵，于是渐寝其事。至明春，黄宰入省贺新节，往谒督辕，方跪拜于地，遽呼冤。督问：“何冤？”乃以汪等被屈之事始末，备诉甚详，且云：“杀王武举乃神所使，非尽杜某之罪，望大人天恩活之。若再抵命，则冤冤相报，更无已时。”言毕叩首，乞录其供。宪呼吏至，录其词毕，令其自视。宰云：“小人目不识丁，词即录矣，即请究办。小人去矣。”宰霍然而醒，问其所言，则茫不知。令自视其词，则俱招认。乃按例宰拟抵，王家产中落，仅交其半。杜某减轻，后以恩赦得免，其居停复委以会计，不数载，称小康焉。

    Explanation

    369

    The emperor also strictly pursued the prince to repay the debt, but the family's assets were insufficient to cover it, and thus the matter gradually came to an end. By early spring, the county magistrate entered the province to celebrate the new festival and went to pay respects to the governor. As he knelt and bowed, he suddenly cried out for injustice. The governor asked, "What injustice?" The magistrate then detailed the entire story of how Wang and others were wronged, explaining in great detail, and said, "Killing Wang the scholar-official was done by divine will, not entirely the fault of Du, I hope your great mercy will spare him. If he must be punished, then the cycle of revenge will never end." After finishing, he knelt and begged to have his testimony recorded. The governor called in an official to record his words and then asked him to review it himself. The magistrate said, "I am illiterate, the words have been recorded, please proceed with the investigation. I will leave now." Suddenly, the magistrate woke up, and when asked about what he had said, he did not know. When asked to review his testimony, he confessed to everything. According to the law, the magistrate was sentenced to death, the Wang family's property was confiscated, but only half was taken. Du was given a reduced sentence and later pardoned by grace. He was entrusted with accounting duties again, and within a few years, he was considered moderately prosperous.

    370

    醉茶子曰：死者已矣，谁复起而相争哉？乃冤气所凭，卒使害己者自言之，岂杀人灭口，即匿迹消声哉！噫！可畏也夫。
    刘玉厅
    　　刘玉厅，闽人，就直督幕府，失馆来津。与邑侯张公善，欲谋栖枝。时宾馆填溢，张公厚赠遣之。未能即行，僦寓于邑城内弥勒庵，行李萧索，携一仆，供给颇窘，每向僧乞食。僧问：“贵上宁勿饭乎？”云：“主人犹未醒，待醒则过其时，饥饿难堪，故望和尚慈悲，稍分伊蒲之馔。”僧怪诘之，云：“主人在保阳时，每一睡辄三五日，或七八日始醒，醒后犹倦息一日余，故常勿饭。督辕以其旷功辞。

    Explanation

    370

    The Drunken Tea Master said: "The dead are gone; who would rise again to contend? It is the lingering spirit of injustice that ultimately causes the one who harmed others to speak for themselves. Is it not possible that they were killed to silence them, or that they concealed their tracks and disappeared? Ah! How terrifying indeed!"

    Dong Yuting

    Dong Yuting, a native of Fujian, served in the office of the Viceroy. After losing his position, he came to Tianjin. He was friendly with Magistrate Zhang of the county and sought a new place to stay. At the time, guesthouses were full, so Magistrate Zhang generously gave him gifts and sent him on his way. Unable to leave immediately, Dong rented a residence in the city within a temple called Miluo Monastery. His belongings were sparse, and he brought only one servant, who struggled to provide for him. Dong often asked the monks for food. When a monk asked, "Are you not eating, Your Excellency?" Dong replied, "My master is still asleep. If he wakes up, it will be too late, and I will be starving. Therefore, I hope the venerable monk will show compassion and share a little of your vegetarian fare." The monk was puzzled and asked why. Dong explained, "My master, while in Baoyang, would sleep for three to five days, or even seven or eight days at a time. After waking, he would still rest for another day or so, so he often does not eat. The Viceroy's office dismissed him for this reason."

    371

    ”僧恐其病累，欲下逐客令，仆力白其无妨。久而知其为冥曹，每隔数日一理阴政。阴事颇繁，大抵皆阳世之漏网，藉阴曹以平反之。僧屡诘其阴曹何官，所理何事，刘不答。因贿其仆，仆言：“彼有一秘箧，封锁甚固，必其要事。”俟其瞑卧，取以示僧。窃发之，中多按牍，断制明决正直。僧窃录数则：一起疑奸谋杀，应抵，官受赃减轻，致酿成贞妇自戕一案。山右某县马陈氏，自幼失父母，寄养其舅杨家。舅妗爱如己出，嫁与同乡马姓，厚其妆奁，舅复源源馈遗省视。
    二载余，氏生一子，夫携抱门外，遇其同窗友王某，问此谁氏子，马以己对。王戏曰：“觍不知耻，渠母久私其舅，人无不知，尔尚认为己物耶！”马疑信参半，王正其色以实之。马愕然退，归诘其妻。氏初不认，马痛拷之，氏怒曰：“诚如君言，且为奈何？”盖愤激之词也。马辗转无语，筹思近村有一僧，刚猛有力，素与己善，诣僧痛哭求助，言词恳切。僧慨然以荆、聂自任，大悦。

    Explanation

    371

    The monk feared that the illness would burden him and wanted to issue an order to expel the guest, but the servant strongly argued that it would not be a problem. After some time, he learned that the guest was an official from the underworld, who administered affairs there every few days. Matters in the underworld were quite numerous, mostly consisting of those who had escaped punishment in the mortal world, who were being made to atone for their crimes. The monk repeatedly asked what rank he held in the underworld and what affairs he handled, but Liu did not answer. Because he bribed his servant, the servant said, "He has a secret chest, tightly sealed, which must contain important matters." The monk waited until he was asleep, then took it to show him. He secretly opened it, and inside were many case files, with decisions that were clear, decisive, and just. The monk secretly recorded several cases:

    One case of suspected adultery and murder, which should have resulted in the death penalty, but the official accepted bribes to reduce the punishment, leading to a case of a chaste woman taking her own life. In a certain county in Shanxi, there was a woman named Chen from the Ma family, who lost her parents at a young age and was raised by her uncle Yang. Her uncle and aunt loved her as if she were their own child, and she was married to a man from the same village with a generous dowry, and her uncle continued to send gifts and visit her.

    After two years or so, she gave birth to a son. Her husband took the baby out to the front door and met his classmate friend Wang, who asked who the child's father was. Ma replied that it was his own child. Wang jokingly said, "Shamelessly unaware of shame, her mother had been having an affair with her uncle for a long time, everyone knew this, do you still think it's your own child?" Ma was half-believing and half-doubting, and Wang firmly stated that it was true. Ma was stunned and retreated, returning home to question his wife. She initially denied it, and Ma severely tortured her. She angrily said, "If what you say is true, what should we do then?" It was a word spoken in anger. Ma was at a loss for words, thought of a monk in a nearby village, who was strong and fierce and had always been friendly with him. He went to the monk, weeping bitterly for help, and his words were sincere and earnest. The monk readily took on the responsibility of Jing Ke and Nie Zheng, and Ma was greatly pleased.

    372

    次日候于途，氏舅杨翁乘车将往他村贺戚家喜，见二人要于路，其势凶暴，怪而下车问故。马曰：“汝之所为，尚不自知，哓哓何为？”僧拔刀立决其首。车夫战栗求免，僧亦欲杀。马云：“不干彼事，可释之。”车夫鼠窜而去，逃归其家，鸣于官。官廉得其情，按律应抵。马氏数世单传，其父母破产贿通。宰暗使人言于马氏，云：“若妇能以奸自认，便可设法减等。”马母劝陈氏，氏初不肯应，母言：“夫死妇寡，且又何益？”氏云：“妇人所争者节，节不失，寡何害？”母泣云：“妇之节，虚名也；夫之死，实祸也。

    Explanation

    372

    The next day, Chen's uncle, Old Man Yang, was waiting on the road when he saw two men blocking the path, their demeanor fierce and violent. Surprised, he got out of his carriage and asked what was happening. Ma said, "Do you not know what you are doing? Why are you making such a fuss?" The monk drew his sword and immediately decapitated them. The driver trembled with fear and begged for mercy, but the monk also wanted to kill him. Ma said, "It has nothing to do with him; let him go." The driver fled like a rat and returned home, reporting the incident to the authorities. The official investigated the matter and discovered the truth, ruling that the monk should be punished according to the law. The Ma family had been single-line descendants for several generations, and their parents went bankrupt trying to bribe the official. The magistrate secretly sent someone to speak to the Ma family, saying, "If the wife can admit to adultery, methods can be found to reduce the punishment." Ma's mother advised Chen Shi, but she initially refused to agree. Ma's mother said, "When the husband dies, the wife becomes a widow; what good does it do?" Chen Shi replied, "What women strive for is chastity; if chastity is not lost, being a widow causes no harm." Ma's mother cried and said, "A woman's chastity is a hollow name; the husband's death is a real disaster."

    373

    贪虚名而受实祸，其何以堪？不如认奸，则夫妇完聚，僧为舅抵，足无憾矣。”氏泣从其请。及至官府，妇云：“认奸，夫得免乎？”官漫应之。妇又云：“若果令夫不死即认，否则已耳。”官力白不抵。翁姑共怂恿之，妇遂认供。画毕，氏云：“合因救夫而屈认奸，节何以明也？”袖出剪刀，自刺其喉，顷刻气绝。宰不得已，仍使马与僧并抵焉。氏母家无人，得以蒙溷消案。宰幸免。判曰：“氏救夫情切，甘冒不洁之名，以致情屈自戕。而夫仍不免死，皆系劣宰贪脏枉法，诪张为幻。按冥律，宰宜受炮烙刑，来生为犬。”一起直隶某县李姓，妻不育，买妾生三子。妻卒，其长子年二十余，次子十九，三子十七。因产起意，三子合谋，诱其父于野杀之。其庶母姑息未报官，饰词父外出。乡人共忿之，而畏事不敢轻举。又数年，其次子娶邻村某家，岳殷富。诸李常讹索财，往往不遂意。三子共谋以母尸诈索。

    Explanation

    373

    To crave a false reputation and suffer real disaster, how can one bear it? It is better to admit guilt, then the couple will be reunited, and the monk will be held responsible as a relative, there will be no regrets. The woman wept and agreed to the suggestion. When they reached the official, the wife said: "If admitting guilt can save my husband, then let us do so; otherwise, it is over." The official vaguely agreed. The wife then said: "If indeed my husband can be saved by admitting guilt, then let us do so; otherwise, it is over." The official insisted that the man was not guilty. The husband and wife's parents encouraged her, and the wife then confessed. After the confession, the woman said: "How can she be considered chaste if she admits guilt to save her husband?" She drew out a pair of scissors and stabbed her throat, dying instantly. The official had no choice but to punish both the man and the monk. The woman's family had no influence, and the case was covered up with a false resolution. The official was fortunate to escape. The verdict stated: "The woman's desire to save her husband was strong, and she willingly accepted the false reputation to avoid disaster, leading to her own death. All this was due to the corrupt official's deceitful actions. According to the laws of the underworld, the official should be punished with the burning punishment and be reborn as a dog in the next life."

    In a certain county of Zhili, there was a family named Li. The wife was barren, and they had three sons by a concubine. Because of the inheritance, the three sons conspired and lured their father into the wilderness to kill him. The concubine was lenient and did not report the crime to the authorities, falsely claiming that the father had left the house. The villagers were angry but feared getting involved and did not dare to act rashly. After several years, the second son married a family in a neighboring village, whose father-in-law was wealthy. The Li family often demanded money, and their demands were often not met. The three sons then conspired to use the mother's body to extort money.

    374

    次子令母服毒，母不肯，长者威胁，且云：“汝年已老，何乐于生？如此一死，棺殓丰富，快何如之！”母觳觫乞免，不听，提耳强灌之。母神色溃乱，舁至次男岳家身故。岳家鸣于官，官以自行服毒身死讹索，重责三子，使领尸回自埋。缘事关重大，未敢究其嗾使之由。而岳家因是拖累，家亦落。三子藁葬其母，事亦遂寝。按冥律：三子行同枭獍，宜凌迟处死，来生五世为豕，受宰割刑。宰贪贿枉法，宜肢解。一起畿南许姓，拐卖良家童男女二十二名口，未经破案。

    Explanation

    374

    The second son ordered his mother to take poison, but she refused. The elder threatened her, saying, "You are already old, what joy is there in living? If you die this way, your coffin will be richly prepared, how much happier would that be!" The mother trembled and begged for mercy, but the elder did not listen. He pulled her ear and forcibly gave her the poison. The mother's expression was disordered, and they carried her to her son-in-law's house where she died. The son-in-law's family reported it to the authorities, who believed she had taken poison on her own and died. The officials severely reprimanded the three sons and ordered them to take the body back and bury it themselves. Because the matter was serious, they did not dare to investigate who incited it. And the son-in-law's family suffered as a result, their family also declined. The three sons buried their mother in a simple manner, and the matter was eventually forgotten. According to the laws of the underworld, the three sons behaved like vultures and wolves; they should be executed by slicing, and in their next five lives, they should be pigs and suffer the punishment of being slaughtered. The official who handled the case was corrupt and perverted the law; he should be dismembered. In a case in the southern part of the capital, a man named Xu kidnapped and sold twenty-two children from good families, and the case remains unsolved.

    375

    一起直隶刘张氏虐遇其夫前妻子，致逃窜身死。一起渤海王刘氏，为津门富室女仆，与男仆赵某通。赵，山右人，主知并逐，遂成夫妇。赁居津门南关外，数年生子一。氏夫王某，寻觅至津，氏设计谋杀。王无亲族，以故未经报官。凡此数案，尚无判语。余案颇繁，不遑备录。刘醒，已知其事，怒鞭仆。呼僧至，责其多事妄为，当有冥谴。僧惧，伏地衰宥。刘云：“泄我机秘，累我非浅。第既为尔所窥，讳之无益，烦尔告知世人，阳律幸免，阴司万不能漏，人宜修善，莫谓冥冥无知也。”越日，刘与仆俱行，不知所之。

    Explanation

    375

    In Zhili, a woman named Liu mistreated her husband's previous wife, leading to her fleeing and dying. In Bohai, a woman named Liu, who was a servant in a wealthy household in Tianjin, had an affair with a male servant named Zhao. Zhao, from Shanxi, was discovered and dismissed by the master, but they eventually became a couple. They rented a house outside the southern gate of Tianjin and had a son after several years. Liu's husband, Wang, found his way to Tianjin, and Liu plotted to kill him. Wang had no relatives, so the incident was not reported to the authorities. None of these cases have been judged yet. There are many other cases, too numerous to list. Upon waking, Liu realized what had happened and angrily whipped her servant. She called a monk and scolded him for meddling and acting recklessly, warning that he would face divine retribution. The monk, fearing retribution, fell to the ground and begged for forgiveness. Liu said, "You have revealed my secrets, causing me great trouble. Since you have already seen them, hiding them will not help. Please tell the world that while I may escape earthly punishment, I cannot avoid divine retribution. People should cultivate virtue and not think that the divine is unaware." The next day, Liu and her servant left together, and their whereabouts were unknown.

    376

    醉茶子曰：昭昭之报，即冥冥之刑，理固可信也。宋儒谓鬼神无非阴阳屈伸之理，驳释氏轮回之说。云人死为鬼，鬼又为人，如此生生不已，造物无权矣。然羊祜探环，非熊再世，显有明证，岂尽诬耶？则又云史传所载，如此等事极多，终不是正理，不可信也。不知理虽非正，事总非虚，岂即置而勿论乎？宋儒之迂拘，往往类此，真不足一噱。

    Explanation

    376

    Zuochazǐ said: Clear rewards are equivalent to hidden punishments, and this principle is certainly credible. The Song dynasty Confucian scholars argued that the existence of ghosts and gods is nothing more than the natural principles of yin and yang, and their expansion and contraction, and thus rejected the Buddhist doctrine of reincarnation. They claimed that when people die, they become ghosts, and these ghosts can be reborn as humans, leading to an endless cycle of life and death, which implies that the creator has no authority. However, there are clear evidences, such as the case of Yang Hu probing the ring and Jiang Shang being reborn, which suggest that these phenomena are not entirely false. Yet, it is also said that historical records contain many such stories, but they are ultimately not the correct principle and therefore not credible. It is unclear whether the principle is correct, but the events are not entirely false, so should they be dismissed without discussion? The narrow-mindedness of Song dynasty Confucian scholars often manifests in this way, and such criticism is not worthy of serious consideration.

    377

    公输二则
    　　鲁班之灵迹昭昭，前曾志其疗疾之事。兹又闻二事，亦甚奇。邑带河门外玉皇阔，古刹也，殿宇巍峨，闬闳壮丽。殿前牌坊，木雕花牙，参差综错，备板精巧。康熙年间，拆坊重修，花牙不能复合，工师懊悔无策。有贩鬃刷子者至阁前，谓工人曰：“若兴土木大工，胡不买此？”众咄之曰：“我等瓦木作，用此奚为？客休矣。”其人徘徊久之，摘一柄赠其小工人曰：“试留之，当有大用。”工受之，视柄上镌一字，不识，持问工师。师视之，乃“班”字，恍然悟其为公输也。急寻其人，已渺。遂洗刷花牙尘垢，既浣，其左右数目字俱见，乃得合焉。

    Explanation

    377

    The Miracles of Lu Ban, Part Two

    The miraculous deeds of Lu Ban are clear and evident, and they have been recorded in previous accounts of his healing feats. Now I have heard of two more events, which are also very extraordinary. The Jade Emperor Pavilion, an ancient temple, is located outside the city gate of the river. Its grand halls and magnificent gates are imposing and splendid. The archway in front of the hall is adorned with intricately carved wooden panels featuring floral patterns, intricately interwoven and exquisitely crafted. During the reign of Emperor Kangxi, the archway was demolished and rebuilt, but the carvings could not be reassembled, leaving the master craftsman regretful and at a loss. A vendor selling brushes made from animal hair approached the workers and said, "If you are undertaking such a large construction project, why not buy one of these?" The workers scoffed and replied, "We are engaged in brick and woodwork; what use would this be? Go away!" The vendor lingered for a while and took one brush, giving it to a small worker and saying, "Try keeping it; it will be useful." The worker accepted it and saw an inscription on the handle, which he did not recognize, so he showed it to the master craftsman. The craftsman looked at it and saw the character "ban," suddenly realizing it was related to Gongshu. He hurriedly searched for the person, but he had disappeared. The workers then washed the carvings to remove the dirt, and after washing, the numbers on both sides became visible, and they were able to put them together.

    378

    邑天后宫建自前明，乾隆年重修大殿告成，众视式不如旧，然均未能指其所以然也。适有颠者至寺前，云美馔甚适口，但短盐耳。言之不休。或嘲之曰：“是何处骗得饮食，犹嫌无滋味也？”其人狂笑而去。工师见其行止非常，忽悟云：“盐者檐也，得毋檐微短乎？”再接檐椽数尺，视之则鸟革翚飞，形势殊可观矣。遍觅其人，不可得，或曰：“是鲁班也。”
    武清乙
    　　武清某乙，勇敢好武，善习拳勇。与里中健男角力，均莫能敌，以故勇名大著。先是乡俗，凡有丧者之家，每择一静室，盛设酒肴，布夹于地，扃门禁人窥探，夜深往往闻室中枷锁叉镮声。次日启视，地上或有人足迹、鸟兽蹄印等形，即知亡者后身托生何物，谓之出殃。此俗闻吾邑昔年亦然，自嘉庆年间始不行焉。某乙偶吊诸戚家，适遇殃期，欲窥其异，阻之不可，乃入室匿帷中。三更许，闻窗外旋风骤起，俄入室，烛摇摇欲灭。

    Explanation

    378

    The Heavenly Mother Palace was built in the early Ming dynasty. During the Qianlong era, the grand hall was rebuilt and completed, but the crowd found the style not as good as the old one, and no one could point out why. Just then, a madman arrived at the temple gate and said, "The delicious food is very tasty, but it lacks salt." He kept talking about it. Someone mocked him and said, "How did he get food from somewhere and still find it tasteless?" The man laughed wildly and left. The master builder saw that his behavior was unusual and suddenly realized and said, "Salt means the eaves. Could it be that the eaves are slightly too short?" He then extended the eaves by several feet, and when he looked at it, the eaves were like birds' wings, and the overall appearance was very impressive. He searched for the man everywhere but could not find him. Someone said, "He is Lu Ban."

    Wu Qing Yi

    Wu Qing had a man named Yi, who was brave and fond of martial arts, skilled in boxing. He competed in strength with strong men in the village and no one could defeat him, so his reputation for bravery became well-known. Previously, there was a custom in the village that whenever a family had a death, they would choose a quiet room, set up a feast, and place trays on the ground, locking the door to prevent people from peeping. At night, they often heard the sounds of shackles and locks inside the room. The next day, when they opened the door to look, they would see footprints or animal tracks on the ground, indicating that the deceased had been reborn as an animal, which was called "out of calamity." This custom was said to have been practiced in our county in the past, but it stopped being practiced since the Jiaqing era. Yi once went to mourn at a relative's house and happened to encounter the time for the calamity ritual. He wanted to observe its strangeness but was stopped, but he could not be stopped, so he entered the room and hid behind the curtain. Around the third watch of the night, he heard a whirlwind suddenly start outside the window, and soon entered the room, causing the candle to flicker and almost go out.

    379

    风定，有一鬼物立堂中，手持利叉，身高如中人，巨面高准深目，顶有双歧如角，皮色黝黑如熊掌，袒臂着朱裙。亡者被枷锁，伛偻随其后，谦抑如延上宾。鬼登筵大嚼，饕餮有声。乙视其状，暗思其貌虽狞恶，而身仅类中人，应无大力，盍出其不意而擒之？乃跃出大喝。物惊起，乙遽捉其发，而脑后殊濯濯然如髡，急按其脖，物伏于地，乙老拳毒手大肆发挥，物嗥叫欲遁，声啾啾然。乙捉其叉猛刺之，如烟而没。回顾亡者亦渺，而叉犹在手，喜其锋利。及晓视之，则银箔所糊，长仅尺许耳。

    Explanation

    379

    The wind had ceased, and a ghostly figure stood in the middle of the hall, holding a sharp fork. It was tall as an average person, with a large face, high cheekbones, and deep-set eyes. On top of its head were two horns like antlers, and its skin was dark as a bear's paw. Its arms were bare, and it wore a red skirt. A deceased person, bound in chains and hunched over, followed behind, humbly as if welcoming a distinguished guest. The ghost sat down to eat heartily, making loud noises.

    Observing its appearance, the man thought to himself that although its appearance was fierce and ugly, its body was only as large as an average person, so it should not have great strength. Why not catch it by surprise? So he leaped out and shouted loudly. The ghost was startled and jumped up. He immediately grabbed its hair, but the back of its head was completely smooth as if it had been shaved. He quickly pressed its neck, and the ghost fell to the ground. He unleashed his fists and hands with great force, and the ghost howled and tried to escape, with a sound like a chirping bird. He grabbed its fork and stabbed it fiercely, and it disappeared like smoke. Looking back, the deceased was also gone, but the fork was still in his hand. He was pleased by its sharpness. When he looked at it in the morning, it turned out to be made of silver foil, only about a foot long.

    380

    张氏
    　　庚申夷警，邑有张氏携眷避地于邑西南村。村固僻静，僦居民房，院颇宽广。北屋三楹，张之家眷自居。其东房三楹，遣住奴婢。屋内惟土炕，覆以苇席，并无他物。居数日，每闻奇臭，地下时有麻蝇，未之异也。有小婢夜未寝，见堂中立一人，腥血遍体，骇呼同人，辄不复睹。众怪之，揭席验视，则砖土错杂，似新拆者。发其砖得一尸，血肉狼藉，肤革已腐，蛆蚋蠕蠕蠢动，始悟味之有由矣。急掩之，私白诸张。张惧，将移居，而主人坚持不可，揣其意，似恐漏泄者。张婉言厚赠，始得脱。后访闻此乡多盗，每劫行旅。尸或前日所杀者也。然张幸免矣。

    Explanation

    380

    In the year of the Battle of Ningyuan, a warning was issued, and a family surnamed Zhang, along with their family, fled to a quiet village in the southwest of their county. The village was secluded and peaceful, and they rented a house from a resident. The yard was quite spacious. In the north house, which had three rooms, the Zhang family lived. The east wing, also with three rooms, was assigned to the servants. Inside the house, there was only a traditional heating system, covered with a reed mat, and no other furnishings. After living there for several days, they often smelled a strange odor, and there were flies on the ground, but they did not find it unusual. One night, a young servant who had not yet gone to sleep saw a person standing in the hall, covered in bloody and foul-smelling blood. She shouted in fright, and the person immediately disappeared. The others found this strange and lifted the mat to inspect. They discovered a mixture of bricks and soil, as if recently dismantled. When they dug up the bricks, they found a corpse, bloody and disordered, with the skin and flesh rotted, and maggots and flies moving about. They finally understood the source of the strange odor. They quickly covered it up and privately informed the Zhang family. The Zhang family was afraid and wanted to move, but the landlord insisted that they not do so. Guessing their intention, they feared that the news might leak out. The Zhang family offered gentle words and generous gifts, and finally managed to escape. Later, they learned that the village had many bandits who often robbed travelers. The corpse was possibly killed the day before. Fortunately, the Zhang family escaped unharmed.

    381

    海惠寺
    　　邑海惠寺藏经阁上有大守宫，长几盈丈。夏夜见阁檐际有双灯，其目光也。阁终岁扃闭，檐下蓄群鸽，恒被守宫食啖，几无遗类。僧惧而祷之，每朔望供鸡子数十枚，辄被食去，而自此群鸽遂安。盖此物似有灵也。后置鸡蛋不食，疑其去也，遂不为备。一夕，僧梦一褐衣女子，颜色娇丽，谓僧曰：“妾寄居阁上久矣，向蒙赐食，心诚感之。近日不茹荤已年余矣，如再见惠，赐以面食，不必鸡蛋也。迟迟吾即去。此寺二十年后当有变革，公亦不久即归道山。”僧醒而异之，为备蒸饼数枚，果食去。明年，老僧圆寂，物亦不见。僧徒登阁，见香案上一物长数尺，状如山甲，疑守宫脱化之皮也。后寺废，乡人立为稽古书院。壬辰四月，寺被灾，阁遂荡然。

    Explanation

    381

    Haihui Temple

    There was a large gecko on the treasure of scriptures hall of Haihui Temple, measuring nearly several feet in length. During summer nights, people saw two lights at the eaves of the hall, which were the gecko's glowing eyes. The hall was closed all year long, and under the eaves, a group of pigeons lived. They were always being eaten by the gecko, almost leaving no survivors. Fearing this, the monks prayed to the gecko, supplying dozens of eggs every new moon and full moon, which were immediately eaten. From then on, the pigeons became safe. This creature seemed to have some spirit or power. When eggs were placed but not eaten, it was suspected that the gecko had left, so they stopped preparing. One night, the monk dreamed of a brown-clothed woman with a beautiful appearance, who said to the monk, "I have been staying on the hall for a long time. I was previously given food, and I am truly grateful. Recently, I have not eaten meat for more than a year. If you see me again, give me some food instead of eggs. I will leave soon. This temple will undergo changes in twenty years. You will also soon return to the mountain of the Dao." The monk woke up and found it strange, and prepared several steamed buns, which it indeed ate. The next year, the old monk passed away, and the creature also disappeared. The monks climbed the hall and saw something several feet long on the incense table, shaped like a pangolin. They suspected it was the shed skin of the gecko. Later, the temple was abandoned, and the locals established it as a Jigu Academy. In the fourth month of the Renchen year, the temple was destroyed by fire, and the hall was completely ruined.

    382

    献王墓
    　　献县农人，偶过献王墓，见地裂一罅，视之深邃，探身入，漆漆然暗不辨物。数武外，一灯焰微如豆，近前剔之，则光辉焕发，皓如白昼，照见宫阙重重，殉葬物率皆金玉。心利之，爱不胜收。忽闻冢外雷声甚厉，恐水骤至，急寻途奔，为物绊踬。俯拾之，乃银一饼，纳诸怀，匍匐而出。视其银铸汉篆一行，云“赏挑灯银五十两。”再诣其处，隙已复合。
    　　醉茶子曰：献王博学重儒，一时通经之士多游其门，洵汉代贤王也。其灵爽不没，宜矣。至千载前知，更叹古人之数学非后人所及。
    金氏祠
    　　邑金氏修葺家祠，移堂中神龛，其台下坐一黄鼠，合掌迭趺如人状，尸已僵。又守祠者闻空屋中人语，私瞰之，见二女子对立，高仅二尺许，蝉鬓蓬松，貌殊不恶，自肩以下则黄鼠也，闻嗽声辄不见。又有白狐，恒出拜月，吐丹如火球，上下旋转，习见不为怪也。

    Explanation

    382

    献王墓
    A Farmer from Xian County, while passing by the tomb of the King of Xian, noticed a crack in the ground. Peering into it, he saw a deep and dark void. He cautiously entered, finding it pitch-black and unable to see anything. After several steps, he saw a faint light, like a small flame of a soybean. As he approached, the light suddenly brightened, illuminating the area as if it were daylight. He could see the palace halls in layers and the burial objects, mostly made of gold and jade. He was tempted by the treasure and couldn’t resist taking it. Suddenly, he heard a loud thunder outside the tomb, fearing that water might rush in, he hurriedly tried to find a way out, but was tripped by something. He picked it up and found it to be a silver ingot, which he put in his pocket and crawled out. Upon examining the ingot, he saw a line of Han dynasty seal script cast on it, which read, “Reward for picking up the lamp silver fifty taels.” When he returned to the place, the crack had already closed again.

    Zui Chazǐ said: The King of Xian was well-versed and valued Confucianism. At one time, many scholars who understood the classics frequented his court, making him indeed a virtuous king of the Han dynasty. His spirit is not extinguished, which is fitting. Knowing something thousands of years in advance, one can only lament that the mathematics of ancient people surpasses that of later generations.

    金氏祠
    The Jin family from the county repaired their ancestral hall and moved the altar in the hall. Beneath the platform, they found a yellow mouse sitting with its hands together and legs crossed, resembling a human. The body had already become stiff. Another person guarding the hall heard human voices in an empty room. He secretly looked and saw two women standing facing each other, each about two feet tall, with loose hair and not unattractive appearances. From the shoulders down, they were yellow mice. They would disappear whenever they heard a cough. There was also a white fox that always came out to worship the moon, spitting out something red like a fireball, moving up and down in rotation. It was common and not considered strange.

    383

    地震
    　　光绪戊子五月初四未刻，忽闻空际如金鼓鸣，旋觉床榻倾侧，已而杯盂倾覆，几案拍簸，屋梁轧轧折声。急出门，则街上老幼男妇四散逃窜，神色张皇，或云墙欲倒，或呼屋欲颓，竭蹶颠跛，刻许始定。有自野外来者，见有烟云从地起，其声随之，河水翻腾喷沸，渡船几覆，亦一奇也。初八日，邑城中西南隅旧有水坑，积潦污黑，忽于是日午，坑中旋流如搅，污浊尽入地中，倏变为黄水如河，或谓地震所致。有老人曰：“果尔幸甚。闻山东某县，忽井水变为混如河水，识者曰：『此黄河水从地中行耳，不久河当徙。』后果如其言。”城中坑水忽变，未知何故，然两三日复依旧黑也。

    Explanation

    383

    Earthquake
    On the fifth day of the fourth month of the Guangxu era's year of the Earth Horse (戊子), at the time of the late afternoon (未刻), a sudden noise like the sound of gongs and drums was heard in the air. Soon after, the beds and tables felt as if they were tilting, followed by cups and bowls falling over, tables and desks shaking, and the beams of the houses creaking as if they were breaking. People rushed out of their homes, and on the streets, men, women, and children of all ages scattered in panic, their faces filled with fear. Some shouted that the walls were about to collapse, while others cried that the houses were about to crumble. People stumbled and limped, and it took a short while for the chaos to settle. Those who came from the countryside saw smoke and clouds rising from the ground, accompanied by strange sounds, and the river was boiling and splashing, nearly capsizing the boats crossing it—truly a strange occurrence. On the eighth day, in the southwest corner of the county town, there was an old pit filled with dirty, black water. Suddenly, at noon on that day, the water in the pit began to swirl as if stirred, and the muddy water drained into the ground. Then, without warning, the water turned yellow and resembled a river. Some said this was caused by the earthquake. An elderly man remarked, "If that is indeed the case, it is fortunate. I have heard that in a certain county in Shandong, the well water suddenly turned muddy and river-like. An expert said, 'This is the Yellow River water flowing underground, and soon the river will change its course.' And indeed, that is what happened." The water in the city's pits suddenly changed, but the reason for it is unknown. However, after a few days, it returned to its original black state.

    384

    木怪
    　　邑东南乡民家榆树忽结果，如鸭蛋，破之有清黄。又其村民家败帚忽一夜生花，蓓蕾色如丁香。均妖异也。
    竹生花己丑夏，竹忽生花，状如小芦穗。邑中城关内外，有竹皆然，是亦异矣。昔闻凤食竹实。夫竹既能实，当必有花，特不见常开，为可奇耳。
    人面豆
    　　上谷某家，蓄黄豆数囤，忽变为人面形，老少男女不一，其状率皆愁惨，颔下有朱痕如血。又邑城外米铺亦有之。

    Explanation

    384

    Final Translation

    Wooden Spirits
    In the southeastern village of the county, a Chinese elm tree suddenly bore fruit, resembling duck eggs. When broken open, it revealed a clear yellow substance. Additionally, a broom from a villager’s home, which had been discarded, suddenly sprouted flowers one night, with buds colored like lilac. Both were considered strange and supernatural phenomena.

    Bamboo Blossoms
    In the summer of the year of the Goat, bamboo suddenly bloomed, with flowers resembling small reed spikes. Bamboo in both inside and outside the city gates of the county exhibited this phenomenon. This was also unusual. I have heard that phoenixes feed on bamboo seeds. Since bamboo can produce seeds, it must also be capable of blooming, though it is rarely seen, making it a remarkable occurrence.

    Bean Faces
    In a certain family in the region of Shanggu, several bins of yellow beans suddenly transformed into shapes resembling human faces, varying in age and gender. Their expressions were mostly sad and sorrowful, with red marks under their chins that looked like blood. This phenomenon was also observed in a rice shop outside the city walls of the county.

    385

    鼬鼠
    　　邑北门外归贾胡同饭馆，忽有黄鼠千百成群，自馆中出。时方卓午，悍不惧人，街衢几满，向西北去。大者口衔其子，狼狈而行。或云鼬鼠移居其宅，主于不祥。
    蛇异
    　　邑人某，乘舟将之豫省，泊舟野岸。岸上有古柳阴广亩余，见树上倒挂一蛇，粗殆如盎，首垂垂然向下欲堕，似有牵引之者。俯视地上，一小黑蛇长裁数寸，其细如箸，昂首向上，吸之以气。大蛇便委顿欲死，状甚困惫。舟子以小能制大，意颇不平，欲击以篙。邑人急止之曰：“彼形虽微，毒必更烈，击之不死，必受其殃。脱令大蛇得活，我辈其何以当？不如观之。”未几，小蛇愈吸，则大蛇愈下，首将及地，小蛇一跃入其口。大蛇屈伸掀动，倏坠于地，掉其尾，响如鸣鞭，草木为之分折，蹶然身挺如梁而毙。旋见其腹划然中裂，小蛇自腹出，越垄而去，其行如飞，瞥不复见。

    Explanation

    385

    A Strange Encounter with Weasels and Snakes

    At the inn located outside the northern gate of the town, in the lane called Guijia, suddenly appeared a group of thousands of yellow rats. They emerged from the inn just as noon approached. Fierce and fearless of people, they nearly filled the streets, heading northwest. The larger ones carried their young in their mouths, moving in a hurried and disorganized manner. Some said that the weasels had moved into their home, signifying misfortune.

    A Strange Snake

    A resident of the town was traveling by boat to Henan Province and docked at a wild shore. There was an ancient willow tree on the shore, casting shade over several acres. He saw a snake hanging upside down from a tree, as thick as a large jar, with its head drooping down as if it were about to fall, as if being pulled by something. Looking down at the ground, he noticed a small black snake, barely a few inches long, as thin as a chopstick, raising its head upward and breathing on it. The large snake became weak and seemed on the verge of death, looking very exhausted. The boatman, thinking the small snake could control the large one, felt quite upset and wanted to strike it with a pole. The townsperson quickly stopped him and said, "Although it looks small, its venom must be even more potent. If we don't kill it, we will surely suffer its consequences. If the large snake survives, how will we deal with it? It's better to observe it." Not long after, the smaller snake continued to breathe on the larger one, and the larger snake continued to lower itself. Its head was about to touch the ground, and the smaller snake leaped into its mouth. The large snake twisted and thrashed, suddenly falling to the ground. It wagged its tail, making a sound like a whip cracking, causing the grass and trees to split apart. It suddenly straightened up like a beam and died. Soon, its belly split open, and the smaller snake emerged from the belly and leaped over the ridge, moving like a flash and disappearing.

    386

    银异
    　　古人云：“大富由命，小富由勤。”至言也。夫人坐拥厚资，或因祖遗，或因自创，锦衣肉食，财雄一乡，是殆有命焉。若夫积铢累寸，薄有余赢，稍不勤俭，贫窘立至，事在人为。又不可尽委之于命也。至于无端而获窖藏，无端而得奇遇，不假经营，陡然富贵，谓非天授可乎？邑有于氏，赁鼓楼西第，每夜辄见鸡雏数十头盘桓床下，捉之瞥不复见。或告之曰：“此银之精也。细穷其出没之处，刺以银针，则巨金可获。”如其法，掘地数尺，得青铜钱十八枚，余无所获，大失所望。

    Explanation

    386

    A Strange Occurrence Involving Silver

    The ancients said, "Great wealth comes from fate, while lesser wealth comes from diligence." This is a profound statement. Those who sit in possession of substantial wealth, whether through inheritance or self-creation, live lives of luxury and comfort, and are powerful in their local communities—such wealth is likely due to fate. On the other hand, those who accumulate wealth through small, gradual efforts, having just a slight surplus, can quickly fall into poverty if they do not remain diligent and frugal. Human effort and action are crucial; one cannot entirely attribute their circumstances to fate. As for those who suddenly acquire hidden treasures or encounter extraordinary good fortune without effort, becoming rich and noble overnight, can such wealth not be considered divinely ordained? There was a family named Yu in a town who rented a residence west of the Drum Tower. Every night, they would see dozens of chicks circling their bed, but when they tried to catch them, the chicks would vanish instantly. Someone advised them, "These are spirits of silver. Carefully trace their movements, and pierce the area with a silver needle to find great wealth." Following this advice, they dug several feet into the ground and found eighteen ancient bronze coins, but nothing more, greatly disappointing them.

    387

    后时露光怪，于氏惧而他徙，遂无税居者。有某宦，浙人，在津候补，清苦异常。租其第，常不给值，主人令下逐客，而宦亦安之。一夕，偶因雨湿，墙塌半颓，拆其炕，见垒垒然白镪无算。于是偿其房值，加捐职衔，不半载，鹏程高举矣。又邑陈氏妇，每晚庭中纳凉，有火球的的然墙隅流走，不可奇也。渐绕其裙下，捉之，得银锞一锭。数夕皆然，计得十余两，后不复见。此二事，虽多寡不同，莫非命也。不然，何以于氏竭力求之而不能得，某宦无意而得之？陈氏不求而自至，他人千方百计而不能得之？银本无知，谁使之然耶？

    Explanation

    387

    Afterward, strange luminous phenomena appeared, and the Yu family, frightened, moved to another place, leaving no one to pay taxes for the property. There was a certain official, a native of Zhejiang, who was on standby in a place called Jin. He lived in extreme poverty. He rented the house but often failed to pay the rent. The owner ordered him to leave, and the official accepted this situation. One night, due to rain, a wall collapsed, and when he tore down the bed, he found a large amount of silver. He then paid the rent, donated a position, and achieved rapid promotion within half a year. Another woman from the Chen family, who often cooled off in the courtyard, saw a glowing ball moving in the corner of the wall. She caught it and found a piece of silver. This happened several nights in a row, and she collected more than ten taels, but then no longer saw it. These two stories, though differing in the amount of silver found, are both examples of fate. Otherwise, why would the Yu family have sought it in vain, while the official found it by chance? Why would the Chen woman receive it without seeking it, while others could not find it despite their efforts? Silver itself is unaware, so who is responsible for this?

    388

    慧海发逆之变，沧州赴义死者甚多，碧血渗地，白骨撑天。和尚慧海发大慈悲，与其徒负土掩胔，夜持火枪驱逐狼犬。以故州城外垒垒新冢，连亘数里，皆和尚之力也。遂因尸气蒸染成疫，以致圆寂。有某甲，结庐城外，贩卖柴草，每夜有叩门求火者，某从窗棂中递与之。日久颇厌，诘其求火何用，答以有要物急需寻觅，否则不敢屡渎。某问是何物，而必待夜求？其人不答，但浼其速赐火炬。某苦诘之，其人曰：“本不愿相告，君既穷究，不敢讳也。殆寻吾头耳。”某大惊，隔窗窥之，一无首人立窗外，大骇，昏晕。及晓，为人救苏，未几寻毙。

    Explanation

    388

    During the upheaval initiated by Huihai, many people in Cangzhou died for justice, their blood seeping into the ground and their bones reaching the sky. The monk Huihai, showing great compassion, and with his disciples, carried soil to cover the dead bodies and at night held guns to drive away wolves and dogs. Thus, outside the city walls, there were numerous new graves stretching for several miles, all due to the efforts of the monk. Eventually, the stench from the corpses spread and caused an epidemic, leading to the monk's peaceful passing. There was a person named A, who built a hut outside the city and sold firewood and straw. Every night, someone would knock on the door asking for a light, and A would pass it to them through the window lattice. After a long time, A became quite tired and questioned why they needed a light. The person replied that they had something important to find urgently and would not dare to disturb again if not necessary. A asked what the item was and why it had to be sought at night. The person did not answer but requested a torch quickly. A persisted in questioning, and the person said, "I originally did not want to tell you, but since you are so persistent, I dare not hide it. I am looking for my own head." A was greatly surprised, looked through the window, and saw a headless person standing outside. A was greatly frightened and fainted. By morning, someone saved and revived A, but soon after, A died.

    389

    厉鬼
    　　邑涌泉寺前烟馆，有吞烟毕命者。其铺因之收市，屋遂空闲，时露光怪，未之奇也。有僦其屋，复作芙蓉馆者，依然灯烛辉煌，烟云缭绕矣。一夜，有更夫过其处，见一鬼高三尺许，头巨如瓮，向其屋起拜作叩首状。更夫叱之，鬼遽反其面，目炯双灯，齿森长锯，嚅嚅作声，遽前相扑。更夫惊倒，鬼亦遂去。更夫狼狈而遁。
    返魂
    　　返魂之说，古或有之。招魂见于《离骚》。杜诗云：“招魂不曾来故乡。”又云：“环佩空归月夜魂。”是返魂之事，非尽诬也。邑有张妪者，患病暴亡，停尸床上，灵帷张而棺木备，但未殓耳。儿女辈衰绖环泣，妪忽呻吟，举家惊骇。妪渐能言，云：“适行数里，见漠漠黄沙，一望无际，不知路之东西，时之昏晓也。遥见一灯如豆，急趋就之，则有一人偃卧于床，灯设其侧。骇异间，身骤与之合，霍然遂醒，始悟为己复苏也。”于是儿女尽释凶服，妪从此亦愈。

    Explanation

    389

    Translation

    Lingui
    A ghost house stood before the Yongquan Temple in the town, where a man died from smoking opium. The shop closed down as a result, and the house became vacant. Strange phenomena occasionally appeared, but nothing unusual had happened until then. Someone rented the house and reopened it as the Furong Pavilion, where the lights still shone brightly, and the opium smoke still lingered. One night, a watchman passed by the place and saw a ghost about three feet tall, with a head as large as a jar, bowing and kneeling before the house. The watchman shouted at the ghost, and the ghost suddenly turned its face, with eyes glowing like lamps and teeth like a long saw, muttering sounds and rushing forward to wrestle. The watchman was startled and fell, and the ghost also left. The watchman fled in a disordered and frightened manner.

    Return of the Soul
    The concept of returning to life, though ancient, may have some basis in truth. The act of calling back a soul is mentioned in Li Sao. Du Fu wrote, "The soul of the deceased never returned to its homeland," and again, "The jade pendants returned empty at night." Thus, the idea of returning to life is not entirely false. In the town, there was an old woman named Zhang who fell gravely ill and died suddenly. Her body was placed on the bed, with the mourning tent set up and the coffin prepared, but she had not yet been buried. Her children and grandchildren were weeping in mourning attire, when the old woman suddenly groaned, shocking the entire family. She gradually began to speak, saying, "I just walked a few miles and saw vast stretches of yellow sand, with no way to tell east from west, or day from night. I saw a small lamp in the distance and hurried towards it. There, I saw a person lying on the bed, with a lamp beside him. In my astonishment, my body suddenly merged with his, and I awoke suddenly, realizing that I had been revived." Thus, her children and grandchildren all removed their mourning clothes, and the old woman recovered from that point onward.

    390

    白夫人
    　　乡愚陋习，呼蛇为柳，猬为白，相沿已久。南门外东塔寺，仙祠也，中塑—老妪像，目为白老太太。一旦凭巫而言曰：“尔辈以我为何如人？”众以白仙对。巫曰：“非也。彼食粪之猬，有何法力？我许状元之母，诰命太夫人也。自受法海禅师之戒，居塔中修炼多年，罚限已满，复见天日。发大愿力，拔苦众生。尔辈休轻视之。”众唯唯，肃然起敬，焚香参拜，颜其寺曰“东塔”。此命名所由来也。愚人遂藉藉传其灵异，以为驾乎猬仙之上，倍敬谨焉。不知猬畜类也，蛇亦畜类也。白蛇之白也，又何异于白猬之白也。

    Explanation

    390

    White Lady

    Village customs and habits of the ignorant have long persisted, calling snakes "willows" and hedgehogs "white." Outside the south gate, there is the East Pagoda Temple, a shrine dedicated to a deity. Inside, there is a statue of an old woman, referred to as White Grandma. One day, through a medium, she spoke and said, "What do you think of me?" The crowd responded, "We think you are White Immortal." The medium replied, "No, that is not correct. That hedgehog that eats feces, what kind of magical power does it have? I am the mother of a top scholar, a noble lady with imperial edict. Since I received the precepts from Master Fahai, I resided in the pagoda for many years, practicing cultivation. The punishment period is over, and I have returned to see the light of day. I make a great vow and exert great effort to relieve the suffering of all beings. You must not look down on her." The crowd nodded in agreement, showing deep respect, and burned incense to pay homage. They renamed the temple "East Pagoda." This is how the name came to be. The foolish people then spread rumors about her miraculous powers, believing her to be superior to the hedgehog spirit, and showed even greater reverence and caution. Not knowing that hedgehogs and snakes are both animals, the whiteness of the white snake is no different from the whiteness of the white hedgehog.

    391

    而祈福邀思者，日相接踵，一倡百和，彼白而我亦白之，亦何不思之甚耶！有阎姓子患病，其母入祠祷之，夜梦一妇人，年三十许，容貌白皙，缟素衣裳，为子按摩者再。次日，子霍然而愈，遂以为白夫人之灵也，诣寺顶礼焉。夫天下事固不必实有其人，人神之则神之，昔贤曾论之矣。愚谓有事虽鄙俚而不可废者，如关外之孟姜女庙是也。考古史传并不载其人，然事关节烈，即不可目为乌有子虚。何也？若谓无其人，则无其事；无其事，并节烈而亦妄矣，鸟可焉？故虽无稽，君子谓实有其事，以其足为人劝也。若白蛇传事本荒唐，亦无关轻重，而必指其人以实之，惑矣！

    Explanation

    391

    Those who seek blessings and invoke thoughts do so day after day, one following the other, and when one person leads, a hundred follow. If someone claims something, others will repeat it without questioning—how could this not be a sign of lack of thought? There was a boy surnamed Yan who fell ill, and his mother entered a temple to pray for him. That night, she dreamed of a woman, around thirty years old, with a fair complexion, dressed in plain white clothing, who massaged the boy twice. The next day, the boy suddenly recovered, and thus she believed it was the spirit of Lady Bai. She went to the temple to perform obeisance. Indeed, not all things in the world need to have a real basis in fact; if people believe in it, it becomes a deity. The wise men of the past have discussed this. I believe that there are matters that are crude but cannot be discarded, such as the temple of Meng Jiangnu in the border regions. Although ancient historical records do not mention this person, the story is about loyalty and integrity, and thus it cannot be dismissed as nonexistent or fabricated. Why is this so? If there is no such person, then there is no such event; without the event, the loyalty and integrity would also be false—how could it be possible? Therefore, even if it lacks basis, gentlemen believe that the event is real, because it serves as a good example for people. As for the White Snake legend, which is considered absurd, it is also of little importance, but insisting on pointing to a real person to validate it is perplexing.

    392

    二童
    　　邑二童子，往城西郊外同捕促织。时已薄暮，捉得数十头，并置诸苇篓中。仍披荆拔茅，以搜求之。忽来一人，面目黧黑，枯瘦露骨，遽向前倒翻其篓，尽放其所捕之虫。童忿与争，其人不顾而去。二童并追之，入丛冢中，蹲伏草际。二童击以挖土之铲，其人起，欲遁。一童急捉之，瞥不复见，视手中，持乱发一团而已。
    鬼恋妇
    　　邑朱某，寓御河北岸。夜深，自隘巷归，见一家后窗临街，有男子伏窗窥视。朱责曰：“私窥人室，此是何意？”其人伏不动，朱怒曳之，其人遽反其面，色如枯木，乱发蓬飞，目眈眈有怒容，忿曰：“与尔何干？”以手拧其背，觉冷如冰而痛入骨，倏不复见。朱惊惶而遁。次日访之，知屋内某新娶再醮妇，是夜方合卺。识者谓：鬼乃妇之前夫。

    Explanation

    392

    Two Boys
    Two youths from the town went to the western outskirts of the city together to catch crickets. By the time evening was drawing near, they had caught several dozen and placed them all in a reed basket. They continued to tear through brambles and pull up reeds in their search. Suddenly, a man appeared, with a dark, sallow complexion and an emaciated, bony frame. He quickly moved forward and overturned the basket, releasing all the crickets they had caught. The boys were angry and argued with him, but the man ignored them and left. The two boys followed him into a dense burial ground, where they crouched among the grass. They hit him with a shovel used for digging, and the man got up, intending to escape. One of the boys quickly grabbed him, but he disappeared in an instant. Looking in his hand, there was only a clump of tangled hair.

    Ghostly Love
    In the town, a man named Zhu lived on the northern bank of the Yuer River. One night, as he was returning home through a narrow alley, he saw a man crouching by a window that overlooked the street, peering inside. Zhu scolded him, saying, “What is the meaning of secretly peering into someone’s home?” The man remained motionless. Angered, Zhu grabbed him, and the man suddenly turned his face, his complexion ashen like dead wood, with wild hair flying about. His eyes glared with anger, and he shouted, “What business do you have with me?” Zhu grabbed his back, feeling it as cold as ice and painful to the bone. In an instant, the man disappeared. Zhu fled in a state of shock. The next day, he inquired about it and learned that the house contained a woman who had recently remarried. That night, she had just consummated her marriage. Those who understood the situation said that the ghost was her former husband.

    393

    醉茶子曰：人恋其妇，死且不忘。一旦别抱琵琶，其饮恨九泉可知矣。力竭计穷，至于伏窗瞻望，其情殊可悯也。顾天下之大，娶再醮妇者如恒河沙数，而鬼之徘徊瞻者亦如恒河沙数。人欲续鸾胶，又何必效绥绥之雄狐哉！
    尸哭
    　　邑张氏妇暴卒，停尸于床，忽自揭衾起坐，悲啼。家人以其苏，集前慰问。妇闭目无语，但有悲泣，至夜仍僵，天明复坐起悲啼。如是数日，家人咸畏恶之，乘其僵时而殓焉。夜闻柩中声如牛鸣，急瘗之。或以为邪祟所凭，或以为气闭，究不知其何故。
    沧州张
    　　沧州张某，贩杂货于山海关，与二三同人共处一室。甫灭烛，众尚未寝，忽门隙一响，有妇人赤身入，直至榻前，揭衾欲卧。张大呼有人，急击以拳，中床有声。众曰：“君梦呓耶！门已闭，人从何来？”张力握其臂，呼众秉烛。妇怒，搔其面，化作金光，破窗飞去。众秉烛至，见张面血流殷湿，爪印宛然，窗破一巨孔，相与骇异久之。

    Explanation

    393

    Tea-Drinking Scholar said: A man loves his wife even after death. Once he remarries, it is clear that he harbors resentment in the netherworld. Exhausted in effort and out of ideas, he crouches by the window to look out, his situation is especially pitiful. Yet, in this vast world, there are countless men who remarry, and countless spirits who linger in the afterlife. If a man wishes to reunite with his lost love, why should he imitate the persistent fox who relentlessly pursues a new relationship?

    Corpse Crying

    A woman from the county suddenly died, her body remained on the bed. Suddenly, she lifted the quilt and sat up, crying bitterly. The family thought she had revived, gathered around to console her. She closed her eyes and remained silent, only sobbing, and by nightfall, she became stiff again. At dawn, she sat up and cried again. This cycle repeated for several days, and the family grew fearful and loathed her. They wrapped her in shrouds while she was stiff. At night, they heard a sound like a cow mooing from the coffin, and they hastily buried her. Some believed she was possessed by an evil spirit, others thought it was due to a blockage of breath, but no one knew the true cause.

    Zhang from Cangzhou

    Zhang from Cangzhou, a merchant trading in miscellaneous goods at Shanhaiguan, shared a room with a few companions. Just as the candles were extinguished, the others had not yet gone to sleep, when a sudden sound came from the crack of the door. A naked woman entered, went straight to the bed, and lifted the quilt to lie down. Zhang cried out that there was someone, and struck with his fist, hitting the bed with a sound. The others said, "Are you talking in your sleep? The door is closed, how could anyone have come in?" Zhang grabbed her arm and called for the others to bring candles. The woman became angry, scratched his face, transformed into golden light, and broke through the window, flying away. The others brought candles and saw that Zhang's face was bloody, with clear claw marks, and a large hole in the window. They were all shocked and amazed for a long time.

    394

    控鬼
    　　介休诸生某，有胆略，善刀笔，乡里稍拂其意，辄健讼不休，人畏之如虎。夜自邻村归，见垄畔卧一大鬼，身长盈亩，曲肱作枕，齁齁酣睡。烛之，青面赤须，貌极狞恶，叱之不应，怒曰：“作怪惊人，毫无忌惮，定有讼尔。”抽笔作词，焚于城隍神前。略曰：“人鬼殊途，阴阳有界，鬼者自宜退藏于密。今庞然凶煞，丑恶异常，当道横陈，见者必惧。倘有单传之子，奉公之流，被其惊毙，则绝人嗣续，殃及善良，贻患甚巨。生问其故，彼默不言，亦殊坦傲。神者聪明正直，亟宜拘究惩责。”次日复经其处，见大鬼长跪，哀曰：“予夜察神也，偶贪杯酒，狼狈醉眠，冒犯文旌，自知罪该万死。昨被君控城隍，将达天庭，罚必不免。望先生恩施，格外作词开释，感德无穷矣。”生笑许之，乃作词焚之，后不复见。

    Explanation

    394

    Controlling the Ghost

    A scholar from Jie County, known for his courage and skill in writing legal documents, was feared by the villagers for his persistent litigation and domineering behavior. One night, returning from a neighboring village, he saw a large ghost lying on the side of a field, its body stretching across an acre, with its elbow bent as a pillow, snoring deeply. Upon closer inspection, the ghost had a green face and red beard, appearing extremely fierce and ugly. When he shouted at it, it did not respond, and he became angry, saying, "Creating chaos and startling people without any fear, there must be a case against you." He then wrote a complaint and burned it at the altar of the city god. The complaint read: "Humans and ghosts have different paths, and the realms of yin and yang have boundaries. Ghosts should naturally retreat and hide. Now, this huge and terrifying ghost, with its grotesque appearance, is lying in the middle of the road, causing fear to all who see it. If it were to frighten to death an only child or a public servant, it would cut off the continuation of the family line, harm the innocent, and cause great suffering. I asked it why it was doing this, but it remained silent, showing a remarkable lack of humility. The gods are wise and upright, and it is urgent to investigate and punish it." The next day, passing by the same place, he saw the large ghost kneeling for a long time, pleading, "I was observing the gods last night, and I happened to indulge in alcohol, resulting in a drunken sleep. I have offended your banner and know that I deserve to die a thousand deaths. Yesterday, you accused me to the city god, and I am about to be judged by the heavenly court, where punishment is inevitable. I hope you will show mercy and write a special plea for forgiveness, for which I will be infinitely grateful." The scholar laughed and agreed, then wrote a plea and burned it. Afterward, the ghost was never seen again.

    395

    孽报
    　　疡医朱某，每晨施药。有男子年四十余，胸生一疮，其孔如杯，直达于背，脓溃腐臭，前后洞然如穿。医云：“症太险，非巨珠数钱和药不能疗。”某云：“果能愈疾，虽百颗不敢吝。”次日，携如豆大者数十枚，任医拣用。朱欢然受之，为之敷药而去。旁一人亦以微疮就医者，谓朱曰：“若疾可愈乎？”朱漫应之。其人曰：“是人必死，劝先生勿药。”朱惊曰：“君亦识症乎？”答曰：“若能识症，敢来妄渎先生？予以其人卜之，知其不可为也。”朱问其故，其人曰：“昔予与若从军楚地，官粮不足，率事劫掠，予亦不免。但予仅攫人财，不敢伤人命。一日，有老叟携二童逃难，男孩十余龄，女孩八九龄，皆其幼孙。

    Explanation

    395

    Retribution for Evil Deeds

    A physician specializing in skin diseases, Zhu, treated patients every morning. A man in his forties came to him with a sore on his chest, the size of a cup, extending all the way to his back. The pus oozed out, and the sore was rotten and foul-smelling, visible from front to back as if it had been drilled through. Zhu said, "The condition is very dangerous, and it cannot be treated without mixing large pearls worth several coins with the medicine." The man replied, "If it can cure the disease, I will not hesitate to give you a hundred pearls." The next day, Zhu brought dozens of beans-sized pearls, letting the doctor pick and use them. Another person who came for treatment with a minor sore asked Zhu, "Can this disease be cured?" Zhu replied vaguely. The other person said, "That person will definitely die; advise you not to treat him." Zhu was surprised and asked, "Do you also recognize the condition?" The other person replied, "If I could recognize the condition, I would not dare to speak to you so lightly. I divined for him and knew he would not survive." Zhu asked why. The other person said, "In the past, I and you served in the army in Chu. The official grain supplies were insufficient, so we resorted to looting. I was also involved. But I only took people's money and did not dare to harm people's lives. One day, an old man carrying two children fleeing from disaster, a boy around ten years old and a girl around eight or nine years old, all his young grandchildren, passed by. I spared them, but they were killed by others."

    396

    某劫以威，叟战栗惶恐，跪云。『仅存川资二十两，聊以买命。』某收而释之。去教武，忽思叟棉衣甚厚，当有财物，追而索之。叟崩角流涕云。『并无他物，前途惟丐食耳。』悲惧殆甚可怜。某欲脱其棉衣，叟不肯云。『严冬酷寒，去衣必冻死。身已衰朽不足惜，其如二童孙何？望垂怜之。』予亦代为劝止。某益怒，曰：『尔尚怜惜二雏，先使尔绝念。』刃下，杀其女孩。叟泣不能仰，某攫其发，以刀槊其胸，洞穿于背，脏腑俱见，须臾而毙，解其衣。其童子惊毙道左。匆匆舍之而归。拆其衣，败絮中明珠甚伙，金叶数十张。归家暴富，田庐产业，居然素封。第数年来死亡相继，今日之疮，与叟之刀伤无异，非天报乎？”后闻果死，家亦败亡殆尽。

    Explanation

    396

    Someone threatened the old man with force, and the old man trembled with fear, kneeling and saying, "I only have twenty taels of travel funds, just to save my life." The person took the money and released him. Later, when he went to teach martial arts, he suddenly thought that the old man's cotton robe was very thick and must have valuables, so he pursued and demanded them. The old man fell to his knees and wept, saying, "I have nothing else, the future is just begging for food." The sorrow and fear were extremely pitiful. The person wanted to strip off his cotton robe, but the old man refused, saying, "It is severe winter cold, without the robe, I would surely freeze to death. My body is already old and worthless, but what about my two grandchildren?" I also tried to persuade him to stop. The person became even angrier, saying, "You still care for your two grandchildren? First, make you give up hope." With a blade, he killed the old man's daughter. The old man cried and couldn't look up, and the person grabbed his hair, stabbed his chest with a knife, piercing through to the back, exposing all his internal organs. He died in a moment, and the person stripped off his robe. The child died in shock on the side of the road. Hurriedly, the person left and returned home. Unpacking the robe, he found many pearls among the rags and several dozen gold leaf sheets. He returned home suddenly rich, with fields, houses, and property, becoming a wealthy landowner. But in the following years, death followed one after another. Today's wound is no different from the old man's knife wound, isn't this divine retribution?" Later it was heard that he indeed died, and his family also perished almost entirely.

    397

    醉茶子曰：报应之说，儒者勿讲，然旷观往事，理却不诬。汉亡于魏，魏亡于晋，篡夺受禅，若合符节。宋诛晋之子孙，齐诛宋之子孙，得天下于寡妇孤儿，即失天下于寡妇孤儿。其间稍存忠厚者，不戳先朝之后，其享国必久，如唐，如宋，如明，其显然者也。天道好还，理自不爽，似不可尽以为诬。至匹夫匹妇，如沧海之一粟，其行事之善恶，天地鬼神，岂屑与较？然报应亦殊不漏。佛氏果报之说，谓补儒书之未言可也。

    Explanation

    397

    醉茶子曰：The concept of retribution, though often avoided by Confucian scholars, is not entirely unfounded when one broadly examines past events. The Han Dynasty fell to the Wei, and the Wei fell to the Jin, with usurpation and abdication occurring as if by design. The Song Dynasty exterminated the descendants of the Jin, and the Qi Dynasty did the same to the Song, seizing power from widows and orphans and losing it in the same manner. Among those who showed some degree of loyalty and kindness, those who did not kill the descendants of the previous dynasty would reign for a long time, as seen in the Tang, Song, and Ming dynasties. Heaven's way favors return, and this principle is inherently just, so it seems that retribution cannot be entirely dismissed as false. As for ordinary people, like grains of sand in the ocean, their good or evil deeds—would Heaven, Earth, gods, and spirits even bother to compare them? Yet, retribution is not entirely absent. The Buddhist doctrine of retribution can be said to supplement what Confucian texts have not fully explained.

    398

    泥桃
    　　房山农人，耕地得古瓦瓮七八具，覆以石板。碎之，得五铢半两钱数万，古绣斑斓，洵汉代物。予曾购得数百枚。有农子羡其事，亦于近处掘之，得一瓦罐，发之，中空无物。罐下有小坎，中有一泥桃大碗许，承以莲座，持归置诸灶台。夜闻满室作响，审视，乃泥桃跳跃如抛球然。怪而灼之以火，吱吱作声，出清烟一缕，自此遂如顽泥。或云是僧家火化后，合骨灰为之。理或然欤？
    千里井
    　　房山农人偶山行，据石小憩。石大如斗，晶莹洞澈，中贮以水。携归示人，或云石胆，可治目疾。诣都求售，并无问者。怒碎之，水溅地上。后又有某乙得一石，大仅如拳，水莹然如玉壶冰。携至都，遇西人请其值，对以百金。西人云：“如此宝，价岂仅是，客得毋戏耶？”乃赠以四百金。乙欣然受之，曰：“实不相欺，仆本拾之山中者，不知其为何宝焉？”西人云：“此名千里井。置诸坎中，水用之不竭，行军赖之。故宝耳。”

    Explanation

    398

    A Clay Peach

    A farmer from Fangshan, while plowing the fields, discovered seven or eight ancient clay jars, covered with stone slabs. When he broke them open, he found tens of thousands of coins of the Wuzhu and Banliang denominations, along with ancient, colorful embroidery, confirming that they were indeed from the Han Dynasty. I once purchased several hundred of these coins. Another farmer, envious of this discovery, also dug near the site and found a clay jar. When he opened it, it was empty. Below the jar, there was a small depression containing a clay peach about the size of a bowl, supported by a lotus base. He carried it back and placed it on his stove. At night, he heard the whole room making noise. Upon closer examination, the clay peach was jumping like a thrown ball. He found this strange and burned it with fire, which made a sizzling sound and produced a thin trail of smoke. From then on, it became like ordinary clay. Some say it was made from the ashes of a monk after cremation. This theory might be true.

    The Thousand-Mile Well

    Another farmer from Fangshan was walking in the mountains and sat on a stone to rest. The stone was as big as a winnowing basket, crystal clear and transparent, containing water inside. He brought it back to show others, who said it was a stone gall, which could treat eye diseases. He went to the capital to sell it, but no one was interested. Angered, he broke it, causing the water to splash on the ground. Later, someone else found a stone as big as a fist, with water as clear as ice in a jade pot. He brought it to the capital and met a Westerner who asked about its value. He said it was worth a hundred taels of silver. The Westerner said, "Such a treasure, its price isn't just this, isn't the guest joking?" Then he gave him four hundred taels of silver. The person happily accepted it and said, "I'm not deceiving you, I originally found it in the mountains, I don't know what kind of treasure it is." The Westerner said, "It's called a 'Thousand-Mile Well.' Placed in a depression, the water never runs out, it's relied upon during military campaigns. That's why it's a treasure."

    399

    灶神
    　　邑牛姥，幼时，其家祭灶，见有神人盔铠骏马，遍体金光，绚烂夺目，自屋内出，冉冉升霄而去，与世所传着皂衣者迥殊，是不可解矣。
    尸变
    　　尸变一事，近世恒有，不可目为乌有子虚也。有营卒永平人，从军去家百里，遇贼断其臂，负痛而返，奔波颇苦。官遣人随之，与之食则食，否则不语。及至家，入门悲喜交集，方欲语，猝然而倒，抚之气绝。有友人客临安时，村中赛会演戏。方卓午，村人聚观甚多，忽见一人缨冠袍服，闭目不语，怀抱担杖跳跃而来。识者曰：“此某家翁新死者，胡为乎来？”众骇奔窜，乱如沸鼎。或投以枕，尸蹶然倒。又典商伙友某卒，停尸未殓，待其亲丁人而未至，尸忽起。守者奔走，尸绕院盘桓数刻，始归，卧原处不动。凡此皆尸变之类。若营卒则猝受重伤，气聚未便即死，归则心事了而气散毙矣，与尸变不同，然亦可惨矣。

    Explanation

    399

    The Kitchen God
    A woman from the village, known as "Ox Grandma," was young when her family performed a ritual to honor the Kitchen God. She saw a divine figure, adorned with a helmet, armor, and riding a fine horse, covered in golden light, vividly dazzling. The figure emerged from inside the house, ascended slowly into the sky, and vanished, which was very different from the commonly believed depiction of the Kitchen God wearing black clothing. This was an inexplicable event.

    Corpse Reanimation
    The phenomenon of corpse reanimation has occurred frequently in recent times and cannot be regarded as nonexistent or imaginary. There was a soldier from Yongping who, while on a military campaign, was attacked and had his arm severed. He returned home, suffering greatly from the pain. The officials sent someone to accompany him. When he was given food, he ate; otherwise, he remained silent. Upon arriving home, he was overwhelmed with a mix of sorrow and joy. As he was about to speak, he suddenly collapsed, and upon touching him, it was discovered that he had died. Another friend of mine, while visiting Lin'an, witnessed a village festival with performances. At noon, many villagers gathered to watch. Suddenly, a man appeared, wearing a ceremonial cap and robe, with closed eyes and not speaking, holding a staff and jumping forward. Those who recognized him said, "This is the newly deceased master of a certain household; why is he here?" The crowd panicked and fled in disorder, like boiling water. Someone threw a pillow at him, and the corpse fell over abruptly. Another partner in trade, who had recently died, was still lying there without being buried, waiting for his relatives to arrive, which had not yet happened. Suddenly, the corpse rose. The guards ran away, and the corpse wandered around the courtyard for a while before returning to lie still in the original place. All these incidents are examples of corpse reanimation. In the case of the soldier, he suddenly suffered a severe injury, and his vital energy was still concentrated, so he did not die immediately. Upon returning home, his affairs were settled, and his vital energy dispersed, leading to his death. This is different from corpse reanimation, but it is still very sad.

    400

    产蛆
    　　静海西村，有农家妇产一物，状如蛆，前锐后齐，长六寸许。有口，饥就母怀食乳，饱则卧枕席间。其家恶而杀之，刀斧不能伤。置之隘巷，辄复自还。数年渐长，家人习见勿怪也。与之糇粮亦能食。今长数尺，粗如匏，颇似有知。妇后又生子，每出，呼蛆守之。蛆蠕蠕然卧橱边，儿匍匐，蛆则阻之。每有亲友至，蛆自匿，客去复出。有老妪曾见之，今仍存，且能隐见不测。

    Explanation

    400

    In the western village of Jinghai, there was a peasant woman who gave birth to a creature that resembled a maggot. It had a pointed front and a flat back, measuring about six inches in length. With a mouth, it would go to its mother's breast to suckle when hungry and lie on the pillow and mat when full. The family hated it and tried to kill it, but neither knife nor axe could harm it.
